Direction | Vision / Mission / Objectives | Top management intent | Strategic intent statement | No | | 2. External Scan | PESTEL, Porter's 5 Forces, EFE | Macro + industry data | List of OT + EFE Total | Yes (EFE) | | 3. Internal Scan | Value Chain, RBV, Resource Audit, IFE | Firm data | List of SW + IFE Total | Yes (IFE) | | 4. Generate Alternatives | SWOT / TOWS Matrix | SW + OT | 3–5 strategies | No | | 5. Evaluate & Choose | QSPM (preferred) / BCG / GE-McKinsey | Weights, Ratings, AS | Selected strategy with highest score | Yes (QSPM) | | 6. Document | Operations Strategy Statement | Chosen strategy | 4 decision areas defined | No | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: The Strategy Formulation Process (Top-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD start1([Start of Formulation Phase]) --> step1A[Step 1: Define Vision Mission and Objectives] step1A --> step1B[Step 2: Conduct External Environmental Scan] step1B --> step1C[PESTEL Analysis] step1B --> step1D[Porters Five Forces] step1C --> step1E[Build EFE Matrix] step1D --> step1E step1E --> step2A[Step 3: Conduct Internal Analysis] step2A --> step2B[Value Chain Analysis] step2A --> step2C[Resource Based View RBV] step2B --> step2D[Build IFE Matrix] step2C --> step2D step2D --> step3A[Step 4: Generate Strategic Alternatives] step3A --> step3B[SWOT TOWS Matrix] step3B --> step3C[Filter 3 to 5 Viable Strategies] step3C --> step4A[Step 5: Evaluate and Choose Strategy] step4A --> step4B{QSPM Tool} step4B --> step4C[Compute TAS for each strategy] step4C --> step4D[Select highest TAS] step4D --> step5A[Step 6: Document Operations Strategy] step5A --> step5B[Process Choice] step5A --> step5C[Supply Chain Design] step5A --> step5D[Capacity Strategy] step5A --> step5E[Quality Strategy] step5E --> end1([Formulation Complete Handoff to Implementation]) ``` ### Diagram 2: SWOT / TOWS Matrix Structure (4-Quadrant Decision Grid) ```mermaid quadrantChart title SWOT TOWS Strategy Generation Grid x-axis Internal Origin --> External Origin y-axis Helpful --> Harmful quadrant-1 SO Use Strengths to Capture Opportunities quadrant-2 WO Overcome Weaknesses to Capture Opportunities quadrant-3 ST Use Strengths to Avoid Threats quadrant-4 WT Minimize Weaknesses and Avoid Threats ``` > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid `quadrantChart` here is a behavioural block-level chart that visually maps the four SWOT quadrants. It captures the *position* of each strategy type (SO, WO, ST, WT) based on the Helpful–Harmful and Internal–External axes. Use this in your answer script to earn quick "presentation" marks. ### Diagram 3: BCG Matrix Block Layout ```mermaid quadrantChart title BCG Portfolio Matrix for Product Strategy x-axis Low Market Share --> High Market Share y-axis Low Market Growth --> High Market Growth quadrant-1 Question Marks High Share Low Growth quadrant-2 Stars High Share High Growth quadrant-3 Dogs Low Share Low Growth quadrant-4 Cash Cows High Share Low Growth in 2D this prints flipped ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Mermaid Quadrant Charts:** Mermaid renders quadrants counter-clockwise starting at top-right. The "Cash Cows" label here is positioned in the bottom-right of the rendered chart, which is its standard academic location (high share, low growth). Do not be confused by the textual coordinates in the chart comment. ### Diagram 4: QSPM Logical Flow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR inp1[IFE Matrix
Weights and Ratings] --> qspm1[QSPM Input Layer] inp2[EFE Matrix
Weights and Ratings] --> qspm1 inp3[List of Strategies
from SWOT] --> qspm1 qspm1 --> qspm2[Step A: List Internal and External Factors] qspm2 --> qspm3[Step B: Assign Weights from IFE and EFE] qspm3 --> qspm4[Step C: Assign Attractiveness Scores AS 1 to 4] qspm4 --> qspm5[Step D: Compute TAS for each strategy] qspm5 --> qspm6[Step E: Sum TAS column for each strategy] qspm6 --> qspm7[Step F: Select strategy with highest TAS] qspm7 --> out1[Selected Strategy
Ready for Implementation] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Define strategy formulation. List its six main steps.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Strategy formulation is the analytical phase of the strategic management process in which an organization identifies and selects the most appropriate courses of action to achieve its vision and mission. **Six Steps (1.5 marks):** 1. Establishing the vision, mission, and objectives. 2. Conducting the external environmental scan (PESTEL, Porter's Five Forces). 3. Conducting the internal organizational analysis (Value Chain, RBV). 4. Generating strategic alternatives (SWOT/TOWS). 5. Evaluating and choosing the strategy (QSPM, BCG, GE-McKinsey). 6. Documenting the operations strategy. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** **Differentiate between IFE Matrix and EFE Matrix in 3 points.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | S.No. | IFE Matrix | EFE Matrix | |---|---|---| | 1 | Evaluates **internal** environment | Evaluates **external** environment | | 2 | Identifies **Strengths and Weaknesses** | Identifies **Opportunities and Threats** | | 3 | Total score > 2.5 implies strong internal position | Total score > 2.5 implies responsive to environment | | 4 | Inputs: resources, capabilities, value chain | Inputs: PESTEL, industry forces, market data | | 5 | Tool used: Resource-Based View | Tool used: PESTEL + Porter's Five Forces | --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — QSPM Computation and Strategy Selection** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Model]** A Kerala-based electric vehicle (EV) startup is evaluating two growth strategies: - **Strategy P:** Mass-market affordable EVs (Cost Leadership) - **Strategy Q:** Premium long-range EVs (Differentiation) The IFE + EFE combined data and the Attractiveness Scores (AS) assigned by the strategic planning team are given below. **Build the QSPM, compute Total Attractiveness Scores (TAS) for both strategies, and recommend the best strategy with justification.** (7 + 7 marks) | # | Factor | Weight | AS (P) | AS (Q) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Strong in-house battery R&D | 0.18 | 2 | 4 | | 2 | Limited charging infrastructure | 0.10 | 4 | 2 | | 3 | Government FAME-II subsidies | 0.12 | 4 | 3 | | 4 | Skilled but small engineering team | 0.08 | 3 | 4 | | 5 | High import duty on lithium cells | 0.10 | 3 | 1 | | 6 | Growing eco-conscious buyers | 0.14 | 3 | 4 | | 7 | Strong competitor (Tata, Ather) | 0.10 | 2 | 3 | | 8 | Available VC funding in Kerala | 0.08 | 4 | 2 | | 9 | Weak service network outside Kerala | 0.10 | 3 | 1 | | **Total** | | **1.00** | | | **Sub-part (a): Build the QSPM and compute TAS (7 marks).** **Step-by-step model solution:** We must first **normalize the weights** to sum to 1.00 (they already do, so we proceed). For each factor, $TAS = AS \times W_i$. **Computation for Strategy P:** $$TAS_P = \sum_{i=1}^{9} (AS_{P,i} \times W_i)$$ $$TAS_P = (2 \times 0.18) + (4 \times 0.10) + (4 \times 0.12) + (3 \times 0.08) + (3 \times 0.10) + (3 \times 0.14) + (2 \times 0.10) + (4 \times 0.08) + (3 \times 0.10)$$ $$TAS_P = 0.36 + 0.40 + 0.48 + 0.24 + 0.30 + 0.42 + 0.20 + 0.32 + 0.30$$ $$TAS_P = 3.02$$ **Computation for Strategy Q:** $$TAS_Q = (4 \times 0.18) + (2 \times 0.10) + (3 \times 0.12) + (4 \times 0.08) + (1 \times 0.10) + (4 \times 0.14) + (3 \times 0.10) + (2 \times 0.08) + (1 \times 0.10)$$ $$TAS_Q = 0.72 + 0.20 + 0.36 + 0.32 + 0.10 + 0.56 + 0.30 + 0.16 + 0.10$$ $$TAS_Q = 2.82$$ **Sub-part (b): Recommend the best strategy and justify (7 marks).** **Step 1 — Comparison:** $$TAS_P = 3.02 \;\; > \;\; TAS_Q = 2.82$$ **Step 2 — Recommendation:** **Strategy P (Mass-market affordable EVs — Cost Leadership)** is recommended. **Step 3 — Justification (3-point argument, mandatory in KTU valuation):** 1. **Highest Total Attractiveness Score:** $TAS_P = 3.02$ exceeds $TAS_Q = 2.82$ by 0.20, making P mathematically the most attractive option. 2. **Strategic Fit with External Environment:** The high weights on FAME-II subsidies (0.12), eco-conscious buyers (0.14), and limited charging infrastructure (0.10) all favor an affordable, high-volume strategy that addresses price-sensitive early adopters. 3. **Leverages Internal Strengths:** The firm has VC funding access (0.08) and skilled engineering (0.08), which are critical for scaling a cost-leadership product line rather than a high-end premium product requiring deep R&D pockets. **Step 4 — Implementation Hint (1 mark):** The startup should focus on operations decisions like **standardized modular platform design**, **high-volume battery sourcing contracts**, and **lean assembly cells** to sustain the cost-leadership position. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Vision, Mission, and Strategic Analysis Tools** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style Model]** **(a)** Explain the role of **Vision and Mission statements** in strategy formulation. Differentiate between them with a suitable example. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain **Porter's Five Forces Model** and the **Value Chain Analysis** as tools of strategy formulation. How are they used together in an operations strategy decision? (7 marks) **Model Solution for Sub-part (a):** **Role of Vision in Strategy Formulation (2 marks):** - The vision statement is a **future-oriented declaration** of what the organization aspires to become. It sets the long-term direction and inspires stakeholders. In strategy formulation, the vision acts as the *anchor point* — every alternative strategy must be tested against the vision for alignment. - Example: ISRO's vision *"To harness space technology for national development while pursuing space science research and planetary exploration."* **Role of Mission in Strategy Formulation (2 marks):** - The mission statement defines the **fundamental purpose, scope, and operations** of the organization — the "why we exist today." It narrows the vision into actionable operational terms and feeds directly into objective setting. - Example: ISRO's mission *"To design and develop launch vehicles, satellites, and associated ground systems for space technology applications."* **Differences Table (3 marks):** | S.No. | Vision | Mission | |---|---|---| | 1 | Future-oriented (where we want to be) | Present-oriented (why we exist now) | | 2 | Broad, abstract, aspirational | Concrete, operational, measurable | | 3 | Long time horizon (10–25 years) | Short to medium term (3–10 years) | | 4 | Set by top leadership with stakeholder input | Derived from vision, owned by all levels | | 5 | Changes rarely | Revised as business evolves | **Model Solution for Sub-part (b):** **Porter's Five Forces Model (3 marks):** The model analyzes the **competitive structure of an industry** through five forces: 1. **Threat of New Entrants** — barriers to entry (capital, patents, regulation). 2. **Bargaining Power of Suppliers** — concentration of suppliers, switching costs. 3. **Bargaining Power of Buyers** — buyer concentration, price sensitivity. 4. **Threat of Substitutes** — availability of alternative products. 5. **Intensity of Rivalry** — number of competitors, industry growth, fixed costs. The collective intensity of these forces determines the **average profitability of the industry** and shapes the firm's choice of generic strategy (cost leadership, differentiation, or focus). **Value Chain Analysis (2.5 marks):** Porter's value chain divides the firm into: - **Primary activities:** Inbound Logistics → Operations → Outbound Logistics → Marketing & Sales → Service. - **Support activities:** Firm Infrastructure, HR Management, Technology Development, Procurement. Each activity contributes to **value creation** and **cost**. The firm identifies which activities are *strategically important* and whether they provide a **cost advantage or differentiation advantage**. **How They Work Together in Operations Strategy (1.5 marks):** - Porter's Five Forces identifies **whether the industry is attractive** (high forces = low attractiveness, low forces = high attractiveness). - Value Chain Analysis identifies **where within the firm** the competitive advantage will be built (e.g., superior operations or unique inbound logistics). - The outputs feed into the **SWOT/TOWS matrix**: Five Forces data → Opportunities/Threats; Value Chain data → Strengths/Weaknesses. - The chosen strategy is then operationalized through the **four operations decisions**: process choice, supply chain design, capacity strategy, and quality strategy. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Conflating strategy formulation with strategy implementation** — formulation is *choosing*; implementation is *executing*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if the answer drifts into staffing, leadership, or KPIs. > 2. **Skipping weight normalization** in IFE/EFE — if weights do not sum to 1.00, the entire IFE/EFE calculation is considered invalid. > 3. **Omitting the interpretation step** — simply tabulating numbers without stating *"Total > 2.5 implies strong internal position"* loses 1 mark. > 4. **Listing all 20 SWOT factors in the QSPM** instead of focusing on the **most strategically relevant** 8–12 — examiners reward conciseness. > 5. **Forgetting the "selection logic" justification** — just stating "TAS is highest, so select this" without linking the chosen strategy to the firm's IFE/EFE profile loses the 2-mark justification. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Strategy formulation = the analytical phase** that produces a chosen course of action; it is *distinct from implementation*. - **Sequence to memorize:** *Vision → Mission → Objectives → External Scan (PESTEL + 5 Forces) → EFE → Internal Scan (Value Chain + RBV) → IFE → SWOT/TOWS → QSPM → Selected Strategy.* - **IFE & EFE weights must sum to 1.00** for that matrix; ratings are on a 1–4 scale. - **Threshold rule:** IFE/EFE total > 2.5 means strong / responsive; < 2.5 means weak / unresponsive. - **QSPM rule:** The strategy with the **highest TAS** is selected; TAS is computed as $TAS = \sum (AS_i \times W_i)$ using combined IFE+EFE weights. - **Porter's Three Generic Strategies:** Cost Leadership, Differentiation, Focus — the foundation of competitive positioning. - **BCG Matrix:** Stars (high share, high growth) → invest; Cash Cows (high share, low growth) → harvest; Question Marks (low share, high growth) → selective investment; Dogs (low share, low growth) → divest. - **Value Chain:** 5 primary + 4 support activities; operations strategy focuses on the **"Operations"** box and how it interacts with logistics, marketing, and service. - **RBV (VRIN):** A resource is a source of sustained competitive advantage only if it is **Valuable, Rare, Inimitable, and Non-substitutable**. - **Always justify the final strategy** by linking it back to the IFE/EFE profile — never leave a QSPM answer as a "pure number" decision. - **Operations-specific output:** The chosen strategy must translate into the four decision areas — *process choice, supply chain, capacity, quality strategy*. - **Common KTU keywords to expect:** "Build the IFE", "Compute TAS", "Interpret the EFE total", "Recommend a strategy with justification", "Differentiate strategy formulation and implementation". > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "VEMOS QI":** > **V**ision → **E**xternal scan → **M**ission → **O**bjectives → **S**WOT → **Q**SPM → **I**mplementation handoff. > Walk into the exam hall humming this and you will not miss a step in any 14-mark answer.

topics.title: Order qualifying and order winning attribute
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Order qualifying and order winning attribute

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Operations Management (PECBT865), the concepts of **Order Qualifying Attributes (OQA)** and **Order Winning Attributes (OWA)** form the strategic bridge between an organization's marketing function and its operations function. These terms were formally codified in the landmark operations strategy framework proposed by **Terry Hill** (1989/2000), and they have since become foundational vocabulary in the KTU syllabus for distinguishing what a company *must* do to compete in a market versus what it *must* do to actually *win* customers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 (Operations Strategy):**
> *Order Qualifying Attributes* are the baseline criteria that a product, service, or process **must possess** for a customer to even **consider** it as a viable purchase option. *Order Winning Attributes* are the differentiating criteria that **cause a customer to actually select and purchase** that product/service over those of competing firms.

### 1.1.1 Order Qualifying Attribute (OQA)

An **Order Qualifying Attribute** is a characteristic of a product or service that a customer expects as a **threshold level of performance**. If the attribute is not present at an acceptable level, the product is **rejected** by the customer from the consideration set. However, possessing this attribute at an exceptionally high level does **not** by itself guarantee that the customer will buy — because *every* competing product in the market is presumed to already meet this minimum threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> "An Order Qualifying Attribute is a feature that is *necessary* for a product/service to be considered by the customer, but is *not sufficient* to win the order."

**Classic Examples of OQA:**
- A car must have functioning brakes (qualifying).
- A bank account must be insured by a recognized body (qualifying).
- A mobile phone must be able to make and receive calls (qualifying).
- A degree from a KTU-affiliated college must be valid for the Kerala Public Service Commission (qualifying).

### 1.1.2 Order Winning Attribute (OWA)

An **Order Winning Attribute** is a characteristic that **differentiates** the firm's product/service from competitors in the eyes of the customer, and is the **decisive factor** that causes the order to be placed. It is the source of **competitive advantage**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> "An Order Winning Attribute is a feature of a product/service that, if provided *better* than competitors, will *win* the customer's order by directly influencing the buying decision."

**Classic Examples of OWA:**
- A luxury car offering heated seats and superior ride comfort (winning).
- A bank offering 24×7 digital onboarding and zero-balance accounts (winning).
- A smartphone offering the best camera system in its price band (winning).
- A university offering 100% placement assistance with top MNCs (winning).

### 1.1.3 The Hidden Third Category — Order Losing Attribute

A closely related concept that examiners frequently use in **Part A 3-mark questions** is the **Order Losing Attribute**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An *Order Losing Attribute* is a feature that, if absent at the threshold level, will cause a customer to **immediately reject** the product — even if many other winning attributes are present. It is essentially a *hard* qualifying attribute with zero tolerance.

**Example:** A laptop with poor battery life, or an airline with multiple safety violations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Real-World Walkthrough

Imagine you are a **Kerala Engineering Graduate** applying for a software job in 2025.

**Stage 1 — The Qualifying Filter (Order Qualifying):**
The HR manager's first filter is automated. Your resume is scanned for:
- A B.Tech degree (any branch from an AICTE-approved university — KTU qualifies).
- A minimum CGPA of 6.0.
- Knowledge of at least one programming language.

If you **fail** any one of these, your resume is **discarded instantly** — no matter how brilliant your projects are. These are the **Order Qualifying Attributes** of the candidate's "product" (you).

**Stage 2 — The Winning Differentiator (Order Winning):**
Among the candidates who passed Stage 1, the HR manager now compares them. The candidates who win the offer have:
- A live GitHub portfolio with deployed projects.
- Internship experience at a recognized firm.
- Strong communication and DSA problem-solving skills.

These are the **Order Winning Attributes** that actually *secured* the job offer.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Shortcut for Exams:** *Qualifiers get you into the interview room. Winners get you the offer letter.*

### 1.3 Why These Concepts Matter in KTU Module 1

In Module 1 of PECBT865, Operations Management, this topic is critical because:
1. It defines the **boundary** between **Marketing Strategy** (what the customer wants) and **Operations Strategy** (what the firm must *build* internally to deliver it).
2. It prevents the classic KTU-marked mistake of trying to be "best at everything," which leads to operational inefficiency.
3. It is the foundation of subsequent Module 1 topics such as **Operations Strategy**, **Competitive Priorities**, and the **Four Performance Objectives** (Quality, Speed, Dependability, Flexibility, Cost).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Valuation Key — Conceptual Clarity:**
> Examiners *expect* you to clearly distinguish between the *threshold* nature of qualifiers and the *differential* nature of winners. Confusing the two is the single most common reason students lose marks on this topic.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix

## 2.1 The Terry Hill Framework — Structured Logic Breakdown

Terry Hill's framework argues that the operational resources of a firm should be deployed in alignment with the order-qualifying and order-winning attributes identified by the marketing function. Let us break this down into its **five logical steps** as expected in a 14-mark answer.

### Step 1 — Market Segmentation and Customer Identification
The firm first segments the market and identifies the target customers and their **buying criteria**. Buying criteria are the *outputs* that customers value.

### Step 2 — Classification into Qualifying and Winning
Each buying criterion is then classified by marketing into one of three buckets:
- **Order Qualifying Attribute (OQA)** — the "must-have."
- **Order Winning Attribute (OWA)** — the "differentiator."
- **Order Losing Attribute** — the "deal-breaker."

### Step 3 — Translation into Performance Objectives (Operations Strategy)
The qualifying and winning attributes are translated into the **four performance objectives** at the operations level:
- **Quality**
- **Speed**
- **Dependability**
- **Flexibility**
- **Cost** (often added as a fifth in modern KTU texts)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expectation:** You must explicitly state which performance objective supports which order attribute. For example, *Speed of delivery* maps to *Speed performance objective*; *On-time delivery* maps to *Dependability*.

### Step 4 — Resource Deployment
Management aligns its **operations resources** (people, machines, processes, supply chain) to deliver the qualifying attributes at threshold and the winning attributes at a level superior to competitors.

### Step 5 — Dynamic Re-Classification
This is the **time-dimension** of the framework. What is a **Winner today** becomes a **Qualifier tomorrow** as competitors copy the innovation, and what is a **Qualifier today** may become a **Winner tomorrow** if customer preferences shift.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Attribute Comparison Matrix

The following table is the **single most important comparison** students are expected to reproduce in a 14-mark answer:

| Dimension | Order Qualifying Attribute (OQA) | Order Winning Attribute (OWA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Threshold / Necessary | Differential / Sufficient |
| **Customer Expectation** | "I expect this." | "I would love this." |
| **If absent** | Product is **rejected outright** | Product may still be considered |
| **If exceeded significantly** | No extra competitive gain (since all meet it) | Significant competitive gain — wins the order |
| **Source** | Industry / market standard | Firm's own innovation and capability |
| **Stability over time** | Relatively stable | Highly dynamic, copied by rivals |
| **Example (Laptop)** | A working keyboard and screen | A 16-hour battery life |
| **Example (Kerala PSC)** | B.Tech degree from a recognized university | A first-class with distinction + GATE score |
| **Operational implication** | Must be **reliably delivered** at threshold | Must be **continuously innovated** to stay ahead |
| **Cost implication** | Drive **efficiency** and **cost minimization** | Drive **investment** in capability building |

## 2.3 The Dynamic Time-Dimension: Why a Winner Becomes a Qualifier

This is a **high-yield KTU question area**. A standard 7-mark question asks:
*"With the help of an example, explain how an Order Winning Attribute can become an Order Qualifying Attribute over time."*

### 2.3.1 The Logic (Why It Happens)

$$ \text{OWA} \xrightarrow{\text{Customer adoption}} \text{New market expectation} \xrightarrow{\text{Competitor imitation}} \text{OQA} $$

**Detailed explanation:**

1. **Innovation Phase:** Firm F introduces a new feature *X* that delights customers. *X* is a **Winner** because it is rare.
2. **Awareness Phase:** Customers begin to *expect* *X* as a baseline.
3. **Imitation Phase:** Competitors copy *X* because the market now demands it.
4. **Maturation Phase:** *X* becomes an **industry standard** — the new threshold.
5. **Re-classification:** *X* is now an **OQA**, and the firm must hunt for a *new* OWA to maintain leadership.

### 2.3.2 Classic Real-World Example — Mobile Phones

| Year | Attribute | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 2007 | Touchscreen | **OWA** (Apple iPhone) |
| 2013 | Touchscreen | **OQA** (every smartphone has it) |
| 2010 | Front camera for selfies | **OWA** |
| 2016 | High-resolution front camera | **OQA** |
| 2019 | 5G connectivity | **OWA** |
| 2024 | 5G connectivity | **OQA** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to illustrate, always use a *time-series* example with at least **3 time points** to show the transition clearly.

## 2.4 Common KTU Pitfalls (Why Students Lose Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Treating OQA and OWA as Fixed Labels**
> A student writes: *"Air India's price is an OWA."* This is wrong if the entire airline industry has standardized on similar pricing. An attribute's status depends on **time, market segment, and competitive context**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing "Customer Requirement" with OWA**
> A customer requirement becomes an OWA *only if* it *differentiates* the firm. A requirement that all competitors meet is an OQA, not a winner.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 — Ignoring the Operations Translation**
> A marketing-only answer (e.g., "customers want good service") is **incomplete** for a 14-mark question. You must show how the OWA/OQA is **translated into operations performance objectives** (Quality, Speed, Dependability, Flexibility, Cost).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical OQA | Typical OWA |
|---|---|---|
| **Automobile (e.g., Maruti vs BMW)** | Functional brakes, basic safety | Brand prestige, luxury interior |
| **Hospitality (Kerala Tourism)** | Cleanliness, basic amenities | Personalized Ayurvedic experience |
| **BPO / IT Services** | Data security, basic communication | Domain expertise, cost efficiency |
| **Civil Engineering Contractor** | Regulatory approvals, code compliance | On-time delivery, quality of finish |
| **Higher Education (KTU College)** | AICTE approval, syllabus coverage | Placement record, industry tie-ups |

## 2.6 Summary Formula Sheet (Conceptual Equations)

The following conceptual "equations" are the **core logic identities** the KTU examiner expects you to be able to write:

$$ \text{Order Won} = f(\text{OQA satisfied}) \times g(\text{OWA delivered better than competitor}) $$

$$ \text{OWA}_{\text{time}=t+1} = f(\text{Competitor imitation}, \text{Customer expectation shift}) \cdot \text{OWA}_{\text{time}=t} $$

$$ \text{OQA}_{\text{new}} = \lim_{t \to T} \text{OWA}_{\text{old}} \quad \text{as technology matures} $$

$$ \text{Operations Strategy} = \text{Align}(\text{Performance Objectives} \mid \text{OQA, OWA, Market Segment}) $$

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Applying the Framework to a Kerala Engineering College

This is the type of **structured analytical response** expected for a 14-mark KTU Module 1 question.

### Step 1: Identify the Market and Customer

The "product" is a **B.Tech degree** offered by a private engineering college in Kerala, affiliated to KTU. The "customer" is a **prospective student** (and their parents) seeking admission.

### Step 2: Identify the Buying Criteria

Through market research, the college identifies the following student criteria:

1. AICTE approval and KTU affiliation.
2. Recognized B.Tech degree (valid for higher studies / PSC).
3. Affordable fee structure.
4. Campus placement record.
5. Modern labs and infrastructure.
6. Industry internships.
7. Hostel and transport facilities.
8. Extracurricular and sports culture.

### Step 3: Classify Each Criterion

| # | Buying Criterion | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AICTE + KTU Affiliation | **OQA (and Order Losing)** | Without this, the degree is worthless in India — *hard threshold*. |
| 2 | Recognized B.Tech Degree | **OQA** | Required for higher studies, PSC, foreign equivalency. |
| 3 | Affordable Fees | **OQA** (becoming OWA for some segments) | Most private colleges cluster around similar fee ranges. |
| 4 | Placement Record | **OWA** | Highly variable between colleges; the *decisive* factor for most families. |
| 5 | Modern Labs | **OWA** | Differentiates; older colleges may lag. |
| 6 | Industry Internships | **OWA** | Differentiation through industry exposure. |
| 7 | Hostel and Transport | **OQA** | Practical necessity for outstation students. |
| 8 | Sports and Culture | **OWA** (for niche segments) | Differentiator for some students. |

### Step 4: Translate to Operations Performance Objectives

Now we map the above to the **four performance objectives** at the *operations level of the college*:

| Attribute | Operations Performance Objective Required |
|---|---|
| AICTE/KTU Affiliation (OQA) | **Dependability** of compliance — zero tolerance for lapses. |
| Affordable Fees (OQA) | **Cost** efficiency in college operations. |
| Placement Record (OWA) | **Quality** of training + **Speed** of industry onboarding. |
| Modern Labs (OWA) | **Flexibility** of curriculum and **Investment** in capex. |
| Industry Internships (OWA) | **Flexibility** + strong industry **partnership network**. |

### Step 5: Specify Resource Deployment

- **Teaching staff**: Recruit PhDs and industry-experienced faculty (supports OWA on placements).
- **Lab equipment**: Periodic capex cycle (supports OWA on labs).
- **T&P cell**: Dedicated 24/7 placement office (supports OWA on placements).
- **Compliance officer**: Ensures AICTE/KTU norms never lapse (supports OQA on affiliation).

### Step 6: Dynamic Re-Classification Planning

The college must anticipate that:
- **Online learning (post-2020)** has shifted "flexible delivery" from OWA → OQA.
- **AI/ML skills** are an emerging OWA; the college must integrate them.
- **International accreditation** (e.g., NBA at Tier-1) may transition from OWA → OQA within 5 years.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — The Evolution of Smartwatch Features

We will explicitly demonstrate the **OWA → OQA transition** step by step.

### Step 1: Initial State (2015)

The smartwatch market is nascent. Apple Watch and a few Android competitors exist.

| Feature | OQA / OWA |
|---|---|
| Time display | **OQA** |
| Step counter | **OWA** (differentiator!) |
| Bluetooth pairing | **OQA** |
| Heart-rate monitor | **OWA** (rare, premium) |

### Step 2: Mid Stage (2018)

The category has matured. The *step counter* is now in every phone anyway, so on a watch it is **expected**. The *heart-rate monitor* has been widely adopted.

| Feature | OQA / OWA |
|---|---|
| Step counter | **OQA** (industry standard) |
| Heart-rate monitor | **OQA** (industry standard) |
| ECG / SpO2 sensor | **OWA** (still differentiating) |
| Cellular (LTE) connectivity | **OWA** (premium) |

### Step 3: Mature Stage (2023)

ECG has become widely available. Cellular has become standard in the premium tier.

| Feature | OQA / OWA |
|---|---|
| ECG | **OQA** (in premium segment) |
| Cellular LTE | **OQA** (in premium segment) |
| Blood glucose trend tracking | **OWA** (emerging) |
| Sleep stage AI analysis | **OWA** (emerging) |

> [!TIP]
> **What the KTU Examiner Wants to See:**
> 1. A *clear time-series* with at least 3 stages.
> 2. An *explicit naming* of the attribute that migrated.
> 3. A *strategic insight* — what the firm should now do (hunt for new OWA).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for the Firm

The following 2x2 decision matrix helps a firm decide *how to invest* in attributes:

|  | Currently an OQA | Currently an OWA |
|---|---|---|
| **Likely to remain OWA** | Maintain at threshold; minimize cost | Invest heavily; protect with IP / patents |
| **Likely to become OQA soon** | Monitor; do not over-invest | Plan for the transition; pre-empt with new innovation |

This matrix is a high-yield concept for KTU Module 1 because it operationalizes Hill's framework for **resource allocation**.

---

## 3.4 The Three Conditional Logics of OWA → OQA Migration

A 7-mark question may ask: *"Under what conditions does an OWA become an OQA?"*

### Condition 1: Competitor Imitation

$$ \text{OWA} \rightarrow \text{OQA} \iff \text{Competitor imitation rate} \geq \text{Customer expectation update rate} $$

If competitors copy the feature faster than customers raise their expectations, the feature rapidly becomes a *standard* (OQA).

### Condition 2: Technological Maturity

$$ \text{OWA} \rightarrow \text{OQA} \iff \text{Technology reaches commodity status} $$

Example: Fingerprint sensors went from OWA (in flagship phones) to OQA (in budget phones) once the underlying technology became cheap.

### Condition 3: Regulatory or Industry Standardization

$$ \text{OWA} \rightarrow \text{OQA} \iff \text{Regulator mandates the feature} $$

Example: ABS brakes in Indian two-wheelers were OWA in 2015; once mandated by the government in 2019, they became OQA.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical / Decision Illustration (for board completeness)

While this topic is largely qualitative, a KTU examiner *may* ask you to compare two competing firms using a simple scoring approach.

Suppose two firms, A and B, operate in a market with three buying criteria: **Price, Quality, Delivery Speed**. Customers score each firm out of 10. The market threshold for OQA is **6/10**.

| Criterion | Firm A Score | Firm B Score | Market Threshold | A Classification | B Classification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Price | 7 | 9 | 6 | OQA (meets) | **OWA** (exceeds) |
| Quality | 8 | 5 | 6 | **OWA** (exceeds) | OQA (just meets) |
| Delivery | 6 | 4 | 6 | OQA (just meets) | **Losing Attribute** (below) |

### Interpretation Logic (Step by Step)

- **Firm A** wins customers who value **quality** (OWA for A). It must not allow quality to slip or competitors will match and it will lose.
- **Firm B** wins customers who value **price** (OWA for B). However, its delivery is *below* the threshold — it is a **losing attribute**. Even its price advantage will not save it because customers reject it on the delivery filter.
- **Strategic insight:** Firm B must immediately fix delivery (move it to OQA) *before* leveraging its price advantage. Otherwise the price OWA is invisible to the customer.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** Whenever you see a numerical-style question on this topic, draw a table to show *threshold*, *current performance*, and *classification*. This visual structure earns full marks.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Flow: From Customer Criteria to Operations Deployment

The following **Mermaid block diagram** captures the full transformation pipeline from customer expectations to operational resource deployment, as expected by KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Buying Criteria Identified by Marketing] --> B{Classify Each Criterion}
    B --> C[Order Qualifying Attribute OQA]
    B --> D[Order Winning Attribute OWA]
    B --> E[Order Losing Attribute]
    C --> F[Operations Performance Objectives Threshold]
    D --> G[Operations Performance Objectives Exceed Competitors]
    F --> H[Resource Deployment to Meet Threshold]
    G --> I[Resource Deployment for Differentiation]
    H --> J[Continuous Compliance Monitoring]
    I --> K[Continuous Innovation Cycle]
    K --> L{Time Elapses}
    L --> M[Customer Expectations Evolve]
    L --> N[Competitors Imitate Feature]
    M --> B
    N --> B
```

### Diagram Node Reference Table

| Node | Meaning |
|---|---|
| A | Starting point: market research identifies what customers value. |
| B | The critical decision diamond — classify each criterion. |
| C, D, E | The three possible buckets. |
| F, G | Translation into performance objectives (Quality / Speed / Dependability / Flexibility / Cost). |
| H, I | Resource allocation: compliance for qualifiers, innovation for winners. |
| J, K | Two distinct feedback loops. |
| L | The **time dimension** — Hill's key insight. |
| M, N | The two drivers of the dynamic re-classification. |

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: The OWA to OQA Migration Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 Innovation OWA Leader Reaps Premium] --> P2[Phase 2 Diffusion Customers Expect It]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Imitation Competitors Copy]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Commoditization Now OQA]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5 Hunt for New OWA]
    P5 --> P1
```

### Why This Loop Matters for KTU

This cyclic diagram is **exactly** what a 7-mark "explain with example" answer should illustrate. It shows the *temporal dynamic* of Hill's model.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: The Decision Matrix for Operations Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Attribute Identified] --> Q1{Does every competitor currently meet threshold?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is firm significantly exceeding competitors?}
    Q1 -->|No| Losing[Order Losing Attribute Immediate Fix Required]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Winning[Order Winning Attribute Invest Aggressively]
    Q2 -->|No| Qualifying[Order Qualifying Attribute Maintain at Threshold]
    Winning --> Q3{Is imitation / regulation likely in 3 years?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Transition[Plan Transition Defend with Next Innovation]
    Q3 -->|No| Defend[Protect with IP and Brand Building]
    Qualifying --> Q4{Might this become a winner in a niche segment?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Explore[Explore Segmentation Opportunity]
    Q4 -->|No| Sustain[Sustain Efficiently at Threshold]
```

### Diagram Reading Guide

- The diamond shapes represent **decision points** the firm must work through.
- Each terminal box is an **operational action** that the firm must take.
- This is essentially a **decision tree** that operationalizes Hill's framework for a real KTU-context firm.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Visual: Qualifier vs Winner Performance Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Customer Satisfaction vs Performance Level"
    A1[Low Performance: Below Threshold] --> A2[Threshold OQA Met]
    A2 --> A3[High Performance: OWA Exceeded]
    end
    A1 --> R1[Customer Rejects Product]
    A2 --> R2[Customer Considers Product - but does not buy]
    A3 --> R3[Customer Chooses Product Wins the Order]
```

### Insight Embedded in the Diagram

- Below the threshold → product is **rejected** (and may even become a *losing* attribute).
- At the threshold → product enters the *consideration set* but does not win.
- Above the threshold and beyond competitors → product **wins** the order.

> [!TIP]
> **For Your Exam Notebook:** A small sketch of this kind of satisfaction curve (with a *kink* at the threshold) is a *powerful visual* to drop into your 14-mark answer. Examiners love diagrams.

---

## 4.5 End-to-End Architecture: Hill's Framework in One View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Market and Customer Analysis] --> C1[Buying Criteria Identified]
    C1 --> C2[Classify as OQA / OWA / Losing]
    C2 --> C3[Translate to Performance Objectives]
    C3 --> C4[Deploy Operations Resources]
    C4 --> C5[Monitor and Re-Classify]
    C5 --> M
```

This is the **macro-level loop** that ties Module 1 together — Operations Strategy is *iterative*, not a one-time plan.
