# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECBT865]**
> **Define Order Qualifying Attribute and Order Winning Attribute. Give one example of each.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

- **Order Qualifying Attribute (OQA):** A characteristic of a product or service that a customer expects as a *minimum threshold* of performance. It is necessary for the product to be considered for purchase, but is **not sufficient** to win the order. *(1 mark)*
- **Order Winning Attribute (OWA):** A characteristic that **differentiates** the firm's offering from competitors and is the **decisive factor** in the customer's purchase decision. *(1 mark)*
- **Examples:** OQA — a smartphone must be able to make calls; OWA — a smartphone offering the best camera quality in its price band. *(1 mark)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECBT865]**
> **What is an Order Losing Attribute? Differentiate it from an Order Qualifying Attribute.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

- **Order Losing Attribute:** A characteristic whose absence at the threshold level causes the customer to **immediately and totally reject** the product, even if other winning attributes are present. *(1 mark)*
- **Difference from OQA:** *(2 marks)*

| Aspect | OQA | Order Losing |
|---|---|---|
| Tolerance | Some variability allowed | **Zero tolerance** |
| Outcome if absent | Product not considered | Product actively rejected |
| Example | Decent after-sales service | Safety failure in a vehicle |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Question (14 Marks)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]*
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the concepts of **Order Qualifying Attributes (OQA)** and **Order Winning Attributes (OWA)** as proposed by Terry Hill. Discuss how these attributes are translated into operations performance objectives. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With the help of a suitable example, illustrate how an **Order Winning Attribute can become an Order Qualifying Attribute** over time. What strategic implications does this have for a firm? *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Terry Hill's framework bridges the marketing-operations gap by classifying customer buying criteria into order-qualifying and order-winning attributes.

**Order Qualifying Attribute (OQA) (1.5 marks):**
- Threshold / necessary performance level.
- A customer expects it; its absence leads to product rejection.
- However, *meeting it well* does not win orders because competitors also meet it.

**Order Winning Attribute (OWA) (1.5 marks):**
- Differential / sufficient performance level.
- The attribute that *converts* a customer from "considering" to "buying."
- Must be delivered *better* than competitors to win.

**Translation into Operations Performance Objectives (3 marks):**
- **Quality** — OWA for premium products (defects, conformance).
- **Speed** — OWA in fast-fashion / food delivery.
- **Dependability** — OQA for any B2B contract.
- **Flexibility** — OWA for customized manufacturing.
- **Cost** — OWA in commodity markets; OQA in budget segments.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**The Dynamic Process (3 marks):**
1. **Innovation** — Firm X introduces feature F as an OWA.
2. **Customer adoption** — Customers begin to expect F.
3. **Competitor imitation** — Rivals copy F; it becomes a market standard.
4. **Re-classification** — F is now an OQA.

**Example (3 marks):**
- *Mobile phones:* Touchscreen (OWA in 2007) → OQA by 2013. Fingerprint sensor (OWA in flagship phones 2014) → OQA by 2018.
- *Automobiles:* Airbags and ABS were OWA in entry-level cars in 2010. By 2019, government regulations made them OQA (or even Order Losing if absent).

**Strategic Implications (1 mark):**
- The firm must **continuously innovate** to find a *new* OWA before its current OWA is commoditized.
- Over-investment in an attribute that is migrating to OQA wastes resources.
- Build an innovation pipeline aligned with the **Hill's time-dimension loop**.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** *[Alternative Choice]*
>
> **(a)** What are the **three categories of customer buying criteria** identified by Terry Hill? Compare Order Qualifying and Order Winning attributes across any **five parameters**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A Kerala-based mobile phone resale startup classifies the following customer criteria: (i) working touchscreen, (ii) 6-month warranty, (iii) bundled free cover and earphones, (iv) on-site pickup, (v) verified IMEI, (vi) lowest price. **Classify each** as OQA, OWA, or Order Losing. Justify each with reasoning. *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Three Categories (2 marks):**
1. **Order Qualifying Attribute (OQA)**
2. **Order Winning Attribute (OWA)**
3. **Order Losing Attribute**

**Comparison across 5 parameters (5 marks):**

| Parameter | OQA | OWA |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Necessary for consideration | Decisive for purchase |
| **Customer perception** | "I expect this" | "I prefer this" |
| **If absent** | Product rejected | Product may still sell |
| **If exceeded** | No extra gain | Significant gain |
| **Time stability** | Stable over time | Migrates to OQA |
| **Operational focus** | Reliable threshold delivery | Innovation and differentiation |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks (1 mark per attribute, 1 mark for overall table insight)

| # | Criterion | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Working touchscreen | **OQA** | Customers presume a working screen; if broken, phone is rejected. |
| (ii) | 6-month warranty | **OQA** | Standard expectation in the resale market. |
| (iii) | Free cover and earphones | **OWA** | Differentiator — most resellers charge extra. |
| (iv) | On-site pickup | **OWA** | Service differentiator in a market where most competitors are store-based. |
| (v) | Verified IMEI | **OQA (Order Losing)** | Without it, phone is unusable on networks — *zero tolerance*. |
| (vi) | Lowest price | **OWA** (in price-sensitive segments) | Differentiator; however, if too low, customers may suspect quality. |

**Overall Insight:** The startup's OQA investment should be in *IMEI verification* and *screen quality*. The OWA investment should be in *on-site pickup* and *bundled accessories*. Competing purely on *lowest price* is risky and unsustainable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not write only definitions.** A 14-mark answer *must* include: example, time-dimension, and operations translation. Definitions alone = 4–5 marks.
> 2. **Do not list OWAs as a static list.** Always show that the list is *dynamic* and changes with time and segment.
> 3. **Do not confuse OQA with Order Losing.** A losing attribute has *zero tolerance*; a qualifier is expected at a *reasonable threshold*.
> 4. **Always link back to operations performance objectives.** Marketing-only answers lose 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Avoid generic examples like "good service" or "quality."** Use *specific, measurable* attributes (e.g., "ECG sensor," "3-hour delivery," "10-year warranty").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **OQA = Threshold / Necessary but not sufficient.**
- **OWA = Differentiator / Sufficient to win the order.**
- **Order Losing = Hard threshold with zero tolerance.**
- **Classification is dynamic** — a winner today becomes a qualifier tomorrow as competitors imitate and customers expect.
- **The framework was given by Terry Hill** (1989/2000) — the most cited name in KTU Module 1.
- **Always translate** OQA and OWA into the **four/five performance objectives** (Quality, Speed, Dependability, Flexibility, Cost).
- **Time dimension is the heart of the model** — never present OWAs as a static list.
- **Strategic implication for firms:** Continuously innovate to find the *next* OWA before the current one is commoditized.
- **Kerala-context examples** (KTU colleges, Kerala tourism, PSC, KSEB) score extra goodwill with examiners.
- **Use a table** to show the comparison; examiners reward visual structure.
- **Avoid jargon-only answers** — every concept must be tied to a *measurable attribute* and an *example*.
- **The framework is iterative** — it forms a feedback loop from market analysis back to operations strategy.
- **OWA → OQA migration has three drivers:** competitor imitation, technology commoditization, and regulatory mandates.
- **A firm should not over-invest in OQA** — drive *efficiency* instead, and channel innovation spend into OWA development.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance management: need of maintenance management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maintenance management: need of maintenance management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maintenance Management** is the systematic and integrated function of an operations management system that plans, directs, controls, and optimizes all activities required to keep physical assets, machinery, equipment, and facilities in a desired operating condition or to restore them to that state. It encompasses a combination of administrative, technical, and managerial actions aimed at retaining an item, or restoring it to, a state in which it can perform its required function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Operations Management):**
> Maintenance management is defined as the organized application of resources (men, materials, machines, methods, and money) to ensure that production equipment and utility services are kept in optimum operating condition so as to maximize plant availability, product quality, and operational safety while minimizing downtime and total lifecycle cost.

> [!NOTE]
> The word **"Maintenance"** is derived from the French words *"maintien"* (to keep) and *"tenir"* (to hold), literally meaning **"to hold in place"** — i.e., to keep equipment in the state in which it was designed to function.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — Your Own Body:**

Think of your body as a complex "machine." You visit a doctor for an annual check-up (preventive maintenance), eat well and exercise to keep your heart healthy (predictive maintenance), wear a cast when you break a bone (breakdown maintenance), and replace worn-out organs or joints (corrective/replacement maintenance). If you ignore all of this, your "machine" breaks down completely and stops "producing" (living normally).

In the same way, every factory, vehicle, computer server, or aircraft is a "machine" that needs continuous care. **Maintenance Management is the "healthcare system" of the industrial world.**

### 1.3 Core Objectives of Maintenance Management

The following are the **primary objectives** as outlined in standard KTU Operations Management texts:

1. **Maximize Equipment Availability (Uptime):** Reduce unplanned stoppages.
2. **Minimize Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):** Balance repair cost vs. downtime cost.
3. **Ensure Product Quality:** Worn-out tools cause defects.
4. **Guarantee Safety:** Eliminate hazards to personnel.
5. **Extend Useful Asset Life:** Delay capital replacement.
6. **Optimize Spare Parts Inventory:** Avoid stockouts and overstocking.
7. **Support Production Schedules:** Maintenance must align with production plans.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Standards)

The following metrics are universally recognized in KTU-approved Operations Management curricula:

- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** **30 days (example benchmark)** for a CNC machine.
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** **2 hours (example benchmark)** for critical equipment.
- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE):** World-class benchmark is **≥ 85%**.
- **Maintenance Cost as % of Replacement Value:** Typically **2% – 8%** per year.
- **Equipment Availability Target:** **≥ 95%** for continuous-process industries.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" — Need for Maintenance Management

Maintenance management is **not optional** in any modern manufacturing or service organization. The need arises from a combination of physical, economic, legal, and strategic reasons.

#### A. Physical / Engineering Need
- All physical assets deteriorate due to **wear, tear, corrosion, fatigue, and aging**.
- Without intervention, equipment will fail — leading to production halts.
- Example: A rolling-element bearing in an electric motor has a finite **L10 fatigue life** (typically **40,000 hours**); it *will* fail if not replaced.

#### B. Economic Need
- **Cost of Downtime** > **Cost of Maintenance** in most cases.
- A single hour of unplanned downtime in a modern automobile assembly line can cost **$1,000,000+** (industry benchmark).
- Preventive maintenance is a fraction of breakdown repair cost.

#### C. Quality Need
- A worn cutting tool produces **defective components**, raising rejection rates.
- Poorly maintained temperature sensors cause **out-of-spec products**.

#### D. Safety & Legal Need
- The **Factories Act, 1948 (India)** and **OSHA standards** mandate safe equipment.
- Negligent maintenance can result in accidents, lawsuits, and criminal liability.
- Example: Boiler explosions, crane collapses — often traced to maintenance lapses.

#### E. Strategic / Competitive Need
- High OEE = high productivity = **lower unit cost** = competitive advantage.
- World-class manufacturers (Toyota, Bosch) attribute 30–40% of their cost advantage to excellent maintenance.

### 2.2 Functions of the Maintenance Department

A structured maintenance department performs the following **6 core functions**:

1. **Planning & Scheduling** — When and what to maintain.
2. **Inspection & Monitoring** — Detecting deterioration early.
3. **Execution of Repairs** — Corrective action.
4. **Spare Parts Management** — Inventory of replacement components.
5. **Record Keeping & Documentation** — Maintenance logs, history cards.
6. **Training & Development** — Skill upgrade of maintenance crew.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and frequently tested in KTU End Semester Examinations. Memorize these for guaranteed marks in Part A and Part B numericals.

| **Sl. No.** | **Formula / Metric** | **Equation** | **Units** | **Interpretation** |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Equipment Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$ | **%** | Fraction of time equipment is ready to operate. |
| 2 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | **hours** | Average uptime between two consecutive failures. |
| 3 | **Mean Time To Repair** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | **hours** | Average time taken to restore equipment. |
| 4 | **Failure Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | **failures / hour** | Frequency of failure events. |
| 5 | **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | **dimensionless** | Probability of no failure up to time $t$. |
| 6 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | **%** | Where $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality. |
| 7 | **Maintenance Cost Ratio** | $MCR = \dfrac{\text{Annual Maintenance Cost}}{\text{Replacement Value of Asset}} \times 100$ | **%** | Ideal range: **2%–8%** per year. |
| 8 | **Optimal PM Interval (Bath Tub Curve Min.)** | $T^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot C_p}{C_f \cdot \lambda}}$ | **hours** | Where $C_p$ = PM cost, $C_f$ = Failure cost, $\lambda$ = failure rate. |
| 9 | **Total Cost of Downtime** | $C_d = (L_p + L_o) \times T_d$ | **₹ / $** | $L_p$ = lost production/hour, $L_o$ = lost overhead/hour, $T_d$ = downtime. |
| 10 | **Breakdown Frequency** | $BF = \dfrac{\text{Number of Breakdowns}}{\text{Period (months)}}$ | **breakdowns / month** | Higher BF → poor maintenance system. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** In Reliability Engineering, the exponential reliability function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ assumes a **constant failure rate**, which is valid only during the **useful life** region of the **Bathtub Curve** (infant mortality and wear-out phases excluded). Many KTU students lose marks by applying this blindly to all phases.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Manufacturing:** Justifies TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) investments in SMEs.
- **Aerospace:** Drives MSG-3 (Maintenance Steering Group) logic for aircraft scheduling.
- **Power Plants:** Determines forced-outage rates and grid stability.
- **IT/Data Centers:** SLA-driven preventive maintenance of servers.
- **Civil Engineering:** Structural health monitoring of bridges and buildings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Equipment Availability Equation

The **Equipment Availability (A)** is the cornerstone of maintenance management metrics and is derived from the fundamental definitions of MTBF and MTTR.

**Step 1 — Define the Total Cycle Time of an Equipment:**
The total time elapsed during one operational cycle of any equipment equals the sum of time it was operating and time it was being repaired.

$$
T_{cycle} = T_{operating} + T_{repair}
$$

**Step 2 — Express MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):**
By definition, MTBF is the average operating time between two successive failures. If the equipment was operated for $T_{operating}$ hours and experienced $N$ failures during that time, then:

$$
MTBF = \frac{T_{operating}}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Express MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):**
Similarly, MTTR is the average time spent repairing the equipment. If the total downtime was $T_{repair}$ hours across $N$ repair events:

$$
MTTR = \frac{T_{repair}}{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio:**
Substituting these into the cycle time equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= MTBF \cdot N + MTTR \cdot N \\
T_{cycle} &= N \cdot (MTBF + MTTR)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive Availability:**
Availability is defined as the fraction of total time the equipment is in an operational (ready) state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \\
A &= \frac{N \cdot MTBF}{N \cdot MTBF + N \cdot MTTR} \\
A &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Percentage:**
To express as a percentage (standard reporting format):

$$
A (\%) = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Reliability Function from Failure Rate

**Step 1 — Define Probability of Survival in a Small Interval:**
For a system with constant failure rate $\lambda$, in a small time interval $\Delta t$, the probability of failure is $\lambda \cdot \Delta t$, and the probability of survival is $(1 - \lambda \cdot \Delta t)$.

**Step 2 — Apply Limit as Δt → 0:**
For an infinitesimally small interval $dt$, the change in reliability is:

$$
dR(t) = -\lambda \cdot R(t) \cdot dt
$$

**Step 3 — Separate Variables and Integrate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} &= -\lambda \cdot dt \\
\int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} &= \int_{0}^{t} -\lambda \cdot dt \\
\ln[R(t)] - \ln[R(0)] &= -\lambda t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Initial Condition:**
At $t = 0$, reliability is 1 (system is brand new and working): $R(0) = 1$, so $\ln[R(0)] = 0$.

$$
\ln[R(t)] = -\lambda t
$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to Obtain Final Form:**

$$
\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A boiler operated for **2,400 hours** in a quarter. During this period, it failed **3 times** and the cumulative downtime for repairs was **24 hours**. Calculate: (a) MTBF, (b) MTTR, (c) Availability.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
$T_{operating} = 2400$ hrs, $N = 3$ failures, $T_{repair} = 24$ hrs.

**Part (a) — MTBF:**

$$
MTBF = \frac{T_{operating}}{N} = \frac{2400}{3} = 800 \text{ hours}
$$

**Part (b) — MTTR:**

$$
MTTR = \frac{T_{repair}}{N} = \frac{24}{3} = 8 \text{ hours}
$$

**Part (c) — Availability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100 \\
A &= \frac{800}{800 + 8} \times 100 \\
A &= \frac{800}{808} \times 100 \\
A &= 99.01\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 99.01% availability seems excellent, but for a **24/7 continuous-process boiler**, KTU examiners expect students to also calculate the **downtime hours per year**: $8760 \times (1 - 0.9901) = 87.1$ hours/year. This is the figure operations managers actually use for planning.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Maintenance Approaches

| **Criterion** | **Breakdown Maintenance** | **Preventive Maintenance** | **Predictive Maintenance** |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Trigger** | Failure occurs | Calendar / Usage hours | Condition monitoring data |
| **Cost (Short-term)** | Lowest | Medium | Highest (instrumentation cost) |
| **Cost (Long-term)** | Highest (downtime losses) | Lowest | Medium-Low |
| **Downtime** | Unplanned, long | Planned, medium | Minimal, planned |
| **Spare Parts** | Emergency procurement | Stocked in advance | Just-in-time |
| **Suitability** | Non-critical, low-cost items | General machinery | Critical, high-value assets |
| **Failure Prediction** | None | Statistical | Real-time, sensor-based |
| **KTU Recommended for** | Disposable / low-value items | Standard production lines | CNC, turbines, transformers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Maintenance Management

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corporate Strategy and Objectives] --> B[Maintenance Policy Formulation]
    B --> C[Maintenance Planning Department]
    C --> D[Maintenance Scheduling System]
    D --> E[Preventive Maintenance Crew]
    D --> F[Predictive Maintenance Crew]
    D --> G[Breakdown Maintenance Crew]
    E --> H[Equipment Uptime Maximization]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Spare Parts Inventory Control]
    H --> J[Maintenance Cost Optimization]
    H --> K[Safety and Compliance Audit]
    I --> L[Feedback Loop - KPI Reporting]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> C
    H --> M[Production Output and Quality]
```

### 4.2 The Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate Over Equipment Life

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infant Mortality Phase] --> B[Useful Life Phase]
    B --> C[Wear Out Phase]
    A1[High but decreasing failure rate] -.-> A
    B1[Constant low failure rate] -.-> B
    C1[Rising failure rate] -.-> C
    A2[Action - Burn-in testing, debugging] -.-> A
    B2[Action - Preventive Maintenance] -.-> B
    C2[Action - Replacement, Overhaul] -.-> C
```

### 4.3 Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa) Diagram for Maintenance Failures

```mermaid
fishbone-beta
    %% Uncomment the next line to enable beta feature
    %% beta
    Problem[Equipment Failure or Low Availability]
    Man[Manpower - Lack of training, absenteeism, low skill]
    Method[Method - Poor planning, no SOPs, weak scheduling]
    Machine[Machine - Aging, design defect, wrong selection]
    Material[Material - Bad spares, lubricant quality, contamination]
    Measurement[Measurement - No sensors, calibration error, no KPIs]
    Environment[Environment - Dust, humidity, vibration, temperature]
    Man --> Problem
    Method --> Problem
    Machine --> Problem
    Material --> Problem
    Measurement --> Problem
    Environment --> Problem
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart for Selecting Maintenance Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Equipment Installed] --> Q1{Critical to Production?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{High Replacement Cost?}
    Q1 -- No --> BM[Use Breakdown Maintenance]
    Q2 -- Yes --> PdM[Use Predictive Maintenance with Sensors]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Failure Pattern Predictable?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> PM[Use Preventive Maintenance Schedule]
    Q3 -- No --> RdM[Use Reliability Centered Maintenance RCM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Ishikawa (Fishbone) diagram above identifies the **6M categories** of root causes for poor maintenance performance. In KTU viva voce examinations, students are frequently asked to apply the 6M framework to a given factory problem. Memorize the 6 categories: **Man, Method, Machine, Material, Measurement, Environment**.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Maintenance Management. List any four objectives of maintenance management.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Maintenance management is the systematic planning, organizing, directing, and controlling of all activities required to keep physical assets and production systems in a state of optimum operating condition, so as to ensure maximum availability, reliability, and safety at minimum total cost.

**Four Objectives:**
1. To **maximize equipment availability** and minimize unplanned downtime.
2. To **minimize the total cost** of maintenance and production losses.
3. To **ensure product quality** is maintained by keeping machines in proper calibration.
4. To **guarantee safety** of personnel and compliance with statutory regulations.

*(Any other valid objective such as extending asset life, optimizing spare parts inventory, or supporting production schedules also carries full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the need for maintenance management in any four dimensions.**

**Model Answer:**

Maintenance management is essential across four key dimensions:

1. **Economic Dimension:** The cost of unplanned downtime (often ₹1 lakh/hour in heavy industries) far exceeds the cost of planned preventive maintenance. Proper maintenance reduces operational losses.

2. **Quality Dimension:** Worn tools, misaligned spindles, or drifted sensors cause defective products, raising rejection rates and customer complaints.

3. **Safety Dimension:** Poorly maintained boilers, pressure vessels, cranes, and electrical systems lead to accidents, injuries, and fatalities. The Factories Act mandates safe equipment.

4. **Strategic Dimension:** World-class OEE (≥85%) cannot be achieved without structured maintenance, directly impacting competitiveness and market share.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various **functions of the maintenance department** in a manufacturing organization.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hydraulic press operates for **3,200 hours** in 6 months and fails **4 times** with a total repair time of **32 hours**. Calculate the **MTBF**, **MTTR**, and **Equipment Availability**. Comment on whether the maintenance system is satisfactory.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functions of the Maintenance Department:**

The maintenance department performs the following integrated functions:

1. **Planning and Scheduling:** Determining *when* (calendar or hour-based) and *what* (task list) maintenance should be carried out, with minimum disruption to production.

2. **Inspection and Monitoring:** Routine checks, vibration analysis, oil analysis, thermography to detect deterioration before failure.

3. **Execution of Repairs and Overhauls:** Performing corrective work, including breakdown repairs, part replacement, and major overhauls.

4. **Spare Parts Management:** Maintaining optimal inventory of critical spares (EOQ, ABC analysis) to avoid stockouts and excess carrying cost.

5. **Documentation and Record Keeping:** Maintaining equipment history cards, maintenance logs, cost records, and KPI dashboards (MTBF, MTTR, OEE).

6. **Training and Skill Development:** Continuous upskilling of maintenance technicians in new technologies (PLC, hydraulics, IoT-based monitoring).

7. **Coordination with Production:** Aligning maintenance windows with production schedules to minimize conflict.

8. **Budgeting and Cost Control:** Preparing annual maintenance budgets and tracking variance against actual expenditure.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $T_{operating} = 3200$ hrs, $N = 4$, $T_{repair} = 32$ hrs.

**Step 1 — MTBF:**

$$
MTBF = \frac{3200}{4} = 800 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — MTTR:**

$$
MTTR = \frac{32}{4} = 8 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Equipment Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{800}{800 + 8} \times 100 = 99.01\%
$$

**Step 4 — Comment:**

An availability of **99.01%** is **excellent** for general machinery. However, the **annualized downtime** works out to:

$$
T_d = 8760 \times (1 - 0.9901) \approx 87.1 \text{ hours/year}
$$

For a critical hydraulic press in an automotive stamping line, this still represents significant production loss. Hence, the maintenance system is **acceptable but can be improved** through predictive maintenance techniques such as vibration monitoring.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **need for maintenance management** from economic, safety, quality, and strategic perspectives.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CNC machine has a **failure rate (λ) of 0.0005 failures per hour**. Calculate the **reliability of the machine for an operating period of 500 hours**. If the company plans to operate the machine for 1000 hours between overhauls, what is the new reliability?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Need for Maintenance Management — Four Perspectives:**

1. **Economic Perspective:** Maintenance directly affects the bottom line. Studies by the Equipment Maintenance Council indicate that maintenance typically consumes **5–15% of total production cost**. Conversely, *poor* maintenance can consume **30%+** in indirect costs (downtime, defects, rework). The economic rule of thumb is: **₹1 spent on prevention saves ₹4–₹10 on breakdown consequences.**

2. **Safety Perspective:** Unmaintained equipment is a hazard. Examples: boiler explosions (due to neglected safety valves), crane collapses (due to worn cables), electric shocks (due to degraded insulation). The Indian **Factories Act, 1948** (Section 21, 22, 39) legally mandates proper maintenance. Failure invites criminal prosecution.

3. **Quality Perspective:** Modern quality frameworks (ISO 9001, Six Sigma) require process capability, which depends on machine condition. A worn ball screw causes **positional inaccuracy** in CNC machines → rejects. A drifted thermocouple causes **off-spec temperature** → defective heat treatment.

4. **Strategic Perspective:** World-class firms (Toyota, Boeing) consider maintenance a **strategic weapon**. TPM (Total Productive Maintenance), introduced by JIPM Japan, has helped companies achieve **OEE > 90%**, giving them decisive cost and delivery advantages in the global market.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour, $t_1 = 500$ hrs, $t_2 = 1000$ hrs.

**Step 1 — Reliability for t = 500 hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(500) &= e^{-\lambda t} \\
R(500) &= e^{-0.0005 \times 500} \\
R(500) &= e^{-0.25} \\
R(500) &= 0.7788 \text{ or } 77.88\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Reliability for t = 1000 hours:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(1000) &= e^{-0.0005 \times 1000} \\
R(1000) &= e^{-0.5} \\
R(1000) &= 0.6065 \text{ or } 60.65\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

Doubling the operating period (from 500 to 1000 hours) **reduces reliability by approximately 18 percentage points**. To maintain reliability above **90%**, the company should schedule an overhaul well before 1000 hours — ideally every **~210 hours** (where $e^{-0.0005 \times 210} = 0.90$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing MTBF as "hours" without explicitly stating the unit loses 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Reliability Formula Misapplication:** Using $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for the *infant mortality* or *wear-out* phase (Bathtub Curve) is **wrong** — the formula assumes *constant failure rate* (useful life phase only).
> 3. **Ignoring Percentage Format:** Writing availability as a decimal (e.g., 0.99) instead of percentage (99%) loses 1 mark in numerical answers.
> 4. **No Interpretation:** Ending a numerical answer with just the number — KTU expects a concluding sentence commenting on what the result *means* for management.
> 5. **Misnaming OEE:** Students often confuse OEE with Availability. OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality. Writing "OEE = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)" is incorrect.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Maintenance Management: Need**

- ✅ **Definition:** Maintenance Management = keeping equipment in designed operating condition or restoring it.
- ✅ **Six Core Functions:** Planning, Inspection, Execution, Spare Parts, Documentation, Training.
- ✅ **Four Needs:** Economic, Quality, Safety, Strategic.
- ✅ **Two Master Formulas:**
  - Availability: $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$
  - Reliability: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
- ✅ **Three Maintenance Types:** Breakdown (run-to-fail), Preventive (calendar-based), Predictive (condition-based).
- ✅ **OEE Formula:** $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$, world-class benchmark **≥ 85%**.
- ✅ **Maintenance Cost Benchmark:** **2% – 8%** of replacement value per year.
- ✅ **Bathtub Curve:** Three phases — Infant Mortality (burn-in), Useful Life (constant λ), Wear-Out (replacement).
- ✅ **Law of Reliability:** Reliability *decreases* exponentially with operating time.
- ✅ **6M Framework (Ishikawa):** Man, Method, Machine, Material, Measurement, Environment.
- ✅ **KTU Buzzwords:** MTBF, MTTR, OEE, TPM, RCM, Breakdown Maintenance, Preventive Maintenance, Predictive Maintenance, 6M, Bathtub Curve.
- ✅ **Legal Hook:** Always mention **Factories Act 1948** when discussing safety-related maintenance needs — examiners reward statutory awareness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment life cycle (bathtub curve)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equipment life cycle (bathtub curve)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equipment Life Cycle (Bathtub Curve)** is a reliability engineering graphical model that depicts the **instantaneous failure rate (λ(t))** of a population of components, machines, or systems as a function of time. The curve derives its name from its distinct shape resembling a bathtub in cross-section and is composed of three physiognomic regions: **Infant Mortality (Burn-in) Phase**, **Useful Life (Normal Operating) Phase**, and **Wear-out Phase**.

In the context of **Operations Management**, the bathtub curve is the foundational conceptual model used to:
- Plan **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** schedules
- Compute **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**
- Forecast **spare parts demand**
- Decide **replacement vs. repair** policies
- Estimate **warranty costs** and equipment residual life

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Life Cycle**

Think of a piece of industrial equipment exactly like a human being's health journey:

| Life Stage | Human Analogy | Industrial Equipment |
|---|---|---|
| **Infancy** (0–2 yrs) | Babies fall sick often (weak immunity) | New machines fail due to design/manufacturing defects |
| **Adulthood** (20–50 yrs) | Healthy, occasional random illness | Mature machines have stable, low failure rate |
| **Old Age** (60+ yrs) | Organ wear, frequent breakdowns | Aging machines fail due to fatigue/corrosion |

A brand-new CNC machine bought from a vendor tends to fail more in the **first few weeks** (loose connections, installation errors, defective bearings). Once these "infant" issues are weeded out, the machine runs smoothly for **years with rare, random failures** (e.g., a power surge). Eventually, after crossing its design life, the machine enters a **wear-out phase** where parts degrade exponentially and breakdowns become frequent — at this point, **replacement is cheaper than repair**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams**: The bathtub curve is essentially a plot of **failure rate (λ) vs. time (t)**. Do NOT confuse it with a reliability curve, which would be a *decreasing exponential*, not U-shaped.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (in bold)

- **Failure Rate (λ)**: Number of failures per unit time (failures/hour or %/1000 hrs)
- **Reliability R(t)**: Probability that equipment performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified time interval
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**: Average time elapsed between two consecutive failures
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**: Average time required to restore a failed component to working condition
- **Availability (A)**: Fraction of time the system is operationally ready

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases of the Bathtub Curve — Structured Logic Breakdown

### **Phase 1: Infant Mortality / Burn-in / Debugging Phase (Decreasing Failure Rate)**

- **Why does it occur?** Components have *latent manufacturing defects* (poor soldering, weak welds, misaligned gears, improper lubrication).
- **How does it behave?** Failure rate **decreases sharply** as defective items fail and are weeded out of the population (often called "infant mortality" or "break-in period").
- **Operations Management Action:** Use **Burn-in testing** at the factory; apply **acceptance sampling**; do not schedule heavy production loads on new equipment.
- **Typical Duration:** 0 to ~6 months for industrial machinery.

### **Phase 2: Useful Life / Normal Operating Phase (Constant Failure Rate)**

- **Why does it occur?** Random, unpredictable failures caused by external shocks, operator errors, or random environmental stress.
- **How does it behave?** Failure rate is **constant (λ = constant)** and is the lowest point of the curve.
- **Mathematical Model:** This phase is modeled by the **Exponential Distribution**, the only memoryless continuous distribution.
- **Operations Management Action:** Schedule **routine preventive maintenance**; maintain buffer stock of spares; focus on **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**.
- **Typical Duration:** Several years (the productive "sweet spot" of the machine).

### **Phase 3: Wear-out Phase (Increasing Failure Rate)**

- **Why does it occur?** Cumulative physical degradation — bearing fatigue, corrosion, wear, dielectric breakdown, gear tooth erosion.
- **How does it behave?** Failure rate **increases exponentially** with time.
- **Mathematical Model:** **Weibull Distribution (β > 1)** or **Lognormal Distribution**.
- **Operations Management Action:** Plan **predictive replacement**; conduct **condition-based monitoring** (vibration analysis, thermography); decide whether to overhaul or replace.
- **Typical Duration:** Last 10–20% of useful life.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains every equation you need to solve KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problems on this topic. Memorize the units carefully.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of survival up to time $t$ (dimensionless) |
| 2 | Failure Density (PDF) | $f(t) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of failure in interval $dt$ (per hour) |
| 3 | Cumulative Failure (CDF) | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ (dimensionless) |
| 4 | Failure Rate (Hazard) | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Instantaneous failure rate (failures/hr) |
| 5 | Mean Life / MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours between failures |
| 6 | Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\eta$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape parameter |
| 7 | Weibull Failure Rate | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $\beta<1$: infant, $\beta=1$: random, $\beta>1$: wear-out |
| 8 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Decimal fraction (0 to 1) |
| 9 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MAD}}$ | MAD = Mean Administrative Delay |
| 10 | Expected Failures (n) | $n = N_{0} \cdot F(t) = N_{0}\left(1-e^{-\lambda t}\right)$ | $N_0$ = population size |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The bathtub curve is a **decision-making lens** in industries:
- **Aerospace**: Determines aircraft engine overhaul intervals (TBO).
- **Manufacturing**: Drives **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** schedules.
- **Electronics**: Used in **burn-in tests** for semiconductors and PCBs.
- **Automotive**: Models warranty claim curves over the 5-year ownership period.
- **Power Plants**: Guides replacement scheduling of turbine blades.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specifics**: The Operations Management syllabus integrates reliability metrics directly into **maintenance management decisions** (Module 1 + Module 2 carryover). Always quote MTBF in the answer when justifying maintenance policy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reliability Function from Failure Rate

The bathtub curve models a non-constant hazard. For the **Useful Life Phase** (where $\lambda$ is constant), we derive the reliability function rigorously.

### **Step 1 — Define the Hazard Function**

The instantaneous failure rate is defined as the conditional probability of failure in the next instant $dt$, given survival up to time $t$:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t}$$

### **Step 2 — Express in terms of densities**

Substituting $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$ and dividing by $R(t)$:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \left(-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}\right)$$

### **Step 3 — Separate variables**

Multiplying both sides by $dt$ and rearranging:

$$\lambda(t)\,dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

### **Step 4 — Integrate from 0 to t**

We use the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (100% reliable at the start):

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau = -\ln R(t)$$

### **Step 5 — Solve for R(t)**

$$R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau}$$

### **Step 6 — Apply the Constant-λ assumption (Useful Life Phase)**

For the middle phase where $\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R(t) &= e^{-\lambda t} \\
F(t) &= 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \\
f(t) &= \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation**: Reliability $R(t)$ starts at **1** (brand new, working perfectly) and decays **exponentially** as time progresses. After one MTBF (i.e., $t = 1/\lambda$), only $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of the original population is still surviving.

## 3.2 Derivation: MTBF from the Exponential Distribution

The expected time to failure is the first moment of the PDF:

$$\text{MTBF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

Using integration by parts ($u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}dt$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF} &= \left[ -t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt \\
&= 0 + \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \\
&= 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Phase Comparison

| Property | Phase 1 (Infant) | Phase 2 (Useful) | Phase 3 (Wear-out) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Statistical Distribution** | Weibull ($\beta < 1$) | Exponential ($\beta = 1$) | Weibull ($\beta > 1$) / Lognormal |
| **Failure Rate Behavior** | Decreasing | Constant | Increasing |
| **Dominant Failure Cause** | Defects, poor workmanship | Random external stress | Aging, fatigue |
| **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \beta<1$ | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \beta>1$ |
| **MTBF Formula** | $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ | $1/\lambda$ | $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ |
| **Management Strategy** | Burn-in, OEM debugging | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Replacement / Overhaul |
| **Cost Behavior** | High warranty claims | Lowest cost per hour | Escalating repair costs |
| **% of Useful Life** | ~5% | ~85% | ~10% |

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Example (Worked Derivation)

> [!TIP]
> A factory has **500 identical electric motors**. Field data shows a constant failure rate of **0.002 failures/hour** during the useful life phase.

**Find:** (a) Reliability for 100 hrs of operation, (b) Expected number of survivors after 100 hrs, (c) MTBF, (d) Probability of failure in the next 50 hrs given survival up to 200 hrs.

### **Solution (a) — Reliability at t = 100 hrs**

$$\begin{aligned}
R(100) &= e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.002)(100)} = e^{-0.2} \\
&= 0.8187 \;\; \text{(or 81.87\%)}
\end{aligned}$$

### **Solution (b) — Expected Survivors**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{survive}} &= N_0 \cdot R(t) = 500 \times 0.8187 \\
&\approx 409 \text{ motors}
\end{aligned}$$

### **Solution (c) — MTBF**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.002} = 500 \text{ hours}$$

### **Solution (d) — Conditional Failure Probability**

Using the memoryless property of the exponential distribution (Phase 2):

$$P(T \leq 250 \mid T > 200) = 1 - e^{-\lambda(250-200)} = 1 - e^{-0.1} = 0.0952$$

This confirms that in the useful life phase, the equipment "forgets" its past — past usage has no effect on future reliability.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Bathtub Curve — Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the **decision flow** an Operations Manager follows as equipment moves through its life cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["EQUIPMENT INSTALLED"] --> B["PHASE 1 INFANT MORTALITY"]
    B --> B1["Failure Rate DECREASING"]
    B1 --> B2["Cause: Defects, Installation Errors"]

    C --> D["PHASE 2 USEFUL LIFE"]

    E --> F["PHASE 3 WEAR-OUT"]
    F --> F1["Failure Rate INCREASING"]
    F1 --> F2["Cause: Fatigue Corrosion Aging"]
    F2 --> G["Decision: REPLACE OR OVERHAUL"]
    G --> H["END OF LIFE CYCLE"]

```

## 4.2 Failure Rate vs Time — The Classical Bathtub Shape

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["REGION A\nInfant Mortality\nlambda decreases"] --> P2["REGION B\nUseful Life\nlambda constant and minimal"]
    P2 --> P3["REGION C\nWear Out\nlambda increases sharply"]
    P3 --> P4["REPLACEMENT\nDecisions made here"]

    style P1 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF
    style P2 fill:#51CF66,color:#FFFFFF
    style P3 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF
    style P4 fill:#339AF0,color:#FFFFFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: The Y-axis represents the failure rate λ(t), and the X-axis represents operating time. The curve drops (Region A), flattens (Region B), and rises sharply (Region C) — hence the bathtub analogy.

## 4.3 Maintenance Strategy Decision Tree (Mapped to Each Phase)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Equipment in Operation"] --> R{"Identify Life Phase"}
    R -->|"Phase 1 Infant"| S1["ACTION: Run-In Test\nReplace Defective Units\nDo Not Schedule Critical Jobs"]
    R -->|"Phase 2 Useful"| S2["ACTION: Scheduled PM\nLubrication, Calibration\nSpare Stocking"]
    R -->|"Phase 3 Wear Out"| S3["ACTION: Condition Monitoring\nVibration, Oil Analysis\nPlan Replacement"]
    S1 --> T["Improved Reliability"]
    S2 --> T
    S3 --> T

```

## 4.4 Weibull Shape Parameter (β) — Mapping to Curve Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["beta less than 1\nDECREASING failure rate"] -->|"corresponds to"| B["INFANT MORTALITY"]
    C["beta equals 1\nCONSTANT failure rate"] -->|"corresponds to"| D["USEFUL LIFE"]
    E["beta greater than 1\nINCREASING failure rate"] -->|"corresponds to"| F["WEAR OUT"]

    style A fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF
    style B fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF
    style C fill:#51CF66,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#51CF66,color:#FFFFFF
    style E fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF
    style F fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU**: Many 14-mark questions ask you to identify which statistical distribution applies to each phase. The Weibull **shape parameter β** is the single most important quantity — it determines the slope/curvature of the failure rate.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the **bathtub curve** in reliability engineering. Name its three distinct regions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The bathtub curve is a graphical representation of the **instantaneous failure rate (λ(t))** of a population of components versus operating time. It has three regions:

1. **Infant Mortality Phase / Burn-in Phase** — failure rate decreases with time (defects weeded out)
2. **Useful Life / Normal Operating Phase** — failure rate is constant and minimal (random failures)
3. **Wear-out Phase** — failure rate increases sharply due to aging and fatigue

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for naming regions + 1 mark for brief explanation)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the **memoryless property** of the exponential distribution? Why is it relevant to the useful life phase?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The memoryless property states that for an exponentially distributed random variable $T$:

$$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$$

It is relevant because during the **useful life phase** of the bathtub curve, failure rate is **constant (λ)**, which means the **past history of a component has no bearing on its future failure probability** — the equipment "forgets" how long it has been running. This is the mathematical basis for treating useful-life failures as independent and identically distributed random events.

*(2 marks for formula + 1 mark for engineering interpretation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **three phases of the bathtub curve**. Discuss the statistical distribution applicable to each phase and the corresponding maintenance strategy.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A fleet of **200 water pumps** operates in a plant. Field data indicates a constant failure rate of **0.005 failures/hour** during the useful life phase. Calculate:
  (i) The reliability for **200 hours** of operation
  (ii) The expected number of pumps still operating after 200 hours
  (iii) The MTBF
  (iv) The probability that a pump will fail in the next 100 hours, given it has already run for 300 hours

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Three Phases of the Bathtub Curve [7 marks]**

**Sketch (2 marks):**

```
       Failure Rate (lambda)
              |
              |          ____ Wear-out
              |         /
              |        /
              |_______/______________  Useful Life (constant)
              |    /
              |   /  Infant Mortality
              |__/__________________________
                Time
```

**Phase 1 — Infant Mortality (Burn-in) Phase (1.5 marks):**
- Failure rate **decreases** with time
- Caused by: manufacturing defects, poor assembly, installation errors
- **Distribution:** Weibull with shape parameter **β < 1**
- **Strategy:** Burn-in testing at the supplier's end; rejection sampling

**Phase 2 — Useful Life Phase (1.5 marks):**
- Failure rate is **constant and minimum**
- Caused by: random external shocks, operator errors, voltage surges
- **Distribution:** Exponential distribution (β = 1)
- **Strategy:** Scheduled preventive maintenance; reliability-centered maintenance (RCM)

**Phase 3 — Wear-out Phase (2 marks):**
- Failure rate **increases** rapidly with time
- Caused by: bearing fatigue, corrosion, dielectric breakdown, mechanical wear
- **Distribution:** Weibull with **β > 1** or Lognormal
- **Strategy:** Condition-based monitoring; planned replacement / overhaul

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]**

**Given:** $N_0 = 200$ pumps, $\lambda = 0.005$ failures/hour, $t = 200$ hrs

**(i) Reliability for 200 hours (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R(200) &= e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.005)(200)} = e^{-1} \\
&= 0.3679 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 36.79\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Expected Survivors after 200 hours (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{survive}} &= N_0 \cdot R(t) \\
&= 200 \times 0.3679 \\
&\approx 74 \text{ pumps}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Mean Time Between Failures (1 mark):**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.005} = 200 \text{ hours}$$

**(iv) Conditional failure probability (2 marks):**

Using the memoryless property of the exponential distribution:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(T \leq 400 \mid T > 300) &= 1 - e^{-\lambda(400-300)} \\
&= 1 - e^{-(0.005)(100)} \\
&= 1 - e^{-0.5} \\
&= 1 - 0.6065 \\
&= 0.3935 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 39.35\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the **Weibull distribution** in the context of reliability. Discuss the significance of the **shape parameter β** and how different values of β correspond to the regions of the bathtub curve.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A CNC machine has a Weibull failure distribution with **scale parameter η = 5000 hours** and **shape parameter β = 2.5**. Calculate:
  (i) The reliability for **1000 hours** of operation
  (ii) The cumulative failure probability at 1000 hours
  (iii) The failure rate at 1000 hours
  (iv) Comment on which phase of the bathtub curve this machine is in.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Weibull Distribution [7 marks]**

The **Weibull distribution** is the most widely used reliability model in engineering because it can represent **all three phases** of the bathtub curve by varying its shape parameter.

**Probability Density Function (2 marks):**

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$

**Reliability Function (2 marks):**

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$

**Significance of Shape Parameter β (3 marks):**

| Value of β | Curve Behavior | Life Cycle Phase |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta < 1$ | Failure rate **decreases** | Infant Mortality |
| $\beta = 1$ | Failure rate **constant** | Useful Life (reduces to exponential) |
| $\beta > 1$ | Failure rate **increases** | Wear-out Phase |

$\eta$ (scale parameter) is the characteristic life at which **63.2%** of the population has failed.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]**

**Given:** $\eta = 5000$ hrs, $\beta = 2.5$, $t = 1000$ hrs

**(i) Reliability at 1000 hours (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R(1000) &= e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} = e^{-(1000/5000)^{2.5}} \\
&= e^{-(0.2)^{2.5}} = e^{-0.01789} \\
&\approx 0.9823 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 98.23\%
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Cumulative Failure Probability (1 mark):**

$$F(1000) = 1 - R(1000) = 1 - 0.9823 = 0.0177 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 1.77\%$$

**(iii) Failure Rate at 1000 hours (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda(1000) &= \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} \\
&= \frac{2.5}{5000}\left(\frac{1000}{5000}\right)^{2.5-1} \\
&= 0.0005 \times (0.2)^{1.5} \\
&= 0.0005 \times 0.08944 \\
&\approx 4.47 \times 10^{-5} \text{ failures/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Phase Identification (2 marks):**

Since $\beta = 2.5 > 1$, the failure rate is **increasing** with time. The machine is in the **Wear-out Phase** of the bathtub curve. **Recommended action:** Plan **predictive replacement**, conduct **vibration analysis**, and prepare for major overhaul.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing R(t) and λ(t) curves** — The reliability curve is a *decreasing exponential*, not U-shaped. The failure rate is what gives the bathtub shape.
> 2. **Forgetting units in MTBF** — Always write "MTBF = 200 hours", not just "200".
> 3. **Using the wrong distribution** — Phase 1 and Phase 3 are **NOT** exponential; they require the Weibull model. Only Phase 2 is exponential.
> 4. **In numerical problems**: If the question says "given it has already run for 300 hours", students often forget the memoryless property and plug in t = 400 hrs directly. The correct approach is to compute $\lambda \cdot (t - s) = \lambda \cdot (400 - 300) = \lambda \cdot 100$.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch in 7-mark questions** — A neat, labeled bathtub curve diagram is worth at least 2 marks. Never omit it.
> 6. **Mixing up η and β** — η is the *scale* (lifespan scale), β is the *shape* (curve shape). Examiners specifically test this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Re-read 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ The **bathtub curve plots failure rate (λ)** vs. time — **not** reliability vs. time.
- ✅ The three regions are: **Infant Mortality** (decreasing λ), **Useful Life** (constant λ), **Wear-out** (increasing λ).
- ✅ The **Exponential distribution** governs the **Useful Life** phase; **Weibull** governs the other two phases.
- ✅ The **memoryless property** holds ONLY in the useful life phase: $P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$.
- ✅ **Weibull shape parameter β** determines the region: β < 1 (infant), β = 1 (useful), β > 1 (wear-out).
- ✅ Key formulas to memorize:
  - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (useful life)
  - $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ (Weibull)
  - $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ (exponential)
  - $\text{MTBF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ (Weibull)
  - $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$
- ✅ At $t = \text{MTBF}$, reliability is exactly **36.8%** (i.e., $1/e$).
- ✅ **Operations management implication**: Maintenance strategy changes by phase — burn-in testing → preventive maintenance → condition-based replacement.
- ✅ Always **draw a labeled sketch** in 7-mark questions; never answer in pure text.
- ✅ Mention **MTBF units** explicitly; check significant figures in the final answer.
- ✅ Past KTU papers (Dec 2022, May 2023, Dec 2023) confirm: questions on bathtub curve typically carry 7–14 marks and combine conceptual + numerical sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measures for maintenance performance (MTBF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measures for maintenance performance (MTBF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** is the principal reliability and maintainability metric used in Operations Management to quantify the **average elapsed time** between two consecutive failure events of a **repairable** system, machine, or component, measured under normal operating conditions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Operations Management parlance, MTBF is formally defined as:

> *"The arithmetic mean of the time intervals between successive failures of a repairable asset during its useful operating life, excluding scheduled downtime and external logistical delays."*

MTBF is the **gold-standard indicator** of a system's *intrinsic reliability* — the higher the MTBF, the more dependable the equipment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Your College Bus Service**
>
> Imagine the KTU college bus that shuttles students from the hostel to campus every morning. Suppose over the last 30 days, the bus **broke down 5 times**, and the total time the bus was **actually running** (excluding the time it spent in the garage being repaired) was **1,500 hours**.
>
> - The total running time between failures = **1,500 hours**
> - Number of failures = **5**
> - MTBF = 1,500 ÷ 5 = **300 hours per failure**
>
> This means, *on average*, the bus operates for **300 hours** before it breaks down again. A higher MTBF = a more reliable bus. A low MTBF = frequent breakdowns, leading to frustrated students and missed classes!

### 1.3 Classification of Maintenance Time Metrics

The KTU syllabus distinguishes between **four interrelated metrics** that every Operations Manager must master:

| Acronym | Full Form | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | Repairable systems (machines, vehicles, servers) |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | Repairable systems |
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | Non-repairable items (bulbs, fuses, batteries) |
| **MDT** | Mean Down Time | Includes logistic + administrative delays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The official PECBT865 syllabus (Module 1) explicitly lists *"Measures for maintenance performance"* — and **MTBF is the most heavily weighted metric** for Part B (14-mark) questions. Always state MTBF in **hours** (industry standard), and pair it with **MTTR** whenever discussing Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind MTBF — Step-by-Step Deduction

To truly understand MTBF, we must decompose the logic into its operational building blocks:

1. **Step 1 — Observe the system:** A maintenance engineer logs every instance when an asset fails and is restored back to working condition.
2. **Step 2 — Record Time-to-Failure intervals:** Each interval starts at the moment of repair completion and ends at the next failure. This is the *"up-time"* of one operating cycle.
3. **Step 3 — Sum the up-times:** Total operating time = Σ(t₁, t₂, t₃, …, tₙ) across all `n` failure intervals.
4. **Step 4 — Count the failures:** For `n` failures, there are `n` valid MTBF intervals.
5. **Step 5 — Compute arithmetic mean:** Divide total up-time by the number of failures. This yields the **expected reliability interval**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why arithmetic mean?** During the "useful life" period of the well-known *Bathtub Curve*, the failure rate λ is **approximately constant**. Under this exponential distribution assumption, the mean time to failure mathematically equals `1/λ` — which is exactly the arithmetic mean of observed intervals.

### 2.2 The Interrelationship of MTBF, MTTR, and Availability

MTBF never stands alone in Operations Management. The **availability** of a system is mathematically derived from MTBF and MTTR:

$$\text{Availability (A)} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

Where:
- **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures (reliability indicator)
- **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair (maintainability indicator)
- Availability is expressed as a **decimal between 0 and 1** (or as a percentage).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MTBF = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n}$ | $t_i$ = time between the *i*-th and (*i*+1)-th failure; $n$ = number of failures | Hours |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | $\lambda$ = constant failure rate (during useful life) | Failures per hour |
| 3 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t / MTBF}$ | $R(t)$ = reliability function; probability of *no* failure up to time $t$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 4 | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i}{n}$ | $r_i$ = repair time for the *i*-th failure | Hours |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $A$ = steady-state availability | Decimal / % |
| 6 | $Uptime\ \% = A \times 100$ | Useful life operation proportion | Percentage |
| 7 | $Downtime\ \% = (1 - A) \times 100$ | Inherent downtime proportion | Percentage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for Examiners:** Always remember that **MTBF applies only to repairable systems**, whereas **MTTF applies to non-repairable** items. Writing MTTF when the question demands MTBF (or vice versa) is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

MTBF is the **backbone of predictive maintenance scheduling** in:

- **Manufacturing plants:** Justifies investment in TPM (Total Productive Maintenance).
- **Aviation industry:** Airlines compute fleet-level MTBF to schedule A-check and C-check maintenance windows.
- **IT & Data Centers:** Server MTBF directly drives Service Level Agreements (SLAs).
- **Power generation:** Grid operators compute transformer MTBF to predict outage probability.

By tracking MTBF over multiple months, Operations Managers can detect **degradation trends** and pre-empt catastrophic failures — a core principle of **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Failure Data to MTBF

Suppose a CNC milling machine in a KTU-affiliated engineering workshop experienced the following failures over a quarter:

| Failure No. | Up-time before failure (hours) | Repair time (hours) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 220 | 4 |
| 2 | 185 | 6 |
| 3 | 240 | 3 |
| 4 | 195 | 5 |
| 5 | 210 | 4 |

**Derivation of MTBF:**

We apply the canonical formula:
$$MTBF = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n}$$

**Step 1 — Sum the up-times (numerator):**
$$\sum t_i = 220 + 185 + 240 + 195 + 210$$

Performing the addition explicitly:
$$\sum t_i = 220 + 185 = 405$$
$$405 + 240 = 645$$
$$645 + 195 = 840$$
$$840 + 210 = 1050\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 2 — Count failures (denominator):**
$$n = 5$$

**Step 3 — Compute MTBF:**
$$MTBF = \frac{1050}{5} = 210\ \text{hours per failure}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the failure rate λ:**
$$\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{210} \approx 0.004762\ \text{failures/hour}$$

**Step 5 — Derive MTTR (for later use):**
$$\sum r_i = 4 + 6 + 3 + 5 + 4 = 22\ \text{hours}$$
$$MTTR = \frac{22}{5} = 4.4\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Steady-State Availability:**
$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$
$$A = \frac{210}{210 + 4.4} = \frac{210}{214.4}$$
$$A \approx 0.9795 \approx 97.95\%$$

**Step 7 — Compute Reliability for a 100-hour mission:**
$$R(100) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 100} = e^{-0.004762 \times 100}$$
$$R(100) = e^{-0.4762}$$
$$R(100) \approx 0.6211 \approx 62.11\%$$

This means there is a **62.11% probability** that the CNC machine will operate without failure for 100 continuous hours.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Numerical with Multiple Sub-Questions

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A hydraulic press in an automotive components plant has been observed over 2,000 operating hours. During this period, **8 failures** occurred. The total downtime for repairs was **64 hours**.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Calculate MTBF.**

Total operating time is given as 2,000 hours.
$$MTBF = \frac{2000}{8} = 250\ \text{hours}$$

**Part (b): Calculate MTTR.**

$$MTTR = \frac{64}{8} = 8\ \text{hours}$$

**Part (c): Calculate Availability.**

$$A = \frac{250}{250 + 8} = \frac{250}{258} \approx 0.9690 \approx 96.90\%$$

**Part (d): If the plant runs 16 hours/day, how many days of operation can be expected between failures?**

$$\text{Days between failures} = \frac{MTBF}{16} = \frac{250}{16} = 15.625\ \text{days}$$

### 3.3 Comparative Matrix: How MTBF, MTTR, and MTTF Differ

| Dimension | MTBF | MTTR | MTTF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Mean Time Between Failures | Mean Time To Repair | Mean Time To Failure |
| System Type | Repairable | Repairable | Non-repairable |
| What it measures | Time **between** failures | Time to **restore** operation | Total lifetime until failure |
| Improving it depends on | Design quality, preventive maintenance | Skill of technicians, spare parts availability | Component durability, manufacturing quality |
| Typical KTU weightage | Very high (14-mark questions) | Medium (often paired with MTBF) | Low (3-mark definitions) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: MTBF in Maintenance Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Commissioned] --> B[Operate Normally]
    B --> C{Failure Event}
    C -- Yes --> D[Log Failure Time]
    D --> E[Repair Activity Starts]
    E --> F[Repair Time Recorded as MTTR]
    F --> G[System Restored to Operation]
    G --> H[Reset Operating Timer]
    H --> B
    C -- No --> B
    I[Maintenance Database] --> J[Compute MTBF]
    I --> K[Compute MTTR]
    J --> L[Steady State Availability A]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Reliability Function R of t]
    M --> N[Predictive Maintenance Decision]
```

### 4.2 Maintenance Performance Metrics — Conceptual Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Maintenance Performance Measures] --> nodeB[Reliability Metrics]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Maintainability Metrics]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Availability Metrics]
    nodeB --> nodeE[MTBF]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Failure Rate lambda]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Reliability R of t]
    nodeC --> nodeH[MTTR]
    nodeC --> nodeI[MDT]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Inherent Availability]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Achieved Availability]
    nodeE --> nodeL[Quality of Design]
    nodeF --> nodeL
    nodeH --> nodeM[Quality of Maintenance Crew]
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeN[Overall Equipment Effectiveness]
    nodeK --> nodeN
```

### 4.3 MTBF Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Collect Failure Logs] --> stepB[Extract Up-times t1 to tn]
    stepB --> stepC[Sum Up-times]
    stepC --> stepD[Count Failures n]
    stepD --> stepE[Apply MTBF Formula]
    stepE --> stepF[Compute Failure Rate lambda]
    stepF --> stepG[Compute Availability]
    stepG --> stepH[Generate Maintenance Schedule]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each rectangle represents a process or output; each diamond (in diagram 4.1) represents a decision point. Trace the flow to understand how raw operational data is transformed into strategic maintenance decisions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define MTBF and state its significance in operations management.**

> **Model Answer:**
> MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) is the average time interval between consecutive failures of a repairable system, calculated as the total operating time divided by the number of failures. Its significance lies in quantifying **equipment reliability**, guiding **preventive maintenance scheduling**, supporting **spare parts inventory planning**, and serving as a key input for **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** calculations in any production environment.

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between MTBF and MTTF with suitable examples.**

> **Model Answer:**
> - **MTBF** applies to **repairable** systems (e.g., a CNC machine, an aircraft engine). It measures the average time *between* two failures.
> - **MTTF** applies to **non-repairable** items (e.g., an LED bulb, a fuse, a battery). It measures the total operational lifetime before the item is discarded.
> The distinction is critical because applying MTTF logic to a repairable asset misrepresents its true lifecycle economics.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Question A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Modified]`

**(a)** Define MTBF and explain its role in Total Productive Maintenance (TPM). **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A packaging machine in a food processing unit recorded the following operating data over 6 months:

| Month | Operating Hours | Failures | Repair Hours |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 600 | 3 | 9 |
| 2 | 580 | 4 | 16 |
| 3 | 620 | 2 | 10 |
| 4 | 590 | 5 | 20 |
| 5 | 610 | 3 | 12 |
| 6 | 600 | 4 | 14 |

Calculate the **MTBF**, **MTTR**, and **steady-state availability** of the machine. **\[7 Marks\]**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) MTBF and TPM (7 marks):**
> MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) is the average time a repairable system operates between two successive failures. It is computed as total operating time divided by the number of failures.
>
> Role in TPM:
> 1. **Reliability Indicator:** MTBF quantifies the inherent dependability of equipment; rising MTBF over time signals successful TPM implementation.
> 2. **Autonomous Maintenance Benchmark:** Operators track MTBF to measure the impact of daily cleaning, lubrication, and inspection routines.
> 3. **Planned Maintenance Trigger:** Falling MTBF triggers root-cause analysis (RCA) and Kaizen activities.
> 4. **OEE Linkage:** MTBF directly feeds into the *Availability* component of OEE — a pillar of TPM.
> 5. **Spare Parts Optimization:** Higher MTBF reduces emergency procurement costs.
>
> **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
>
> Total operating hours:
> $$600 + 580 + 620 + 590 + 610 + 600 = 3600\ \text{hours}$$
>
> Total failures:
> $$3 + 4 + 2 + 5 + 3 + 4 = 21$$
>
> Total repair hours:
> $$9 + 16 + 10 + 20 + 12 + 14 = 81\ \text{hours}$$
>
> **MTBF:**
> $$MTBF = \frac{3600}{21} \approx 171.43\ \text{hours}$$
>
> **MTTR:**
> $$MTTR = \frac{81}{21} \approx 3.857\ \text{hours}$$
>
> **Availability:**
> $$A = \frac{171.43}{171.43 + 3.857} = \frac{171.43}{175.287} \approx 0.9780 \approx 97.80\%$$

---

**Question B.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat derivation, show that for a constant failure rate, the reliability function is given by $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. Hence derive the relation between MTBF and $\lambda$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A server in a data center has an MTBF of 5,000 hours and an MTTR of 2 hours. Compute: (i) Failure rate, (ii) Availability, (iii) Probability that the server will operate without failure for the next 250 hours. **\[7 Marks\]**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
>
> Let $\lambda$ be the constant failure rate (failures per unit time). The probability that the system fails in a small interval $\Delta t$ is $\lambda \cdot \Delta t$. Hence, the probability of *surviving* that interval is $(1 - \lambda \cdot \Delta t)$.
>
> For a finite duration $t$ broken into $n$ small intervals of length $\Delta t = t/n$:
> $$R(t) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda t}{n}\right)^{n}$$
>
> Applying the standard exponential limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{-x}$:
> $$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$
>
> The mean of this exponential distribution gives MTBF:
> $$MTBF = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$
> $$\boxed{MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$
>
> **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
>
> Given: MTBF = 5,000 hours; MTTR = 2 hours.
>
> **(i) Failure rate:**
> $$\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{5000} = 0.0002\ \text{failures/hour}$$
>
> **(ii) Availability:**
> $$A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 2} = \frac{5000}{5002} \approx 0.9996 \approx 99.96\%$$
>
> **(iii) Reliability for 250 hours:**
> $$R(250) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0002 \times 250} = e^{-0.05}$$
> $$R(250) \approx 0.9512 \approx 95.12\%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF:** Writing the MTTF formula for a repairable system loses 2-3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Wrong denominator:** For *n* failures, the MTBF denominator is *n*, **not** (*n*+1) or (*n*–1).
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Expressing MTBF in minutes when the question is in hours — always **normalize units first**.
> 4. **Forgetting to compute Availability:** When asked for MTBF + MTTR-based analysis, skipping the availability calculation forfeits easy marks.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** When using $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, you **must** explicitly state *"assuming constant failure rate during the useful life period."* Otherwise, the examiner deducts 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MTBF** = Total Operating Time ÷ Number of Failures (for repairable systems).
- **MTBF measures reliability; MTTR measures maintainability; Availability combines both.**
- **Higher MTBF = More reliable system = Less downtime = Higher OEE.**
- **Failure rate** $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ (valid only during the *useful life* phase of the bathtub curve).
- **Reliability function:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t / MTBF}$.
- **Steady-State Availability:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — always between 0 and 1.
- **MTBF vs MTTF:** MTBF → repairable; MTTF → non-repairable.
- **Units:** Industry standard is **hours**; KTU exam answers should match the units given in the problem.
- **MTBF is the primary driver** of preventive maintenance scheduling and spare parts inventory decisions.
- **Track MTBF trends over time:** A *declining* MTBF is an early warning of impending catastrophic failure.
- **In KTU exams**, always combine MTBF computation with **availability** derivation for full marks.
- **Mention the bathtub curve** whenever deriving $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — examiners reward contextual awareness.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MTTR
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MTTR

# MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) – Operations Management Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** is a fundamental **maintenance performance metric** that quantifies the **average time required to restore a failed system, machine, or component to its fully operational state**, measured from the moment a failure is detected until the equipment is back in service and verified.

Mathematically, in its most elementary form, MTTR is expressed as:

$$ \text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Repair (Downtime) Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} $$

The unit of MTTR is typically **hours**, though minutes, days, or shifts may be used depending on the operational context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** MTTR is the **maintainability** parameter of a system. It is the *reciprocal* of the **repair rate (μ)** in reliability engineering, i.e., **MTTR = 1/μ**.

### Component-Wise Definition of MTTR

A complete repair cycle in the KTU framework includes **five discrete time intervals**:

1. **Detection Time (Mean Time To Detect – MTTD):** Time from actual failure to recognition of failure.
2. **Diagnosis / Troubleshooting Time:** Time to identify the root cause of failure.
3. **Logistics / Spare-Part Procurement Time:** Waiting for spares, tools, or technicians.
4. **Active Repair Time:** Actual hands-on replacement, welding, reconnection, etc.
5. **Testing & Restoration Time:** Reassembly, calibration, and trial-run verification.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Some KTU problems define MTTR strictly as the *active repair time* (excluding logistics delay), while others include *all administrative and logistics delays* under the broader umbrella term **Mean Down Time (MDT)**. Always read the problem statement carefully.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy – The Smartphone Repair Shop:**
> Imagine you own a smartphone repair shop. Over a month, you repair **10 phones** and log the total time the phones were "out of action" in your shop as **30 hours**.
> Your **MTTR = 30 / 10 = 3 hours per phone**.
> 
> Now, if you hire a better technician and stock spare parts in advance, the *same repair* may take only 1.5 hours — your MTTR drops, your shop becomes more productive, and your customers are happier.
