# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Lean Production. Mention its origin.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Lean Production is a systematic approach to producing goods and services using **minimum resources** while **maximizing customer value**, by continuously identifying and eliminating waste (*muda*) from every process. It originated from the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** developed by **Taiichi Ohno** in post-WWII Japan, and was formally named "Lean" by **John Krafcik (1988)** in his MIT Sloan Management Review article. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for origin in Japan, 1 for Krafcik naming)*

---

### Q2. List the **two pillars** of the Toyota Production System.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The two pillars of TPS are:

1. **Just-In-Time (JIT)** — a pull-based production system in which each process produces **only what the next downstream process needs, in the right quantity, at the right time**, thereby minimizing inventory.
2. **Jidoka (Autonomation)** — *"automation with a human touch"*, which empowers workers and machines to **detect abnormalities and stop the line** the moment a defect occurs, preventing defective products from flowing downstream.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks per pillar with one-line example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 1 Internal Choice Rule**: Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define Lean Production. Explain the **5 principles of Lean** as given by Womack and Jones. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A furniture manufacturing unit in Kochi operates **one shift of 8 hours per day, 6 days a week**. Break time is **1 hour per shift**. Daily demand is **120 chairs**. The actual processing time per chair is **22 minutes** and the total lead time is **8 days**.
Calculate the **Takt Time** and the **Value-Added Ratio**, and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a) — Definition and 5 Principles (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Lean Production is a socio-technical methodology, derived from the Toyota Production System, aimed at **eliminating waste** (overproduction, waiting, transport, over-processing, inventory, motion, defects) and creating **continuous flow** of value to the customer via a **pull system**, so that products and services are delivered **at the right time, in the right quantity, at the right quality, and at the lowest cost**.

**5 Principles (5 marks, 1 mark each):**

1. **Specify Value** — Value is defined only by the **end customer**, in terms of a specific product that meets a need at a given price and time.
2. **Identify the Value Stream** — Map every step from raw material to customer and classify each as value-adding, necessary non-value-adding, or waste.
3. **Make Value Flow** — Reorganize the remaining steps so that the product moves continuously without stops, queues, or backflows.
4. **Establish Pull** — Production is triggered by **actual customer demand**, not by forecasts. Upstream processes produce only on a Kanban signal from downstream.
5. **Pursue Perfection** — Lean is a continuous journey; through *Kaizen*, the organization relentlessly removes the remaining waste and creates new value.

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- 1 shift × 8 hours × 6 days per week
- Break time = 1 hour per shift
- Daily demand = 120 chairs
- Processing time per chair = 22 minutes
- Total lead time = 8 days

**Step 1 — Net available time per shift:**

$$ \text{Net time per shift} = (8 \times 60) - 60 = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ min} $$

**Step 2 — Available production time per day:**

$$ \text{Available time per day} = 420 \text{ min} $$

**Step 3 — Takt Time:**

$$ TT = \frac{420}{120} = 3.5 \text{ min/chair} $$

In seconds:

$$ TT = 3.5 \times 60 = 210 \text{ seconds/chair} $$

**Step 4 — Total lead time in minutes:**

$$ LT = 8 \text{ days} \times 420 \text{ min/day} = 3360 \text{ min} $$

**Step 5 — Value-Added Ratio:**

$$ VAR = \frac{22}{3360} \times 100\% = 0.6548\% \approx 0.65\% $$

**Step 6 — Comment (2 marks):**
- The VAR of only **0.65%** indicates that the process spends **99.35% of its lead time on non-value-adding activities** (waiting, transport, inspection, idle time).
- The Takt Time of **3.5 min/chair** is the production pace required to satisfy demand; the current processing time of 22 min far exceeds this pace, indicating a serious bottleneck.
- **Recommendations:** implement cellular layout for single-piece flow, introduce SMED to reduce changeover, and apply 5S to remove motion waste.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **concept of waste (muda)** in Lean Production. Enumerate and briefly describe the **7 wastes (TIMWOOD)** with one industrial example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Mass Production** and **Lean Production** along **any 8 dimensions**, highlighting which approach is more suitable in the **Indian automotive industry** today. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a) — Concept of Waste and 7 Wastes (7 marks)

**Concept of Muda (1 mark):**
*Muda* is a Japanese term for any activity that **consumes resources but creates no value** for the customer. Lean defines waste as the opposite of value; eliminating muda is the central objective of Lean Production.

**7 Wastes (TIMWOOD) — 1 mark each:**

| # | Waste | Description | Industrial Example |
|---|-------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **T**ransport | Unnecessary movement of materials between locations | A Kerala spice factory moving sacks across the yard 4 times before packaging |
| 2 | **I**nventory | Excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods tying up capital | A garment warehouse in Tirupur holding 6 months of unsold stock |
| 3 | **M**otion | Unnecessary human movement (walking, reaching, bending) | Operator walking 20 m to fetch a tool every cycle |
| 4 | **W**aiting | Idle time for people, machines, or information | CNC machine idle while waiting for the operator to load the next part |
|  5 | **O**verproduction | Producing more than, or earlier than, needed | A bakery baking 500 buns at 6 AM when only 300 are sold per day |
| 6 | **O**ver-processing | Doing more work than the customer requires | Painting a surface that will be hidden under another panel |
| 7 | **D**efects | Rework, scrap, and warranty costs | A printed circuit board that fails final test and must be reworked |

### Part (b) — Comparison and Indian Context (7 marks)

**8 Dimensions (1 mark each) + Indian context (1 mark for recommendation) = 7 marks**

| Dimension | Mass Production | Lean Production |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Batch size** | Large | Small / one-piece flow |
| **Inventory** | High buffer | Minimal Kanban-controlled |
| **Lead time** | Long (months) | Short (days) |
| **Quality** | Inspect at end | Built-in at source |
| **Flexibility** | Low (rigid line) | High (cellular) |
| **Supplier** | Adversarial, many | Partnership, few |
| **Workforce** | Narrow specialization | Multi-skilled teams |
| **Improvement** | Project-based, rare | Continuous Kaizen |

**Indian Automotive Industry Verdict (2 marks):**
The Indian automotive industry — facing **diverse customer demand, short product life-cycles, rising customization, and global competition** — is more suited to **Lean Production**. Mass production's large inventories cannot survive in the volatile, multi-model Indian market. Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki, and Ashok Leyland have successfully adopted Lean (and its cousin, the **Toyota Production System**) to remain globally competitive.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks
> 1. **Confusing Lean with Six Sigma or TQM.** Lean is **waste elimination**; Six Sigma is **variation reduction**; TQM is a **broader quality philosophy**. Mixing them costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Omitting Japanese terms.** KTU examiners reward the use of correct terminology: *muda*, *mura*, *muri*, *Kanban*, *Kaizen*, *Jidoka*, *Poka-Yoke*, *Heijunka*. Bonus marks for 2+ Japanese terms in any 14-mark answer.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Always write *3.5 min/chair*, not just *3.5*. Missing units = 0.5 mark deducted per instance.
> 4. **Writing the 7 wastes in the wrong order or with incorrect names.** Use the standard **TIMWOOD** ordering; do not invent acronyms.
> 5. **Treating Lean as "just-in-time inventory".** Lean is much broader — it is a **socio-technical management philosophy**, not merely an inventory tool. A narrow definition loses up to 1 mark in Part A.
> 6. **No diagram in Part B on Lean.** A neatly labelled **House of Lean** or **Value-Stream block diagram** typically earns **1–2 extra marks** for presentation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP] High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Lean Production** = systematic elimination of waste + creation of continuous value flow to the customer.
- **Origin**: Toyota Production System (TPS), **Taiichi Ohno**, post-1950 Japan.
- **Naming**: "Lean" coined by **John Krafcik (1988)**; popularized by **Womack, Jones and Roos (1990)**.
- **Goal**: deliver the right product, in the right quantity, at the right time, at the right quality, at the lowest cost.
- **Two Pillars**: **Just-In-Time (JIT)** and **Jidoka (Autonomation)**.
- **Foundation of TPS**: **5S, Standardization, Heijunka, Kaizen**.
- **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD)**: Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects (8th: Unused talent).
- **5 Principles (Womack–Jones)**: Value → Value Stream → Flow → Pull → Perfection (mnemonic: **VWFP-P**).
- **Push vs Pull**: Mass = forecast-driven push; Lean = demand-driven pull (Kanban).
- **Key Metrics to Memorize**: Takt Time, Cycle Time, Lead Time, VAR, OEE, FTR, Inventory Turns.
- **Takt Time formula**: $TT = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Daily Customer Demand}}$.
- **VAR formula**: $VAR = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\%$.
- **Always use net available time** (gross time minus breaks) when computing Takt Time.
- **Lean ≠ Just-In-Time inventory.** Lean is a **socio-technical management system**.
- **Indian examples to quote**: Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki, Ashok Leyland, TVS, Larsen and Toubro.
- **Japanese bonus terms** to weave into answers: *muda, mura, muri, kanban, kaizen, jidoka, poka-yoke, heijunka, andon, gemba*.
- **Diagrams to draw**: House of Lean (TPS House), Value-Stream Map, 7-Waste map, Kaizen PDCA wheel.
- **Differentiate**: Lean (waste elimination), Six Sigma (variation reduction), TQM (total quality culture), Agile (Lean in software).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lean demand-pull logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lean demand-pull logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lean demand-pull logic** is a production and inventory control philosophy in which the *actual consumption* (customer demand) of a product at the downstream end of the supply chain acts as the **trigger** to authorize the production and movement of the *upstream* items. Materials are only "pulled" into production when a downstream operation signals a need, replacing the traditional forecast-driven "push" approach. It is the operational backbone of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and forms the engineering foundation of **Just-in-Time (JIT)** manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Lean demand-pull logic is the principle under which *nothing is produced or moved until the downstream customer (internal or external) requests it*, thereby eliminating overproduction, excess inventory, and waste (*muda*).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Supermarket

Imagine walking into a **supermarket** to buy milk.

- You walk to the dairy shelf, pick a bottle, and pay at the counter.
- The cashier's system automatically triggers a **replenishment signal** to the stockroom.
- The stockboy brings a fresh carton *only after* one is taken by a customer.
- The delivery truck brings more milk *only after* the stockboy restocks the shelf.

Notice what is **NOT** happening:
- The dairy factory does not push 10,000 bottles hoping someone buys them.
- The supermarket does not pre-stock a year's supply "just in case."

> [!NOTE]
> **The Customer's Hand = The Pull Signal.** The entire chain (Customer → Shelf → Stockroom → Truck → Factory) only reacts because the *final demand occurred*. This is **pull** in its purest form.

In a manufacturing context:
- The **final assembly line** consumes Part A.
- This consumption sends a **Kanban card** upstream to the fabrication cell.
- The fabrication cell produces *only the exact quantity consumed*.
- This signal continues upstream until it reaches raw material suppliers.

## 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | KTU-Standard Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pull System** | Production triggered by actual downstream demand, not forecasts. |
| **Kanban** | Japanese word meaning *"visual signal"* or *"card"*. The physical/digital trigger. |
| **JIT (Just-in-Time)** | Producing only what is needed, only when needed, in the exact quantity needed. |
| **Takt Time** | The beat/rhythm of production matched to customer demand rate. |
| **Muda** | Japanese word for **waste** — any activity that consumes resources without creating value. |
| **Heijunka** | Leveling production volume and mix to match demand. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor:** *Pull = Reactive. Push = Predictive.* Demand-pull logic converts the entire production system into a reactive organism that breathes with the customer's pulse.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 5 Foundational Principles of Lean Demand-Pull

### Principle 1 — Customer Defines Value
- The **final customer** (internal or external) is the only entity authorized to define "what is needed."
- Nothing is produced without an explicit downstream request.

### Principle 2 — Zero Inventory Buffer ("Water Flow Logic")
- Imagine a river: it flows smoothly only when there are no rocks (batches/inventory) obstructing it.
- **Small lot sizes** + **continuous flow** + **no stockpiling** = smooth, fast production.
- Inventory is treated as a **liability**, not an asset — it hides defects, ties up cash, and obscures problems.

### Principle 3 — Takt Time Governs Pace
- Every workstation must complete its task within the **takt time** window, otherwise the entire line falls behind.
- This synchronizes the heartbeat of the factory to the customer's purchase rate.

### Principle 4 — Kanban as the Signaling Mechanism
- A **Kanban** (card, light, bin, or electronic signal) authorizes the release of a fixed quantity of material.
- Two classical Kanban types:
  - **Production Kanban (P-Kanban):** Triggers production at the upstream cell.
  - **Withdrawal Kanban (W-Kanban):** Authorizes the movement of material between cells.

### Principle 5 — Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
- The pull system *exposes* problems immediately (because there are no inventory buffers to hide them).
- Exposed problems are solved systematically → **Kaizen** → reduced muda.

## 2.2 The Three Cardinal Wastes Eliminated by Pull

1. **Overproduction** — the most dangerous waste, because it *creates* all other wastes.
2. **Waiting** — workers/machines idle due to uncoordinated pushes.
3. **Excess Inventory** — capital frozen in raw materials, WIP, and finished goods.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Variables | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| $$T = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{D}$$ | $T$ = Takt Time; $T_{avail}$ = Net available production time; $D$ = Customer demand (units/period) | Determines the **production rhythm** to match demand. |
| $$N = \left\lceil \dfrac{d \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{C} \right\rceil$$ | $N$ = Number of Kanban cards; $d$ = Demand rate (units/time); $L$ = Lead time; $S$ = Safety factor (0 ≤ S ≤ 1); $C$ = Container capacity | Calculates the **number of Kanban cards** required. |
| $$WIP_{max} = N \cdot C$$ | $WIP_{max}$ = Maximum Work-In-Process inventory | Quantifies **bounded WIP** enforced by pull. |
| $$\text{Lead Time} = \text{Processing Time} + \text{Queue Time} + \text{Wait Time}$$ | Aggregate flow time through the system | The metric pull aims to **minimize**. |
| $$\text{Throughput Time} = \dfrac{WIP}{\text{Completion Rate}} \quad \text{(Little's Law)}$$ | Relationship between inventory and flow | Foundation of **flow efficiency** analysis. |
| $$\text{Cycle Time}_{eff} = \text{Value-Added Time} + \text{Non-Value-Added Time}$$ | Decomposition of process time | Quantifies **muda** in any process. |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Notation Safeguard:** All absolute-value notations in your exam must use `\vert` (e.g., $x \vert y$) — **never** the unescaped pipe `\vert` inside a markdown table row.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Industry | Application of Demand-Pull |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Toyota's assembly lines; every bolt, seat, and engine arrives exactly when needed. |
| **Healthcare** | Hospital pharmacy replenishment triggered by actual ward consumption. |
| **Food & Beverage** | Starbucks' "made-to-order" beverage flow. |
| **Electronics** | Apple's build-to-order supply chain. |
| **Software / DevOps** | Kanban boards in Agile; user story completion triggers next task. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kanban Sizing Formula

The **number of Kanban cards** is engineered to ensure that the upstream cell always has *enough* material to cover demand during the lead-time replenishment cycle, *without* building excess inventory.

### Step 1 — Identify Demand Rate
Let $d$ be the average demand for a part at the downstream workstation, measured in **units per unit time** (e.g., units/hour).

### Step 2 — Account for Lead Time
During the lead time $L$ (time taken to replenish a container after a withdrawal signal), the downstream station will consume:

$$
\text{Demand during lead time} = d \cdot L
$$

### Step 3 — Add the Safety Buffer
To absorb variability (machine breakdowns, demand spikes, defects), multiply by the safety factor $(1+S)$:

$$
\text{Buffered demand} = d \cdot L \cdot (1+S)
$$

### Step 4 — Divide by Container Size
Since Kanban cards authorize movement of $C$ units at a time, the number of cards required is:

$$
N = \dfrac{d \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{C}
$$

### Step 5 — Round Up to the Nearest Integer
Because you cannot issue *half a Kanban*, apply the ceiling function:

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{d \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{C} \right\rceil
$$

### Worked Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

> **Problem:** A workstation consumes a part at a rate of $d = 50$ units/hour. The supplier's lead time is $L = 2$ hours. The container holds $C = 25$ units. The safety factor is $S = 0.2$ (i.e., 20%). Find the number of Kanban cards.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \left\lceil \dfrac{d \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{C} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \dfrac{50 \times 2 \times (1+0.2)}{25} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \dfrac{50 \times 2 \times 1.2}{25} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \dfrac{120}{25} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil 4.8 \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= 5 \text{ cards}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Five Kanban cards are circulating in the loop. At any instant, the system holds up to:

$$
WIP_{max} = N \cdot C = 5 \times 25 = 125 \text{ units}
$$

This is the **bounded WIP** — the *maximum* inventory that can ever exist, regardless of upstream overproduction. This is the mathematical "ceiling" that pull logic enforces.

## 3.2 Derivation of Takt Time

### Step 1 — Define Net Available Time
Let $T_{avail}$ be the total production time available, *minus* planned breaks, changeovers, and maintenance:

$$
T_{avail} = T_{total} - T_{breaks} - T_{changeover} - T_{maintenance}
$$

### Step 2 — Define Customer Demand
Let $D$ be the total customer demand for the planning period (e.g., 8-hour shift).

### Step 3 — Compute Takt Time
Takt time is the *maximum* time allowed to produce one unit in order to satisfy demand:

$$
T = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{D}
$$

### Worked Example

> A factory operates 8 hours/day with 1 hour of breaks and 30 minutes of changeovers. Customer demand is 390 units/day. Find the Takt Time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avail} &= (8 \times 60) - 60 - 30 = 480 - 90 = 390 \text{ minutes} \\[6pt]
T &= \dfrac{T_{avail}}{D} = \dfrac{390}{390} = 1 \text{ minute/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Every workstation must complete its task in **≤ 1 minute** to keep pace with demand.

## 3.3 Push vs. Pull — Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Push System (Traditional) | Pull System (Lean) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Forecast / schedule | Actual downstream consumption |
| **Inventory** | High (buffers everywhere) | Low / bounded by Kanban size |
| **Lead Time** | Long (hidden by inventory) | Short (exposed & minimized) |
| **Flexibility** | Low (locked by forecast) | High (responds in real time) |
| **WIP** | Unbounded | $WIP_{max} = N \cdot C$ (bounded) |
| **Defect Visibility** | Low (defects hidden in WIP) | High (defects surface immediately) |
| **Capital Tie-Up** | High | Minimal |
| **Wastes Created** | Overproduction, waiting, transport, inventory | None (system inherently eliminates these) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Pull System Information Flow (Kanban Loop)

The following diagram illustrates the closed-loop information flow in a demand-pull system. Notice how the **only signal** propagating *upstream* is a Kanban authorizing production, and the **only flow** propagating *downstream* is the material itself.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Customer[Customer Demand] --> FinalAssembly[Final Assembly Station]
    FinalAssembly -- consumes Part A --> WIPStore[Supermarket WIP Store]
    WIPStore -- empty bin triggers --> KanbanW[Withdrawal Kanban Signal]
    KanbanW -- moves upstream --> FabCell[Fabrication Cell A]
    FabCell -- produces to refill --> ProdKanban[Production Kanban Issued]
    ProdKanban -- authorizes production --> FabCell
    FabCell -- finished parts flow downstream --> WIPStore
    WIPStore -- replenished stock available --> FinalAssembly
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this as a closed loop:** When the Final Assembly consumes a part, the empty bin at the WIP Store triggers a Withdrawal Kanban. This signal reaches the Fabrication Cell, which issues a Production Kanban to fabricate replacement parts. The new parts flow downstream to refill the store, completing the cycle.

## 4.2 Push vs. Pull — Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUSH[Push System Architecture]
        FC1[Forecast] --> S1[Schedule]
        S1 --> P1[Plant A Produces Blindly]
        P1 --> P2[Plant B Produces Blindly]
        P2 --> B1[Buffer Inventory Builds Up]
        B1 --> C1[Customer Receives from Stock]
        C1 -. feedback gap .-> FC1
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PULL[Pull System Architecture]
        C2[Customer Orders] --> AS[Assembly Cell]
        AS -- kanban signal --> FC2[Fabrication Cell]
        FC2 -- kanban signal --> SUP[Supplier]
        SUP -- material flow --> FC2
        FC2 -- material flow --> AS
        AS -- finished goods --> C2
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Architectural Insight:** In the Push model, materials *flow forward* but *information about demand is delayed* (or absent). In the Pull model, materials flow forward *only after* an explicit, real-time, customer-anchored signal authorizes them. The information flow direction is *identical* to the material flow, just opposite in direction.

## 4.3 The Five-Step Pull Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Downstream station consumes one container] --> A2[Step 2: Empty container returned with Kanban to supermarket]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Withdrawal Kanban travels to upstream process]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Upstream produces one container of parts]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Filled container with Production Kanban moves downstream]
    A5 -. loop restart when consumed .-> A1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Q:** Define *Lean demand-pull logic*. How does it differ fundamentally from a traditional push-based production system?

**Model Answer:**
Lean demand-pull logic is an operations philosophy in which the *actual consumption* of a product by a downstream operation (or end customer) acts as the trigger to authorize the production and movement of upstream materials. It eliminates forecast-driven overproduction.

**Difference:** In a **push system**, production is initiated based on *forecasted demand* and scheduled in advance — materials are pushed into the system regardless of whether they are immediately needed. In a **pull system**, production only begins when a *downstream signal* (Kanban) indicates actual consumption. Push results in high WIP and poor responsiveness; pull results in minimal WIP, high flexibility, and immediate defect visibility. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q:** What is a *Kanban*? State the two principal types of Kanban and briefly explain the function of each.

**Model Answer:**
A Kanban (Japanese for *"visual card"*) is an authorization signal used in a pull system to trigger the production or movement of a fixed quantity of material. It is the *information carrier* that links downstream consumption to upstream production.

The two principal types are:
1. **Withdrawal Kanban (W-Kanban):** Authorizes the *movement* of parts from an upstream supermarket or buffer to the downstream workstation.
2. **Production Kanban (P-Kanban):** Authorizes the *production* of a specific quantity at the upstream fabrication cell.

Together, they form the closed-loop signaling mechanism that makes the pull system self-regulating. *(3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Module 1 Choice Option 1]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **5 foundational principles** of Lean demand-pull logic. Why is *overproduction* considered the most dangerous form of waste in such a system?

**Model Answer:**

The five foundational principles are:

1. **Value Defined by Customer:** Only the end customer (internal or external) defines what is valuable. Production is anchored to actual demand, not managerial assumption.
2. **Value Stream Mapping:** Identify every step from raw material to customer and eliminate those that add no value (muda).
3. **Continuous Flow:** Establish smooth, uninterrupted movement of products through the value stream with minimal WIP.
4. **Pull System:** Let the downstream customer *pull* items; nothing is pushed upstream without an explicit signal.
5. **Perfection / Kaizen:** Continuous incremental improvement until all waste is eliminated.

**Why Overproduction is the Most Dangerous Waste:**

Overproduction is the *root* waste because it *manufactures all other wastes simultaneously*. Excess production:
- Creates excess inventory (waste of capital and space).
- Causes unnecessary transportation and motion.
- Generates excess waiting when downstream cannot absorb it.
- Causes over-processing to "find homes" for surplus.
- **Hides defects**, since defective parts get buried under mountains of good ones.

In a pull system, overproduction is structurally *impossible* because the Kanban mechanism only authorizes production of an exact quantity, only when a withdrawal signal arrives. Hence, eliminating overproduction automatically eliminates seven of the seven classical muda. *(7 marks)*

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A workstation consumes a component at a rate of 80 units/hour. The supplier's lead time is 3 hours. Each container holds 40 units, and the safety factor is 0.25. Compute (i) the number of Kanban cards required, and (ii) the maximum Work-In-Process inventory.

**Model Answer:**

Given:
- Demand rate: $d = 80$ units/hour
- Lead time: $L = 3$ hours
- Container size: $C = 40$ units
- Safety factor: $S = 0.25$

**(i) Number of Kanban cards:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \left\lceil \dfrac{d \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{C} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \dfrac{80 \times 3 \times (1+0.25)}{40} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \dfrac{80 \times 3 \times 1.25}{40} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil \dfrac{300}{40} \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= \left\lceil 7.5 \right\rceil \\[6pt]
&= 8 \text{ Kanban cards}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum WIP inventory:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WIP_{max} &= N \times C \\[6pt]
&= 8 \times 40 \\[6pt]
&= 320 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** **8 Kanban cards** are needed, enforcing a strict ceiling of **320 units** of WIP at all times. This mathematical bound is what gives a pull system its inventory discipline. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Module 1 Choice Option 2]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Define *Takt Time*. A factory operates for 7 hours/day, with 45 minutes of breaks and 30 minutes of planned changeovers. Customer demand for the day is 365 units. Calculate the Takt Time and explain its operational significance in a pull system.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Takt Time (from the German *Taktzeit*, meaning *"cycle time"*) is the maximum allowable time to produce one unit in order to satisfy customer demand. It sets the rhythm of the entire production line.

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avail} &= (7 \times 60) - 45 - 30 \\[6pt]
&= 420 - 75 \\[6pt]
&= 345 \text{ minutes} \\[6pt]
T &= \dfrac{T_{avail}}{D} = \dfrac{345}{365} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.945 \text{ minutes/unit} \\[6pt]
&\approx 56.7 \text{ seconds/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Operational Significance:**
1. **Pacing:** Every workstation must complete its task in ≤ 56.7 seconds. This synchronizes the entire line.
2. **Capacity Planning:** If a station's cycle time exceeds Takt Time, the line cannot meet demand — a clear *signal* to add capacity or rebalance.
3. **Pull Synchronization:** Takt Time dictates the rate at which Kanban cards must be circulated. If Kanban circulation lags Takt Time, the pull rhythm breaks.
4. **Continuous Improvement Benchmark:** Reducing Takt Time = increasing throughput without adding resources. *(7 marks)*

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare Push and Pull production systems across **any five operational parameters**. Why is demand-pull logic often described as the "nervous system" of Lean manufacturing?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Push System | Pull System |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Forecast (predicted demand) | Actual consumption (real demand) |
| **Inventory Level** | High; safety stock everywhere | Low; bounded by $N \times C$ |
| **Lead Time** | Long (hidden by inventory) | Short (exposed & actively reduced) |
| **Defect Visibility** | Low (defects buried in WIP) | High (defects surface immediately) |
| **Responsiveness** | Slow (requires forecast revision) | Real-time (Kanban signaling) |

**Why Pull is the "Nervous System" of Lean:**

Just as the human nervous system transmits signals from sensory receptors to muscles *only* when needed (without flooding every muscle with constant impulses), the pull system transmits Kanban signals from downstream demand to upstream production *only* when a real consumption event occurs. This ensures that every "muscle" (workstation) is activated precisely when required — preventing wasteful, unsolicited activity. The Kanban functions like a *nerve impulse*, the supermarket like a *synaptic junction*, and the upstream cell like the *effector muscle*. Without this signaling architecture, Lean collapses into a forecast-driven pseudo-lean, defeating its purpose. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Ceiling Function** — Always apply $\lceil \, \cdot \, \rceil$ when computing Kanban cards. Writing $N = 7.5$ instead of $N = 8$ costs **2 marks** in Part B numericals.
> 2. **Confusing Withdrawal Kanban with Production Kanban** — W-Kanban = *movement authorization*; P-Kanban = *production authorization*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Stating "Pull = No Inventory"** — This is wrong. Pull = *bounded* inventory ($WIP_{max} = N \cdot C$). Zero inventory is impossible and breaks the system.
> 4. **Ignoring Safety Factor $(1+S)$** — A common oversight. Always include it unless the question explicitly states $S = 0$.
> 5. **Defining Takt Time as "Production Time"** — Takt Time is a *rate* (time/unit), not a duration. Mis-stating this invites mark loss on definition questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lean Demand-Pull Logic:**

- ✅ **Core Idea:** Production is *triggered* by downstream consumption, *not* by upstream forecast.
- ✅ **Origin:** Toyota Production System (TPS) — Taiichi Ohno's philosophy.
- ✅ **Kanban:** The signaling mechanism; can be a card, light, bin, or electronic signal.
- ✅ **Two Kanban Types:** Withdrawal (W-Kanban) and Production (P-Kanban).
- ✅ **Takt Time Formula:** $T = T_{avail} / D$ — sets the heartbeat of production.
- ✅ **Kanban Number Formula:** $N = \lceil d \cdot L \cdot (1+S) / C \rceil$ — enforces bounded WIP.
- ✅ **WIP Ceiling:** $WIP_{max} = N \cdot C$ — the mathematical guarantee of inventory discipline.
- ✅ **Five Principles:** Value, Value Stream, Flow, Pull, Perfection (Kaizen).
- ✅ **Biggest Waste Eliminated:** Overproduction — it *creates* all other wastes.
- ✅ **Pull ≠ Zero Inventory:** Pull means *minimum necessary* inventory, not zero.
- ✅ **Pull is Reactive:** Push is Predictive — this contrast is a guaranteed exam question.
- ✅ **Push Result:** Forecast errors → inventory pile-up → hidden defects → high cost.
- ✅ **Pull Result:** Real-demand signals → bounded WIP → visible defects → continuous improvement.
- ✅ **Cross-Industry Relevance:** Automotives, healthcare, food, electronics, software (Agile/Kanban boards).
- ✅ **KTU Golden Rule:** Always show the formula, plug in values, apply the ceiling, and *interpret the result* in operational terms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waste in operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Waste in operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Operations Management and Lean Production Systems, **waste** (Japanese: **Muda**) is formally defined as any human activity, operational process, or resource consumption that **absorbs resources but creates no value** for the end customer. The concept is rooted in the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** developed by Taiichi Ohno and is the foundational pillar of **Lean Manufacturing** philosophy.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 outcomes, waste in operations refers to **non-value-adding activities (NVA)** that increase cycle time, inflate operational cost, reduce throughput, degrade quality, and ultimately diminish customer-perceived value. A complementary concept, **Mura** (unevenness) and **Muri** (overburden), are the systemic cousins of Muda, and together they form the **3M model** of operational inefficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Recall-Ready):**
> *"Waste is any activity that consumes resources (time, labour, material, machine hours, or capital) without contributing to the conversion of inputs into outputs that the customer is willing to pay for."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a small restaurant kitchen preparing a single dish — a butter chicken curry for one customer.

- The chef **chops onions, marinates chicken, simmers the sauce** — these are *value-adding* steps. The customer pays for these.
- The chef **walks 8 metres to fetch a spice jar that was misplaced**, **waits 4 minutes for the previous pot to be washed**, **re-cuts a tomato that was poorly sliced the first time**, or **prepares an extra 500g of chicken "just in case"** — these are **wastes**.

None of these activities change the flavour, temperature, or quality of the curry. Yet they consume:
- ⏱ **Time** (waiting, walking)
- 💰 **Money** (extra material, extra labour)
- 🏭 **Capacity** (the burner is occupied with a pot no one ordered)

This is exactly what happens in a factory, hospital, bank, or software firm. **Waste is the silent tax on every operation.** Recognising it is the first step toward Lean transformation.

## 1.3 The Two Categories of Activity (Value-Added vs Non-Value-Added)

| Activity Type | Code | Description | Customer Willingness to Pay? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Value-Added (VA)** | VA | Transforms the product/service towards customer requirements | ✅ Yes |
| **Non-Value-Added (NVA)** | NVA | Consumes resources but adds no customer value | ❌ No |
| **Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA)** | NNVA | Required by regulation, law, or current technology limitations (e.g., safety inspections) | ❌ No, but unavoidable today |

> [!NOTE]
> The Lean goal is **not** to eliminate all NVA, but to systematically reduce NVA to the absolute minimum, and where possible, eliminate it entirely through process redesign, automation, or Kaizen (continuous improvement).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Waste

- **Value-Added Ratio (VAR):** 
  $$\text{VAR} = \frac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\%$$
- **First-Time Right (FTR):** Percentage of outputs produced without rework.
- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE):** Availability × Performance × Quality.
- **Inventory Days of Supply:** Average days of inventory held against consumption rate.
- **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE):** Same as VAR, commonly used in service operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark:** World-class Lean manufacturers achieve a **Value-Added Ratio of >25%**, while typical non-Lean operations hover around **5–10%**. This single statistic is enough to convince any KTU examiner of waste's financial magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD) — Original Toyota Classification

Taiichi Ohno identified **seven classical wastes**, memorably encoded as the acronym **TIMWOOD**. Every KTU question on this topic begins with this framework.

### 2.1.1 **T — Transportation**
- **Definition:** Unnecessary movement of materials, parts, or information between processes.
- **Root Cause:** Poor facility layout, batch processing, distant storage, fragmented supply chain.
- **Cost Impact:** Handling damage, lead-time inflation, fuel/labour cost, capital tied in Work-In-Progress (WIP) during transit.
- **Real-World Engineering Example:** Moving a component between 5 workstations on different floors when an inline U-shaped cell could do the job.
- **Countermeasure:** *Cellular manufacturing, point-of-use storage, U-shaped lines, milk-run logistics.*

### 2.1.2 **I — Inventory**
- **Definition:** All held stock — Raw Materials, Work-In-Progress (WIP), and Finished Goods — beyond immediate need.
- **Root Cause:** Overproduction, long setup times, unreliable suppliers, demand forecasting errors, batch-and-queue mentality.
- **Cost Impact:** Carrying cost (15–30% of inventory value/year), obsolescence, capital lock-up, storage space, risk of damage.
- **Hidden Dangers:** Hides other wastes (defects, delays, scheduling chaos) — often called "the root of all evil" in Lean literature.
- **Countermeasure:** *Just-In-Time (JIT), Kanban, level scheduling (Heijunka), demand-driven MRP.*

### 2.1.3 **M — Motion**
- **Definition:** Unnecessary movement of **people** (or equipment) within a workstation.
- **Root Cause:** Poor ergonomic design, cluttered workplace, misplaced tools, inconsistent work methods.
- **Cost Impact:** Worker fatigue, musculoskeletal disorders, increased cycle time.
- **Distinct from Transportation:** Motion = people move; Transportation = materials move.
- **Countermeasure:** *5S workplace organisation, ergonomic redesign, shadow boards, point-of-use tools.*

### 2.1.4 **W — Waiting**
- **Definition:** Idle time when people, machines, or information are stalled due to imbalances.
- **Root Cause:** Uneven workflow, breakdowns, missing information, approval delays, late supplier deliveries.
- **Cost Impact:** Capacity loss, idle labour cost, delayed order fulfilment, customer dissatisfaction.
- **Countermeasure:** *Takt-time balancing, SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die), Total Productive Maintenance (TPM).*

### 2.1.5 **O — Overproduction**
- **Definition:** Producing more, sooner, or faster than the customer requires.
- **Why It Is the "Worst" Waste:** It directly causes **Inventory waste**, hides **Defects**, and creates **Waiting** downstream.
- **Root Cause:** Long setup times, push-based systems, poor demand visibility, large batch incentive, departmental efficiency metrics.
- **Countermeasure:** *Pull system (Kanban), One-Piece Flow, Takt-time synchronisation, Mixed-Model Production.*

### 2.1.6 **O — Over-processing (Extra-Processing)**
- **Definition:** Doing more work, or more precise work, than the customer requires or is willing to pay for.
- **Examples:** Tight tolerances beyond functional need, excessive polishing, redundant approvals, duplicate data entry, over-engineered reports.
- **Root Cause:** Lack of Voice of Customer (VOC), outdated specifications, copy-paste process design.
- **Countermeasure:** *Quality Function Deployment (QFD), simplification, poka-yoke, standard work.*

### 2.1.7 **D — Defects**
- **Definition:** Production of products or services that fail to meet specifications, requiring rework, repair, or scrap.
- **Cost Impact:** Rework labour, material scrap, warranty claims, reputational damage, lost customers.
- **Industry Tag:** Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) typically runs **15–40% of total operations cost** in un-Lean firms.
- **Countermeasure:** *Poka-yoke (mistake-proofing), Statistical Process Control (SPC), root-cause analysis (5-Why, Ishikawa), Six Sigma.*

## 2.2 The Eighth Waste (Modern Extension)

Modern Lean thinking (post-2000) adds an **eighth waste** that is people-centric:

### 2.2.1 **Non-Utilized Talent (Skills)**
- **Definition:** Failing to engage, develop, or leverage the intellect, creativity, and skills of employees.
- **Examples:** Ignoring Kaizen suggestions, no empowerment, no cross-training, autocratic supervision, hoarding talent in silos.
- **Why It Matters in KTU Context:** Service operations (banks, hospitals, IT) are heavily talent-driven. A disengaged employee is a walking inventory of unused potential.
- **Countermeasure:** *Suggestion systems, cross-functional teams, training, respect-for-people culture, employee autonomy.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **DOWNTIME** acronym is an alternative teaching mnemonic used in academic texts:
> **D**efects, **O**verproduction, **W**aiting, **N**on-utilized talent, **T**ransportation, **I**nventory, **M**otion, **E**xtra-processing.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol | Equation | Unit / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Value-Added Ratio** | VAR | $\text{VAR} = \frac{T_{VA}}{T_{LT}} \times 100$ | % (0–100) | Measures process leanness |
| 2 | **Lead Time** | $T_{LT}$ | $T_{LT} = T_{VA} + T_{NVA}$ | seconds/minutes/hours | Total time to fulfil one order |
| 3 | **Takt Time** | $T_{takt}$ | $T_{takt} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | seconds/unit | Pace of production to match demand |
| 4 | **Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle}$ | Measured at each station | seconds/unit | Time per unit at a workstation |
| 5 | **OEE** | OEE | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | % (World-class ≥ 85%) | Equipment effectiveness |
| 6 | **OEE — Availability** | A | $A = \frac{\text{Planned Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Time}}$ | % | Equipment uptime |
| 7 | **OEE — Performance** | P | $P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Run Time}}$ | % | Speed efficiency |
| 8 | **OEE — Quality** | Q | $Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | % | First-pass yield |
| 9 | **COPQ** | COPQ | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | ₹ / $ / % of sales | Total quality cost |
| 10 | **Inventory Turnover** | IT | $\text{IT} = \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | times/year | Stock movement efficiency |
| 11 | **First Pass Yield** | FPY | $\text{FPY} = \frac{\text{Units passing inspection first time}}{\text{Units inspected}}$ | % | Defect-free output |
| 12 | **Rolled Throughput Yield** | RTY | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | % | Multi-stage yield |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on table notation:** All absolute value bars and conditional symbols are rendered with `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to preserve markdown table integrity, as required by KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol.

## 2.4 Why This Topic Matters Across Engineering Domains

- **Mechanical / Production Engineering:** Waste reduction is the literal goal of CNC programming (cycle-time minimisation), tool-path optimisation, and TPM on shop floors.
- **Civil Engineering:** Waste in construction (material wastage, idle labour, rework) is quantified via Earned Value Management.
- **Electrical / Electronics:** Yield loss in PCB assembly is a defect-waste problem; SMT line balancing addresses waiting waste.
- **Computer Science / IT:** Software waste = unnecessary code, redundant features (over-processing), waiting servers, transport of data over networks, defects (bugs), and motion (context-switching).
- **Service / Management:** Hospital wait times, bank queue lengths, and airline turnaround are all classic waste studies.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Computing Value-Added Ratio (VAR)

A B.Tech project team is documenting the order-fulfilment process for a small electronics firm. They observe the following for a single customer order:

| Step | Description | Time (minutes) | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Order receipt & entry in system | 15 | NVA |
| 2 | Credit check & approval | 20 | NNVA (regulatory) |
| 3 | Component pick from warehouse | 10 | VA |
| 4 | Component transport to assembly line | 8 | NVA |
| 5 | Assembly (soldering + testing) | 25 | VA |
| 6 | Quality inspection | 12 | NNVA (mandatory) |
| 7 | Rework (one defect found) | 18 | NVA |
| 8 | Packaging | 6 | VA |
| 9 | Storage in finished-goods warehouse | 30 | NVA |
| 10 | Dispatch & invoicing | 22 | NVA |

**Step 1 — Identify and separate VA and NVA/NNVA components.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{VA} &= T_{3} + T_{5} + T_{8} \\
T_{VA} &= 10 + 25 + 6 \\
T_{VA} &= 41 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Lead Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{LT} &= \sum_{i=1}^{10} T_i \\
T_{LT} &= 15 + 20 + 10 + 8 + 25 + 12 + 18 + 6 + 30 + 22 \\
T_{LT} &= 166 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Value-Added Ratio (VAR).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAR} &= \frac{T_{VA}}{T_{LT}} \times 100 \\
\text{VAR} &= \frac{41}{166} \times 100 \\
\text{VAR} &= 0.2469 \times 100 \\
\text{VAR} &= 24.69\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result (Board-quality answer).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Only **24.69%** of the customer's waiting time is spent on activities that genuinely add value. The remaining **75.31%** is pure waste (or unavoidable non-value-adding activity). The biggest culprits are: storage (30 min), order processing (15 min), rework (18 min), and dispatch (22 min). This is a textbook case for **Lean process redesign** — moving to pull-based production, cellular layout, and poka-yoke to eliminate the rework and reduce storage.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Takt Time Calculation

A plant operates **2 shifts of 8 hours each**, with a **30-minute** break per shift, and **60 minutes** of planned maintenance per day. **Daily customer demand** is **480 units**.

**Step 1 — Compute Available Production Time per day.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{available} &= (2 \times 8 \times 60) - (2 \times 30) - 60 \\
T_{available} &= 960 - 60 - 60 \\
T_{available} &= 840 \text{ minutes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Takt Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{takt} &= \frac{T_{available}}{\text{Demand}} \\
T_{takt} &= \frac{840 \text{ min}}{480 \text{ units}} \\
T_{takt} &= 1.75 \text{ minutes/unit} \\
T_{takt} &= 105 \text{ seconds/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Decision.**

Every workstation in the production line must have a **cycle time ≤ 105 seconds** to meet customer demand. If any station exceeds this, a **bottleneck (waiting waste)** is created, and either (a) the line must be rebalanced, (b) a second parallel station added, or (c) the process improved via Kaizen.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example 3 — OEE Calculation

A CNC machine operates for **one 8-hour shift (480 minutes)**. Records show:
- Planned production time = 480 min
- Actual downtime (breakdowns + setup) = 60 min
- Ideal cycle time = 1.2 min/unit
- Total pieces produced (run time) = 320 units
- Good pieces (passed final inspection) = 288 units

**Step 1 — Availability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{480 - 60}{480} \\
A &= \frac{420}{480} \\
A &= 0.8750 = 87.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Performance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{1.2 \times 320}{420} \\
P &= \frac{384}{420} \\
P &= 0.9143 = 91.43\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Quality.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{288}{320} \\
Q &= 0.9000 = 90.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — OEE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= A \times P \times Q \\
\text{OEE} &= 0.8750 \times 0.9143 \times 0.9000 \\
\text{OEE} &= 0.7198 = 71.98\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

World-class OEE is ≥ 85%. The current 71.98% indicates significant **hidden waste** — most likely a combination of waiting (downtime), motion (setup), and defects. Each 1% OEE gap typically represents 1% lost throughput, which directly translates to lost revenue.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Waste × Countermeasure × KPI

| Waste | Root Cause Tool | Primary Countermeasure | KTU-Aligned KPI | Target Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Defects** | 5-Why, Ishikawa, FMEA | Poka-yoke, SPC, Six Sigma | First Pass Yield (FPY) | ≥ 99% |
| **Overproduction** | Value Stream Map | Pull / Kanban, Takt-time | WIP days of supply | < 1 day |
| **Waiting** | Spaghetti diagram, line balance | SMED, TPM, Heijunka | Line stoppage frequency | 0 unplanned |
| **Non-utilized Talent** | Skill matrix, Gemba | Suggestion system, cross-training | Ideas implemented / employee / year | ≥ 10 |
| **Transportation** | Layout analysis | Cellular layout, milk-run | Material move distance (m/unit) | < 5 m |
| **Inventory** | Value Stream Map | JIT, level scheduling | Inventory turnover | ≥ 12×/year |
| **Motion** | Spaghetti diagram | 5S, ergonomics | Steps per cycle | Reduced ≥ 30% |
| **Over-processing** | VOC analysis, DFSS | Simplification, QFD | Specs-to-need ratio | 1 : 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU Part B (14-mark) question template**. Memorise the linkage between waste type, tool, countermeasure, and KPI — examiners reward integrated answers.

## 3.5 Analytical Derivation — The Relationship Between Waste and Profit

Let us derive a simple financial model linking waste reduction to profit.

- Let $S$ = Sales revenue, $C_{m}$ = Material cost, $C_{l}$ = Labour cost, $C_{oh}$ = Overhead cost, $C_{waste}$ = Cost of waste (hidden inside overhead).
- Assume $C_{waste} = k \cdot C_{oh}$, where $k$ is the fraction of overhead due to waste (typical: $0.20 \le k \le 0.40$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit} &= S - (C_{m} + C_{l} + C_{oh}) \\
\text{Profit}_{after} &= S - (C_{m} + C_{l} + C_{oh} - \Delta C_{waste})
\end{aligned}
$$

If the firm eliminates a fraction $\alpha$ of waste:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{waste} &= \alpha \cdot k \cdot C_{oh} \\
\Delta \text{Profit} &= \alpha \cdot k \cdot C_{oh} \\
\% \Delta \text{Profit} &= \frac{\alpha \cdot k \cdot C_{oh}}{\text{Profit}_{before}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical illustration:** A firm with $C_{oh} = $ ₹ 50,00,000, $k = 0.30$, current profit margin 8% (i.e., profit = ₹ 8,00,000 on ₹ 1,00,00,000 sales).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \text{Profit} &= 0.50 \times 0.30 \times 50{,}00{,}000 \\
\Delta \text{Profit} &= \text{₹ } 7{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

A 50% waste reduction **nearly doubles** the firm's profit. This is the financial heart of Lean.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define waste in operations. Differentiate between value-added and non-value-added activities with one example each.**

**Model Answer (Board-Key Pattern):**
- *Definition:* Waste (Muda) is any activity that consumes resources (time, labour, material, machine-hours, capital) without creating value from the customer's perspective. *(1 mark)*
- *Value-Added (VA) activity:* An activity that transforms the product/service in a way the customer is willing to pay for.
  - *Example:* Welding two metal sheets to form a car door panel. *(1 mark)*
- *Non-Value-Added (NVA) activity:* An activity that consumes resources but does not add customer-perceived value.
  - *Example:* Moving the welded door panel 50 m across the shop floor to the next station. *(1 mark)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the seven wastes (TIMWOOD) of Lean Manufacturing. Identify which one is considered the "worst" waste and justify why.**

**Model Answer:**
- *List of TIMWOOD:* Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects. *(2 marks — 1 for full list, partial credit for ≥4)*
- *Worst waste:* **Overproduction** is considered the worst waste. *(0.5 marks)*
- *Justification:* Overproduction directly causes inventory accumulation, hides defects (because they are buried in WIP), increases lead time, blocks the identification of waiting and motion waste, and locks up capital in unsold goods. Eliminating it is the gateway to revealing and solving all other wastes. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): The Eight Wastes and Their Countermeasures

**[KTU University Exam – Module 1, Model QP – Variant A]**

**(a) Classify the eight wastes of operations (TIMWOOD + Non-utilized Talent). For each waste, state the root cause and propose one engineering / managerial countermeasure. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Waste | Root Cause | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transportation** | Poor facility layout, distant storage | Cellular manufacturing, point-of-use delivery |
| 2 | **Inventory** | Push system, large batch sizes, long setups | Just-In-Time (JIT), Kanban, SMED |
| 3 | **Motion** | Cluttered, non-ergonomic workstation | 5S workplace organisation, ergonomic redesign |
| 4 | **Waiting** | Unbalanced line, equipment breakdown | Takt-time balancing, TPM |
| 5 | **Overproduction** | Long setups, departmental efficiency KPIs | Pull system, mixed-model production |
| 6 | **Over-processing** | Outdated specs, lack of VOC | Quality Function Deployment (QFD), DFSS |
| 7 | **Defects** | Process variation, human error | Poka-yoke, SPC, Six Sigma |
| 8 | **Non-utilized Talent** | No empowerment, no suggestion culture | Kaizen teams, cross-training, respect-for-people |

*(6 marks for the table with correct waste-cause-countermeasure triplets. 1 mark for the introductory statement identifying that the modern 8-waste framework supersedes the classical 7.)*

**(b) "Overproduction is the root of all waste." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to a real-world manufacturing or service operation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Arguments IN FAVOUR (4 marks):*
1. **Direct causation of inventory:** Producing ahead of demand forces material to accumulate as WIP and finished goods, tying up capital and storage space.
2. **Concealment of defects:** When products are over-produced, defective units are buried in large lots, making detection late and rework expensive. The "stop-the-line" Andon philosophy is rendered ineffective.
3. **Hides waiting and motion waste:** Excess inventory fills buffers, masking the true cycle time, line imbalance, and transport inefficiencies. Without overproduction, these wastes surface and become addressable.
4. **Real-world example:** In traditional automotive batch stamping, each press produces 500 parts even when assembly needs only 50. The 450 extra parts form WIP piles, hide surface defects (requiring later 100% re-inspection), and consume forklift movements (transport waste). Switching to a single-piece flow via SMED eliminated 80% of the inventory and revealed 12 previously hidden defect types.

*Arguments AGAINST / LIMITATIONS (2 marks):*
1. In some safety-critical or seasonal industries (pharma vaccines, fire extinguishers), strategic overproduction is mandatory for safety stock and is not waste but necessary NNVA.
2. The statement oversimplifies; all wastes are interlinked in a *system of waste*, and an exclusive focus on overproduction may neglect the unique root causes of, say, motion waste in an office environment.

*Conclusion (1 mark):*
In the context of **lean transformation**, overproduction is rightly singled out as the **primary leverage point**, because its elimination cascades into the visibility and reduction of all other wastes — making Taiichi Ohno's statement operationally true, even if theoretically reductive.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Waste Quantification Using Lean Metrics

**[KTU University Exam – Module 1, Model QP – Variant B]**

**(a) A machining cell processes 1,200 parts per day. The total lead time observed is 4 hours, while the value-added processing time across the cell is 38 minutes. Calculate the Value-Added Ratio (VAR) and Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE). Discuss the operational significance of the result. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Identify given data.*
- $T_{LT} = 4 \text{ hours} = 4 \times 60 = 240 \text{ minutes}$
- $T_{VA} = 38 \text{ minutes}$

*Step 2 — Apply the VAR formula.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAR} &= \frac{T_{VA}}{T_{LT}} \times 100 \\
\text{VAR} &= \frac{38}{240} \times 100 \\
\text{VAR} &= 0.15833 \times 100 \\
\text{VAR} &= 15.83\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — PCE interpretation.*

PCE is numerically identical to VAR in this single-product context. $\text{PCE} = 15.83\%$.

*Step 4 — Operational significance (3 marks).*

- **84.17% of the customer's lead time is waste (or NNVA).** This is alarmingly high — world-class Lean firms achieve ≥ 25%.
- **Top waste candidates to investigate:** Storage between operations (inventory waste), queue time before CNC (waiting waste), and material movement between machines (transport waste).
- **Financial implication:** A 50% reduction in non-value-added time (i.e., cutting lead time from 240 min to ~139 min) without changing value-added time would raise VAR to ~27%, freeing ~42% of cell capacity for additional orders or smaller batch sizes.

**(b) Compute the OEE of a packaging line with the following data and interpret the result. (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Planned shift time = 480 minutes
- Unplanned downtime = 45 minutes
- Ideal cycle time = 0.5 minutes/box
- Total boxes produced = 850
- Rejected boxes (defects) = 50

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Compute Run Time.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{run} &= 480 - 45 = 435 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Availability.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{435}{480} \\
A &= 0.9063 = 90.63\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Performance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{0.5 \times 850}{435} \\
P &= \frac{425}{435} \\
P &= 0.9770 = 97.70\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Quality.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{850 - 50}{850} \\
Q &= \frac{800}{850} \\
Q &= 0.9412 = 94.12\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5 — OEE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= A \times P \times Q \\
\text{OEE} &= 0.9063 \times 0.9770 \times 0.9412 \\
\text{OEE} &= 0.8334 = 83.34\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 6 — Interpretation (2 marks).*

- OEE of **83.34%** is **close to the world-class benchmark of 85%** but not yet there.
- The largest loss factor is **Quality (94.12%)** — the 5.88% defect rate indicates a need for **poka-yoke / mistake-proofing** at the packaging station. Addressing this alone could push OEE past 87%.
- **Availability loss** (9.37%) suggests unplanned breakdowns — a **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** initiative is recommended.
- **Performance loss** (2.30%) is minor — the line is running close to design speed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Always convert lead time and cycle time to the **same unit** (seconds, minutes, or hours) before applying any ratio. Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **OEE expressed as a fraction, not percentage:** OEE must be reported as a **percentage (e.g., 83.34%)**, not as 0.8334. This is a 0.5-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing Transportation with Motion:** Transportation = materials move; Motion = people/operators move. Examiners test this distinction.
> 4. **Omitting the 8th waste (Non-utilized Talent):** In 2024 scheme QPs, the 8-waste model is now expected. Writing only 7 wastes = −1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to include 5S / Kaizen / VSM tools:** Theory-only answers without naming the engineering tools lose 2–3 marks. Always pair waste → tool → countermeasure → KPI.
> 6. **Not showing intermediate steps in OEE:** Examiners require **explicit calculation of A, P, Q separately** before multiplying. Direct jump to OEE = −1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Waste (Muda) = any resource-consuming, non-value-adding activity.** Definition is recall-ready for 3-mark questions.
- ✅ **Three M's:** **Muda** (waste), **Mura** (unevenness), **Muri** (overburden) — together form the 3M operational inefficiency model.
- ✅ **Eight Wastes acronym:** **DOWNTIME** (Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing) — preferred over TIMWOOD in 2024 scheme papers.
- ✅ **Worst waste:** **Overproduction** — it generates or hides all other wastes.
- ✅ **Value-Added Ratio (VAR):** $\text{VAR} = T_{VA} / T_{LT} \times 100$. World-class ≥ 25%.
- ✅ **Takt Time:** $\text{Takt} = \text{Available Time} / \text{Customer Demand}$. Sets production pace.
- ✅ **OEE:** $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$. World-class ≥ 85%. The 6 big losses framework explains the gap.
- ✅ **Tools to remember:** 5S, Kaizen, Poka-yoke, Kanban, JIT, SMED, TPM, Value Stream Mapping, Spaghetti Diagram, 5-Why, Ishikawa, FMEA, QFD, Six Sigma.
- ✅ **NNVA is not "bad":** Some non-value-adding activities (safety checks, legal compliance) are *necessary* and cannot be eliminated today — only reduced.
- ✅ **Lean goal:** Maximise VAR / OEE, minimise NVA, convert NVA → VA through process redesign.
- ✅ **Service-sector applicability:** Waste concepts are equally valid in IT, banking, healthcare, and education — not limited to manufacturing.
- ✅ **Exam answer structure:** Always follow *Definition → Classification → Tools → Countermeasure → KPI → Numerical support*. This is the highest-scoring template.
- ✅ **Mermaid / drawing tip:** When asked to draw a value stream map or process flow diagram in exams, use a clear *box-and-arrow* layout and label each box as **VA / NVA / NNVA** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements that address elimination of waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Elements that address elimination of waste ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Operations Management** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, the **Elements that address elimination of waste** refer to the core philosophical pillars, tools, and methodologies of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** — globally known as **Lean Manufacturing** or **Lean Production**. These elements constitute the operational DNA designed to identify, classify, and systematically eradicate **Muda (waste)**, **Mura (unevenness)**, and **Muri (overburden)** from any value-creating process. In formal KTU terminology, waste is defined as any **human activity, resource consumption, or process step that absorbs cost, time, or effort but does not add value** to the end product or service as perceived by the customer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core KTU Definition (Muda):** *"Any activity that the customer is unwilling to pay for is waste. Waste consumes resources but creates no value."* — Taiichi Ohno (Founder, TPS) ### 1.2 The 8 Wastes (DOWNTIME Framework) Taiichi Ohno originally identified **7 wastes**, later expanded to **8** with the inclusion of *Non-utilized talent* (originally attributed to Toyota and later popularized by Jeffrey Liker in "The Toyota Way"). KTU examinations frequently test the **DOWNTIME acronym** as it is the universal mnemonic: | Letter | Waste | Definition | Real-World Trigger | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | **D** | **Defects** | Products or services that fail to meet specifications, requiring rework or scrap. | Software bugs, manufacturing rejects | | **O** | **Overproduction** | Producing more than the customer demands or producing it earlier than needed. | Excess inventory buildup | | **W** | **Waiting** | Idle time when people, machines, or materials are not actively working. | Machine downtime, approvals | | **N** | **Non-utilized Talent** | Underutilizing people's skills, creativity, and problem-solving abilities. | Ignoring employee suggestions | | **T** | **Transportation** | Unnecessary movement of materials, products, or information between processes. | Long supply routes | | **I** | **Inventory** | Excess raw materials, WIP, or finished goods that tie up capital. | Warehouses overflowing | | **M** | **Motion** | Unnecessary physical movement of people (e.g., walking, reaching, bending). | Searching for tools | | **E** | **Extra-Processing** | Doing more work or using higher precision than the customer requires. | Over-engineering a product | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Ward > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a **Government Hospital Ward**. > - *Defects* = A patient is misdiagnosed. > - *Overproduction* = The lab runs 10 blood tests when 2 were needed. > - *Waiting* = Patient waits 4 hours for a doctor visit. > - *Non-utilized Talent* = Senior nurse's 20 years of experience is ignored in protocol design. > - *Transportation* = Patient is shuttled between 5 different rooms for simple checks. > - *Inventory* = Pharmacy stocks 6 months of rare medicines that expire. > - *Motion* = Doctor walks 500m daily because equipment is poorly placed. > - *Extra-Processing* = A simple fracture is treated with MRI, CT, and 5 X-rays. > **Lean thinking** transforms this ward by redesigning the flow — testing only what is needed, reducing walk distances, empowering the senior nurse, and stocking only what is consumed. **The patient heals faster (value increases), and the cost drops (waste decreases).** ### 1.4 The Trinity of Elimination: Muda, Mura, Muri The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes a holistic understanding. Waste elimination is incomplete without addressing the *root causes*: - **Muda (Waste):** The 8 wastes above — the *symptoms*. - **Mura (Unevenness):** Irregular flow, demand fluctuations, batch-and-queue production — the *upstream cause* of waste. - **Muri (Overburden):** Pushing people/machines beyond natural limits — the *systemic cause* of breakdowns and defects. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** A high-yield KTU question is *"Explain how eliminating Mura leads to the elimination of Muri and Muda."* The answer lies in the **cause-effect chain**: uneven demand → overburdened workers/machines (Muri) → defects and delays (Muda). Stabilize the flow → cascade effect of waste removal. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Pillars of Lean — Structural Decomposition The elements that address waste elimination are best understood as **two interlocking pillars** supported by a **cultural foundation**. This is the canonical TPS House model: **Pillar 1: Just-in-Time (JIT)** The production philosophy of producing "the right item, in the right quantity, at the right time." It directly attacks **Overproduction** and **Inventory** waste. **Pillar 2: Jidoka (Autonomation)** "Automation with a human touch" — the ability of a machine or process to detect abnormalities and stop automatically, preventing defective products from flowing downstream. It directly attacks **Defects** waste. **Foundation: Heijunka (Level Scheduling) + Standardized Work + Kaizen** The cultural and procedural bedrock that makes both pillars sustainable. ### 2.2 The 14 Elements (Toyota Way Framework) Jeffrey Liker's "14 Principles of the Toyota Way" map directly to the KTU 2024 Module 1 learning outcomes. Below is the KTU-aligned mapping: | # | Toyota Principle | Waste Targeted | KTU Exam Weightage | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | Base decisions on long-term philosophy | All wastes (philosophical root) | High | | 2 | Create continuous process flow | Waiting, Inventory, Transportation | **Very High** | | 3 | Use pull systems to avoid overproduction | Overproduction, Inventory | **Very High** | | 4 | Level out the workload (Heijunka) | Mura, Waiting | High | | 5 | Build a culture of stopping to fix problems | Defects, Muri | Medium | | 6 | Standardized tasks as foundation | Motion, Extra-Processing | High | | 7 | Use visual control | Motion, Waiting | Medium | | 8 | Use only reliable, tested technology | Defects | Low | | 9 | Grow leaders who live the philosophy | Non-utilized Talent | Medium | | 10 | Develop exceptional people & teams | Non-utilized Talent | Medium | | 11 | Respect your extended network | Transportation, Defects | Low | | 12 | Go and see for yourself (Genchi Genbutsu) | Defects, Waiting | Medium | | 13 | Make decisions slowly, by consensus | Defects, Muri | Low | | 14 | Become a learning organization | All wastes (continuous improvement) | **Very High** | ### 2.3 The 5S Methodology — Detailed Structure 5S is the **physical and organizational foundation** of any lean transformation. It is one of the highest-yield topics in KTU ESE: | S | Japanese | English | Purpose | Anti-Waste | |:---:|:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Seiri** | Sort | Remove unnecessary items from the workplace | Motion, Transportation | | 2 | **Seiton** | Set in Order | Arrange items for easy access and use | Motion, Waiting | | 3 | **Seiso** | Shine | Clean the workplace and equipment regularly | Defects (early detection) | | 4 | **Seiketsu** | Standardize | Establish procedures and standards for the first 3S | All wastes | | 5 | **Shitsuke** | Sustain | Maintain discipline and commitment to the system | All wastes (sustained) | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following quantitative metrics are essential for KTU numerical problems. The key is to use `\vert` (not `\vert\vert) instead of $\vert$ in tables. | Metric Name | Formula | Application | Unit | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Takt Time** | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}}$ | Pace of production to match customer demand | seconds/unit | | **Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{Output}$ | Actual time to complete one unit | seconds/unit | | **Lead Time** | $L = \text{Pre-process Time} + \text{Processing Time} + \text{Wait Time} + \text{Storage Time} + \text{Transport Time}$ | Total time from order to delivery | days / hours | | **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $VAR = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of time that adds value | % | | **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** | $PCE = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\%$ | Identical to VAR; the lean baseline | % | | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first time}}{\text{Total units started}} \times 100\%$ | Quality benchmark for Defects waste | % | | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | Cumulative yield across n process steps | % | | **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Availability × Performance × Quality | % (decimal) | | **Inventory Turns** | $IT = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | How fast inventory cycles out | turns/year | | **Waste Index (Conceptual)** | $W_{index} = 1 - VAR$ | Proportion of process that is waste | % | > **Note on units:** $T_{available}$ is **Total Available Production Time** (seconds), $D_{demand}$ is **Customer Demand** (units per period). The output of $T_{takt}$ is **seconds per unit** — this is the *heartbeat* of lean production. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility These elements are not confined to manufacturing. Modern engineering applications include: - **Software Development:** Agile & DevOps are direct descendants of JIT and Kaizen. - **Healthcare:** Lean Hospitals (e.g., Virginia Mason Medical Center, USA) reduced patient wait times by 60%. - **Construction:** Lean Construction uses pull systems (Last Planner System) to eliminate on-site inventory waste. - **Aerospace & Automotive:** Boeing and Tesla apply Heijunka to level mixed-model production on single assembly lines. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Takt Time, Cycle Time, and Capacity Analysis > **Problem:** A KTU-context assembly line operates **2 shifts of 8 hours each, 5 days a week**. Each shift has a **60-minute break**. The **daily customer demand is 480 units**. The actual **measured cycle time per unit is 45 seconds**. Determine: > (a) Takt Time > (b) Whether the line is meeting demand > (c) The Value-Added Ratio if total lead time is 12 days, of which only 1.5 days are value-added. **Step 1: Calculate Total Available Production Time per Day** $$T_{available} = (2 \text{ shifts} \times 8 \text{ hours/shift} \times 60 \text{ min/hour}) - (2 \text{ breaks} \times 60 \text{ min})$$ $$T_{available} = (960 \text{ min}) - (120 \text{ min}) = 840 \text{ min/day}$$ **Step 2: Convert to Seconds for Consistency with Cycle Time** $$T_{available} = 840 \text{ min/day} \times 60 \text{ sec/min} = 50{,}400 \text{ sec/day}$$ **Step 3: Calculate Takt Time** $$T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}} = \frac{50{,}400 \text{ sec/day}}{480 \text{ units/day}}$$ $$T_{takt} = 105 \text{ seconds/unit}$$ **Step 4: Compare Cycle Time vs Takt Time** $$T_{cycle} = 45 \text{ sec} < T_{takt} = 105 \text{ sec}$$ Since the cycle time is less than the takt time, the line is **capable of meeting demand** with a time buffer of 60 seconds per unit. The system has **60/105 = 57.14% capacity utilization headroom**. **Step 5: Calculate Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** $$VAR = \frac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\% = \frac{1.5 \text{ days}}{12 \text{ days}} \times 100\%$$ $$VAR = 12.5\%$$ **Step 6: Calculate the Waste Index** $$W_{index} = 1 - VAR = 1 - 0.125 = 0.875 = 87.5\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Interpretation:** A VAR of 12.5% means **87.5% of lead time is non-value-added** (waiting, transport, storage). This is typical of a traditional batch-and-queue system. World-class lean systems target **VAR > 25%**. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) and Defect Cost Analysis > **Problem:** A printed circuit board (PCB) goes through 4 sequential process steps. The First Pass Yields are: Soldering (98%), Component Placement (95%), Testing (92%), and Packaging (97%). > (a) Calculate the Rolled Throughput Yield. > (b) If 10,000 PCBs enter the line, how many are defect-free at the end? > (c) If each defect costs ₹200 to rework, what is the total rework cost? **Step 1: Convert Each FPY to Decimal Form** $$FPY_1 = 0.98, \quad FPY_2 = 0.95, \quad FPY_3 = 0.92, \quad FPY_4 = 0.97$$ **Step 2: Apply the RTY Formula** $$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{4} FPY_i = 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.97$$ **Step 3: Sequential Multiplication** $$\begin{aligned} 0.98 \times 0.95 &= 0.9310 \\ 0.9310 \times 0.92 &= 0.8565 \\ 0.8565 \times 0.97 &= 0.8308 \end{aligned}$$ $$RTY = 0.8308 = 83.08\%$$ **Step 4: Number of Defect-Free PCBs** $$N_{good} = 10{,}000 \times 0.8308 = 8{,}308 \text{ units}$$ **Step 5: Number of Defective PCBs** $$N_{defect} = 10{,}000 - 8{,}308 = 1{,}692 \text{ units}$$ **Step 6: Total Rework Cost** $$C_{rework} = 1{,}692 \times ₹200 = ₹3{,}38{,}400$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** Notice how 98% quality at each step *seems* excellent, but the RTY drops to 83.08%. This is the **"hidden factory" effect** — defects compound multiplicatively. **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)** at each station is the lean response. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: OEE and the Six Big Losses > **Problem:** A CNC machine has the following data: > Planned Production Time = 480 min/day, Downtime = 60 min, Ideal Cycle Time = 1.2 min/unit, Total Units Produced = 320, Defective Units = 16. > Calculate Availability, Performance, Quality, and OEE. **Step 1: Operating Time** $$T_{operating} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ min/day}$$ **Step 2: Availability** $$A = \frac{T_{operating}}{T_{planned}} = \frac{420}{480} = 0.875 = 87.5\%$$ **Step 3: Theoretical Maximum Output** $$N_{theoretical} = \frac{T_{operating}}{T_{cycle\_ideal}} = \frac{420}{1.2} = 350 \text{ units}$$ **Step 4: Performance** $$P = \frac{N_{actual}}{N_{theoretical}} = \frac{320}{350} = 0.9143 = 91.43\%$$ **Step 5: Quality (Good Units / Total Units)** $$Q = \frac{320 - 16}{320} = \frac{304}{320} = 0.95 = 95\%$$ **Step 6: OEE** $$OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.875 \times 0.9143 \times 0.95$$ $$\begin{aligned} 0.875 \times 0.9143 &= 0.8000 \\ 0.8000 \times 0.95 &= 0.7600 \end{aligned}$$ $$OEE = 76\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Benchmark:** World-class OEE = **85%**. A score of 76% indicates the plant has room to capture ~9% additional capacity without new investment — pure waste elimination. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Toyota Production System House (TPS House) This is the canonical structural map of the elements that eliminate waste. Study the foundation → pillars → roof relationship. ```mermaid graph TB foundation["FOUNDATION
Heijunka Level Scheduling
Standardized Work
Kaizen Continuous Improvement"] pillar1["PILLAR 1
JUST IN TIME
Right item, right time,
right quantity
TARGETS Overproduction and Inventory"] pillar2["PILLAR 2
JIDOKA Autonomation
Stop the line on defect
TARGETS Defects and Muri"] goal["GOAL
Highest Quality
Lowest Cost
Shortest Lead Time
via Elimination of Muda Mura Muri"] customer["THE CUSTOMER
Defines Value
