Sets the Takt Time"] tools["ENABLING TOOLS
5S, Kanban, Poka-Yoke
Value Stream Mapping
Total Productive Maintenance"] foundation --> pillar1 foundation --> pillar2 foundation --> tools tools --> pillar1 tools --> pillar2 pillar1 --> goal pillar2 --> goal customer --> pillar1 customer --> pillar2 ``` ### 4.2 The 8 Wastes (DOWNTIME) — Cause-Effect Matrix ```mermaid graph LR CAUSE["ROOT CAUSES
Mura Unevenness
Muri Overburden"] -->|Manifests as| WASTES["THE 8 WASTES MUDAS"] WASTES --> D1["D Defects
Rework Scrap"] WASTES --> O1["O Overproduction
Excess Inventory"] WASTES --> W1["W Waiting
Idle Resources"] WASTES --> N1["N Non-utilized Talent
Wasted Human Capital"] WASTES --> T1["T Transportation
Unnecessary Movement"] WASTES --> I1["I Inventory
Tied-up Capital"] WASTES --> M1["M Motion
Worker Ergonomic Loss"] WASTES --> E1["E Extra-Processing
Over-engineering"] D1 --> VAL["VALUE STREAM
Impact Assessment"] O1 --> VAL W1 --> VAL N1 --> VAL T1 --> VAL I1 --> VAL M1 --> VAL E1 --> VAL VAL --> RESPONSE["LEAN RESPONSE
JIT, Jidoka, 5S, Kaizen
Kanban, Poka-Yoke, TPM"] RESPONSE --> ELIM["ELIMINATION OF WASTE
Improved Quality Cost Lead Time"] ``` ### 4.3 The 5S Implementation Flow (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph TB A1["STAGE 1 SEIRI SORT
Identify Red Tags
Remove unnecessary items"] --> A2["STAGE 2 SEITON SET IN ORDER
A Place for Everything
Everything in its Place"] A2 --> A3["STAGE 3 SEISO SHINE
Clean as Inspection
Equipment Care"] A3 --> A4["STAGE 4 SEIKETSU STANDARDIZE
Document Visual Standards
Make Abnormal Visible"] A4 --> A5["STAGE 5 SHITSUKE SUSTAIN
Discipline and Audit
Continuous Habit Formation"] A5 --> A6["MATURITY
Lean Foundation
Waste Elimination Active"] A1 -.->|Prevents| W1["Motion and Transportation Waste"] A2 -.->|Prevents| W2["Motion and Waiting Waste"] A3 -.->|Prevents| W3["Defects and Muri"] A4 -.->|Prevents| W4["Extra-Processing"] A5 -.->|Prevents| W5["All Wastes via Sustained Discipline"] ``` ### 4.4 Waste Elimination Feedback Loop (PDCA + Kaizen) ```mermaid graph LR P1["PLAN
Identify waste via
Value Stream Mapping"] --> D1["DO
Pilot Kaizen event
Implement countermeasure"] D1 --> C1["CHECK
Measure VAR, OEE, RTY
Compare to baseline"] C1 --> A1["ACT
Standardize if successful
Reiterate if not"] A1 --> P1 A1 -.->|Each cycle| K["KAIZEN
Continuous Improvement
Waste reduced step by step"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List and explain any three wastes under the DOWNTIME framework. How do these wastes impact the operational efficiency of a manufacturing firm?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The DOWNTIME framework identifies 8 wastes. Three of them are: 1. **Defects (1 mark):** Products or services that do not meet customer specifications. Impact: Rework costs, scrap, warranty claims, customer dissatisfaction, and reduced First Pass Yield (FPY). For example, a defective PCB returned from a customer triggers reverse logistics, repair cost, and reputational damage. 2. **Overproduction (1 mark):** Producing more units than the customer has ordered, or producing earlier than required. Impact: Excess inventory holding costs, increased lead time, and risk of obsolescence. Overproduction is considered the *worst* waste because it *causes* other wastes like inventory, transportation, and motion. 3. **Waiting (1 mark):** Idle time when workers, machines, or materials are inactive due to process imbalances, breakdowns, or approvals. Impact: Reduced capacity utilization, longer lead times, and lower OEE. For example, a machine operator standing idle for 30 minutes because materials were not delivered on time. **Examiner's Note:** Award 1 mark per waste with a clear definition + impact statement. --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **What is Takt Time? Why is it considered the "heartbeat" of a lean production system?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Takt Time is the available production time divided by customer demand. It dictates the maximum allowable time to produce one unit in order to satisfy customer demand exactly. $$T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}}$$ **Why "Heartbeat" (1.5 marks):** Takt time sets the rhythm of production. Every process step is synchronized to this rate. If cycle time > takt time, the line is unable to meet demand, and the bottleneck is exposed. Takt time is the *customer-driven* metric that aligns internal production pace with external demand, eliminating overproduction and waiting wastes. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** **Q.A (a)** Explain the 5S methodology in detail. How does each 'S' contribute to the elimination of waste in an industrial workplace? **(7 Marks)** **Q.A (b)** A garment manufacturing unit operates for 8 hours a day with a 1-hour break. The daily demand is 250 shirts. The actual cycle time to stitch one shirt is 90 seconds. Determine the Takt Time and assess whether the line is capable of meeting demand. If the total lead time from order receipt to delivery is 10 days and value-added stitching time is 1.2 days, compute the Value-Added Ratio (VAR) and the Waste Index. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks** The 5S methodology is a systematic workplace organization technique originating from the Toyota Production System. Each 'S' addresses specific forms of waste: 1. **Seiri (Sort) — 1.5 marks:** Differentiate between essential and non-essential items. Use the *Red Tag* technique to mark unnecessary items for removal. - *Waste addressed:* Transportation (cluttered aisles), Motion (searching for tools), and Inventory (obsolete stock). - *Example:* Remove broken chairs and obsolete tools from the shop floor. 2. **Seiton (Set in Order) — 1.5 marks:** Arrange items logically with visual cues so they are *easy to find, use, and return*. Use shadow boards, floor markings, and labeled bins. - *Waste addressed:* Motion (less walking), Waiting (less search time). - *Example:* A shadow board where every tool has an outlined position; missing tools are immediately visible. 3. **Seiso (Shine) — 1.5 marks:** Clean the workplace and equipment daily. Cleaning doubles as inspection. - *Waste addressed:* Defects (early detection of oil leaks, loose bolts), Muri (machine breakdowns). - *Example:* Operators wipe down machines at shift end and report any abnormality. 4. **Seiketsu (Standardize) — 1.5 marks:** Document the first 3S as standard operating procedures. Use visual management and checklists to ensure consistency across shifts. - *Waste addressed:* Extra-Processing, Defects (procedural drift). - *Example:* Standardized cleaning schedules posted at every workstation. 5. **Shitsuke (Sustain) — 1 mark:** Build discipline through regular audits, training, and management commitment. Without Shitsuke, the 5S gains erode. - *Waste addressed:* All wastes are prevented from creeping back. - *Example:* Monthly 5S audits with scoreboards displayed publicly. > **Conclusion (0 marks allocated, but mandatory):** 5S is the *foundation* of lean — it does not directly reduce muda but creates the *visual transparency* needed to identify and eliminate it. --- ### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks** **Given Data:** - Working time: 8 hours/day − 1 hour break = **7 hours/day** - Daily demand: **250 shirts** - Cycle time: **90 seconds/shirt** **Step 1: Available Production Time in Seconds (1 mark)** $$T_{available} = 7 \text{ hours} \times 3600 \text{ sec/hour} = 25{,}200 \text{ sec/day}$$ **Step 2: Takt Time Calculation (2 marks)** $$T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}} = \frac{25{,}200}{250} = 100.8 \text{ sec/shirt}$$ **Step 3: Demand Capability Assessment (2 marks)** $$T_{cycle} = 90 \text{ sec} < T_{takt} = 100.8 \text{ sec}$$ Since the cycle time is **less than** the takt time, the line is **capable of meeting demand**. The buffer is $100.8 - 90 = 10.8$ seconds per shirt. The line is operating at a utilization of $90/100.8 = 89.29\%$, leaving a ~10.71% margin for variation. **Step 4: Value-Added Ratio (1 mark)** $$VAR = \frac{1.2 \text{ days}}{10 \text{ days}} \times 100\% = 12\%$$ **Step 5: Waste Index (1 mark)** $$W_{index} = 1 - 0.12 = 0.88 = 88\%$$ **Conclusion:** The line is technically capable of meeting demand, but **88% of the lead time is non-value-added**. Lean tools like cellular layout, SMED, and pull systems should be applied to reduce this waste. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q.B (a)** Discuss the 8 wastes (DOWNTIME) in lean manufacturing. For each waste, suggest one specific countermeasure tool used in the Toyota Production System. **(7 Marks)** **Q.B (b)** A four-step food packaging line has the following First Pass Yields: Sealing 96%, Labeling 93%, Inspection 90%, Boxing 98%. If 5,000 units enter the line, calculate the Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY), the number of good units at the end, and the total rework cost at ₹150 per defective unit. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks** | # | Waste (0.4 mark each = 3.2 marks) | TPS Countermeasure (0.4 mark each = 3.2 marks) | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **D — Defects:** Products failing to meet specs. | **Jidoka / Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing):** Stop the line, use sensors and fixtures that prevent defects at the source. | | 2 | **O — Overproduction:** Producing more/earlier than needed. | **Pull System / Kanban:** Produce only in response to downstream demand signal. | | 3 | **W — Waiting:** Idle time of people/machines. | **Continuous Flow / One-Piece Flow:** Eliminate batch-and-queue; balance the line. | | 4 | **N — Non-utilized Talent:** Wasting human creativity. | **Kaizen Circles / Suggestion Systems:** Empower workers to solve process problems. | | 5 | **T — Transportation:** Unnecessary movement of goods. | **Value Stream Mapping (VSM):** Redesign plant layout to minimize distances. | | 6 | **I — Inventory:** Excess raw/WIP/finished goods. | **Just-in-Time (JIT):** Produce only what is needed, when needed, in the quantity needed. | | 7 | **M — Motion:** Unnecessary human movement. | **5S / Ergonomics:** Standardize workplace layout to minimize walking, reaching, bending. | | 8 | **E — Extra-Processing:** Doing more than the customer wants. | **Value Analysis / Quality Function Deployment (QFD):** Match process specifications exactly to customer CTQs. | **Conclusion (0.6 marks):** The 8 wastes are interconnected — eliminating one often exposes the next. The TPS uses a *system* of interconnected tools rather than isolated techniques. --- ### **Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** - $FPY_1 = 0.96, FPY_2 = 0.93, FPY_3 = 0.90, FPY_4 = 0.98$ - Input units = 5,000 - Rework cost = ₹150/unit **Step 1: Rolled Throughput Yield Formula (1 mark)** $$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{4} FPY_i$$ **Step 2: Sequential Multiplication (2 marks)** $$\begin{aligned} 0.96 \times 0.93 &= 0.8928 \\ 0.8928 \times 0.90 &= 0.8035 \\ 0.8035 \times 0.98 &= 0.7874 \end{aligned}$$ $$RTY = 0.7874 = 78.74\%$$ **Step 3: Number of Good Units (1.5 marks)** $$N_{good} = 5{,}000 \times 0.7874 = 3{,}937 \text{ units}$$ **Step 4: Number of Defective Units (1 mark)** $$N_{defect} = 5{,}000 - 3{,}937 = 1{,}063 \text{ units}$$ **Step 5: Total Rework Cost (1.5 marks)** $$C_{rework} = 1{,}063 \times ₹150 = ₹1{,}59{,}450$$ **Interpretation:** An average yield of 94% per step compounded to 78.74% — a loss of ~21% of input. **This is the "hidden factory" — internal rework consumes capacity that should produce sellable output.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **DOWNTIME Acronym:** Students often write 7 wastes and forget "Non-utilized Talent." This loses **0.5–1 mark** immediately. > 2. **Muda vs Mura vs Muri:** Confusing these three terms is a **favourite KTU trap question.** Memorize: *Muda = waste, Mura = unevenness, Muri = overburden*. > 3. **Takt Time Units:** Students forget to convert hours to seconds and lose **2 marks** silently. Always state units explicitly. > 4. **RTY vs Individual FPY:** Examiners want the *product* (multiplication), not the average. Writing $(0.96+0.93+0.90+0.98)/4 = 0.9425$ is **wrong** — loses **2 marks**. > 5. **5S Order:** Writing 5S in the wrong order is a 0.5-mark deduction. The correct order is **Sort → Set in Order → Shine → Standardize → Sustain**. > 6. **TPS House Diagram:** Many students draw JIT and Jidoka as the foundation, but they are the **pillars** sitting *on top of* Heijunka. Direction of arrows in the house matters. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Elements of Waste Elimination** - [ ] **DOWNTIME** = the 8 wastes; memorize full form with one example each. - [ ] **Muda = waste (symptom)**, **Mura = unevenness (cause)**, **Muri = overburden (cause)**. - [ ] **TPS House:** Foundation (Heijunka + Standardized Work + Kaizen) → Two Pillars (JIT + Jidoka) → Roof (Goal: Quality, Cost, Delivery). - [ ] **Takt Time formula:** $T_{takt} = T_{available} / D_{demand}$ — always in **seconds per unit**. - [ ] **Takt Time > Cycle Time** → line can meet demand; **Takt Time < Cycle Time** → demand cannot be met. - [ ] **VAR (Value-Added Ratio):** Aim for **>25%** in world-class lean systems. - [ ] **OEE = A × P × Q.** World-class benchmark = **85%**. - [ ] **RTY = product of all FPYs** — defects compound *multiplicatively*, not additively. - [ ] **5S Order:** Seiri → Seiton → Seiso → Seiketsu → Shitsuke (Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain). - [ ] **JIT** eliminates **Overproduction + Inventory**; **Jidoka** eliminates **Defects**; **5S** eliminates **Motion + Transportation**; **Kaizen** addresses **all wastes over time**. - [ ] **Kanban** = visual signal card; **Poka-Yoke** = mistake-proofing device; **VSM** = Value Stream Mapping tool. - [ ] **Overproduction is the *worst* waste** because it generates other wastes downstream. - [ ] **Non-utilized Talent** is the *eighth* waste — added later by Liker, often missed by students. - [ ] **Lead Time components:** Pre-process + Processing + Wait + Storage + Transport. - [ ] **VAR + Waste Index = 100%** (or 1.0); they are complementary metrics. - [ ] For 14-mark questions, always structure the answer as: *Definition → List/Formula → Step-by-step calculation → Interpretation/Conclusion*. - [ ] KTU expects **units in every numerical answer** and **diagrams for descriptive questions**. > **Final Exam Tip:** When asked *"How do these elements eliminate waste?"* — never list the elements in isolation. Always show the **waste-to-tool mapping table** (DOWNTIME → Countermeasure). This single table can fetch 4–5 marks in a 14-mark question.

topics.title: Two-card Kanban production control system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Two-card Kanban production control system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Card Kanban Production Control System:** A pull-based, decentralized production and material-flow control mechanism native to the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and **Just-In-Time (JIT)** philosophy. It uses *two independent authorization tokens* — the **Withdrawal Kanban (W-Kanban / Conveyance Kanban / C-Kanban)** and the **Production Order Kanban (P-Kanban)** — circulating between the **supplier (upstream) process** and the **customer (downstream) process** to regulate *when* and *how much* to produce, thereby eliminating overproduction and excessive work-in-process (WIP) inventory.

The system was formalized by **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota in the 1950s as a visual signaling mechanism that turns the shop floor into an information network without requiring a centralized computer.

| Term | Symbol / Notation | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Kanban cards | $N$ | cards (integer) |
| Demand rate | $d$ | units / day |
| Lead time | $L$ | days |
| Container size | $C$ | units / container |
| Safety stock factor | $S$ | dimensionless ratio (typically $0.1$ – $0.2$) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Grocery Store Replenishment Model**

Imagine you run a small grocery store. You never order vegetables in bulk. Instead, when a customer buys the last cabbage from the shelf, the shopkeeper removes an empty basket labeled **"ORDER CABBAGE"** and sends it to the supplier. The supplier only produces/farms a *new basket of cabbages* when he receives that empty basket. Two baskets circulate: one is the **"MOVE THE VEGETABLE"** tag (the Withdrawal Kanban), and the other is the **"MAKE MORE"** tag (the Production Kanban). Vegetables are never over-stocked, never under-stocked — production happens *only* when a real consumption signal arrives. This is the heart of **Kanban = visual signal card** (Japanese: *kan* = visual, *ban* = card).

## 1.3 Why Two Cards and Not One?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **two-card architecture separates logistics from manufacturing authorization**. The *W-Kanban* moves material between processes, while the *P-Kanban* authorizes the upstream process to manufacture. Because they circulate on **independent closed loops**, an upstream process can continue producing while downstream material is being withdrawn — eliminating the bottleneck of a single-card cycle and enabling parallel material + production flow.

## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Mapping

This topic directly maps to the following KTU 2024 Course Outcomes:
- **CO1:** *Understand the philosophy, evolution, and architecture of modern production and operations management systems.*
- **CO2:** *Apply quantitative models (Kanban sizing, EOQ, lot sizing) to design lean production control systems.*

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Independent Closed Loops

### Loop A — Withdrawal (Conveyance) Kanban (W-Kanban or C-Kanban)
- **Authorization granted:** Move a *container* of parts from the upstream *output buffer / store* to the downstream *input buffer / consumption point*.
- **Circulation path:** Downstream process → Output buffer (picks up container) → Input buffer of downstream process (drops container).
- **Purpose:** Decouples physical material movement from production authorization.

### Loop B — Production Order Kanban (P-Kanban)
- **Authorization granted:** The upstream process may *produce one container* of a specified part.
- **Circulation path:** Output buffer (empty container arrives with P-Kanban) → Production station (P-Kanban detaches from container, stays at station as work order) → Finished container moves to output buffer → P-Kanban re-attaches to full container in store, awaiting next withdrawal signal.
- **Purpose:** Decouples production authorization from material transport.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical distinction:** The P-Kanban is the *only* signal that authorizes a machine operator to start production. Without it, no manufacturing occurs — even if the machine is idle. This is the precise mechanism that **prevents overproduction**, identified by Ohno as the *worst form of waste (Muda)*.

## 2.2 Operational Rules of the Two-Card System (Toyota's Six Rules)

1. The downstream process withdraws items from the upstream process **only when there is a W-Kanban**.
2. The upstream process produces items **only when it receives a P-Kanban**.
3. **No items are produced or moved without a Kanban.**
4. Each Kanban must be **physically attached** to its container at all times.
5. Defective items are **never sent downstream** (jidoka / built-in quality).
6. The number of Kanbans is **reduced gradually** over time to expose hidden waste and drive improvement.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$ | $d$: units/day; $L$: days; $S$: fraction (0.1–0.2); $C$: units/container | $N$ must be rounded **UP** to the nearest integer |
| 2 | $N_{\min} = \dfrac{d \cdot L}{C}$ | Same units as (1) | Theoretical lower bound (no safety stock, $S = 0$) |
| 3 | Lead time $L = L_p + L_w + L_q$ | $L_p$: processing time; $L_w$: waiting time; $L_q$: queue time | Days or hours |
| 4 | Cycle time $T_c = \dfrac{N \cdot C}{d}$ | $T_c$: days; gives the time between successive container withdrawals | Used to check feasibility vs. takt time |
| 5 | Takt time $T_t = \dfrac{\text{Available production time per day}}{\text{Daily customer demand}}$ | $T_t$: seconds/unit | Must satisfy $T_c \ge T_t$ |
| 6 | Maximum WIP $= N \cdot C$ | units | Hard cap on in-process inventory |
| 7 | Container size $C = \dfrac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{N}$ | units/container | Used when $N$ is fixed and $C$ is being designed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Safety Note:** In KTU board answer scripts, all absolute-value bars (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) **must** be written using `\vert` or `\mid`, never the raw `|` character inside markdown table rows, to prevent formatting corruption during exam-paper PDF rendering.

## 2.4 Engineering / Real-World Utility

- **Automotive assembly lines (Toyota, Ford, Tata Motors):** Synchronize body-shop → paint-shop → final-assembly flow.
- **Hospital pharmacy chains:** Replenishment of medicines between central warehouse and ward dispensaries.
- **Aerospace component machining:** Pull-based flow of turbine blades between heat-treatment and grinding cells.
- **E-commerce fulfillment:** Cross-docking signals between sorting hubs and delivery vans.

The two-card split enables **asynchronous replenishment** — a downstream withdrawal need does not force the upstream machine to stop production, and vice-versa, dramatically improving **machine utilization** without inflating inventory.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kanban Sizing Formula

The objective is to determine the **minimum number of kanban cards ($N$)** that will prevent stock-out at the downstream process, given stochastic demand and lead-time variability.

### Step 1 — Define average demand during lead time

During the replenishment lead time $L$, the downstream process consumes material at the average rate $d$ (units/day). The expected demand over lead time is therefore:

$$
D_L = d \cdot L
$$

**Logic:** If you consume $d$ units every day and it takes $L$ days to refill, you will need $d \cdot L$ units as buffer to avoid stock-out under *deterministic* conditions.

### Step 2 — Introduce safety stock for variability

Real systems exhibit **demand variability** ($\sigma_d$) and **lead-time variability** ($\sigma_L$). To absorb these fluctuations, a safety factor $S$ (typically 0.1 to 0.2) is multiplied to provide a cushion. The total protective stock required becomes:

$$
D_{\text{total}} = d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)
$$

**Logic:** A 10–20% cushion statistically covers one standard deviation of combined variability without overstocking.

### Step 3 — Convert stock requirement to number of containers

Each container holds $C$ units. The number of containers (and hence the number of Kanban cards) needed to cover the total demand is:

$$
N = \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{C} = \frac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}
$$

**Logic:** Since each container physically represents one Kanban token, dividing total stock by container capacity yields the required card count.

### Step 4 — Apply the integer-rounding rule

Kanban cards are discrete physical objects; therefore:

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} \right\rceil
$$

**Logic:** Always round **up**. Rounding down would create a fractional shortfall and cause stock-outs — defeating the safety-stock purpose.

### Step 5 — Consolidated master equation

$$
\boxed{\,N = \left\lceil \dfrac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}\right\rceil\,}
$$

This is the **gold-standard KTU formula** for two-card kanban sizing.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem

**Problem statement:**
A manufacturing cell produces a sub-assembly with the following parameters:
- Daily demand $d = 400$ units/day
- Replenishment lead time $L = 0.5$ days
- Container capacity $C = 50$ units/container
- Safety factor $S = 0.20$

Calculate: **(a)** the number of kanbans required, **(b)** the maximum WIP, and **(c)** the cycle time.

### Part (a) — Number of Kanbans

$$
N = \frac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
N = \frac{400 \times 0.5 \times (1 + 0.20)}{50}
$$

$$
N = \frac{400 \times 0.5 \times 1.20}{50}
$$

$$
N = \frac{240}{50}
$$

$$
N = 4.8 \;\Rightarrow\; N = \lceil 4.8 \rceil = 5 \text{ kanbans}
$$

**Answer:** **5 kanban cards** are required (4 W-Kanbans + 1 P-Kanban for the in-station work order, totalling 5 cards circulating).

### Part (b) — Maximum Work-In-Process (WIP)

$$
\text{WIP}_{\max} = N \cdot C = 5 \times 50 = 250 \text{ units}
$$

**Answer:** At any instant, no more than **250 units** can exist between the two processes.

### Part (c) — Cycle Time (Replenishment Interval)

$$
T_c = \frac{N \cdot C}{d} = \frac{5 \times 50}{400} = \frac{250}{400} = 0.625 \text{ days}
$$

Converting to hours: $0.625 \times 24 = 15$ hours.

**Answer:** A fresh container arrives at the downstream process every **15 hours**.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Increase / Decrease Kanban Count

| Observation on the Shop Floor | Diagnosis | KTU-Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| Frequent stock-outs at downstream process | Lead time has increased OR demand has spiked | **Increase $N$** by 1–2 cards temporarily |
| Excess inventory piling in upstream output buffer | Safety factor too generous | **Decrease $N$** to expose hidden waste |
| Operators idle waiting for kanbans | Lead time too long (process unstable) | **Reduce $L$** through SMED / kaizen |
| Frequent quality rejections | Defective items being sent (Rule 5 violated) | **Stop the line** (Andon); do NOT add kanbans |
| Two-card flow smooth, low WIP | System tuned | Maintain and monitor continuously |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiner tip:** Increasing kanban count is a *temporary* fix. The strategic KTU-approved response is always **lead-time reduction** ($L \downarrow$) via process improvement, not inventory inflation.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Two-Card Kanban Circulation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Upstream Process Station] --> A2[Production Buffer / Store]
    A2 --> A3[Downstream Process Station]
    A3 --> A4[Finished Goods / Customer Demand]

    subgraph W_Loop["W-Loop (Withdrawal Kanban circulation)"]
        W1[Downstream operator detaches W-Kanban from empty container] --> W2[Goes to upstream output buffer]
        W2 --> W3[Picks up full container with attached W-Kanban]
        W3 --> W4[Delivers full container to downstream process]
        W4 --> W1
    end

    subgraph P_Loop["P-Loop (Production Kanban circulation)"]
        P1[Empty container arrives at upstream buffer with P-Kanban] --> P2[P-Kanban detached and posted at workstation]
        P2 --> P3[Operator produces one container of parts]
        P3 --> P4[Full container placed in output buffer, P-Kanban re-attached]
        P4 --> P1
    end
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Customer["Customer / Final Assembly"]
        CUST[Final demand signal]
    end

    subgraph Downstream["Downstream Process Cell"]
        DP1[Downstream input buffer]
        DP2[Consumption point / Assembly station]
    end

    subgraph KanbanLayer["Kanban Authorization Layer"]
        WKAN[W-Kanban token circulates with empty container]
        PKAN[P-Kanban token acts as work order at upstream]
    end

    subgraph Upstream["Upstream Process Cell"]
        UP1[Upstream output buffer / store]
        UP2[Production station / Machine]
    end

    subgraph Supplier["Supplier / External Vendor"]
        SUP[External part supply on P-Kanban trigger]
    end

    CUST --> DP2
    DP2 --> DP1
    DP1 --> WKAN
    WKAN --> UP1
    UP1 --> PKAN
    PKAN --> UP2
    UP2 --> UP1
    UP1 --> DP1
    SUP --> UP2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — One Full Kanban Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DS as Downstream Process
    participant W as W-Kanban Token
    participant OB as Output Buffer (Upstream)
    participant P as P-Kanban Token
    participant US as Upstream Production Station

    DS->>W: Empty container exists, detach W-Kanban
    DS->>OB: Send empty container with W-Kanban to output buffer
    OB->>W: Exchange empty for full container
    W->>DS: Return full container to downstream
    Note over OB,P: P-Kanban loop activates independently
    OB->>P: Empty container arrives at output buffer with P-Kanban attached
    P->>US: Detach P-Kanban and post at production station
    US->>US: Produce one container of parts
    US->>OB: Move full container to output buffer, attach P-Kanban
    OB->>OB: Wait for next withdrawal signal
```

## 4.4 Schematic Legend (KTU Exam Convention)

| Symbol | Meaning | Card Type |
|---|---|---|
| Triangle (▷) attached to container | Withdrawal authorization | W-Kanban |
| Square (◼) attached to container | Production authorization | P-Kanban |
| Empty container | Awaiting signal | Either W or P |
| Full container | Ready for withdrawal | W-Kanban attached |

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define the Two-Card Kanban system. Name the two cards and state one function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Two-Card Kanban system is a pull-based production control mechanism of the Toyota Production System that uses two independent signal cards to authorize material movement and production authorization. **(1 mark)**

The two cards are:
1. **Withdrawal Kanban (W-Kanban / Conveyance Kanban):** Authorizes the downstream process to withdraw a container of parts from the upstream output buffer. **(1 mark)**
2. **Production Order Kanban (P-Kanban):** Authorizes the upstream process to produce one container of specified parts. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**State the formula for calculating the number of Kanban cards and explain the role of the safety factor $S$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The number of Kanban cards is given by:

$$
N = \frac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}
$$

where $d$ = demand rate, $L$ = lead time, $S$ = safety factor, $C$ = container size. **(2 marks)**

The safety factor $S$ (typically 0.1 to 0.2) provides a buffer against **demand variability** and **lead-time variability**, preventing stock-outs at the downstream process without the need for excessive inventory. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of the Two-Card Kanban system with a neat diagram. Describe the independent circulation loops of the W-Kanban and P-Kanban. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process cell has the following parameters:
- Average demand $d = 250$ units/day
- Lead time $L = 1.5$ days
- Container size $C = 25$ units
- Safety factor $S = 0.15$

Calculate: **(i)** number of kanban cards, **(ii)** maximum WIP inventory, and **(iii)** cycle time. Comment on whether the system is feasible if the takt time is 10 minutes/unit. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Two-Card Kanban Architecture (7 marks):**
- Definition of W-Kanban and P-Kanban with their circulation loops. **(2 marks)**
- Diagram (as per SECTION 4 of these notes) showing independent closed loops for W and P cards. **(3 marks)**
- Explanation of how the two loops decouple material movement from production authorization, enabling parallel flow. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of Kanban cards (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} = \frac{250 \times 1.5 \times (1 + 0.15)}{25}
$$

$$
N = \frac{250 \times 1.5 \times 1.15}{25} = \frac{431.25}{25} = 17.25 \;\Rightarrow\; N = \lceil 17.25 \rceil = 18 \text{ cards}
$$

**(ii) Maximum WIP (2 marks):**

$$
\text{WIP}_{\max} = N \cdot C = 18 \times 25 = 450 \text{ units}
$$

**(iii) Cycle time (2 marks):**

$$
T_c = \frac{N \cdot C}{d} = \frac{18 \times 25}{250} = \frac{450}{250} = 1.8 \text{ days}
$$

Converting: $1.8 \times 24 \times 60 = 2592$ minutes/day; per unit: $2592 / 450 = 5.76$ minutes/unit.

**Feasibility check (1 mark):** Calculated effective replenishment interval per unit = 5.76 min/unit < Takt time 10 min/unit → **System is feasible** (downstream will not be starved).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`**
**(a)** Compare the **Single-Card Kanban system** with the **Two-Card Kanban system**. Discuss the advantages of the two-card architecture in terms of machine utilization and material flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Toyota supplier must deliver 800 brake-disc units/day to the assembly plant. The lead time is 2 days, the safety factor is 0.10, and the company wants to use containers of 80 units each. **(i)** Determine the number of kanban cards needed. **(ii)** If management wishes to reduce inventory by 20%, how many kanbans must be removed, and what reduction in container size would achieve the same WIP reduction? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Single vs Two-Card Comparison (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Single-Card System | Two-Card System |
|---|---|---|
| Number of tokens | 1 (combined W+P card) | 2 (separate W and P) |
| Material + production authorization | Coupled | Decoupled |
| Machine utilization | Lower (waits for card return) | Higher (parallel flows) |
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Inventory level | Higher (cycle = full round trip) | Lower (cycles run in parallel) |
| Suitable for | Simple, low-volume lines | Complex, high-volume lines |

**Advantages of two-card:** Parallel circulation → upstream machine never waits for downstream withdrawal → higher OEE; lower WIP for same service level. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of kanbans (3 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} = \frac{800 \times 2 \times 1.10}{80}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1760}{80} = 22 \text{ cards exactly (no rounding needed)}
$$

**(ii) Reduction analysis (4 marks):**

20% reduction in WIP → new WIP target = $0.80 \times (22 \times 80) = 0.80 \times 1760 = 1408$ units.

Two alternative methods:
- **Method A — Reduce $N$:** New $N = 0.80 \times 22 = 17.6 \Rightarrow 18$ cards. So **4 cards must be removed** ($22 - 18 = 4$). **(2 marks)**
- **Method B — Reduce $C$:** Keep $N = 22$; new $C = 1408 / 22 = 64$ units/container. So **container size reduces by 16 units** (from 80 → 64). **(2 marks)**

Both methods achieve the same 20% WIP reduction; choice depends on whether container redesign is operationally feasible.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP ($N = \lceil \cdot \rceil$):** Many students write $N = 17.25$ and stop. Always round up to the next integer; a fractional kanban is physically meaningless. **Loss: 1 mark per instance.**
> 2. **Mixing W-Kanban and P-Kanban functions:** Examiners strictly test whether you know that **W-Kanban moves material** while **P-Kanban authorizes production**. Confusing these costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring units:** Writing $L$ in hours while $d$ is in units/day without conversion triggers an **immediate 1-mark deduction** under KTU's unit-consistency clause.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark architecture questions:** A neat two-loop diagram is worth **at least 3 marks** in Part B (a). Always draw it.
> 5. **Recommending "increase kanban" as a permanent fix:** Examiners deduct marks because Ohno's philosophy demands **lead-time reduction**, not inventory inflation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Two-Card Kanban System**
> - Two cards: **W-Kanban** (withdrawal/conveyance) and **P-Kanban** (production order). **(Core)**
> - Each Kanban is **physically attached** to its container — no card, no movement, no production. **(Toyota Rule 3)**
> - The two loops circulate **independently**, enabling parallel material and production flow.
> - Master formula: $N = \lceil d \cdot L \cdot (1 + S) / C \rceil$ — **always round up**.
> - $S \in [0.10, 0.20]$ is the **safety factor**, not a permanent demand buffer.
> - Maximum WIP = $N \cdot C$ — this is the **hard inventory ceiling** enforced by Kanban.
> - Cycle time $T_c = N \cdot C / d$ must be **greater than or equal to takt time** for feasibility.
> - Defective items are **never passed downstream** — stop the line (jidoka) instead of issuing extra kanbans.
> - **Strategic goal** is to *reduce N over time* by attacking lead time, not to inflate it.
> - Six Toyota Kanban rules (withdrawal only on W-Kanban; production only on P-Kanban; always attached; no defects; gradual reduction) form the **operational discipline**.
> - Practical applications: Toyota assembly lines, hospital pharmacies, aerospace component machining, e-commerce cross-docking.
> - Exam pattern: KTU typically asks the **formula derivation (3 marks)** + **numerical calculation (7 marks)** + **architectural explanation with diagram (4 marks)** = 14 marks total.
> - LaTeX safety: write $\vert x \vert$ not $\vert x \vert$ with raw pipes inside markdown tables.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Two-Card Kanban Production Control System (Module 1, PECBT865)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition, characteristics that influence the choice of alternative processes (volume and variety)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Definition, characteristics that influence the choice of alternative processes (volume and variety) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of a Process In the context of Operations Management (OM), a **process** refers to a structured, systematic, and repeatable set of activities, tasks, or operations that transforms a defined set of inputs (raw materials, information, labour, capital, and managerial attention) into defined outputs (goods and services) of greater value to the customer. As per KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a process is the **engine of conversion** that defines how work actually gets done inside a production or service system. Mathematically, the process function is represented as: $$ P(x) : \{\text{Inputs}\} \;\longrightarrow\; \{\text{Outputs}\} $$ Where the output is always designed to possess **higher utility, accuracy, or economic value** than the input bundle. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECBT865 Module 2):** > The syllabus explicitly frames this sub-topic around the *characteristics that influence the choice of alternative processes*. This means the exam does not just test the definition of a process — it tests the **decision logic** a manager uses to *select* the correct process type from alternatives. --- ## 1.2 What are "Alternative Processes"? When a firm designs its production or service system, it rarely has only one choice. It must choose between **alternative processes** — fundamentally different technological and structural ways of organising work to produce the same product or service. For example, a company that needs to manufacture wooden chairs can choose between: | Alternative Process | Description | Best Suited For | |---|---|---| | **Project Process** | Building one chair at a time at the customer's site | Custom luxury, antique reproduction | | **Job Shop (Batch)** | Producing 5–50 chairs at a time | Boutique furniture stores | | **Assembly Line** | Hundreds of identical chairs on a moving line | IKEA-style mass market | | **Continuous Flow** | Fully automated robotic production | Industrial-grade furniture for offices | Each alternative has a **different cost structure, different capital investment, different workforce skill requirements, and different flexibility levels**. Choosing the wrong one leads to high unit costs, lost customers, or wasted capacity. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation Imagine you want to make **fresh orange juice**. - If you need **1 glass** for yourself → you just squeeze the orange by hand. (*Project / Job Shop approach*). - If you need **10 glasses** for a small party → you use a manual juicer. (*Batch process approach*). - If you need **500 glasses** for a wedding → you rent an electric juicing machine. (*Assembly line approach*). - If you need **50,000 bottles per day** for a supermarket chain → you need a fully automated bottling plant. (*Continuous flow process*). The **volume (how much)** and **variety (how different the outputs are)** decide which juicing method — i.e., which *process alternative* — is most economical and efficient. This everyday example directly maps to the KTU framework. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Insight for Examiners:** > The choice of process is *not* a purely technical decision. It is a **strategic trade-off** between *volume efficiency* and *variety flexibility*. No single process is universally best — context (volume + variety) decides. --- ## 1.4 The Two Master Characteristics: Volume and Variety According to Slack, Chambers and Johnston (the foundational OM reference framework adopted across KTU modules), the choice of process is dominated by **two characteristics**: ### (a) Volume **Volume** is the **quantity of outputs produced per unit of time**. It is a measure of *scale*. - High volume → process must be **capital-intensive, standardised, and efficient**. - Low volume → process must be **labour-intensive, flexible, and customised**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** Volume is measured in **units per day, units per shift, or units per year**. The KTU board often uses the term *"throughput"* interchangeably with volume. ### (b) Variety **Variety** is the **range of different products or services a process is required to produce**. It is a measure of *complexity and heterogeneity*. - High variety → process must be **general-purpose, adaptable, and re-configurable**. - Low variety → process can be **highly specialised and dedicated**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** Variety is measured by the **number of distinct product types (SKUs)** a process can produce, or by the **number of distinct operations** a process must perform. ### (c) The Inverse Volume-Variety Relationship The most critical insight in this topic is that **volume and variety are almost always inversely related**: $$ \text{As Volume} \uparrow \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Variety} \downarrow $$ $$ \text{As Variety} \uparrow \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Volume} \downarrow $$ This inverse relationship is the **foundation of the Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix** (discussed in Section 2) and is one of the most heavily tested concepts in KTU ESE papers for this course. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four (Plus One) Generic Process Types Operations Management literature (and the KTU 2024 syllabus) classifies all manufacturing and service processes into **five generic process types**, arranged along the volume-variety spectrum: ### (i) Project Process - **Volume:** Very low (often 1 unit) - **Variety:** Very high (every project is unique) - **Characteristics:** Long duration, complex, high cost, highly customised - **Examples:** Construction of a bridge, shipbuilding, custom software development - **Resource type:** Mobile resources brought to a fixed site ### (ii) Jobbing Process (Job Shop) - **Volume:** Low (often single units or very small batches) - **Variety:** High (each job may differ significantly) - **Characteristics:** General-purpose equipment, highly skilled labour, high unit cost - **Examples:** Custom tailor, repair workshop, prototype manufacturing - **Layout:** Functional (process) layout ### (iii) Batch Process - **Volume:** Medium (groups of similar items) - **Variety:** Medium (several variants produced in batches) - **Characteristics:** Some standardisation, batch changeovers required - **Examples:** Bakery producing different breads, pharmaceutical batches, university course delivery - **Layout:** Process or cellular layout ### (iv) Assembly Line (Mass Production) - **Volume:** High (thousands to millions) - **Variety:** Low (few variants, often one standard model) - **Characteristics:** Dedicated equipment, line balancing, division of labour - **Examples:** Automobile assembly, consumer electronics, fast food chains - **Layout:** Product layout ### (v) Continuous Flow Process - **Volume:** Extremely high (24×7 operation) - **Variety:** Extremely low (often a single standardised commodity) - **Characteristics:** Fully automated, capital-intensive, minimal labour, continuous flow - **Examples:** Oil refineries, petrochemicals, electricity generation, water treatment - **Layout:** Fixed-position layout (for the plant itself) --- ## 2.2 The Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix The **Product-Process Matrix**, originally proposed by Robert H. Hayes and Steven C. Wheelwright (1979), is the **single most important analytical model** for this KTU topic. ### Structure of the Matrix The matrix plots: - **X-axis:** Product life cycle stage (from introduction → growth → maturity → decline) - **Y-axis:** Process life cycle stage (from manually operated job shop → batch → assembly line → continuous flow) The key principle is **Strategic Fit**: > A firm achieves competitive advantage when the **product life-cycle stage** is matched to the **process life-cycle stage** — neither over-investing nor under-investing in process capability. | Process Life Cycle ↓ \ Product Life Cycle → | Introductory | Growth | Mature | Declining | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Job Shop (manually operated)** | ✅ Best Fit | ❌ Under-invested | ❌ Severely under-invested | ❌ | | **Batch** | ⚠️ Acceptable | ✅ Best Fit | ⚠️ Acceptable | ⚠️ | | **Assembly Line** | ❌ Over-invested | ⚠️ Acceptable | ✅ Best Fit | ⚠️ | | **Continuous Flow** | ❌ Massively over-invested | ❌ Over-invested | ⚠️ Acceptable | ✅ Best Fit | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** > The diagonal cells (✅) represent **strategically appropriate matches**. Off-diagonal cells represent either: > - **Under-investment** (process too flexible/inefficient for high volume) → unit cost will be too high > - **Over-investment** (process too rigid for low volume) → capacity will be wasted > > The 45° diagonal from Job-Shop→Introductory to Continuous-Flow→Declining is the **"Strategic Fit Trajectory"** — and a frequent 14-mark question asks students to plot a firm's journey on this matrix. --- ## 2.3 Other Characteristics That Influence Process Choice While **Volume** and **Variety** are the dominant characteristics, KTU 2024 syllabus also requires students to understand **secondary factors** that influence the choice between alternative processes: ### (a) Capital Intensity - High volume, low variety → favours **high capital intensity** (machines do the work). - Low volume, high variety → favours **labour intensity** (humans do the work). ### (b) Flexibility Required - Process must be matched to the **required mix of products**, **changeover frequency**, and **lead time tolerance**. ### (c) Skill Level of Workforce - Custom processes need **multi-skilled artisans**. - Repetitive processes need **trained semi-skilled operators** or **engineers** for automated systems. ### (d) Customer Involvement - High customer contact (services) → process often must be **visible, fast, and location-flexible**. - Low customer contact (manufacturing) → process can be **centralised and optimised**. ### (e) Quality Requirements - Tight tolerance → favours **automated, controlled processes**. - Loose tolerance → manual processes acceptable. ### (f) Unit Cost Target - Determined by **competitive market pricing**. The chosen process must allow the firm to hit the target unit cost. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following formulas and metrics are repeatedly tested in KTU ESE papers for this topic. **Memorise this table.** | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Purpose | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Process Conversion Function | $P: I \rightarrow O$ | Symbolic | Defines process as an input-output transformation | | 2 | Volume | $V = \dfrac{N_{units}}{T}$ | units / time | Measures throughput scale | | 3 | Variety | $\text{Var} = \vert S \vert$ where $S$ = set of SKUs | dimensionless count | Measures heterogeneity of output | | 4 | Volume-Variety Inverse Law | $V \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Var}}$ | — | Foundation of product-process matrix | | 5 | Unit Cost (Process Economics) | $C_{unit} = \dfrac{F_c}{N} + V_c$ | ₹ / unit | Fixed cost per unit falls as volume rises | | 6 | Capacity Utilisation | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Maximum Possible Output}} \times 100\%$ | % | Indicates efficiency of process choice | | 7 | Break-even Volume | $N_{BE} = \dfrac{F_{c1} - F_{c2}}{V_{c2} - V_{c1}}$ | units | Identifies the volume where two process alternatives cost the same | | 8 | Process Type Index (PTI) | $PTI = \dfrac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Variety}}$ | ratio | Quantifies dominance of volume over variety | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note on Formula 8 (Process Type Index):** > A high PTI value (e.g., >1000) indicates a process suited to **continuous flow**. > A low PTI value (e.g., <10) indicates a process suited to **job shop**. > This index is the simplest way to *justify* a process choice in a 14-mark exam answer — examiners reward this explicitly. ### Real-World Utility This entire framework is used in industries ranging from **automotive manufacturing** (Toyota choosing between assembly line and cell-based production) to **hospital operations** (deciding between dedicated ICU vs. flexible ward beds) to **software services** (choosing between custom project delivery vs. SaaS product-line delivery). Mastery of this framework signals to the examiner that the student can apply OM theory to real managerial decision-making — the highest level of Bloom's taxonomy (Analysing/Evaluating). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Break-Even Volume Formula The break-even volume between two process alternatives is one of the most common numerical problems in KTU 14-mark questions. Below is the **complete, exhaustive derivation** — every algebraic step is shown. ### Setup Let us suppose a firm is choosing between **Process A** (e.g., a Job Shop — high flexibility, high variable cost) and **Process B** (e.g., an Assembly Line — low flexibility, low variable cost). Let: - $F_{cA}$ = Fixed cost of Process A (₹ / year) - $F_{cB}$ = Fixed cost of Process B (₹ / year) - $V_{cA}$ = Variable cost per unit under Process A (₹ / unit) - $V_{cB}$ = Variable cost per unit under Process B (₹ / unit) - $N$ = Annual volume (units / year) ### Step 1: Total Cost of Process A $$ TC_A(N) \;=\; F_{cA} + V_{cA} \cdot N $$ **Logic:** The total annual cost is the sum of fixed cost (incurred regardless of volume) plus the variable cost multiplied by the number of units produced. ### Step 2: Total Cost of Process B $$ TC_B(N) \;=\; F_{cB} + V_{cB} \cdot N $$ ### Step 3: Define the Break-Even Condition At break-even volume $N_{BE}$, the total costs of both processes are exactly equal: $$ TC_A(N_{BE}) \;=\; TC_B(N_{BE}) $$ ### Step 4: Substitute the Cost Equations $$ F_{cA} + V_{cA} \cdot N_{BE} \;=\; F_{cB} + V_{cB} \cdot N_{BE} $$ ### Step 5: Rearrange to Isolate $N_{BE}$ Move all fixed-cost terms to the left and all variable-cost terms to the right: $$ F_{cA} - F_{cB} \;=\; V_{cB} \cdot N_{BE} - V_{cA} \cdot N_{BE} $$ ### Step 6: Factor $N_{BE}$ on the Right-Hand Side $$ F_{cA} - F_{cB} \;=\; N_{BE} \cdot (V_{cB} - V_{cA}) $$ ### Step 7: Solve for $N_{BE}$ $$ N_{BE} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{cA} - F_{cB}}{V_{cB} - V_{cA}} $$ ### Step 8: Interpret the Result $$ \text{If } N_{actual} < N_{BE} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Choose Process A (lower fixed cost)} $$ $$ \text{If } N_{actual} > N_{BE} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Choose Process B (lower variable cost)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Numerical Trick:** > This formula only makes sense when $F_{cA} \neq F_{cB}$ and $V_{cA} \neq V_{cB}$. Always check that the denominator is non-zero — failing this check is a frequent student error. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style) A furniture manufacturer is choosing between a **manual job shop** (Process A) and an **automated assembly line** (Process B). | Parameter | Process A (Job Shop) | Process B (Assembly Line) | |---|---|---| | Fixed Cost ($F_c$) | ₹ 5,00,000 / year | ₹ 25,00,000 / year | | Variable Cost ($V_c$) | ₹ 1,500 / unit | ₹ 500 / unit | **Find the break-even volume and state which process should be chosen for an expected annual demand of 8,000 units.** ### Solution **Step 1:** Apply the break-even formula: $$ N_{BE} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{cA} - F_{cB}}{V_{cB} - V_{cA}} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values: $$ N_{BE} \;=\; \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000}{500 - 1500} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the numerator: $$ \text{Numerator} \;=\; -20{,}00{,}000 $$ **Step 4:** Compute the denominator: $$ \text{Denominator} \;=\; -1000 $$ **Step 5:** Divide: $$ N_{BE} \;=\; \dfrac{-20{,}00{,}000}{-1000} \;=\; 2000 \;\text{units} $$ **Step 6:** Interpretation: $$ N_{actual} = 8000 \;>\; N_{BE} = 2000 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Choose Process B (Assembly Line)} $$ **Step 7 (Bonus verification — always write this in KTU answers for full marks):** Compare actual total costs: $$ TC_A(8000) = 5{,}00{,}000 + 1500 \times 8000 = 1{,}25{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ TC_B(8000) = 25{,}00{,}000 + 500 \times 8000 = 65{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ \Delta TC = TC_A - TC_B = 60{,}00{,}000 $$ Since Process B saves ₹ 60 lakh per year, the choice of Assembly Line is **economically correct and strongly justified** — this level of verification earns full 14 marks. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Heuristics for Process Selection The following decision matrix summarises the *if-then* reasoning a manager (and a KTU answer script) should follow: | If Volume is... | AND Variety is... | Then Choose | Because... | |---|---|---|---| | Very Low | Very High | **Project** | One-off, custom, mobile resources | | Low | High | **Job Shop** | General-purpose equipment, skilled labour | | Medium | Medium | **Batch** | Group processing, changeovers acceptable | | High | Low | **Assembly Line** | Dedicated line, division of labour | | Very High | Very Low | **Continuous Flow** | 24×7 automation, extreme cost efficiency | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** > The "AND Variety is..." column is what most students forget to mention in process-choice answers. Always explicitly state **both** volume AND variety levels — never justify process choice using volume alone. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Volume-Variety Spectrum (Mermaid Flow Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PROJECT PROCESS

with Volume-Variety Justification"] style A fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366 style N fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600 style O fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7f37,stroke-width:2px ``` **How to use this tree in a KTU answer:** When a 14-mark question asks *"Which process should a firm choose given X volume and Y variety?"*, a student who draws (or verbally traces) this decision tree automatically demonstrates **Apply-level** thinking, which is the highest cognitive level tested in KTU ESE for OM. --- ## 4.4 Process Type Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph COMMON["ALL PROCESS TYPES SHARE"] X1["INPUTS"] --> X2["TRANSFORMATION"] --> X3["OUTPUTS"] end subgraph DIFF["WHAT DIFFERENTIATES PROCESS ALTERNATIVES"] Y1["Capital Intensity Level"] Y2["Skill Level Required"] Y3["Volume Capacity"] Y4["Variety Capability"] Y5["Unit Cost Profile"] end X3 --> DIFF style COMMON fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366 style DIFF fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECBT865]** *Define the term "process" in Operations Management. List the two primary characteristics that influence the choice between alternative processes.* #### Model Answer (3 marks) A **process** in Operations Management is a structured and repeatable set of activities that transforms inputs (materials, labour, information, capital) into outputs (goods or services) of higher value to the customer. The two primary characteristics that influence the choice of alternative processes are: 1. **Volume** — the quantity of outputs produced per unit time. High volume processes favour capital-intensive, standardised operations. 2. **Variety** — the range of different products or services the process must handle. High variety processes favour flexible, general-purpose operations. > **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Two characteristics with brief explanation (1.5 marks). --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECBT865]** *State the Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix. What does "strategic fit" mean in this context?* #### Model Answer (3 marks) The **Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix** is a 2-D framework that maps **product life cycle stages** (introductory, growth, mature, declining) against **process life cycle stages** (job shop, batch, assembly line, continuous flow). **Strategic fit** refers to the alignment between the firm's process capability and the requirements of its product's market stage. When fit is achieved (i.e., a firm lies on the matrix's diagonal), the firm achieves **operational efficiency and competitive advantage**. A mismatch results in either over-investment (process too capital-intensive) or under-investment (process too primitive), both of which raise unit costs. > **Mark Allocation:** Matrix description (1.5 marks) + Strategic fit concept (1.5 marks). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECBT865, Module 2 Internal Choice Set A]** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the five generic process types in Operations Management. For each, clearly state the volume and variety levels, and give one industry example. **(b) [7 marks]** A bakery is choosing between two production processes: - **Process A (Hand-made):** Fixed cost = ₹ 2,00,000 / year, Variable cost = ₹ 80 / unit - **Process B (Semi-automated):** Fixed cost = ₹ 10,00,000 / year, Variable cost = ₹ 30 / unit If the expected annual demand is 25,000 units, calculate the break-even volume and recommend which process should be chosen. Justify your answer. --- ### Model Answer for Question A #### Part (a) — Five Generic Process Types [7 marks] The five generic process types, arranged from highest variety / lowest volume to lowest variety / highest volume, are: **1. Project Process** [1 mark] - **Volume:** Very low (typically one unit) - **Variety:** Very high (each project is unique) - **Characteristics:** Long duration, complex coordination, resources are brought to a fixed site, customer is highly involved - **Example:** Construction of a high-rise building, custom ship-building, film production **2. Jobbing Process (Job Shop)** [1 mark] - **Volume:** Low (single units or very small batches) - **Variety:** High (each job is somewhat unique) - **Characteristics:** General-purpose equipment, highly skilled labour, functional layout, high unit cost - **Example:** Custom tailor shop, machine repair workshop, prototype engineering firm **3. Batch Process** [1.5 marks] - **Volume:** Medium (10s to 1000s of units per batch) - **Variety:** Medium (several product variants produced periodically) - **Characteristics:** Repetitive in nature but with changeovers between batches, group technology or cellular layouts common - **Example:** Bakery producing different breads in batches, pharmaceutical drug manufacturing, university class delivery **4. Assembly Line (Mass Production)** [1.5 marks] - **Volume:** High (1000s to millions per year) - **Variety:** Low (few standardised variants) - **Characteristics:** Product layout, dedicated equipment, line balancing, division of labour, semi-skilled workers - **Example:** Maruti Suzuki automobile assembly, Apple iPhone assembly, McDonald's burger line **5. Continuous Flow Process** [2 marks] - **Volume:** Extremely high (24×7 operation, millions of units per year) - **Variety:** Extremely low (often a single commodity) - **Characteristics:** Fully automated, capital-intensive, minimal labour, extremely low unit cost, fixed-position plant layout - **Example:** Oil refinery, natural gas processing plant, electricity generation, cement kiln, bottled water plant #### Part (b) — Break-Even Volume Numerical [7 marks] **Given:** - $F_{cA}$ = ₹ 2,00,000 / year - $F_{cB}$ = ₹ 10,00,000 / year - $V_{cA}$ = ₹ 80 / unit - $V_{cB}$ = ₹ 30 / unit - $N_{actual}$ = 25,000 units / year **Step 1 — State the break-even formula:** $$ N_{BE} = \dfrac{F_{cA} - F_{cB}}{V_{cB} - V_{cA}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values:** $$ N_{BE} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000}{30 - 80} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the numerator and denominator:** $$ N_{BE} = \dfrac{-8{,}00{,}000}{-50} = 16{,}000 \;\text{units} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** Since $N_{actual} = 25{,}000 > N_{BE} = 16{,}000$, the bakery should choose **Process B (Semi-automated)** because the actual volume is well above the break-even threshold. **Step 5 — Verification by total cost comparison:** $$ TC_A = 2{,}00{,}000 + 80 \times 25{,}000 = 22{,}00{,}000 $$ $$ TC_B = 10{,}00{,}000 + 30 \times 25{,}000 = 17{,}50{,}000 $$ $$ \Delta TC = TC_A - TC_B = 4{,}50{,}000 $$ **Conclusion:** Process B saves ₹ 4,50,000 per year and is therefore the economically correct choice at the expected volume of 25,000 units. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECBT865, Module 2 Internal Choice Set B]** **(a) [7 marks]** What is the volume-variety relationship in process selection? Explain how this relationship influences the choice of process type with two real-world examples (one of high-volume/low-variety and one of low-volume/high-variety). **(b) [7 marks]** Draw and explain the Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix. With the help of an example, discuss what happens when a firm has a **mis-fit** between its product life-cycle stage and its process life-cycle stage. --- ### Model Answer for Question B #### Part (a) — Volume-Variety Relationship [7 marks] **Statement of the inverse relationship:** Volume and variety are almost always **inversely related**. As the volume of production rises, the variety of products that process can efficiently handle falls, and vice versa. This inverse law is the foundation of process choice in Operations Management. [1 mark] **Mathematical representation:** $$ V \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Var}} \quad \text{(inverse proportionality)} $$ **Mechanism of the relationship:** [2 marks] - High-volume processes achieve low unit cost through **repetition, standardisation, and dedicated machinery** — this standardisation is *only* possible when variety is low. - High-variety processes require **flexible, general-purpose equipment and skilled labour** — these resources are inherently slower and more expensive per unit, restricting efficient volume. **Example 1 — High Volume / Low Variety:** [2 marks] The Maruti Suzuki assembly line at Manesar produces approximately 1,500 cars per day of essentially the same few variants (Swift, Baleno, Dzire). Volume is high, variety is low, and the process is a dedicated assembly line with minimal changeover — economically optimal. **Example 2 — Low Volume / High Variety:** [2 marks] A custom furniture workshop in Kerala's artisan sector produces one-of-a-kind pieces based on individual customer designs. Volume is low (often a single piece per customer), variety is extremely high, and the process is a job shop using general-purpose tools and master craftsmen. --- #### Part (b) — Product-Process Matrix and Mis-fit Analysis [7 marks] **Drawing the matrix** [2 marks]: The matrix has Product Life Cycle (Introductory, Growth, Mature, Declining) on the X-axis and Process Life Cycle (Job Shop, Batch, Assembly Line, Continuous Flow) on the Y-axis. **Description of diagonal strategic fit:** [2 marks] | Product Stage | Best Process Match | |---|---| | Introductory | Job Shop (low volume, high variety) | | Growth | Batch Process | | Mature | Assembly Line | | Declining | Continuous Flow | **Mis-fit analysis — Example:** [3 marks] Consider a traditional handloom textile firm in Kerala that has achieved brand recognition and is now in the **growth stage** of its product life cycle. Demand is rising rapidly. However, the firm continues to operate a **job shop** process with hand-looms and artisan weavers. **Type of mis-fit:** **Under-investment** — the process is too primitive for the product's market reality. **Consequences:** - Unit cost stays high (because fixed cost is spread over few units) - Lead time lengthens, customers become dissatisfied - Quality variability remains high - The firm cannot scale to meet demand - Competitors with batch or semi-automated processes win market share **Corrective action:** The firm should **invest in batch processing** — semi-automated looms that can produce higher volume while still preserving some product variety. This moves the firm diagonally up the matrix to **strategic fit**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > **Most common mistakes students make on this question:** > 1. **Forgetting to plot the diagonal** — Examiners specifically look for the "strategic fit diagonal" arrow. Without it, you lose 2 marks. > 2. **Confusing "variety" with "quality"** — Variety refers to *the number of distinct products*, not the level of craftsmanship. Using these terms interchangeably loses marks. > 3. **Not justifying the mis-fit type** — Always state explicitly whether the firm is *over-invested* or *under-invested*, and explain the *economic consequence* (high unit cost, wasted capacity, lost customers). > 4. **Missing the verification step in numerical answers** — For any break-even calculation, KTU examiners expect you to *also* compute the actual total costs and compare them. This verification step is worth 1–2 marks by itself. > 5. **Defining volume and variety without giving units or metrics** — Always state that volume is measured in *units per time* and variety is measured in *count of SKUs*. Generic definitions lose marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PECBT865 Module 2** ### Core Definitions - ☐ **Process:** A repeatable set of activities transforming inputs into higher-value outputs. - ☐ **Volume:** Quantity of output per unit time (units/day, units/year). - ☐ **Variety:** Range of distinct products/services a process must produce (count of SKUs). - ☐ **Strategic Fit:** Alignment of product life-cycle stage with process life-cycle stage. ### The Five Generic Process Types (Memorise in order) - ☐ Project → Job Shop → Batch → Assembly Line → Continuous Flow - ☐ Volume: Very Low → Very High - ☐ Variety: Very High → Very Low ### The Hayes-Wheelwright Matrix - ☐ Diagonal = Strategic Fit = Optimal choice - ☐ Off-diagonal = Mis-fit (Over-invested or Under-invested) - ☐ Mature product + Assembly line = textbook example of fit ### Key Formulas to Memorise - ☐ $N_{BE} = \dfrac{F_{cA} - F_{cB}}{V_{cB} - V_{cA}}$ - ☐ $C_{unit} = \dfrac{F_c}{N} + V_c$ - ☐ $PTI = \dfrac{V}{\text{Var}}$ ### Secondary Factors (often asked as 3-mark sub-questions) - ☐ Capital intensity, workforce skill, flexibility, customer involvement, quality tolerance, unit cost target. ### Exam Strategy - ☐ Always answer process-choice questions by stating **BOTH volume AND variety levels**. - ☐ Always verify numerical answers by computing actual total costs. - ☐ Always explicitly state **"over-invested" or "under-invested"** when discussing mis-fits. - ☐ Draw the Product-Process Matrix for any 14-mark answer on this topic — visual aids earn marks.

topics.title: Type of processes: job shop, batch, mass and continuous
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Type of processes: job shop, batch, mass and continuous # MODULE 2: PROCESS TYPES IN OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a "Process" in Operations Management? In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme lexicon, a **process** is defined as a structured set of interrelated work activities, machinery, tools, and human resource actions that transform a defined set of **inputs** (materials, information, customer requirements) into a specified set of **outputs** (goods, services, semi-finished products) of greater value. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of processes is foundational to designing the **process flow architecture**, **layout strategy**, and **capacity planning** of any manufacturing or service organization. Under NEP 2020 outcome-based education, students must be able to *identify* and *select* the appropriate process type for a given production scenario. The four canonical process types (often called the **Process Continuum** or **Volume-Variety Spectrum**) recognized by KTU are: 1. **Job Shop (Project)** 2. **Batch Production** 3. **Mass (Repetitive/Assembly Line) Production** 4. **Continuous (Process) Production** --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Spectrum" 🍳 Imagine a single street over a distance of 1 kilometre, lined with food shops. At one end sits a **five-star restaurant kitchen** — every dish is unique, cooked one at a time by a master chef, customized to the customer's order. At the other end is an **automated soft-drink bottling plant** — millions of identical bottles of the same cola roll off the conveyor every hour, 24/7, untouched by human hands. In between, you will find: - A **cloud kitchen** that prepares food in *batches* (e.g., 50 portions of Biryani at a time). - A **canteen** that cooks large *masses* of rice and curry on a standard daily menu. **This spectrum is exactly what process types describe — the trade-off between Volume (quantity produced) and Variety (customization/differentiation).** --- ### 1.3 The Volume-Variety Trade-off (KTU's Core Axiom) > [!NOTE] > **The KTU Volume-Variety Axiom:** As the **volume of output increases**, the **variety (customization)** that the process can economically handle **decreases**, and vice versa. This is the single most-tested concept in Module 2. Mathematically expressed as an inverse relationship: $$V_{\text{output}} \propto \frac{1}{V_{\text{variety}}}$$ where $V_{\text{output}}$ is the production volume and $V_{\text{variety}}$ is the product mix breadth. --- ### 1.4 Formal Definitions (Examination-Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 1 — Job Shop Process:** A *low-volume, high-variety* production process in which goods are produced to a *unique specification* of an individual customer, with each job requiring its own distinct routing through functionally grouped resources. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 2 — Batch Process:** A *medium-volume, medium-variety* production process in which similar items are produced in *finite lots* (batches), with each batch moving through a sequence of operations before the equipment is reset for the next batch. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 3 — Mass (Repetitive) Process:** A *high-volume, low-variety* production process in which *standardized* products flow through a *fixed sequence* of operations, typically on a dedicated assembly line, for an extended period. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 4 — Continuous Process:** A *very-high-volume, zero-variety* production process in which production runs *24/7 without interruption*, with goods flowing in a continuous stream (liquids, gases, powders) through highly automated, capital-intensive equipment. --- ### 1.5 The Process Selection Triangle (Volume, Variety, Volume-Variety Mapping) | Process Type | Volume | Variety | Customer Involvement | Skill Level | Capital Intensity | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Job Shop** | Very Low | Very High | Very High | Very High | Very Low | | **Batch** | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | Low–Medium | | **Mass** | High | Low | Low | Low | High | | **Continuous** | Very High | Very Low / Nil | Negligible | Very Low (Automated) | Very High | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Process Type Analysis ### 2.1.1 JOB SHOP PROCESS — The Custom Craftsman **Operational Logic Flow:** - Each customer order is treated as a **distinct project**. - Resources (machines, tools, labour) are **grouped by function** (e.g., all lathes in one section, all milling machines in another) — this is the **Functional Layout** (also called *Process Layout*). - Routing of a job through the shop is **unique** and changes with each order. - Production lead time is **long and unpredictable** because jobs compete for shared resources. **Why it works (KTU Logic):** - The grouping by function allows a *single highly-skilled worker or machine* to handle *many types* of jobs. - Variety is achieved by **flexibility of routing**, not by dedicated lines. **Where it is used (KTU expects real-world examples):** - Custom furniture workshops in Kerala (Ottamoola furniture). - Custom machine tool jobs in small-scale engineering industries. - Prototype manufacturing in R&D labs. - Heavy engineering project sites (ship-building, bridge construction). - Hospital surgery rooms (services analogy). **Drawbacks (High-Yield for KTU):** - Long **throughput time**. - High **work-in-process (WIP) inventory**. - Complex **scheduling and routing**. - Difficult to **automate**. --- ### 2.1.2 BATCH PROCESS — The Flexible Mid-Player **Operational Logic Flow:** - A **batch (lot)** of similar items is produced together. - After the batch is completed, the equipment is **re-set (changeover / setup)** to produce the next batch of a *different* item. - The shop floor follows a **cellular or grouped layout** — machines grouped by the *process required* for a *family of parts*. **Two sub-categories (KTU frequently tests this distinction):** 1. **Pure Batch (True Batch):** Different products in different batches; no overlap in time. 2. **Mixed Batch (Batch-in-Flow / Repetitive Batch):** Several products are produced in mixed sequence; smaller lot sizes; faster changeovers. **Why it works:** - Balances the *flexibility of a job shop* with the *efficiency of mass production*. - Allows **make-to-stock (MTS)** for a small range of variants. **Where it is used:** - Bakeries producing 100 loaves of white bread, then switching to 100 brown bread. - Garment manufacturing in Tirupur (T-shirts, shirts in batches). - Pharmaceutical tablet production. - Kerala-based spice-packing units. **Drawbacks:** - **Setup time and setup cost** between batches reduce efficiency. - **Inventory holding** of finished batches. - Production planning complexity (deciding *batch size* and *sequence*). --- ### 2.1.3 MASS (REPETITIVE) PROCESS — The Assembly Line **Operational Logic Flow:** - A **fixed product** (or a very narrow family) is produced in **very high volume** for a **long period**. - The product flows along a **Production Line / Assembly Line** — a **Product Layout**. - Workstations are arranged *in the sequence of operations*. - Each station performs a **standardized, short, repetitive task** (often a fraction of a minute). - High use of **special-purpose machines, transfer lines, and conveyors**. **The Line-Balancing Principle (KTU Formula):** - The output rate of the line is governed by the **slowest workstation (bottleneck)**. - The **Cycle Time (CT)** must equal the *demand rate* (or planned output rate). $$CT = \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Period}}{\text{Demand per Period}}$$ **Where it is used:** - Maruti Suzuki assembly line. - TVS / Hero motorcycle assembly. - Mobile phone assembly (Foxconn). - Coca-Cola bottling line. **Drawbacks:** - **Extremely high** capital investment. - **Brittle** — a single breakdown halts the entire line. - **Zero flexibility** — switching to a new product is a major re-engineering project. --- ### 2.1.4 CONTINUOUS PROCESS — The Flowing Stream **Operational Logic Flow:** - Production runs **24 hours a day, 7 days a week, 365 days a year** (or for very long campaigns). - The output is a **homogeneous, indivisible stream** — liquid, gas, powder, slurry, electricity. - The process is governed by **physical, chemical, or thermal transformations** rather than discrete assembly. - It is the *most automated* and *least labour-intensive* form of production. **Why it works (The Economics):** - The **unit cost drops dramatically** as volume increases because fixed costs are spread over millions of units. - Shutting down the plant is **extremely costly** — so the process is *highly reliable*. **The Reliability Metric (KTU Formula):** - The economic viability depends on **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** exceeding the **cycle length of the campaign**. $$\text{Plant Availability} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$$ where $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair. **Where it is used:** - Oil refineries (BPCL Kochi). - Petrochemicals (FACT Kochi, Hindustan Organic). - Steel mills (SAIL). - Power generation (NTPC thermal plants). - Cement manufacturing. - Water purification plants. **Drawbacks:** - **Highest capital investment** of all four types. - **Cannot be stopped or changed** easily. - Requires **sophisticated process control** (DCS – Distributed Control Systems). - The **reverse** — switching product grade is a *campaign-level* decision. --- ## 2.2 The Process Life-Cycle (Bonus High-Yield Concept) > [!NOTE] > As a product moves through its **Product Life-Cycle (PLC)** — Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline — the *appropriate process type* also evolves.
