KTU commonly frames this as a **"Process Choice over the PLC"** question. | PLC Stage | Demand Pattern | Appropriate Process | |---|---|---| | Introduction | Low, uncertain, custom | **Job Shop** | | Growth | Rising, some variants | **Batch** | | Maturity | High, standardized | **Mass Production** | | Decline | Falling, niche | **Batch / Job Shop** (return) | --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Volume-Variety Inverse | $V_{\text{volume}} \propto 1 / V_{\text{variety}}$ | Qualitative ratio | | 2 | Cycle Time (Mass) | $CT = T_{\text{available}} / D$ | $T$ in minutes, $D$ in units | | 3 | Line Output (Mass) | $R = 1 / CT$ | Units per minute | | 4 | Plant Availability (Continuous) | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) | | 5 | Utilization (Batch/Mass) | $U = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Maximum Possible Output}}$ | Percentage (0–100%) | | 6 | Batch Size Trade-off (EOQ-relevant) | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ (Economic Batch Quantity) | Units; $D$=demand, $S$=setup cost, $H$=holding cost | | 7 | Throughput Time | $T_t = \text{Process Time} + \text{Queue Time} + \text{Move Time} + \text{Wait Time}$ | Time units | | 8 | Capacity (Continuous) | $C = \text{Rated Capacity} \times \text{Availability} \times \text{Yield}$ | Units per period | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** In Part-B questions, students are expected to *qualitatively justify* the choice of process type using **at least three of the above factors** (volume, variety, capital, skill, demand variability). A bare statement of "use mass production" without justification scores ≤ 4 of 7 marks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — The Cycle Time of a Mass Production Line > **Problem Setup (KTU Pattern):** A motorcycle assembly line operates **8 hours per day, 250 working days per year**. The annual demand forecast is **25,000 units**. Determine the **Cycle Time (CT)** of the line. ### Step 1: Convert available time to a common unit. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{available per day}} &= 8 \text{ hours/day} \times 60 \text{ min/hour} \\ &= 480 \text{ min/day} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Compute daily demand. $$ \begin{aligned} D_{\text{per day}} &= \frac{25{,}000 \text{ units}}{250 \text{ days}} \\ &= 100 \text{ units/day} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Apply the Cycle Time formula. $$ \begin{aligned} CT &= \frac{T_{\text{available}}}{D} \\ &= \frac{480 \text{ min/day}}{100 \text{ units/day}} \\ &= 4.8 \text{ min/unit} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** Each workstation on the line must complete its task in **≤ 4.8 minutes** for the line to meet demand. The workstation with the highest task time becomes the **bottleneck** and dictates the actual CT. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Plant Availability of a Continuous Process > **Problem Setup (KTU Pattern):** An ammonia synthesis plant in Kerala has an **MTBF of 720 hours** and an **MTTR of 80 hours**. Compute the **plant availability** and the **expected annual downtime**. ### Step 1: Substitute into the availability formula. $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\ &= \frac{720}{720 + 80} \\ &= \frac{720}{800} \\ &= 0.90 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 90\% \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Compute total annual hours. $$ \begin{aligned} H_{\text{annual}} &= 365 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours/day} \\ &= 8{,}760 \text{ hours/year} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Compute expected downtime. $$ \begin{aligned} D_{\text{down}} &= (1 - A) \times H_{\text{annual}} \\ &= (1 - 0.90) \times 8{,}760 \\ &= 876 \text{ hours/year} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** The plant is expected to be **non-operational for 876 hours per year** (≈ 36.5 days), which is critical for a continuous process because every shutdown hour represents *lost revenue and a costly restart*. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — The Volume-Variety Cost Trade-off A foundational insight in KTU Module 2 is the **Unit Cost Behaviour across Process Types**. Let us derive it qualitatively. ### Step 1: Define unit cost. $$ C_{\text{unit}} = \frac{C_{\text{fixed}} + C_{\text{variable}}}{N} $$ where $C_{\text{fixed}}$ = fixed costs (machinery, plant), $C_{\text{variable}}$ = variable costs (labour, material per unit), and $N$ = units produced. ### Step 2: Substitute for each process type. | Process Type | $C_{\text{fixed}}$ | $C_{\text{variable}}$ per unit | Behaviour of $C_{\text{unit}}$ as $N \uparrow$ | |---|---|---|---| | Job Shop | Very Low | Very High | High & slow to fall | | Batch | Low–Medium | Medium | Falls steadily with $N$ | | Mass | High | Low | Falls sharply; asymptote | | Continuous | Very High | Very Low | Falls fastest; lowest asymptote | ### Step 3: Show the asymptote (continuous process). $$ \lim_{N \to \infty} C_{\text{unit}} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{C_{\text{fixed}} + C_{\text{variable}}}{N} = \frac{C_{\text{variable}}}{N} \approx \text{Variable cost only} $$ > **Interpretation:** The continuous process can theoretically drive **unit cost down to the variable cost alone** at very high volumes — this is the **economies-of-scale** principle. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comprehensive KTU Comparison Table | Decision Factor | Job Shop | Batch | Mass (Repetitive) | Continuous | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Volume** | Very Low (1 – 100) | Low – Medium (100 – 10,000) | High (10,000 – millions) | Very High (millions) | | **Variety** | Very High (custom) | Medium (families) | Low (1–few variants) | Nil / single grade | | **Layout** | Functional / Process | Cellular / Group | Product / Line | Fixed / Pipeline | | **Capital Intensity** | Very Low | Low–Medium | High | Very High | | **Skill of Labour** | Very High (craftsmen) | Medium (semi-skilled) | Low (repetitive tasks) | Very Low (supervisory) | | **WIP Inventory** | High | Medium | Low | Negligible | | **Lead Time** | Very Long | Medium | Short | Shortest | | **Flexibility** | Very High | High | Low | Very Low | | **Automation Level** | Manual / CNC islands | Semi-automatic | High | Fully automated (DCS) | | **Setup Time** | Negligible (per job) | Significant (per batch) | Negligible (continuous run) | Negligible (long campaign) | | **Product Examples** | Custom furniture, prototype ship | Bakery bread, garments, tablets | Cars, motorcycles, TVs | Petrol, cement, electricity | | **Kerala Example** | Athani furniture cluster | Bakeries in Thrissur, cashew processing | Maruti Udyog, TVS | BPCL Kochi Refinery, FACT | | **Best Suited For** | MTO (Make-to-Order) | MTS / MTO hybrid | MTS (Make-to-Stock) | Stock / Pipeline inventory | | **Customer Involvement** | Very High | Medium | Low | Nil | | **Plant Sensitivity to Breakdown** | Low (one station can be bypassed) | Medium | High (line stops) | Critical (full plant halt) | --- ## 3.5 The Process Selection Decision Logic (KTU Exam Favourite) A student must be able to traverse this decision tree for a 7-mark KTU sub-question: ``` START │ ├─ Is annual demand < 100 units? │ └─ YES → JOB SHOP │ ├─ Is the product highly customized / customer-specified? │ └─ YES → JOB SHOP │ ├─ Is demand 100 – 10,000 units with several variants? │ └─ YES → BATCH PRODUCTION │ ├─ Is the product highly standardized with annual demand > 10,000? │ └─ YES → MASS / REPETITIVE PRODUCTION │ ├─ Is the output a homogeneous stream (liquid/gas/powder)? │ └─ YES → CONTINUOUS PROCESS │ └─ DEFAULT → Batch (most flexible mid-ground) ``` > **Decision Rule (KTU Sanctioned):** > $$\text{Process} = f(\text{Volume}, \text{Variety}, \text{Capital}, \text{Skill}, \text{Flexibility})$$ This 5-variable function is the **canonical exam answer** for a "select the appropriate process type" question. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Process Type Continuum (Volume-Variety Spectrum) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["JOB SHOP
Low Volume
High Variety
Custom Craftsmen"] nodeB["BATCH
Medium Volume
Medium Variety
Lot-based Production"] nodeC["MASS / REPETITIVE
High Volume
Low Variety
Assembly Line"] nodeD["CONTINUOUS
Very High Volume
Zero Variety
24/7 Stream"] nodeA -- Volume increases, Variety decreases --> nodeB nodeB -- Volume increases, Variety decreases --> nodeC nodeC -- Volume increases, Variety decreases --> nodeD style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Movement from left to right represents an **increase in volume and a decrease in variety**, accompanied by **higher capital intensity and lower labour skill requirements**. --- ## 4.2 Layout Architecture by Process Type ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph layoutA["LAYOUT A: JOB SHOP — Functional Layout"] dirA1["All Lathes"] dirA2["All Mills"] dirA3["All Grinders"] dirA4["All Welders"] JobA1["Job 1"] -.routes.-> dirA1 JobA1 -.routes.-> dirA3 JobA2["Job 2"] -.routes.-> dirA4 JobA2 -.routes.-> dirA2 JobA2 -.routes.-> dirA1 end subgraph layoutB["LAYOUT B: BATCH — Cellular Layout"] cellB1["Cell 1
Lathe + Mill + Drill"] cellB2["Cell 2
Grinder + Polisher"] cellB3["Cell 3
Welder + Paint Booth"] BatchB1["Part Family A"] --> cellB1 BatchB1 --> cellB2 BatchB2["Part Family B"] --> cellB3 end subgraph layoutC["LAYOUT C: MASS — Product Layout"] L1["Station 1
Frame"] --> L2["Station 2
Engine"] L2 --> L3["Station 3
Wheels"] L3 --> L4["Station 4
Paint"] L4 --> L5["Station 5
Testing"] end subgraph layoutD["LAYOUT D: CONTINUOUS — Fixed Pipeline"] R1["Reactor 1"] --> R2["Reactor 2"] R2 --> R3["Separator"] R3 --> R4["Purifier"] R4 --> R5["Storage Tank"] end style layoutA fill:#FFF8DC style layoutB fill:#E0FFFF style layoutC fill:#F0FFF0 style layoutD fill:#FFF0F5 ``` --- ## 4.3 Process Type Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startStart["Production Requirement Given"] q1{"Annual demand
is high?"} q2{"Product is
customised?"} q3{"Output is a
homogeneous stream?"} outA["Select JOB SHOP"] outB["Select BATCH"] outC["Select MASS PRODUCTION"] outD["Select CONTINUOUS"] startStart --> q2 q2 -- Yes --> outA q2 -- No --> q1 q1 -- No --> outB q1 -- Yes --> q3 q3 -- No --> outC q3 -- Yes --> outD style startStart fill:#FFD700 style outA fill:#FFE4B5 style outB fill:#B0E0E6 style outC fill:#98FB98 style outD fill:#FFB6C1 ``` --- ## 4.4 The Process–Product Life-Cycle Map ```mermaid flowchart LR intro["INTRODUCTION
Low & Uncertain Demand
Design Changes Frequent"] growth["GROWTH
Rising Demand
Few Variants"] maturity["MATURITY
High & Stable Demand
Standardised Product"] decline["DECLINE
Falling Demand
Niche Market"] intro -- "Process: JOB SHOP" --> growth growth -- "Process: BATCH" --> maturity maturity -- "Process: MASS" --> decline decline -- "Return to JOB SHOP / BATCH" --> intro style intro fill:#FFE4B5 style growth fill:#B0E0E6 style maturity fill:#98FB98 style decline fill:#D3D3D3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between Job Shop Production and Batch Production.** **Model Answer (Examiner's Key Pattern):** | Parameter | Job Shop | Batch | |---|---|---| | Volume | Very low (1–100) | Low to medium (100–10,000) | | Variety | Very high, customer-specific | Medium, part-family based | | Layout | Functional (process layout) | Cellular / group layout | | Lead time | Long & unpredictable | Medium & predictable | | Setup | None between jobs | Required between batches | | Skill | Highly skilled craftsmen | Semi-skilled operators | | Example | Custom furniture | Bakery production | *(Award 3 marks for any 3 well-explained differentiating points with examples.)* --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is a Continuous Production process? Give two examples.** **Model Answer:** A **Continuous Production process** is a high-volume, zero-variety production system in which the output flows in an uninterrupted stream (liquid, gas, powder, or energy) for 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. The process is fully automated and capital-intensive, with very low labour content per unit. **Two examples:** 1. Crude oil refining (e.g., **BPCL Kochi Refinery**). 2. Electricity generation (e.g., **NTPC Kayamkulam thermal plant**). *(Award 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for examples.)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 14 Marks]** > **"Explain the four types of production processes with suitable examples. Discuss the volume–variety relationship in detail."** #### Part (a) — Explain the four types of production processes with examples. **[7 marks]** **Solution:** The four canonical process types in operations management form a continuum based on the trade-off between **production volume** and **product variety**: 1. **Job Shop Production** — A low-volume, high-variety process where each job is unique. Example: a custom furniture workshop in Kerala producing a one-of-a-kind dining table. Resources are grouped by *function* (all saws together, all polishers together). It is highly flexible but has long lead times and high WIP. 2. **Batch Production** — A medium-volume, medium-variety process where items are produced in *finite lots*. Example: a pharmaceutical company producing 50,000 tablets of Paracetamol, then switching to 50,000 tablets of Ibuprofen. Requires *changeover / setup* between batches. 3. **Mass (Repetitive) Production** — A high-volume, low-variety process on a *product layout* (assembly line). Example: a Maruti Suzuki plant producing 1,200 cars per day. Cycle time is dictated by the bottleneck workstation. 4. **Continuous Production** — A very-high-volume, zero-variety process producing a *homogeneous stream* (liquid/gas/powder) 24/7. Example: BPCL's Kochi Refinery processing 15.5 million tonnes of crude annually. *(Award 7 marks: ~1.5 marks per process + 0.5 mark for example.)* --- #### Part (b) — Discuss the volume–variety relationship in detail. **[7 marks]** **Solution:** The **Volume-Variety relationship** is the foundational principle governing process selection. **Statement of the Relationship:** As the **volume of production increases**, the **variety of products that can be economically produced decreases**, and vice versa. This inverse relationship is the central trade-off in process choice. **Mathematical Intuition:** $$V_{\text{volume}} \propto \frac{1}{V_{\text{variety}}}$$ **The Underlying Economic Logic:** - **Low volume + High variety (Job Shop):** Fixed costs (machinery) are *low*, but variable costs (skilled labour, setup, idle time) are *high per unit*. → Suitable for custom orders. - **High volume + Low variety (Mass/Continuous):** Fixed costs (specialised machinery, automation) are *very high*, but variable costs per unit are *very low*. → Economies of scale drive unit cost down as the formula below shows. $$ C_{\text{unit}} = \frac{C_{\text{fixed}}}{N} + C_{\text{variable}} $$ - As $N \uparrow$ (volume increases), $\dfrac{C_{\text{fixed}}}{N} \downarrow$ and variety must $\downarrow$ because specialised machines cannot make many different products. - As $N \downarrow$ (volume decreases), variety must $\uparrow$ to attract enough customers to recover fixed costs. **Strategic Implication:** The **process choice** is essentially a **decision on the optimal point** on the volume-variety curve for a given market demand. **Diagrammatic Representation (Volume-Variety Curve):** ``` Variety ▲ High │ ●Job Shop │ \ │ \ │ ●Batch │ \ │ ●Mass │ \ │ ●Continuous Low │___________________________▶ Low Volume High ``` **Conclusion:** The volume-variety relationship is the *most important determinant* of process selection; ignoring it leads to inefficiencies such as *over-capitalization* (using a continuous plant for low-volume demand) or *over-flexibility* (using a job shop for high-volume standardized products). *(Award 7 marks: 2 marks statement + 2 marks economic logic + 2 marks curve/figure + 1 mark conclusion.)* --- ### **Question B. [Alternative Choice – KTU 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]** > **"Compare Job Shop, Batch, Mass, and Continuous production processes across any seven relevant parameters. Which process type is most suitable for an automobile assembly plant producing 1,500 cars per day, and why?"** #### Part (a) — Compare the four process types across seven parameters. **[7 marks]** **Solution:** | # | Parameter | Job Shop | Batch | Mass (Repetitive) | Continuous | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Volume** | Very Low (1–100/yr) | Low–Medium (100–10k) | High (10k–millions) | Very High (millions+) | | 2 | **Variety** | Very High (custom) | Medium (family) | Low (few variants) | Nil (single grade) | | 3 | **Layout** | Functional / Process | Cellular | Product / Line | Fixed pipeline | | 4 | **Capital Investment** | Very Low | Low–Medium | High | Very High | | 5 | **Labour Skill** | Highly Skilled | Semi-skilled | Low-skilled | Supervisory / Automated | | 6 | **WIP Inventory** | Very High | Medium | Low | Negligible | | 7 | **Lead Time** | Very Long | Medium | Short | Shortest | | 8 | **Flexibility** | Very High | High | Low | Very Low | *(Award 7 marks: 1 mark per parameter × 7 = 7.)* --- #### Part (b) — Justify the process choice for an automobile assembly plant producing 1,500 cars/day. **[7 marks]** **Solution:** **Selected Process: Mass (Repetitive) Production — Assembly Line.** **Justification (with quantitative reasoning):** **Step 1: Compute the annual volume.** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{\text{annual}} &= 1{,}500 \text{ cars/day} \times 300 \text{ working days/year} \\ &= 450{,}000 \text{ cars/year} \end{aligned} $$ This is **very high volume**, clearly eliminating Job Shop and Batch. **Step 2: Compute the cycle time (assuming 2 shifts of 8 hours each = 16 hours/day).** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{available}} &= 16 \text{ hours/day} \times 60 \text{ min/hour} = 960 \text{ min/day} \\ CT &= \frac{960 \text{ min/day}}{1{,}500 \text{ cars/day}} = 0.64 \text{ min/car} = 38.4 \text{ sec/car} \end{aligned} $$ A 38-second-per-car cycle time is *achievable only* on a **highly automated, well-balanced assembly line** — confirming **Mass Production**. **Step 3: Verify against Continuous Process (why not continuous?).** - A continuous process is suitable for **homogeneous streams** (oil, chemicals, cement). - An automobile is a **discrete, complex assembly of thousands of parts** — it cannot flow as a homogeneous stream. - Therefore, the **product nature** (discrete vs. continuous) eliminates the continuous option. **Step 4: Confirm Mass Production characteristics.** - **Standardized product** (few variants — same model on the line) → matches Mass. - **High capital investment** in robotic welders, paint shops, conveyor lines → matches Mass. - **Low labour skill** required (each station does a repetitive task) → matches Mass. - **Product Layout** — stations in sequence of operations → matches Mass. - **Fixed sequence** of operations — body → paint → assembly → testing → matches Mass. **Step 5: Real-world validation.** The Maruti Suzuki plant in Gurugram, Hyundai in Sriperumbudur, and Tata Motors in Pune all use **Mass Production assembly lines** to produce 1,000–3,000 cars per day — empirically validating the choice. **Conclusion:** For 1,500 cars per day of a standardized model, **Mass (Repetitive) Production on a Product Layout** is the only economically and operationally viable process. *(Award 7 marks: 2 marks selection + 1 mark calculation + 2 marks disqualification of alternatives + 2 marks real-world validation.)* --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Process-Type Questions:** > 1. **Stating the process type without justification** — KTU 2024 examiners deduct up to **3 of 7 marks** if a student simply writes "Mass Production" without showing *why* via volume, variety, capital, and product nature. > 2. **Confusing "Mass" with "Continuous"** — A car assembly line is **Mass Production**, not Continuous. Continuous applies only to liquids/gases/stream products. This is the **#1 conceptual error** in KTU answer sheets. > 3. **Confusing "Batch" with "Job Shop"** — A batch has a *defined lot size*; a job shop produces *one unique unit at a time*. If a student mixes these up, 2–3 marks are lost. > 4. **Forgetting to mention examples** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates that definitions must be **illustrated with at least one industry example** (Kerala-based or otherwise) to score full marks. > 5. **Ignoring the volume-variety curve in part (b) questions** — Drawing the curve (or describing it qualitatively) is worth **2 of 7 marks** in any "discuss" sub-question. > 6. **Mismatched units in calculations** — When computing cycle time, ensure time units are consistent (e.g., convert hours to minutes *before* dividing). --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ The **four process types** are Job Shop, Batch, Mass (Repetitive), and Continuous. - ✅ They form a **continuum** based on the **Volume–Variety trade-off**: as volume ↑, variety ↓. - ✅ **Job Shop** = low volume, high variety, custom, functional layout, skilled labour. - ✅ **Batch** = medium volume, medium variety, cellular layout, changeovers required. - ✅ **Mass** = high volume, low variety, **product layout (assembly line)**, cycle time governed by bottleneck. - ✅ **Continuous** = very high volume, **zero variety (homogeneous stream)**, 24/7, highest capital, fully automated. - ✅ **Cycle Time formula:** $CT = T_{\text{available}} / D_{\text{demand}}$ — used for Mass Production. - ✅ **Plant Availability formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ — used for Continuous Production. - ✅ **Unit Cost Behaviour:** As $N \uparrow$, $C_{\text{unit}} \downarrow$ — drives economies of scale in Mass and Continuous. - ✅ **Process choice is a function of:** Volume, Variety, Capital, Skill, Flexibility (5-variable rule). - ✅ **Process life-cycle matches product life-cycle:** Job Shop (Introduction) → Batch (Growth) → Mass (Maturity) → Batch/Job Shop (Decline). - ✅ **Always justify your answer** with at least 3 of the 5 selection factors in KTU exams. - ✅ **Kerala-based examples to remember:** - BPCL Kochi Refinery → Continuous - Athani Furniture Cluster → Job Shop - Thrissur Bakeries → Batch - Maruti Udyog (Gurugram) → Mass - ✅ **Disqualification logic:** Use it to *eliminate* wrong choices (e.g., eliminate Continuous for discrete products). - ✅ **The "kitchen analogy"** is a great way to begin a 7-mark answer — it shows conceptual clarity and scores the opening mark.

topics.title: Product-process design matrix and services design matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Product-process design matrix and services design matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Product-Process Matrix (Hayes-Wheelwright Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Product-Process Matrix** is a strategic framework developed by **Robert H. Hayes and Steven C. Wheelwright (1979)** that maps a company's **product life cycle stage** (measured by volume and variety) against the most appropriate **process life cycle stage** (the type of production system employed). It guides managers in aligning manufacturing process capabilities with the evolving market demand for a product.

The matrix has **two axes**:
- **Horizontal Axis (Product Life Cycle):** Stages move from low-volume, high-variety (left) to high-volume, standardized (right) — namely **Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline**.
- **Vertical Axis (Process Life Cycle):** Stages of process sophistication move from flexible/less structured (bottom) to rigid/highly structured (top) — namely **Job Shop → Batch → Assembly Line → Continuous Flow**.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

Imagine a **tailor shop on one end** and a **T-shirt factory on the other**:
- The tailor shop makes one custom suit at a time — high variety, low volume. That is a **Job Shop**.
- The T-shirt factory produces 10,000 identical shirts per day — low variety, massive volume. That is a **Continuous Flow**.
- In between, there are production environments that handle batches or assembly lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key Insight:** As a product becomes more successful and moves into maturity, its volume rises and variety drops. The process that makes it must also evolve. Misalignment (e.g., a job shop producing mass-market goods) results in **inefficiency, high cost, and lost competitiveness**.

### 1.2 Services Design Matrix (Schmenner's Service Process Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **Services Design Matrix**, developed by **Roger W. Schmenner (1986)**, is a 2×2 strategic framework for service operations that classifies services based on two key dimensions: the **degree of labor intensity** and the **degree of customer interaction and customization**.

The two axes are:
- **Horizontal Axis:** Degree of **customer interaction and customization** (Low → High)
- **Vertical Axis:** Degree of **labor intensity** (Low → High)

It yields **four service process categories**:
1. **Service Factory** (Low labor intensity, Low customization)
2. **Mass Service** (Low labor intensity, High customization)
3. **Professional Service** (High labor intensity, High customization)
4. **Service Shop** (High labor intensity, Low customization)

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🏥

Think of four types of service experiences:
- **Airlines / Fast-food chains** — machines, automation, low staff involvement per customer → **Service Factory**.
- **Retail Banks / Schools** — standardized processes but lots of customer contact → **Mass Service**.
- **Consulting firms / Law firms** — highly skilled professionals, deeply customized → **Professional Service**.
- **Hospitals / Auto repair shops** — skilled labor, but services are somewhat standardized once a diagnosis is made → **Service Shop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Use:** Firms use this matrix to decide whether to **industrialize** a service (move toward Service Factory) or **differentiate** it (move toward Professional Service). For example, McDonald's industrialized fast food; boutique consultancies differentiate.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

| Metric | Standard Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Product life cycle stages | **4** (Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline) |
| Process life cycle stages | **4** (Job Shop, Batch, Assembly Line, Continuous Flow) |
| Service matrix quadrants | **4** (Service Factory, Mass Service, Service Shop, Professional Service) |
| Hayes-Wheelwright publication year | **1979** |
| Schmenner publication year | **1986** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Product-Process Matrix — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

The Hayes-Wheelwright matrix is structured as a **2×4 (or 4×2) grid**. The diagonal cells represent **"natural fit"** between product and process — this is where competitive advantage is maximized.

#### The Four Process Types (Rows / Columns of the Matrix):

**1. Job Shop**
- **Volume:** Very Low (often one-of-a-kind)
- **Variety:** Very High
- **Process Layout:** Functional (grouped by type of equipment)
- **Examples:** Custom furniture, prototype machining, ship repair
- **Skill Level:** Highly skilled workers, flexible routing
- **Cost:** High per unit, low fixed investment

**2. Batch**
- **Volume:** Low to Moderate
- **Variety:** Moderate (families of similar products)
- **Process Layout:** Functional or cellular
- **Examples:** Bakery (rotating batches), printing press, machine tool jobbing
- **Skill Level:** Moderate
- **Cost:** Moderate per unit

**3. Assembly Line (Repetitive Manufacturing)**
- **Volume:** High
- **Variety:** Low (few standard models with options)
- **Process Layout:** Product layout (line)
- **Examples:** Automobile assembly, consumer electronics assembly
- **Skill Level:** Low to moderate, task-specialized
- **Cost:** Low per unit, high fixed investment

**4. Continuous Flow**
- **Volume:** Very High
- **Variety:** Very Low (standardized commodity)
- **Process Layout:** Fully product-aligned, automated
- **Examples:** Petroleum refining, chemicals, steel rolling mills, electricity generation
- **Skill Level:** Highly automated, monitoring by technicians
- **Cost:** Lowest per unit, highest fixed investment

#### The "Why" Behind the Matrix — Hayes & Wheelwright's Logic

- **Early product life cycle (Introduction/Growth):** Markets are small, demand is uncertain, design changes are frequent → The process must be **flexible**, even if inefficient. A **job shop** or **batch process** fits best.
- **Mature product life cycle:** Demand is large and stable, product is standardized → The process must be **efficient and capital-intensive**. An **assembly line** or **continuous flow** fits best.
- **Decline phase:** Volumes may rise even further (commoditization) or collapse — firms must decide whether to **harvest** (continuous flow) or **exit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Implication:** A firm that builds a continuous-flow plant for a product still in its introduction phase will be unable to handle design changes. Conversely, a firm that retains a job shop for a mature commodity product will be **wiped out by lower-cost competitors**.

#### Areas of the Matrix (Cells):

| Cell | Description |
|---|---|
| **On-diagonal (NW → SE)** | Naturally fit. **Competitive strength.** |
| **Above the diagonal (Process too advanced)** | Over-invested in capacity; process is too rigid for product's flexibility needs. |
| **Below the diagonal (Process under-developed)** | Process is too flexible/inefficient for the product's volume demands. |

---

### 2.2 Services Design Matrix — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

Schmenner adapted the Hayes-Wheelwright logic to services. Services differ from manufacturing in two key ways:
1. **Intangibility** — services cannot be inventoried.
2. **Customer involvement** — the customer is often part of the process.

Schmenner argued that two dimensions are most useful for service classification:

#### The Two Dimensions Explained:

**Dimension 1: Degree of Labor Intensity**
$$L_I = \frac{\text{Labor Cost}}{\text{Total Operating Cost}}$$

- **High Labor Intensity:** Human judgment and skills dominate (hospitals, law firms).
- **Low Labor Intensity:** Capital/equipment dominates (airlines, ATM networks).

**Dimension 2: Degree of Customer Interaction & Customization**
$$C_I = \frac{\text{Time spent customizing service}}{\text{Total service time}}$$

- **High Customization:** Each customer's service is unique (surgery, consulting).
- **Low Customization:** Service is standardized (mass transit, fast food).

#### The Four Quadrants:

**1. Service Factory** (Low Labor Intensity + Low Customization)
- Highly capitalized, standardized, low customization
- Customers have little interaction with operations
- **Examples:** Airlines, hotels, fast-food chains (McDonald's), cargo firms
- **Strategy:** Volume, efficiency, cost leadership

**2. Mass Service** (Low Labor Intensity + High Customization)
- High customer contact, but processes are labor-light
- Often large workforces but with low individual skill levels
- **Examples:** Retail banking branches, public schools, wholesale/retail trade
- **Strategy:** High volume + tailored offerings

**3. Professional Service** (High Labor Intensity + High Customization)
- Highly skilled professionals delivering customized services
- **Examples:** Law firms, management consultancies, doctors' practices, accounting firms
- **Strategy:** Customization, expertise, premium pricing

**4. Service Shop** (High Labor Intensity + Low Customization)
- Skilled workers providing relatively standardized services
- **Examples:** Hospitals, auto repair shops, restaurants, maintenance/repair
- **Strategy:** Skilled labor + moderate volume

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The Hayes-Wheelwright and Schmenner matrices are **conceptual frameworks**, not computational models. The "formulas" below are the qualitative relationships you must memorize for KTU exams.

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Form | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Labor Intensity | $L_I = \frac{\text{Labor Cost}}{\text{Total Cost}}$ | Classifying service processes |
| Customization Index | $C_I = \frac{\text{Customization Time}}{\text{Total Service Time}}$ | Classifying service processes |
| Process-Product Fit | On-diagonal = **Optimum** | Strategic alignment test |
| Volume-Variety Tradeoff | $V \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Variety} \downarrow$ | Hayes-Wheelwright axis logic |
| Diagonal Compliance | **Process sophistication should match product maturity** | Strategic principle |
| Off-diagonal Penalty | Above diagonal → **Inflexibility**; Below diagonal → **Inefficiency** | Diagnostic of misalignment |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Plant Location & Layout Design:** Engineers use the Hayes-Wheelwright matrix to choose between **functional layout** (job shop) and **product layout** (assembly line) when designing factories.
- **Capacity Planning:** Helps decide whether to invest in **flexible manufacturing systems (FMS)** or dedicated automation.
- **Service Operations:** Hospitals apply the Schmenner matrix to balance **specialist staff** (professional service) with **standardized wards** (service shop).
- **Banking & IT Services:** Retail banks use the matrix to segment offerings — ATMs (service factory) vs. wealth management (professional service).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation of the Product-Process Alignment Logic

Although the Hayes-Wheelwright matrix is qualitative, the **alignment logic** can be expressed through a step-by-step analytical argument.

#### Step 1 — Define Product Life Cycle Stage (PLC)

A product's life cycle position determines its expected demand profile:

$$
PLC = f(V, \sigma_V, D)
$$

Where:
- $V$ = expected unit volume
- $\sigma_V$ = variability in demand
- $D$ = degree of design standardization

**Logic:**
- Introduction → $V$ small, $\sigma_V$ high, $D$ low (frequent design changes)
- Maturity → $V$ large, $\sigma_V$ low, $D$ high (stable design)

#### Step 2 — Define Process Capability Profile (PCP)

A process has a capability vector:

$$
PCP = (X, F, U, C)
$$

Where:
- $X$ = throughput capacity (units/time)
- $F$ = flexibility (ability to switch product types)
- $U$ = utilization efficiency at steady state
- $C$ = capital investment required

**Logic:**
- Job Shop: $F$ very high, $X$ low, $C$ low, $U$ low
- Continuous Flow: $F$ very low, $X$ very high, $C$ very high, $U$ very high

#### Step 3 — Define Misalignment Cost Function

When process capability does not match product requirements, a **misalignment penalty** is incurred:

$$
\text{Penalty} = \alpha \cdot \vert X - X_{req} \vert + \beta \cdot \vert F - F_{req} \vert + \gamma \cdot \vert C - C_{budget} \vert
$$

Where:
- $X_{req}$ = required throughput for the product
- $F_{req}$ = required flexibility for design changes
- $C_{budget}$ = available capital
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = weighting coefficients (firm-specific)

**Logic Step-by-Step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3a: Identify } X_{req} \text{ from product volume } V. \\
X_{req} = k_1 \cdot V \quad \text{where } k_1 = \text{capacity safety factor} \\
\\
\text{Step 3b: Identify } F_{req} \text{ from product design change frequency } D. \\
F_{req} = k_2 \cdot D \quad \text{where } k_2 = \text{flexibility scaling constant} \\
\\
\text{Step 3c: Compute Penalty.} \\
\text{Penalty} = \alpha \cdot \vert X - k_1 V \vert + \beta \cdot \vert F - k_2 D \vert + \gamma \cdot \vert C - C_{budget} \vert \\
\\
\text{Step 3d: Optimal process choice minimizes Penalty.} \\
P_{opt} = \arg\min_{P \in \{\text{Job, Batch, Line, Flow}\}} \text{Penalty}(P)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Diagonal Mapping Justification

The diagonal cells of the matrix represent the process $P$ that simultaneously satisfies:
- $X \approx k_1 V$ (capacity matches demand)
- $F \approx k_2 D$ (flexibility matches design change rate)
- $C \leq C_{budget}$ (capital is within budget)

For a **mature, high-volume product**: $V$ is large → $X$ must be large → **Assembly Line / Continuous Flow** wins.
For an **early-stage, low-volume product**: $V$ is small, $D$ is large → $F$ must be high → **Job Shop** wins.

Hence the **diagonal relationship** emerges from the optimization in Step 3.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Mapping for the Services Matrix

The Schmenner matrix follows a similar two-dimensional classification:

$$
\text{Service Type} = g(L_I, C_I)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Service Factory} &: L_I \leq 0.3,\ C_I \leq 0.3 \\
\text{Mass Service} &: L_I \leq 0.3,\ C_I > 0.3 \\
\text{Service Shop} &: L_I > 0.3,\ C_I \leq 0.3 \\
\text{Professional Service} &: L_I > 0.3,\ C_I > 0.3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The thresholds $0.3$ are **illustrative**. In practice, firms use industry-specific benchmarks. KTU exams typically test the **concept**, not numeric thresholds.

#### Strategic Movement on the Service Matrix

Firms can deliberately move across quadrants:

- **McDonald's** industrialized a restaurant business — moved from **Service Shop** to **Service Factory** by standardizing menus and using equipment.
- **Luxury hotels** often move the opposite way — differentiating from **Service Factory** (chain hotel) to **Mass Service** or **Professional Service** (boutique, concierge-driven).

$$
\text{Service Strategy} \in \{\text{Industrialize},\ \text{Differentiate},\ \text{Maintain}\}
$$

$$
\text{Industrialize} = \text{move toward Service Factory} \quad (\downarrow L_I,\ \downarrow C_I)
$$

$$
\text{Differentiate} = \text{move toward Professional Service} \quad (\uparrow L_I,\ \uparrow C_I)
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (Conceptual, for KTU Exam Practice)

**Scenario:** A company has developed a new electric vehicle battery technology. Currently, the product is in the **introduction stage**, with low demand (200 units/month) and frequent design modifications every 2 months. The company has ₹5 crore capital available.

**Solution Using the Hayes-Wheelwright Matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } V &= 200\ \text{units/month (low volume)} \\
\text{Step 2: } D &= \text{High (frequent design changes)} \\
\text{Step 3: } F_{req} &= k_2 \cdot D \Rightarrow \text{High} \\
\text{Step 4: } X_{req} &= k_1 \cdot 200 \Rightarrow \text{Low} \\
\\
\text{Decision: A \textbf{Job Shop} or small \textbf{Batch} process is optimal.} \\
\\
\text{Why not Assembly Line?} \\
&\text{An assembly line has } F \text{ very low, which conflicts with the high } F_{req}. \\
&\text{Also, capital cost } C \text{ of an assembly line exceeds } C_{budget} = \text{₹5 crore}.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **As the product matures** and volumes rise to 20,000 units/month with stable design, the firm should **migrate along the diagonal** toward an **Assembly Line** or even **Continuous Flow** process for battery cells.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Summary Table (Product vs Service Matrix)

| Aspect | Hayes-Wheelwright (Product) | Schmenner (Service) |
|---|---|---|
| **Originator** | Hayes & Wheelwright (1979) | Schmenner (1986) |
| **Axes** | Product LC × Process LC | Labor Intensity × Customization |
| **Number of Categories** | 4 (Job, Batch, Line, Flow) | 4 (Factory, Mass, Shop, Professional) |
| **Strategic Decision** | Process selection/evolution | Service positioning |
| **Off-diagonal Implication** | Misalignment = inefficiency/inflexibility | Mispositioned service = poor margins |
| **Movement Direction** | Along diagonal as product matures | Industrialize or Differentiate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph axisTop["Product Life Cycle Stage (Horizontal)"]
        A1["INTRODUCTION\nLow Volume\nHigh Variety"] --> A2["GROWTH\nRising Volume\nMany Variants"]
        A2 --> A3["MATURITY\nHigh Volume\nFew Variants"]
        A3 --> A4["DECLINE\nVery High Volume\nStandardized"]
    end

    subgraph matrix["HAYES-WHEELWRIGHT MATRIX 2x4"]
        direction TB
        R1["CONTINUOUS FLOW\nCapital: Very High\nFlexibility: Very Low\nExample: Oil refinery"] 
        R2["ASSEMBLY LINE\nCapital: High\nFlexibility: Low\nExample: Car assembly"]
        R3["BATCH\nCapital: Moderate\nFlexibility: Moderate\nExample: Bakery, Printing"]
        R4["JOB SHOP\nCapital: Low\nFlexibility: Very High\nExample: Custom machining"]
    end

    R1 --- A4
    R2 --- A3
    R3 --- A2
    R4 --- A1

    diag["DIAGONAL = NATURAL FIT\nOff-diagonal = MISALIGNMENT"]:::highlight
    matrix -.- diag

    classDef highlight fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px;
```

### 4.2 Schmenner Services Design Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph xAxis["Customer Interaction / Customization"]
        X1["LOW"] --> X2["HIGH"]
    end

    subgraph yAxis["Labor Intensity"]
        Y1["HIGH"]
        Y2["LOW"]
    end

    Q1["SERVICE SHOP\nHigh Labor + Low Customization\nExamples: Hospital, Auto Repair"]
    Q2["PROFESSIONAL SERVICE\nHigh Labor + High Customization\nExamples: Law firm, Surgeon"]
    Q3["MASS SERVICE\nLow Labor + High Customization\nExamples: Retail bank, School"]
    Q4["SERVICE FACTORY\nLow Labor + Low Customization\nExamples: Airline, Fast food"]

    Y2 --- Q3
    Y2 --- Q4
    Y1 --- Q1
    Y1 --- Q2

    X1 --- Q4
    X1 --- Q1
    X2 --- Q3
    X2 --- Q2

    note["STRATEGIC MOVEMENT:\nIndustrialize = Move toward Service Factory\nDifferentiate = Move toward Professional Service"]:::note
    Q4 -.- note

    classDef note fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px;
```

### 4.3 Evolution Path Along the Product-Process Diagonal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["STAGE 1\nIntroduction\nJob Shop\nLow Vol, High Var"] --> P2["STAGE 2\nGrowth\nBatch Process\nMid Vol, Mid Var"]
    P2 --> P3["STAGE 3\nMaturity\nAssembly Line\nHigh Vol, Low Var"]
    P3 --> P4["STAGE 4\nDecline / Commodity\nContinuous Flow\nVery High Vol, Std"]

    P1 -.Misalignment.-> P3
    P4 -.Inflexible.-> P1

    note["Dashed arrows indicate misaligned moves\nthat cause inefficiency or inflexibility"]:::warning
    P3 -.- note
    P1 -.- note

    classDef warning fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** Always label the **axes** and the **off-diagonal penalty** clearly. Examiners reward explicit reference to the **diagonal = fit principle**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the Product-Process Matrix. Who developed it?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Product-Process Matrix** is a strategic framework developed by **Robert H. Hayes and Steven C. Wheelwright in 1979**. It maps a product's life cycle stage (introduction, growth, maturity, decline) against the most suitable process type (job shop, batch, assembly line, continuous flow). The matrix guides managers in aligning manufacturing flexibility with product volume and variety to maximize competitive advantage. The **diagonal of the matrix represents natural fit** between product and process, while off-diagonal placements cause inefficiency or inflexibility. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023] List the four quadrants of Schmenner's Service Process Matrix with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Schmenner's (1986) Service Process Matrix classifies services along two axes — **labor intensity** and **degree of customer interaction/customization**. The four quadrants are:

1. **Service Factory** — Low labor intensity, low customization. *Example: Airline catering, fast food.*
2. **Mass Service** — Low labor intensity, high customization. *Example: Retail banking, schools.*
3. **Service Shop** — High labor intensity, low customization. *Example: Hospitals, auto repair.*
4. **Professional Service** — High labor intensity, high customization. *Example: Law firm, consulting.*

**(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Choice)

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix** in detail. Discuss the four process types and their characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical company is launching a new drug. Currently, the product is in the **introduction stage** with very low volumes and frequent formulation changes. The company has limited capital. Recommend an appropriate process type using the Product-Process Matrix and justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix (7 marks):**

The Hayes-Wheelwright Matrix, proposed in 1979, is a 2-dimensional strategic tool used in operations management. The **horizontal axis** represents the **product life cycle** stages (Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline), while the **vertical axis** represents the **process life cycle** stages (Job Shop → Batch → Assembly Line → Continuous Flow).

**Four Process Types:**

1. **Job Shop:** Very low volume, very high variety. Functional layout. Highly skilled workers. Examples: custom machining, prototype building. *Capital: Low; Flexibility: Very High.* **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Batch:** Low to moderate volume, moderate variety. Grouped by product family. Examples: bakery, printing press. *Capital: Moderate; Flexibility: Moderate.* **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Assembly Line:** High volume, low variety. Product layout. Examples: automobile assembly. *Capital: High; Flexibility: Low.* **(1.5 marks)**
4. **Continuous Flow:** Very high volume, very low variety. Fully automated. Examples: oil refinery, chemicals. *Capital: Very High; Flexibility: Very Low.* **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Diagonal Fit Principle:** Cells along the diagonal represent natural fit. Off-diagonal placements (e.g., job shop for a mature commodity) cause inefficiency. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Recommendation for the Pharmaceutical Company (7 marks):**

- **Product Stage:** Introduction → Low volume, high design change frequency, low standardization. **(1 mark)**
- **Volume:** Small initial batches for clinical trials and limited market launch. **(1 mark)**
- **Design Variability:** Formulation changes are frequent as the drug undergoes iteration. **(1 mark)**
- **Process Choice:** **Job Shop or small-scale Batch process.** **(1 mark)**
- **Justification (using matrix logic):**
  - A **job shop** offers very high flexibility to handle frequent formulation changes. **(1 mark)**
  - Capital requirement is low, fitting the company's limited capital. **(1 mark)**
  - An **assembly line** or **continuous flow** would be over-invested, rigid, and capital-intensive — inappropriate at the introduction stage. **(1 mark)**
- **Future Strategy:** As the drug matures and demand rises, the firm should migrate along the diagonal to a **batch** or even **continuous flow** process. **(Bonus 1 mark if mentioned)**

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain **Schmenner's Services Design Matrix**. Discuss the four service categories with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A large hospital chain is facing long patient waiting times and inconsistent service quality. Using Schmenner's matrix, suggest **two strategic moves** the hospital can make to improve service delivery. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Schmenner's Services Design Matrix (7 marks):**

Schmenner's (1986) Service Process Matrix classifies services along two dimensions — **labor intensity** (high/low) and **degree of customer interaction/customization** (high/low). This 2×2 grid produces four categories. **(1 mark)**

**Four Service Categories:**

1. **Service Factory (Low Labor, Low Customization):** Capital-intensive, standardized. Low customer interaction. Examples: airlines, fast-food chains, hotel chains. *Strategy: Volume and cost efficiency.* **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Mass Service (Low Labor, High Customization):** High customer contact with relatively low skill intensity per worker. Examples: retail banks, schools, retail trade. *Strategy: Volume + customization.* **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Service Shop (High Labor, Low Customization):** Skilled workers delivering standardized services. Examples: hospitals, auto repair shops, restaurants. *Strategy: Skilled labor + moderate volume.* **(1.5 marks)**
4. **Professional Service (High Labor, High Customization):** Highly skilled professionals providing unique solutions. Examples: law firms, consultancies, specialist surgeons. *Strategy: Premium, expertise-led.* **(1.5 marks)**

**(b) Strategic Moves for the Hospital Chain (7 marks):**

A typical large hospital is a **Service Shop** — high labor intensity (doctors, nurses), relatively standardized procedures once diagnosis is made.

**Strategic Move 1 — Industrialize (move toward Service Factory):** **(3.5 marks)**
- Standardize protocols for common treatments (e.g., cataract surgery, dialysis).
- Invest in **medical equipment automation** (e.g., automated dispensing, diagnostic AI).
- Use **triage algorithms** and **pre-admission testing** to reduce waiting.
- This **reduces labor intensity per case** and **standardizes** routine services.

**Strategic Move 2 — Differentiate (move toward Professional Service) for Complex Cases:** **(3.5 marks)**
- For complex surgeries and rare diseases, retain a **professional service** model — senior consultants, case-by-case decisions.
- Offer **concierge/premium services** for high-paying patients.
- This **justifies premium pricing** and improves outcomes for complex cases.

> By selectively industrializing routine care and differentiating specialty care, the hospital **reduces wait times** and **improves quality** — a classic Schmenner-style segmentation strategy.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the diagonal principle:** Many students describe the four process types but **fail to state explicitly that diagonal cells = natural fit**. This is a **mandatory 2-mark component** in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Confusing product and service matrices:** Hayes-Wheelwright is for **manufacturing**; Schmenner is for **services**. Mixing them up is a **fatal error**.
> 3. **Mislabelling Schmenner quadrants:** A common mistake is placing "Hospitals" under Professional Service. Hospitals are **Service Shops** (high labor, low customization) — not Professional Services. Doctors' private practices are Professional Service.
> 4. **Not justifying the recommendation:** In scenario-based questions, simply naming the process type without linking to **volume, variety, capital, and flexibility** loses 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Omitting the year/originator:** KTU examiners often award **1 mark** for correctly citing Hayes & Wheelwright (1979) or Schmenner (1986).
> 6. **Skipping the migration/diagonal movement logic:** Always mention that firms must **evolve along the diagonal** as the product/service matures.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Hayes-Wheelwright Matrix (1979):** Aligns **product life cycle** with **process life cycle**; **4 process types** — Job Shop, Batch, Assembly Line, Continuous Flow.
- ✅ **Diagonal = Natural Fit:** On-diagonal cells = competitive strength; off-diagonal = misaligned (inefficiency or inflexibility).
- ✅ **Volume-Variety Tradeoff:** As products mature, **volume ↑** and **variety ↓**; the process must evolve accordingly.
- ✅ **Schmenner's Service Matrix (1986):** Two axes — **labor intensity** and **customer interaction/customization**; **4 quadrants**.
- ✅ **Four Service Quadrants:** Service Factory, Mass Service, Service Shop, Professional Service.
- ✅ **Strategic Service Moves:** **Industrialize** (toward Service Factory) or **Differentiate** (toward Professional Service).
- ✅ **Key Real-World Examples:** Airlines → Service Factory; Banks → Mass Service; Hospitals → Service Shop; Law Firms → Professional Service.
- ✅ **Migration Principle:** Firms should move **along the diagonal** as their product/service matures; staying put causes strategic drift.
- ✅ **Originator & Year** is a guaranteed **1-mark** question — memorize it.
- ✅ **Volume ↔ Flexibility Inverse Relationship:** Higher-volume processes (Continuous Flow) are **less flexible**; lower-volume processes (Job Shop) are **more flexible**.
- ✅ **Capital-Intensity Progression:** Job Shop < Batch < Assembly Line < Continuous Flow (in capital investment per unit capacity).
- ✅ **Cross-Domain Tip:** If the question gives a **service example** (bank, hospital, airline), use Schmenner. If it gives a **manufacturing example** (car, chemical, machine), use Hayes-Wheelwright.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **(1) Definition + Originator + Year (1 mark), (2) Diagram/Description of Matrix (3 marks), (3) Explanation of Each Quadrant/Category (4 marks), (4) Application/Recommendation with Justification (4 marks), (5) Strategic Implication/Migration Logic (2 marks).** This covers all KTU 2024 marking expectations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology issues in process design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Technology issues in process design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Operations Management** and **Process Design**, **Technology Issues** refer to the strategic, tactical, and operational considerations surrounding the selection, acquisition, integration, and management of process technologies that transform inputs (raw materials, information, labor) into outputs (goods and services). As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, technology issues in process design encompass **technology selection, technology forecasting, technology transfer, automation strategy, process capability, and the alignment of technology with product design, volume, and variability requirements**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Technology issues in process design involve the systematic evaluation of process technology alternatives, the forecasting of emerging technologies, the methods of acquiring new technology (in-house development vs. licensing vs. purchase), and the degree of automation appropriate to the organization's product-market position. The objective is to ensure that the chosen technology supports the firm's competitive priorities — cost, quality, flexibility, and speed.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are opening a small restaurant in Kerala serving *Appam and Stew*. - **Low Volume, High Variety** (e.g., a homestyle kitchen) → You use a **traditional clay oven (chulha)** and manual preparation. This is **labor-intensive, low-technology, flexible process design**. - **High Volume, Low Variety** (e.g., a chain of 50 restaurants) → You invest in **automated appam-making machines, standardized pre-mixed batter dispensers, and centralized bulk cooking units**. This is a **capital-intensive, high-technology, standardized process design**. The decision of *which oven to buy*, *how much to automate*, *whether to develop the recipe in-house or buy a franchise formula* — these are all **Technology Issues in Process Design**. The same logic applies to a manufacturing firm choosing between manual assembly, CNC machines, robotics, or a fully automated Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS). ## 1.3 Key Dimensions of Technology Issues | Dimension | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Technology Selection** | Choosing the most appropriate process technology | CNC vs. manual lathe | | **Technology Forecasting** | Predicting future technology evolution | Trend curves, S-curve analysis | | **Technology Acquisition** | Mode of obtaining new technology | In-house R&D, licensing, joint venture | | **Automation Strategy** | Degree of mechanization and computerization | Manual → Mechanized → Automated → Integrated | | **Process Capability** | Ability of technology to meet specifications | Cpk, Six Sigma metrics | | **Technology Transfer** | Movement of tech across organizational/geographic boundaries | Parent-to-subsidiary, cross-border licensing | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric in Technology Issues:** > - **Capital Intensity** = (Investment in Equipment & Machinery) / (Number of Workers) — measured in **₹/worker** or **$/employee** > - **Process Flexibility Index (PFI)** = Number of product variants a process can produce without major retooling > - **Technology S-curve**: S-shaped diffusion model predicting adoption lifecycle --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework ### 2.1.1 Technology Selection in Process Design Technology selection is the process of identifying and choosing the **process technology** that best fits the organization's product, volume, and competitive requirements. The selection involves a structured evaluation across multiple criteria. **Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix** (1979) — a foundational KTU-recommended framework: - The **Product-Process Matrix** plots products on a spectrum from **low-volume, one-of-a-kind** (job shop) to **high-volume, standardized** (continuous flow). - The diagonal of the matrix represents the **ideal alignment** between product life-cycle stage and process type. - **Off-diagonal positions** indicate strategic vulnerability: e.g., a job shop trying to make mass-produced goods will suffer from high cost and low quality. **Key Insight for KTU:** Selecting a technology that is too advanced for the volume leads to **under-utilization and high per-unit cost**. Selecting a technology that is too primitive leads to **inability to meet quality/speed requirements**. ### 2.1.2 Technology Forecasting Technology forecasting involves predicting the **future capability, cost, and adoption rate** of process technologies. Standard KTU-mandated techniques include: 1. **Trend Extrapolation (Time-Series Forecasting):** Plotting historical performance/cost data and extrapolating into the future. 2. **S-Curve Analysis:** Most technologies follow an S-shaped diffusion curve — slow start, rapid growth, maturity, decline. 3. **Morphological Analysis:** Breaking a technology into sub-functions and exploring alternative solutions for each. 4. **Delphi Method:** Iterative expert consensus forecasting. 5. **Technology Roadmapping:** Visual timeline of technology evolution mapped against market needs. ### 2.1.3 Technology Acquisition Strategies | Acquisition Mode | Description | Risk Level | Speed | |---|---|---|---| | **Internal R&D** | Develop in-house | High | Slow | | **Purchase of Equipment** | Buy turnkey machinery | Low | Fast | | **Licensing** | Pay royalty to use others' IP | Low-Medium | Fast | | **Joint Venture / Alliance** | Co-develop with partner | Medium | Medium | | **Acquisition** | Buy the technology owner firm | High | Fast | | **Contract Research** | Outsource R&D to universities/labs | Low | Medium | ### 2.1.4 Automation Strategy Automation progresses through **four stages** (a key KTU examination concept): 1. **Manual Operations** — Human performs the task entirely. 2. **Mechanization** — Machine assists the human (e.g., power tools). 3. **Automation** — Machine performs the task with minimal human intervention (e.g., CNC, robotics). 4. **Computerization / Integration** — Information systems integrate the automated machines (e.g., CIM, Industry 4.0, IIoT). **Automation Justification** is typically evaluated using: - **Payback Period** = (Initial Investment) / (Annual Cash Inflow) - **Return on Investment (ROI)** = (Net Annual Benefit / Initial Investment) × 100% - **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** comparison across alternatives ### 2.1.5 Process Capability and Technology **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** is a standard metric linking technology to quality: $$Cpk = \min\left\{\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right\}$$ where: - $USL$ = Upper Specification Limit - $LSL$ = Lower Specification Limit - $\mu$ = Process mean - $\sigma$ = Process standard deviation A **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the KTU-standard benchmark for a **capable process**; **Cpk ≥ 1.67** indicates a **Six-Sigma-quality process**. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Model | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Capital Intensity | $CI = \dfrac{\text{Investment in Equipment}}{\text{Number of Workers}}$ | ₹/worker or $/employee | | 2 | Payback Period | $PP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Years | | 3 | ROI on Technology | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Annual Benefit}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100$ | Percentage | | 4 | Process Capability | $Cpk = \min\left\{\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right\}$ | Dimensionless (target $\geq 1.33$) | | 5 | Machine Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Maximum Possible Output}} \times 100$ | Percentage | | 6 | Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost | $EUAC = P \cdot (A/P, i, n) + A_{O\&M}$ | ₹/year | | 7 | Break-Even Volume (Automation) | $Q^* = \dfrac{F_{A} - F_{M}}{V_{M} - V_{A}}$ | Units/year | | 8 | Technology S-Curve (Logistic) | $y(t) = \dfrac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}$ | Adoption fraction | | 9 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | ₹ (currency) | | 10 | Degree of Automation | $DoA = \dfrac{\text{Number of Automated Operations}}{\text{Total Number of Operations}} \times 100$ | Percentage | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Always use **EUAC** when comparing technologies with **different useful lives**. Do NOT compare total costs directly — convert to equivalent annual cost first. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Industry (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki):** Uses Flexible Manufacturing Systems (FMS) to produce multiple car models on the same production line, balancing volume and variety. - **Electronics (e.g., Samsung, Apple Foxconn plants):** Heavy investment in **Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)** and **surface-mount technology (SMT)** lines for high-volume, high-precision assembly. - **Biotechnology / Pharma:** Strict regulatory process capability (Cpk ≥ 1.67) is mandatory — technology selection is dominated by validation and traceability needs. - **Aerospace (e.g., HAL, Boeing):** Low-volume, high-variability processes using **5-axis CNC, additive manufacturing (3D printing)** — technology selection is flexibility-driven, not cost-driven. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Break-Even Volume for Automation Decision A KTU favourite problem type. A firm must choose between a **Manual Process (M)** and an **Automated Process (A)**. **Given:** - Manual Process: Fixed Cost = $F_M$, Variable Cost per unit = $V_M$ - Automated Process: Fixed Cost = $F_A$, Variable Cost per unit = $V_A$ - Typically, $F_A > F_M$ (automation has higher capital cost) and $V_A < V_M$ (automation has lower per-unit operating cost) **Total Cost of Manual Process:** $$TC_M(Q) = F_M + V_M \cdot Q$$ **Total Cost of Automated Process:** $$TC_A(Q) = F_A + V_A \cdot Q$$ **Setting $TC_M = TC_A$ to find break-even volume $Q^*$:** $$F_M + V_M \cdot Q^* = F_A + V_A \cdot Q^*$$ **Rearranging terms (group Q terms on one side):** $$F_M - F_A = V_A \cdot Q^* - V_M \cdot Q^*$$ **Factoring out $Q^*$:** $$F_M - F_A = (V_A - V_M) \cdot Q^*$$ **Multiplying both sides by $-1$ to get positive convention:** $$F_A - F_M = (V_M - V_A) \cdot Q^*$$ **Solving for $Q^*$:** $$Q^* = \frac{F_A - F_M}{V_M - V_A}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** > - If expected annual volume $Q > Q^*$, choose **Automation** (cheaper total cost at high volume). > - If expected annual volume $Q < Q^*$, choose **Manual Process** (cheaper total cost at low volume). > - If $Q = Q^*$, both options are economically equivalent — the decision is then based on **strategic / qualitative factors** (flexibility, quality, future scalability). ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A Kerala-based cashew processing firm is evaluating two technologies: - **Option A (Manual Sorting):** $F_M = ₹2,00,000$/year, $V_M = ₹50$/kg - **Option B (Automated Optical Sorting):** $F_A = ₹10,00,000$/year, $V_A = ₹20$/kg **Step 1:** Identify the parameters $$F_A - F_M = 10,00,000 - 2,00,000 = 8,00,000$$ $$V_M - V_A = 50 - 20 = 30$$ **Step 2:** Compute the break-even volume $$Q^* = \frac{8,00,000}{30} = 26,667 \text{ kg/year}$$ **Step 3:** Decision rule - If the firm processes more than **26,667 kg/year** → choose **Automation (Option B)** - If the firm processes less than **26,667 kg/year** → choose **Manual Sorting (Option A)** **Step 4:** Verification at $Q = 40,000$ kg (above break-even) - $TC_M = 2,00,000 + 50 \times 40,000 = ₹22,00,000$ - $TC_A = 10,00,000 + 20 \times 40,000 = ₹18,00,000$ - **Saving with automation** = $₹4,00,000$/year ✓ (Automation is indeed cheaper at high volume) ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Technology Selection The following weighted scoring model is a KTU-recommended approach when **multiple qualitative and quantitative factors** must be considered: | Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Manual Score $s_M$ | Automated Score $s_A$ | |---|---|---|---| | Initial Cost | 0.20 | 9 | 4 | | Operating Cost | 0.25 | 5 | 9 | | Quality (Cpk) | 0.20 | 6 | 9 | | Flexibility | 0.15 | 9 | 6 | | Reliability | 0.10 | 7 | 8 | | Safety | 0.10 | 6 | 9 | | **Total Weighted Score** | $\sum w_i = 1.00$ | $W_M$ | $W_A$ | **Weighted Score for Manual:** $$W_M = (0.20 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 6)$$ **Step-by-step evaluation:** $$W_M = 1.80 + 1.25 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 0.70 + 0.60$$ $$W_M = 6.90$$ **Weighted Score for Automated:** $$W_A = (0.20 \times 4) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 9)$$ **Step-by-step evaluation:** $$W_A = 0.80 + 2.25 + 1.80 + 0.90 + 0.80 + 0.90$$ $$W_A = 7.45$$ **Decision:** $W_A > W_M$ → Choose **Automated Optical Sorting**. The quantitative result is consistent with the break-even analysis for high-volume operation. ## 3.4 Derivation: Net Present Value (NPV) of Technology Investment When comparing technologies with different useful lives, **NPV** is the gold standard. $$NPV = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{SV_n}{(1+r)^n}$$ where: - $I_0$ = Initial investment - $CF_t$ = Net cash flow in year $t$ - $SV_n$ = Salvage value at end of year $n$ - $r$ = Discount rate (cost of capital) - $n$ = Useful life of technology **Decision Rule:** Choose the technology with the **higher NPV** (if NPVs are positive and mutually exclusive). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Technology Decision Framework in Process Design ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Process Design Initiative] --> B{Product-Volume Profile} B -->|Low Volume, High Variety| C[Job Shop Process] B -->|Moderate Volume| D[Batched / Assembly Line] B -->|High Volume, Low Variety| E[Continuous Flow] C --> F[Technology Selection] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Evaluate Acquisition Mode] G --> G1[In-house R&D] G --> G2[Licensing] G --> G3[Equipment Purchase] G --> G4[Joint Venture] F --> H[Automation Level Decision] H --> H1[Manual] H --> H2[Mechanized] H --> H3[Automated] H --> H4[Computer Integrated] H --> I[Capability Validation] I --> J{Cpk >= 1.33?} J -->|Yes| K[Deploy Technology] J -->|No| L[Process Re-engineering] L --> I K --> M[Monitor and Improve] M --> N[Technology Forecasting Loop] N --> F ``` ## 4.2 Technology S-Curve Adoption Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Emergence Phase: Slow Growth] --> B[Growth Phase: Rapid Diffusion] B --> C[Maturity Phase: Saturation] C --> D[Decline Phase: Obsolescence] A --> A1[R&D Investment High
Few Adopters] B --> B1[Standards Emerge
Economies of Scale] C --> C1[Incremental Innovation
Market Saturation] D --> D1[Disruptive Tech Threat
Replacement Cycle] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Technology Acquisition Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Strategic Need Identified] --> P2[Feasibility Study] P2 --> P3[Technology Forecasting] P3 --> P4{Build or Buy Decision} P4 -->|Build| P5[Internal R&D Lab] P4 -->|Buy| P6[Vendor Evaluation] P5 --> P7[Prototype Testing] P6 --> P7 P7 --> P8[Piloting in Production Line] P8 --> P9{Capability Meets Specs?} P9 -->|No| P10[Re-engineer / Reject] P9 -->|Yes| P11[Full Scale Deployment] P11 --> P12[Training and Documentation] P12 --> P13[Performance Monitoring] P13 --> P14[Continuous Improvement] P14 --> P3 ``` ## 4.4 Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PM[Product Stages] P1[Low Volume One-off] P2[Low Volume Batch] P3[High Volume Few Models] P4[High Volume High Variety] P5[Mass Production] end subgraph PCM[Process Stages] Q1[Job Shop] Q2[Batched Assembly] Q3[Assembly Line] Q4[Flexible Manufacturing] Q5[Continuous Flow] end P1 -.aligned.-> Q1 P2 -.aligned.-> Q2 P3 -.aligned.-> Q3 P4 -.aligned.-> Q4 P5 -.aligned.-> Q5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagonal Alignment Rule (KTU):** A firm ideally matches product stage with the corresponding process stage. Off-diagonal mismatch (e.g., job shop attempting mass production) creates cost and quality problems. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define "Technology Issues in Process Design". Mention any four key dimensions. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 model]** **Model Answer:** Technology issues in process design refer to the set of strategic and operational decisions related to the **selection, acquisition, forecasting, and integration of process technologies** that transform inputs into outputs. Key dimensions are: 1. **Technology Selection** — choosing the right process technology for the product. 2. **Technology Forecasting** — predicting future technology evolution (S-curve, Delphi). 3. **Technology Acquisition** — mode of obtaining technology (R&D, licensing, purchase). 4. **Automation Strategy** — determining the appropriate level of mechanization and computerization. 5. **Process Capability** — ensuring the technology meets quality specifications (Cpk). ### Question 2: What is the Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix? Why is off-diagonal positioning risky? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]** **Model Answer:** The Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix is a strategic tool that maps **product life-cycle stages** (low-volume to high-volume) against **process types** (job shop to continuous flow). The **diagonal** represents the **ideal alignment** between product and process. **Off-diagonal risk:** If a firm operates a job shop trying to produce mass-market goods, it suffers from **high unit costs, quality inconsistency, and inability to scale**. Conversely, a continuous flow process making one-off customized goods results in **low capacity utilization and inflexibility**. The matrix guides firms to maintain strategic alignment. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option A **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 model]** A precision components manufacturer in Kochi is evaluating two alternative process technologies for producing a new gearbox housing: | Parameter | Technology X (CNC) | Technology Y (VMC + Robotics) | |---|---|---| | Initial Investment | ₹30,00,000 | ₹80,00,000 | | Useful Life | 6 years | 10 years | | Annual Operating Cost | ₹12,00,000 | ₹6,00,000 | | Annual Production Capacity | 20,000 units | 35,000 units | | Estimated Annual Demand | 25,000 units | 25,000 units | | Salvage Value | ₹2,00,000 | ₹5,00,000 | | Discount Rate | 10% | 10% | **Required:** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of each technology and recommend the better option. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **qualitative factors** (flexibility, quality, future scalability) that should also influence this decision. --- #### Model Solution for Question A ### Part (a) — NPV Computation **Step 1: NPV of Technology X (CNC)** $$NPV_X = -I_{0,X} + \sum_{t=1}^{6} \frac{CF_X}{(1.10)^t} + \frac{SV_X}{(1.10)^6}$$ **Annual cash flow for X** (assuming revenue offsets the question; we treat cost-savings vs. baseline as benefit): - Operating cost = ₹12,00,000/year (treated as cost, so cash flow = -₹12,00,000) - For NPV comparison, we treat each technology independently. **Using Present Worth of an Annuity Factor:** $$(P/A, 10\%, 6) = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10} = \frac{1 - 0.5645}{0.10} = 4.3553$$ $$(P/F, 10\%, 6) = (1.10)^{-6} = 0.5645$$ **Substituting values:** $$NPV_X = -30,00,000 - \left[12,00,000 \times 4.3553\right] + \left[2,00,000 \times 0.5645\right]$$ $$NPV_X = -30,00,000 - 52,26,360 + 1,12,900$$ $$NPV_X = -81,13,460$$ The negative NPV reflects that we are computing the **present worth of cost** — the more negative figure, the higher the lifetime cost. Alternatively, if we treat the avoided-baseline savings as the cash flow, the sign convention flips; the **higher NPV** is the better choice. **Step 2: NPV of Technology Y (VMC + Robotics)** $$(P/A, 10\%, 10) = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-10}}{0.10} = \frac{1 - 0.3855}{0.10} = 6.1446$$ $$(P/F, 10\%, 10) = (1.10)^{-10} = 0.3855$$ $$NPV_Y = -80,00,000 - \left[6,00,000 \times 6.1446\right] + \left[5,00,000 \times 0.3855\right]$$ $$NPV_Y = -80,00,000 - 36,86,760 + 1,92,750$$ $$NPV_Y = -1,14,94,010$$ **Step 3: Decision** Both NPVs are negative under cost-treatment; comparing **absolute cost-pw values**: - $NPV_X$ (cost) = ₹81,13,460 - $NPV_Y$ (cost) = ₹1,14,94,010 Since the question is about *which technology has lower lifetime cost*, **Technology X (CNC)** is financially better. However, we must also examine capacity: X can produce only 20,000 units/year vs. demand of 25,000 units/year → **capacity shortfall of 5,000 units/year**, which represents lost sales or additional outsourcing cost. **Refined recommendation:** Compute **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** for proper comparison. $$EUAC_X = (P/A \text{ factor})^{-1} \text{ adjustment...} \Rightarrow \text{Better computed as } A = P \times (A/P, i, n)$$ **Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, 10%, 6):** $$(A/P, 10\%, 6) = \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^6}{(1.10)^6 - 1} = \frac{0.10}{1 - 0.5645} = \frac{0.10}{0.4355} = 0.2296$$ **EUAC for X:** $$EUAC_X = (30,00,000 - 2,00,000 \times 0.5645) \times 0.2296 + 12,00,000$$ $$EUAC_X = (30,00,000 - 1,12,900) \times 0.2296 + 12,00,000$$ $$EUAC_X = 28,87,100 \times 0.2296 + 12,00,000$$ $$EUAC_X = 6,62,910 + 12,00,000 = ₹18,62,910 \text{ /year}$$ **Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, 10%, 10):** $$(A/P, 10\%, 10) = \frac{0.10}{1 - 0.3855} = \frac{0.10}{0.6145} = 0.1628$$ **EUAC for Y:** $$EUAC_Y = (80,00,000 - 5,00,000 \times 0.3855) \times 0.1628 + 6,00,000$$ $$EUAC_Y = (80,00,000 - 1,92,750) \times 0.1628 + 6,00,000$$ $$EUAC_Y = 78,07,250 \times 0.1628 + 6,00,000$$ $$EUAC_Y = 12,70,917 + 6,00,000 = ₹18,70,917 \text{ /year}$$ **Comparison:** - $EUAC_X = ₹18,62,910$/year - $EUAC_Y = ₹18,70,917$/year Difference is marginal (~₹8,000/year). **But X has 15,000 units less annual capacity than needed** — this is the deciding factor. **Technology Y is recommended** because it meets demand, despite slightly higher annual equivalent cost. ### Part (b) — Qualitative Factors 1. **Flexibility:** CNC machines can be reprogrammed quickly for new products, ideal for a manufacturer expecting **frequent design changes**. VMC + robotics has higher changeover overhead. 2. **Quality (Cpk):** Robotics offers higher repeatability and lower human error → typically higher Cpk, critical for safety-critical gearboxes. 3. **Future Scalability:** VMC + robotics can be reconfigured for higher volumes; CNC may become a bottleneck. 4. **Workforce Skill Requirements:** CNC needs skilled operators; robotics needs higher initial training but lower ongoing labor. 5. **Maintenance and Downtime:** CNC has simpler maintenance; VMC + robotics requires specialized technicians. 6. **Strategic Alignment:** If the firm's competitive priority is **speed-to-market and customization**, CNC aligns. If priority is **scale, consistency, and export-quality**, VMC + robotics aligns. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A:** > - Students often compute NPV but **fail to consider capacity mismatch with demand**. KTU specifically tests the linkage between **technology choice and capacity-volume fit** — losing up to 3 marks if omitted. > - Do NOT compare NPVs of technologies with **different useful lives directly**. Always use **EUAC** for fair comparison. Direct NPV comparison is a **2-mark deduction** in valuation. > - Show all **factor table values** (P/A, P/F, A/P) explicitly — examiners look for these in step marking. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option B **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]** A pharmaceutical company is launching a new drug and must decide between **in-house manufacturing**, **contract manufacturing (licensing)**, and **technology acquisition through joint venture**. The expected annual demand is 5,00,000 units, the product requires **high process capability (Cpk ≥ 1.5)**, and the technology is evolving rapidly. **Required:** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the three technology acquisition modes across **cost, risk, speed-to-market, and capability-building** perspectives using a structured matrix. **(b) [7 Marks]** If the manual production alternative has $F_M = ₹50,00,000$, $V_M = ₹200$/unit, and the automated alternative has $F_A = ₹5,00,00,000$, $V_A = ₹80$/unit, determine the **break-even volume** and recommend the appropriate choice. --- #### Model Solution for Question B ### Part (a) — Comparison Matrix | Criterion | In-House R&D | Contract / Licensing | Joint Venture | |---|---|---|---| | **Initial Cost** | Very High | Low | Medium-High | | **Time to Market** | Slow (3–5 years typical) | Fast (6–12 months) | Medium (1–2 years) | | **Technical Risk** | High (uncertainty in R&D) | Low (proven technology) | Medium (shared risk) | | **Capability Building** | Very High (core competency) | Low (no internal learning) | High (knowledge transfer) | | **IP Ownership** | Full | Licensed (limited) | Shared | | **Quality Control** | Full control | Indirect (vendor-dependent) | Shared | | **Flexibility for Change** | High | Low (locked to licensor) | Medium | | **Long-term Strategic Value** | High | Low | High | **Recommended choice for the pharmaceutical scenario:** - Demand is **high (5,00,000 units/year)** — favours in-house or JV to retain margin. - **Rapid technology evolution** — favours **Joint Venture**, which balances speed and capability building. - **High Cpk requirement** (≥ 1.5) — favours **In-house or JV** (contract manufacturers may not guarantee this consistently). -
