# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Facility Layout?

A **facility layout** is the physical arrangement of resources, machines, workstations, departments, storage areas, and aisles within a production or service facility. It defines the workflow pattern and material handling routes, directly influencing productivity, cost, lead time, and worker safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Board Reference):**
> *"Layout planning is the analysis and design of the spatial configuration of facilities to optimize the flow of work, materials, and information while satisfying operational, safety, and ergonomic constraints."*

The four principal types of layouts recognized in the KTU Operations Management syllabus are:

| # | Layout Type | Alternate Name | Core Idea |
|---|-------------|----------------|-----------|
| 1 | Process Layout | Functional / Job-shop Layout | Group similar resources together |
| 2 | Product Layout | Line / Assembly Line Layout | Arrange resources in sequence of operations |
| 3 | Group Technology Layout | Cellular Layout | Form work groups (cells) for part families |
| 4 | Fixed Position Layout | Project / Site Layout | Product stays, resources move around it |

---

## 1.2 Process Layout (Functional Layout)

### Formal Definition
A **process layout** groups together machines, equipment, and personnel that perform similar functions or processes in a single department. Parts move *between departments* based on the operations required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Layout** is also called the **"Job-shop Layout"** because it is most suited to **low-volume, high-variety** production (e.g., a custom machine shop, a hospital's diagnostic wing).

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital OPD"
Think of a **multi-specialty hospital outpatient department (OPD)**. There is one large room for **X-Ray**, one for **Pathology Lab**, one for **Cardiology**, one for **Pharmacy**, etc. A patient (the "product") moves from one room to another depending on the doctor's prescription. Each room has the right *type* of equipment grouped together. The patient (not the doctor) moves.

This is exactly how a **process layout** works in a factory: the **part travels** through a sequence of *specialized departments* (Milling → Drilling → Grinding → Inspection), each department containing *all* milling machines, *all* drilling machines, etc.

### Key Characteristics
- **Grouping logic:** By *function/process type*
- **Product flow:** Intermittent, variable, crisscross
- **Workforce:** Highly skilled (multi-task operators)
- **Capital investment:** Lower (general-purpose machines)
- **Material handling cost:** **High** (due to complex routing)
- **Best suited for:** Job shops, hospitals, libraries, custom manufacturing

---

## 1.3 Product Layout (Line Layout)

### Formal Definition
A **product layout** arranges machines and workstations in the exact sequence of operations required to manufacture a *standardized* product. The product flows in a *continuous, straight-line* path — often on a conveyor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Layout** is the **heart of mass production**. It is also called a **"Flow Line Layout"** or **"Assembly Line Layout"**. Think of the **Maruti Suzuki car assembly line** in Manesar — the car body moves station by station and at each station one specific operation is performed.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Domino Chain of a Pizza Counter"
Imagine a **Domino's pizza assembly counter**:
1. **Station 1** → Roll out the dough
2. **Station 2** → Spread the sauce
3. **Station 3** → Add cheese & toppings
4. **Station 4** → Oven entry
5. **Station 5** → Box & dispatch

Each worker at each station does **one fixed job repeatedly** as the pizza (the "product") flows past them. The product *never backtracks*. That is a **product layout**.

### Key Characteristics
- **Grouping logic:** By *sequence of operations*
- **Product flow:** Straight-line, unidirectional
- **Workforce:** Low-skilled, narrowly trained
- **Capital investment:** **High** (special-purpose machines, conveyors)
- **Material handling cost:** **Low** (often automated)
- **Best suited for:** Assembly lines, bottling plants, mass production

---

## 1.4 Group Technology Layout (Cellular Layout)

### Formal Definition
**Group Technology (GT) layout** (or **Cellular Manufacturing Layout**) identifies families of parts with similar processing requirements and groups the *machines required to make each part-family* into a dedicated **cell**. A cell thus behaves like a "mini product layout" inside a larger job-shop environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** GT layout is a **hybrid** that captures the flexibility of process layout with the efficiency of product layout. It is built on the principle of **part-family grouping** using classification & coding systems (e.g., Opitz, MICLASS).

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Apartment Block of Specialists"
Imagine a hospital where instead of having *one huge Pathology lab* serving every patient, the hospital creates **specialty "clinics"** — a "Diabetes Clinic" room that contains an *endocrinologist's chair, a dietician's table, a foot-scanner, and a blood-sugar machine*, **all in one room**. A diabetic patient walks in and gets *everything* they need in that single clinic without bouncing between departments.

That is a **GT cell** — it contains *all* the diverse machines needed for *one part family* in one compact zone, eliminating inter-departmental travel.

### Key Characteristics
- **Grouping logic:** By *part family* (similar geometric features / processing)
- **Product flow:** Mostly intra-cell, short
- **Workforce:** Multi-skilled cell operators
- **Material handling cost:** **Moderate to low**
- **Best suited for:** Batch production with repeated part families

---

## 1.5 Fixed Position Layout

### Formal Definition
In a **fixed position layout**, the product — being too large, too heavy, or too immovable to be transported — remains at a *single location*, and all the necessary resources (machines, tools, workers, materials) are brought to it.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Examples:** Ship-building yards, aircraft assembly (Boeing 747), construction of skyscrapers, locomotives, large boilers, and dam construction.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Wedding Hall Cooking on Site"
When a large traditional **wedding feast** is prepared in a village, the cooks, the tandoor ovens, the gas cylinders, the rice sacks, the water barrels — *everything* is **brought to the venue (the fixed position)**. The wedding hall cannot be moved to the kitchen. The "product" (the feast) is too big and too location-bound to be transported, so resources converge on it.

### Key Characteristics
- **Product flow:** Zero (product is stationary)
- **Resource flow:** Workers, tools, and materials come to the site
- **Workforce:** Highly skilled, project-based teams
- **Capital investment:** Often *moderate* per project
- **Material handling cost:** **High** (resources travel to site)
- **Best suited for:** Project-based, custom-built, large-scale products
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Selection Logic — "Which Layout Fits What?"

The KTU board frequently tests the **decision logic** for choosing a layout. The selection is driven by **three pillars**:
1. **Volume of production** (units/year)
2. **Variety of products** (number of distinct part types)
3. **Process requirements** (operation sequence similarity)

### Step-by-Step Decision Logic

1. **Is the product very large / immovable?** → **Fixed Position**
2. **Is volume HIGH and variety LOW with stable sequence?** → **Product Layout**
3. **Is volume LOW and variety HIGH with different sequences?** → **Process Layout**
4. **Is volume MEDIUM and parts can be grouped into families?** → **Group Technology**

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the KTU Board Exam:** **"Big & Bulky → Fixed. High & Homogeneous → Product. Low & Lumpy → Process. Middle & Mixed → GT."**

---

## 2.2 Comparative Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Process Layout — Detailed Analysis

**Why** it is chosen:
- Accommodates **high variety** and **frequent changeovers**
- General-purpose machines handle diverse operations
- Equipment breakdowns do not halt the entire plant (since multiple machines of the same type exist)

**How** it works:
- The part follows a **route sheet** that lists the sequence of departments
- Inter-departmental travel creates crisscrossing material flows
- The **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** dictates order release into departments

**Advantages**
- Low capital investment
- High flexibility for product mix
- Easier supervision of like machines

**Disadvantages**
- Long material flow paths
- High work-in-process (WIP) inventory
- Complex scheduling & control
- Higher material handling cost

### 2.2.2 Product Layout — Detailed Analysis

**Why** it is chosen:
- Mass production of standardized goods
- Short cycle times
- Just-in-Time (JIT) friendly

**How** it works:
- Stations are arranged in the **order of operations**
- Pacing may be **machine-paced** (conveyor) or **worker-paced**
- **Line balancing** equalizes the workload at each station

**Advantages**
- Low material handling cost per unit
- Short throughput time
- Lower WIP
- Easier flow control

**Disadvantages**
- Very **high capital cost** (specialized machines)
- **Boring** for workers (monotonous)
- **Brittle** — equipment failure stops the whole line
- No product variety tolerated

### 2.2.3 Group Technology Layout — Detailed Analysis

**Why** it is chosen:
- Captures **economies of repetition** within a job-shop environment
- Reduces setup times through similarity

**How** it works — **The 5-Step GT Procedure**
1. **Part Coding** — Assign a code (e.g., Opitz) describing features
2. **Part Family Formation** — Group parts with similar codes
3. **Machine Grouping** — Identify machines needed for each family
4. **Cell Formation** — Place machine groups adjacently as cells
5. **Layout Finalization** — Arrange cells on the shop floor

**Advantages**
- Reduced setup time
- Lower material handling
- Smaller batch sizes become economical
- Improves worker satisfaction (focused specialization)

**Disadvantages**
- Initial coding effort is significant
- Idle capacity in some machines
- Re-balancing needed when part mix changes

### 2.2.4 Fixed Position Layout — Detailed Analysis

**Why** it is chosen:
- The product's **size, weight, or strategic value** prevents movement
- Example: Building a 100-floor skyscraper, the queen's royal carriage

**How** it works:
- The site is organized into **work zones**
- Resources (cranes, crews, materials) are **scheduled** to arrive at the right time
- **Project management techniques** (CPM, PERT) drive scheduling

**Advantages**
- Product is handled minimally (less risk of damage)
- Flexibility in resource deployment

**Disadvantages**
- High movement of resources to site
- Coordination complexity
- Higher storage cost of materials at site
- Worker fatigue and lower productivity

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The KTU board often tests **quantitative layout evaluation** using the **Load-Distance (L-D) approach** and **CRAFT-style** heuristics. The following formulas are **mandatory** for the exam.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | **Total Load-Distance Score** | $\displaystyle LD = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}$ | $L_{ij}$ = number of loads between depts *i* & *j*; $D_{ij}$ = distance | loads × units |
| 2 | **Rectilinear Distance** | $D_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$ | Manhattan distance between coordinates | meters |
| 3 | **Euclidean Distance** | $D_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ | Straight-line distance | meters |
| 4 | **Cost of Material Handling** | $TMHC = LD \times C$ | $C$ = cost per unit distance per load | ₹ |
| 5 | **Number of Departments (Theory of Constraints note)** | $n \ge 5$ for CRAFT | Minimum to apply pair-wise exchange | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Cycle Time (Line Balancing)** | $CT = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Desired Output}}$ | Time per unit at bottleneck | sec/unit |
| 7 | **Number of Stations (minimum)** | $N_{\min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum t_i}{CT} \right\rceil$ | $\sum t_i$ = sum of task times | stations |
| 8 | **Line Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N_{act} \times CT} \times 100\%$ | $N_{act}$ = actual number of stations | % |
| 9 | **Balance Delay** | $BD = 1 - \eta$ | Idle time fraction | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Throughput Time (Process Layout)** | $T_{proc} = T_{proc} + T_{wait} + T_{move} + T_{queue}$ | Total time in system | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The **Rectilinear distance** (also called **Manhattan / Taxicab distance**) is the default assumption in CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique) and most KTU numericals. Use **Euclidean** only if the question explicitly says "straight-line distance."

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Layout | Industrial Application | KTU-Relevant Case |
|--------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Process | Multi-product job shop, tool room | BHEL Heavy Machine Shop |
| Product | Maruti Alto line, FMCG bottling | iPhone assembly at Foxconn |
| Group Tech | CNC machine shop with part families | Tata Steel component cell |
| Fixed Position | Aircraft carrier construction, Shipyard | Cochin Shipyard aircraft carrier INS Vikrant |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Load-Distance (LD) Score

The **Total Load-Distance metric** is the cornerstone of comparative layout evaluation. The KTU board expects students to be able to **derive and apply** the formula step by step.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption

Consider a layout with **n departments**. For every ordered pair $(i, j)$ where $i \neq j$, we define:
- $L_{ij}$ = number of loads (or trips) moving *from* department *i* *to* department *j* per planning period
- $D_{ij}$ = distance between department *i* and department *j*

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Cost of a single inter-department move**
If $C$ is the cost (or time) per unit of distance per load, then the cost of moving the loads between departments $i$ and $j$ is:

$$
C_{ij} = L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij} \cdot C
$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all pairs**
Summing over every ordered pair of distinct departments:

$$
C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij} \cdot C
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the unit cost** $C$ (which is constant for all pairs):

$$
C_{total} = C \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the Load-Distance score** $LD$ as the bracketed sum (a pure number, independent of currency):

$$
LD = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{C_{total} = C \cdot LD}
$$

**Step 5 — For undirected flow** (the load moving between $i$ and $j$ is the same regardless of direction), the expression is often written with a factor of 2 absorbed into $L_{ij}$, or the double-counted term is halved. Most KTU textbooks use the **directed** form above.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layout comparison logic:** A layout is *better* than another if its $LD$ score is *lower*. The KTU board often asks: *"Given two layouts A and B with LD scores $LD_A$ and $LD_B$, which is preferable?"* — Answer: the one with **lower LD**.

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical — Rectilinear Distance Computation

**Problem (typical KTU 3-mark style):**
A layout has 3 departments at coordinates: A(2, 3), B(5, 7), C(8, 1). Compute the rectilinear distance between every pair.

**Step 1: Distance A–B**

$$
D_{AB} = \vert x_A - x_B \vert + \vert y_A - y_B \vert = \vert 2 - 5 \vert + \vert 3 - 7 \vert = 3 + 4 = 7 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Distance B–C**

$$
D_{BC} = \vert x_B - x_C \vert + \vert y_B - y_C \vert = \vert 5 - 8 \vert + \vert 7 - 1 \vert = 3 + 6 = 9 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Distance A–C**

$$
D_{AC} = \vert x_A - x_C \vert + \vert y_A - y_C \vert = \vert 2 - 8 \vert + \vert 3 - 1 \vert = 6 + 2 = 8 \text{ m}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** Always draw a **3×3 distance matrix** with zeros on the diagonal (distance from a department to itself is zero). This makes any KTU numerical effortless.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Line Balancing Efficiency

Product layout design requires **line balancing** — distributing tasks across stations so that each station has roughly equal workload.

### 3.2.1 Derivation of Number of Stations

Let:
- $T_a$ = available production time per day (e.g., 480 minutes)
- $Q$ = desired output per day (units)
- $t_i$ = standard time of task $i$, $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$

**Step 1 — Define the cycle time**
The maximum time any station is allowed to take:

$$
CT = \frac{T_a}{Q}
$$

**Step 2 — Lower bound on the number of stations**
A station cannot fit more than $CT$ time. Therefore, the *minimum* number of stations is the ceiling of the ratio of total work content to cycle time:

$$
N_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i}{CT} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Line efficiency**
The *actual* number of stations $N_{act}$ is usually $\ge N_{\min}$ (because of precedence constraints). The line efficiency is:

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Total productive time}}{\text{Total available time at all stations}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i}{N_{act} \cdot CT} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4 — Balance delay**
The fractional idle time, expressed as a decimal:

$$
BD = 1 - \eta = 1 - \frac{\sum t_i}{N_{act} \cdot CT}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "Given a precedence diagram and task times, compute $CT$, $N_{min}$, an actual feasible assignment, and $\eta$." Memorize this **four-step pipeline**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Master Reference)

This is the **single most important KTU exam table** — students should reproduce it from memory.

| Parameter | Process Layout | Product Layout | GT (Cellular) Layout | Fixed Position Layout |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|----------------------|-----------------------|
| **Grouping Basis** | Function / Process | Product sequence | Part family | Product immobility |
| **Volume** | Low | High | Medium | Single unit / Project |
| **Variety** | High | Low | Medium | One-of-a-kind |
| **Cycle Time** | Long | Short | Medium | Very long |
| **WIP Inventory** | High | Low | Medium | Low (at point) |
| **Material Handling Cost** | High | Low | Moderate | High (resources to site) |
| **Capital Investment** | Low | High | Moderate | Variable |
| **Workforce Skill** | High | Low | Medium | High (specialists) |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low | Medium | High |
| **Production Type** | Job shop / Batch | Mass / Continuous | Batch (family) | Project |
| **Best Example** | Tool room | Car assembly | CNC machining cell | Ship-building |
| **Layout Shape** | U / L / T / S shaped | Straight line | Cluster / Cell | Open site |
| **Supervision** | By function | By product line | By cell | By project |
| **Risk on Breakdown** | Low (redundancy) | High (line stop) | Moderate | Low |
| **KTU Real-World Indian Example** | BHEL Heavy Machine | Maruti Manesar line | Tata Steel Cell | Cochin Shipyard |

---

## 3.4 Decision Tree for Layout Selection (KTU Board Favorite)

$$
\text{Start} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Is product movable?}
$$

$$
\text{Yes} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Is volume high + variety low?}
$$

$$
\text{Yes} \;\rightarrow\; \textbf{Product Layout}
$$

$$
\text{No} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Is volume low + variety high?}
$$

$$
\text{Yes} \;\rightarrow\; \textbf{Process Layout}
$$

$$
\text{No} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Can parts be grouped into families?}
$$

$$
\text{Yes} \;\rightarrow\; \textbf{Group Technology Layout}
$$

$$
\text{No} \;\rightarrow\; \textbf{Product Layout (assemble-to-order)}
$$

$$
\text{No} \;\rightarrow\; \textbf{Fixed Position Layout}
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Layout — Functional Block Diagram

The diagram below shows a typical **U-shaped process layout** of 5 departments. Note that similar machines are clustered: all milling machines in M, all grinding in G, etc. Material crisscrosses between departments based on route sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Receiving] --> M[Milling Dept]
    M --> D[Drilling Dept]
    D --> G[Grinding Dept]
    G --> I[Inspection Dept]
    I --> S[Shipping]
    M --> I
    D --> M
    G --> D
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Multiple crisscrossing arrows are the **hallmark of a process layout**. They represent material moving between functionally-grouped departments.

---

## 4.2 Product Layout — Line Flow Diagram

A classic **linear / straight-line product layout** for, say, a pen assembly. The product moves strictly left-to-right; backflow is impossible.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Station 1: Inject Barrel] --> S2[Station 2: Fit Tip]
    S2 --> S3[Station 3: Ink Filling]
    S3 --> S4[Station 4: Cap Assembly]
    S4 --> S5[Station 5: Quality Check]
    S5 --> S6[Station 6: Packing]
    style S1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Key visual cue:** **Unidirectional arrows, no backflow, all stations in sequence.** This is the visual signature of a product layout.

---

## 4.3 Group Technology Layout — Cellular Architecture

The diagram shows **3 part families** flowing through **3 dedicated cells**, with each cell containing only the machines needed for that family. Note the dramatic reduction in inter-cell travel compared to process layout.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CellA[Cell A - Shaft Family]
        LA1[Lathe] --> MA1[Milling]
        MA1 --> GA1[Grinding]
    end
    subgraph CellB[Cell B - Gear Family]
        LB1[Lathe] --> MB1[Milling]
        MB1 --> GB1[Hobbing]
        GB1 --> GB2[Grinding]
    end
    subgraph CellC[Cell C - Housing Family]
        LC1[Lathe] --> MC1[Drill]
        MC1 --> GC1[Milling]
        GC1 --> GC2[Inspection]
    end
    In[Receiving] --> CellA
    In --> CellB
    In --> CellC
    CellA --> Out[Shipping]
    CellB --> Out
    CellC --> Out
    style CellA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CellB fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CellC fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each cell is a **closed loop** of machines serving *one part family*. Material entering a cell exits after the family is fully processed — no inter-cell jumps.

---

## 4.4 Fixed Position Layout — Resource Convergence Model

In a fixed position layout, the **product (the center node) is stationary**, and all the *resource classes* — labor, machines, materials, tools — flow *inward* from the periphery.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P((STATIC PRODUCT e.g. Ship))
    subgraph Resources[Converging Resources]
        R1[Heavy Cranes]
        R2[Welder Teams]
        R3[Steel Plates]
        R4[Pipes and Valves]
        R5[Electricians]
        R6[Painters]
        R7[QA Inspectors]
    end
    R1 --> P
    R2 --> P
    R3 --> P
    R4 --> P
    R5 --> P
    R6 --> P
    R7 --> P
    style P fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style Resources fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** All arrows point *toward* the product. This convergence is the defining feature of a fixed-position layout.

---

## 4.5 Master Comparison — Layout Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Production Requirement]) --> Q1{Is product movable?}
    Q1 -->|No| FP[Fixed Position Layout]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is volume high AND variety low?}

    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is volume low AND variety high?}
```
