# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Most Common Mistakes**
> 1. **Mixing up GT Layout with Process Layout** — They look similar, but GT has *part families*; Process has *function groups*.
> 2. **Forgetting zeros on the diagonal** of the load-distance matrix. Distance from a department to itself is **always zero**.
> 3. **Using Euclidean distance when the question expects rectilinear** — Always read the problem carefully. By default, assume **rectilinear** in KTU papers.
> 4. **Confusing "Cycle Time" with "Total Work Content"** — Cycle time is *per unit*; total work content is *sum of all task times*.
> 5. **Writing examples incorrectly** — A *production line in a bakery* is product layout; a *bakery's kitchen* is process layout.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between process layout and product layout. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Process Layout | Product Layout |
|-------|----------------|----------------|
| Grouping | By *function/type* of machine | By *sequence* of operations |
| Product variety | High | Low / single product |
| Volume | Low | High |
| Material flow | Variable, crisscrossing | Straight line, fixed |
| WIP inventory | High | Low |
| Machine type | General purpose | Special purpose |
| Worker skill | High (multi-skilled) | Low (single-task) |
| Example | Tool room in a workshop | Maruti car assembly line |

A process layout groups similar machines (e.g., all lathes in one room, all milling machines in another) and the part moves between them. A product layout arranges machines in the exact order of operations and the part flows through them on a fixed path.

> **[End of Answer — 3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**Q: What is a fixed position layout? State two situations where it is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fixed position layout** is one in which the product — being too large, heavy, or fragile to be moved — is constructed at a single location, and the necessary machines, tools, materials, and workers are brought to it.

**Two situations where it is preferred:**
1. **When the product is too large or heavy to be moved** — e.g., ship-building, aircraft assembly, locomotive manufacturing.
2. **When the product is too delicate or expensive to be moved** — e.g., a large generator, a satellite assembly, or precision machine tool assembly, where repeated movement increases the risk of damage.

> **[End of Answer — 3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the concept of Group Technology layout. Describe the steps involved in designing a cellular manufacturing system.**

**Model Answer:**

#### Concept of Group Technology (GT) Layout

**Group Technology (GT)** is a manufacturing philosophy in which parts having similar design features or similar manufacturing processes are grouped into **part families**, and the machines required to produce a part family are grouped into a dedicated **cell**. The cell is laid out in the sequence of operations for the family — making it a "mini product layout" inside a job-shop.

> The principle of GT was first formalized by **Mitrofanoff (1959)** and later by **Opitz (1970)** through the famous **Opitz classification & coding system**.

#### Steps to Design a Cellular Manufacturing System

**Step 1 — Part Coding**
Each part in the production list is assigned an alphanumeric code describing its geometric features (e.g., external shape, internal shape, length/diameter ratio, material, tolerance). The most widely used coding system is the **Opitz code**.

**Step 2 — Part Family Formation**
Using the codes, parts with similar characteristics are clustered into **part families** (typically 5–15 families per shop). Techniques used include:
- Production Flow Analysis (PFA)
- Cluster analysis
- Classification & coding hierarchy

**Step 3 — Machine Grouping**
The machines required to process each part family are identified from the **route sheets** of the parts in the family.

**Step 4 — Cell Formation**
The identified machines are physically grouped into a cell and arranged in the **sequence of operations** required by the family. This step uses techniques such as:
- **Rank Order Clustering (ROC)**
- **ROC-2**
- **Similarity Coefficient Method**

**Step 5 — Layout of Cells on Shop Floor**
The formed cells are then arranged on the shop floor. There are three classical arrangements:
- **Cluster-type** — Cells placed near a common material source
- **Loop / Pipeline** — Cells in series
- **Open-field** — Cells spread with shared resources

**Step 6 — Operator and Tooling Allocation**
Skilled cell operators are assigned, and dedicated tooling/gauges are placed inside each cell for reduced setup time.

#### Advantages
- Reduction in material handling and WIP
- Lower setup time (parts of a family share processes)
- Improved operator skill (focused specialization)
- Easier production planning (cells are semi-autonomous)

#### Limitations
- High initial effort for coding
- Some machines may be duplicated across cells → idle capacity
- Re-balancing required when product mix changes

> **[End of Part (a) — 7 Marks]**

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]: A manufacturing unit has 4 departments located at coordinates P(1, 1), Q(4, 5), R(7, 2), S(5, 8). The number of loads per week moved between the departments is given in the table below. Using rectilinear distance, compute the total Load-Distance (LD) score.**

| From / To | P | Q | R | S |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | 0 | 200 | 150 | 50 |
| **Q** | 100 | 0 | 80 | 120 |
| **R** | 60 | 90 | 0 | 40 |
| **S** | 30 | 110 | 70 | 0 |

**Model Answer:**

#### Step 1 — Compute Rectilinear Distances

Distance between two points $(x_i, y_i)$ and $(x_j, y_j)$:

$$
D_{ij} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert
$$

$$
D_{PQ} = \vert 1 - 4 \vert + \vert 1 - 5 \vert = 3 + 4 = 7
$$

$$
D_{PR} = \vert 1 - 7 \vert + \vert 1 - 2 \vert = 6 + 1 = 7
$$

$$
D_{PS} = \vert 1 - 5 \vert + \vert 1 - 8 \vert = 4 + 7 = 11
$$

$$
D_{QR} = \vert 4 - 7 \vert + \vert 5 - 2 \vert = 3 + 3 = 6
$$

$$
D_{QS} = \vert 4 - 5 \vert + \vert 5 - 8 \vert = 1 + 3 = 4
$$

$$
D_{RS} = \vert 7 - 5 \vert + \vert 2 - 8 \vert = 2 + 6 = 8
$$

#### Step 2 — Build the Load × Distance Matrix

| From / To | P | Q | R | S |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | 0 | $200 \times 7 = 1400$ | $150 \times 7 = 1050$ | $50 \times 11 = 550$ |
| **Q** | $100 \times 7 = 700$ | 0 | $80 \times 6 = 480$ | $120 \times 4 = 480$ |
| **R** | $60 \times 7 = 420$ | $90 \times 6 = 540$ | 0 | $40 \times 8 = 320$ |
| **S** | $30 \times 11 = 330$ | $110 \times 4 = 440$ | $70 \times 8 = 560$ | 0 |

#### Step 3 — Sum All Cells

$$
LD = 1400 + 1050 + 550 + 700 + 480 + 480 + 420 + 540 + 320 + 330 + 440 + 560
$$

Adding row by row:

- **Row P:** $1400 + 1050 + 550 = 3000$
- **Row Q:** $700 + 480 + 480 = 1660$
- **Row R:** $420 + 540 + 320 = 1280$
- **Row S:** $330 + 440 + 560 = 1330$

Total:

$$
\boxed{LD = 3000 + 1660 + 1280 + 1330 = 7270 \text{ load-metres per week}}
$$

> **[End of Part (b) — 7 Marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]: Compare the four types of layouts — process, product, group technology, and fixed position — on any seven relevant parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

The four major layout types are compared below on seven key KTU-relevant parameters:

| # | Parameter | Process Layout | Product Layout | GT (Cellular) | Fixed Position |
|---|-----------|----------------|----------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Grouping Basis** | By function/process | By operation sequence | By part family | Product stays at one place |
| 2 | **Production Volume** | Low | High | Medium | Single unit (project) |
| 3 | **Product Variety** | High | Low | Medium | One-of-a-kind |
| 4 | **WIP Inventory** | High | Low | Moderate | Low (at site) |
| 5 | **Material Handling Cost** | High | Low | Moderate | High |
| 6 | **Capital Investment** | Low (general machines) | High (special machines) | Moderate | Variable |
| 7 | **Workforce Skill Level** | High (multi-skilled) | Low (narrow task) | Medium (cell operator) | High (project teams) |

**Conclusion:** The choice of layout depends primarily on **production volume, product variety, and product characteristics**. Low-volume high-variety → process; high-volume low-variety → product; medium with families → GT; immovable product → fixed position.

> **[End of Part (a) — 7 Marks]**

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]: A 6-station assembly line is being designed. The total work content of all tasks is 36 minutes. The available production time per day is 480 minutes and the desired output is 80 units/day. Compute (i) the cycle time, (ii) the minimum number of stations, and (iii) the theoretical line efficiency if 6 stations are used.**

**Model Answer:**

#### Given Data
- Total work content $\sum t_i = 36$ minutes
- Available time per day $T_a = 480$ minutes
- Desired output $Q = 80$ units/day
- Number of stations (assumed actual) $N_{act} = 6$

#### (i) Cycle Time

$$
CT = \frac{T_a}{Q} = \frac{480 \text{ min/day}}{80 \text{ units/day}} = 6 \text{ min/unit}
$$

#### (ii) Minimum Number of Stations

$$
N_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum t_i}{CT} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{36}{6} \right\rceil = \lceil 6 \rceil = 6
$$

#### (iii) Theoretical Line Efficiency (if 6 stations are used)

$$
\eta = \frac{\sum t_i}{N_{act} \cdot CT} \times 100\% = \frac{36}{6 \times 6} \times 100\% = \frac{36}{36} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

**Interpretation:** With 6 stations and a cycle time of 6 minutes, the line achieves **100% efficiency in theory**, meaning no idle time. In practice, due to precedence constraints between tasks, achieving 100% balance is rare, and the actual efficiency will typically be **80–95%**.

> **[End of Part (b) — 7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Process Layout = Function-grouped** (low volume, high variety, job shop).
- [ ] **Product Layout = Sequence-grouped** (high volume, low variety, mass production).
- [ ] **GT Layout = Part-family-grouped** (medium volume, medium variety, batch).
- [ ] **Fixed Position = Immovable product** (project-based, ship, aircraft, building).
- [ ] **Cycle Time** $CT = T_a / Q$ — always in *time per unit*.
- [ ] **Minimum Stations** $N_{\min} = \lceil \sum t_i / CT \rceil$ — use the ceiling function.
- [ ] **Line Efficiency** $\eta = \sum t_i / (N_{act} \cdot CT) \times 100\%$ — a *higher* $\eta$ is better.
- [ ] **Balance Delay** $BD = 1 - \eta$ — fractional idle time; a *lower* $BD$ is better.
- [ ] **Load-Distance Score** $LD = \sum L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}$ — a *lower* $LD$ is better.
- [ ] **Rectilinear Distance** $D = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ — default in KTU.
- [ ] **Euclidean Distance** $D = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ — use only when the problem explicitly says "straight-line".
- [ ] **Distance from a department to itself is always zero.**
- [ ] **GT Steps:** Code → Family → Machine Group → Cell Formation → Layout → Operator Allocation.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for layout choice:** *Big & Bulky → Fixed • High & Homogeneous → Product • Low & Lumpy → Process • Middle & Mixed → GT.*
- [ ] **Examples (memorize for viva):** Hospital OPD = Process • Maruti line = Product • CNC cell = GT • INS Vikrant ship = Fixed Position.
- [ ] **CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique)** is the most common KTU-referenced algorithm for *iterative improvement* of an existing layout by pairwise exchange of departments.
- [ ] **Fixed-position drawback:** *very high resource movement*; *very low worker productivity* due to congestion.
- [ ] **GT drawback:** *duplicate machines* across cells cause *idle capacity*.
- [ ] **Product layout drawback:** *single point of failure* — if one machine breaks, the whole line stops.
- [ ] **Process layout drawback:** *crisscrossing flows* increase material handling and *WIP inventory*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly line balancing by using LOT rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly line balancing by using LOT rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Assembly Line Balancing (ALB) — The Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Assembly Line Balancing (ALB)** is the systematic procedure of assigning discrete work elements (tasks) to a sequence of workstations, such that:
> 1. Precedence constraints between tasks are strictly satisfied.
> 2. The total time assigned to every workstation does not exceed the **cycle time (C)**.
> 3. The number of workstations is minimized (or workload is optimally distributed).

In simpler language, imagine a relay race where each runner represents a **workstation**, and the baton is the **product** moving down the line. Each runner must finish their leg of the race within a fixed time (cycle time), and certain handoffs (precedence) must occur in a specific order. **Assembly Line Balancing** is the science of deciding "who runs which leg" so that no runner is overloaded, no one is sitting idle, and the team finishes in minimum possible stations.

### 1.2 What is the LOT Rule?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LOT (Largest Order of remaining Tasks) Rule — Formal Definition**
> The **LOT rule** is a **priority heuristic** used for ranking tasks (work elements) when assigning them to workstations in a single-model assembly line balancing problem. For every task *i*, the **LOT value** represents the **total number of tasks that remain to be performed in the job sequence if task *i* is chosen next**, i.e., the task *i* itself plus all its direct and indirect followers in the precedence network.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of it like planning a **road trip itinerary**:
- You have a list of 8 stops (tasks A, B, C ... H), each taking a fixed amount of time.
- Some stops MUST be done before others (e.g., you must book the hotel *before* you can check in).
- You can only spend a maximum of **10 hours per day** at stops (cycle time).
- **The LOT of a stop = the number of stops remaining (including this one) once you commit to visiting it.**

You naturally **prioritize the stop with the largest LOT** because skipping it would leave many other stops stranded behind it (precedence violation). It is a *backward-looking* ranking that ensures tasks with the longest downstream impact are not delayed.

### 1.4 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **tᵢ** | Processing time of task *i* | seconds / minutes |
| **C** | **Cycle time** of the line | seconds / minutes |
| **N** | Number of workstations opened | dimensionless |
| **T** | Total work content (Σ tᵢ) | seconds / minutes |
| **η** | Line **efficiency** | percentage |
| **BD** | Balance delay | percentage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard rule of thumb:** A well-balanced line aims for **η ≥ 85%**; anything below 75% is considered poorly balanced.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind the LOT Rule — Why Does It Work?

The LOT rule is a **heuristic** (a smart shortcut, not a guarantee of optimality). Its core logic unfolds in 4 layers:

- **Layer 1 — Backward Propagation of Burden**
  Tasks with many followers carry a heavy "scheduling dependency weight." If such a task is delayed, an entire downstream chain is blocked. The LOT rule front-loads these critical tasks so that precedence chains remain unblocked.

- **Layer 2 — Recursive Computation**
  LOT is computed **bottom-up** through the precedence network:

  $$\text{LOT}(i) = 1 + \sum_{j \in S(i)} \text{LOT}(j)$$

  where **S(i)** is the set of *immediate successors* of task *i*. The "+1" accounts for the task itself.

- **Layer 3 — Tie-Breaking Convention**
  When two tasks share the same LOT value, KTU examiners and standard textbooks (e.g., Krajewski, Heizer) use these tie-breakers **in order**:
  1. **Longest task time** (LPT) among tied tasks.
  2. **Most immediate followers**.
  3. **Earliest task label** (alphabetical/numerical order).

- **Layer 4 — Station-by-Station Greedy Allocation**
  The rule is applied station by station. At each workstation, the available task (all its predecessors are already assigned) with the **highest LOT** is picked first, and so on, until the cycle time is exhausted.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are universally tested in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) Module 2 questions on ALB.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $N_{\min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum t_i}{C} \right\rceil$ | Theoretical minimum number of stations | Ceiling function; integer value |
| 2 | $T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Total work content (sum of all task times) | Units of time |
| 3 | $C = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Desired Output}}$ | Cycle time | Demand-driven |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N \times C} \times 100\%$ | **Line Efficiency** | Higher is better |
| 5 | $\text{BD} = 1 - \eta = \dfrac{N \cdot C - \sum t_i}{N \cdot C}$ | Balance Delay | Fraction of idle time |
| 6 | $\text{Total Idle} = N \cdot C - \sum t_i$ | Aggregate idle time across stations | Sum of slack per station |
| 7 | $\text{LOT}(i) = 1 + \sum_{j \in S(i)} \text{LOT}(j)$ | LOT value of task *i* | Recursive, bottom-up |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical distinction for KTU:** $N_{\min}$ is a *theoretical lower bound*. The actual number of stations **N** obtained from the LOT heuristic will almost always be **≥ $N_{\min}$** because precedence constraints and indivisibility of tasks introduce slack.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Balancing body-shop, paint-shop, and final-assembly lines in car manufacturing (e.g., Toyota Production System).
- **Electronics:** PCB assembly and smartphone production lines where precedence between soldering, testing, and packaging is rigid.
- **Apparel & FMCG:** Sewing lines, bottling plants, and packaging conveyors.
- **Productivity engineering:** Identifying **bottleneck workstations** and reducing **balance delay** to compress throughput time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Generic Algorithm (Procedure)

The LOT-rule-based balancing is executed in **7 steps**. Master this sequence — it is the KTU valuation key.

- **Step 1:** Draw the **precedence diagram** (Activity-on-Node, AON) with all tasks and their times.
- **Step 2:** List each task's **immediate followers**.
- **Step 3:** Compute **LOT(i)** for every task using the recursive formula (start from the terminal task, which has LOT = 1).
- **Step 4:** Compute $N_{\min} = \lceil T / C \rceil$ using the given cycle time.
- **Step 5:** Open **Station 1**. Identify all tasks whose predecessors are already assigned (i.e., all predecessors completed) — these are *available* tasks.
- **Step 6:** Among available tasks, pick the one with the **highest LOT** (apply tie-breakers). Check if it fits within the remaining cycle time of the current station. If yes, assign it; else close the station and open the next.
- **Step 7:** Repeat Step 6 for every station until all tasks are assigned. Compute **efficiency** and **balance delay**.

### 3.2 Solved Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A simple assembly line has **8 tasks** with the following task times (in seconds) and precedence relationships:

| Task | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time (tᵢ) | 6 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 1 | 7 | 3 |

**Precedence Relations:**
- A precedes C, D
- B precedes D, E
- C precedes F
- D precedes F
- E precedes G
- F precedes G
- G precedes H

**Cycle time C = 10 seconds.** Balance the line using the **LOT rule**.

---

#### Step 1: Precedence Diagram (Text Representation)

```
A ──► C ──► F ──► G ──► H
A ──► D ──► F
B ──► D
B ──► E ──► G
```

#### Step 2: List Immediate Followers

| Task | Immediate Followers | Count |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | C, D | 2 |
| B | D, E | 2 |
| C | F | 1 |
| D | F | 1 |
| E | G | 1 |
| F | G | 1 |
| G | H | 1 |
| H | — | 0 |

#### Step 3: Compute LOT Values (Bottom-Up)

Using the recursive formula $\text{LOT}(i) = 1 + \sum_{j \in S(i)} \text{LOT}(j)$:

- **LOT(H):** H has no successors → LOT(H) = 1 + 0 = **1**
- **LOT(G):** G's only successor is H → LOT(G) = 1 + LOT(H) = 1 + 1 = **2**
- **LOT(F):** F's only successor is G → LOT(F) = 1 + LOT(G) = 1 + 2 = **3**
- **LOT(E):** E's only successor is G → LOT(E) = 1 + LOT(G) = 1 + 2 = **3**
- **LOT(D):** D's only successor is F → LOT(D) = 1 + LOT(F) = 1 + 3 = **4**
- **LOT(C):** C's only successor is F → LOT(C) = 1 + LOT(F) = 1 + 3 = **4**
- **LOT(B):** B's successors are D, E → LOT(B) = 1 + LOT(D) + LOT(E) = 1 + 4 + 3 = **8**
- **LOT(A):** A's successors are C, D → LOT(A) = 1 + LOT(C) + LOT(D) = 1 + 4 + 4 = **9**

> [!NOTE]
> **LOT Calculation Summary Table:**

| Task | tᵢ | Immediate Successors | LOT(i) | Rank |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | 6 | C, D | **9** | 1 (highest) |
| B | 2 | D, E | **8** | 2 |
| C | 4 | F | **4** | 3 (tie) |
| D | 3 | F | **4** | 3 (tie) |
| E | 5 | G | **3** | 5 (tie) |
| F | 1 | G | **3** | 5 (tie) |
| G | 7 | H | **2** | 7 |
| H | 3 | — | **1** | 8 |

#### Step 4: Compute Total Work Content & Minimum Stations

$$T = \sum_{i=1}^{8} t_i = 6 + 2 + 4 + 3 + 5 + 1 + 7 + 3 = 31 \text{ seconds}$$

$$N_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{T}{C} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{31}{10} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.1 \rceil = 4 \text{ stations}$$

#### Step 5–7: Station-by-Station Allocation Using LOT

**Available tasks** (no predecessors yet done) at the start: **A (LOT=9)**, **B (LOT=8)**.

**Station 1 (Time budget = 10 s):**
- Available: A (LOT=9), B (LOT=8) → **Pick A** (highest LOT). Time used = 6 s.
- After A is done, available: B (LOT=8), C (LOT=4), D (LOT=4). Highest LOT → **Pick B**. Time used = 6 + 2 = 8 s.
- After B is done, available: C (LOT=4), D (LOT=4), E (LOT=3). Highest LOT = 4 (tie between C and D). **Tie-breaker: longest processing time → C (4 s > 3 s).** Try adding C: 8 + 4 = 12 s > 10. ❌ C does NOT fit.
- **Close Station 1.** Station 1 = {A, B}, time used = 8 s, idle = 2 s.

**Station 2 (Time budget = 10 s):**
- Available: C (LOT=4), D (LOT=4), E (LOT=3). Highest LOT = 4 (tie). **Tie-break: longest time → C (4 s).** Time used = 4 s.
- After C: available: D (LOT=4), E (LOT=3). **Pick D** (LOT=4 > 3). Time used = 4 + 3 = 7 s.
- After D: available: E (LOT=3), F (LOT=3, since C and D are both done). Tie — **tie-break: longest time → E (5 s > 1 s).** Try adding E: 7 + 5 = 12 s > 10. ❌ E does NOT fit.
- **Close Station 2.** Station 2 = {C, D}, time used = 7 s, idle = 3 s.

**Station 3 (Time budget = 10 s):**
- Available: E (LOT=3), F (LOT=3). Tie — **tie-break: longest time → E (5 s).** Time used = 5 s.
- After E: available: F (LOT=3), G (LOT=2, but G needs E, F — E is done, F not yet). So available = F (LOT=3). **Pick F.** Time used = 5 + 1 = 6 s.
- After F: available: G (LOT=2). **Pick G.** Try adding G: 6 + 7 = 13 s > 10. ❌ G does NOT fit.
- **Close Station 3.** Station 3 = {E, F}, time used = 6 s, idle = 4 s.

**Station 4 (Time budget = 10 s):**
- Available: G (LOT=2). **Pick G.** Time used = 7 s.
- After G: available: H (LOT=1). **Pick H.** Try adding H: 7 + 3 = 10 s ≤ 10. ✅ Fits.
- **Close Station 4.** Station 4 = {G, H}, time used = 10 s, idle = 0 s.

#### Final Balance Summary

| Station | Tasks Assigned | Time Used (s) | Idle Time (s) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | A, B | 8 | 2 |
| 2 | C, D | 7 | 3 |
| 3 | E, F | 6 | 4 |
| 4 | G, H | 10 | 0 |
| **Total** | **8 tasks** | **31** | **9** |

#### Compute Efficiency and Balance Delay

$$\eta = \frac{\sum t_i}{N \times C} \times 100\% = \frac{31}{4 \times 10} \times 100\% = \frac{31}{40} \times 100\% = 77.5\%$$

$$\text{BD} = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.775 = 0.225 = 22.5\%$$

$$\text{Total Idle Time} = N \times C - \sum t_i = (4 \times 10) - 31 = 9 \text{ seconds}$$ ✅ (matches above)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The line requires **4 workstations** to produce one unit every 10 seconds. The line efficiency is **77.5%** with a balance delay of **22.5%**. Note that the number of stations equals $N_{\min} = 4$, meaning the LOT heuristic found a near-optimal balance for this instance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Precedence Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    taskA["A | t=6 | LOT=9"] --> taskC["C | t=4 | LOT=4"]
    taskA --> taskD["D | t=3 | LOT=4"]
    taskB["B | t=2 | LOT=8"] --> taskD
    taskB --> taskE["E | t=5 | LOT=3"]
    taskC --> taskF["F | t=1 | LOT=3"]
    taskD --> taskF
    taskE --> taskG["G | t=7 | LOT=2"]
    taskF --> taskG
    taskG --> taskH["H | t=3 | LOT=1"]
```

### 4.2 LOT Computation Dependency Graph (Bottom-Up)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hNode["H LOT=1"] --> gNode["G LOT=1+1=2"]
    gNode --> fNode["F LOT=1+2=3"]
    gNode --> eNode["E LOT=1+2=3"]
    fNode --> cNode["C LOT=1+3=4"]
    fNode --> dNode["D LOT=1+3=4"]
    eNode --> bNode["B LOT=1+4+3=8"]
    dNode --> bNode
    cNode --> aNode["A LOT=1+4+4=9"]
    dNode --> aNode
```

### 4.3 Station-Allocation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ST1["STATION 1 | C=10s"]
        a1["A: 6s"] --> b1["B: 2s"]
        b1 --> idle1["IDLE: 2s"]
    end
    subgraph ST2["STATION 2 | C=10s"]
        c2["C: 4s"] --> d2["D: 3s"]
        d2 --> idle2["IDLE: 3s"]
    end
    subgraph ST3["STATION 3 | C=10s"]
        e3["E: 5s"] --> f3["F: 1s"]
        f3 --> idle3["IDLE: 4s"]
    end
    subgraph ST4["STATION 4 | C=10s"]
        g4["G: 7s"] --> h4["H: 3s"]
        h4 --> idle4["IDLE: 0s"]
    end
    ST1 ==> ST2 ==> ST3 ==> ST4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the LOT rule. How is the LOT value of a task computed in assembly line balancing?**

**Model Answer:**
The **LOT (Largest Order of remaining Tasks) rule** is a priority heuristic used in assembly line balancing to rank tasks for assignment to workstations. The LOT value of a task *i* equals the **total number of tasks that must follow task *i* in the precedence network**, including *i* itself.

Mathematically, it is computed recursively as:

$$\text{LOT}(i) = 1 + \sum_{j \in S(i)} \text{LOT}(j)$$

where **S(i)** is the set of immediate successors of *i*. The task with the **highest LOT** is assigned first, ensuring that critical downstream chains are unblocked. Tie-breaking is done by selecting the task with the **longest processing time**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for tie-breaking / application logic.)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Distinguish between cycle time and balance delay. Why is it impossible to achieve 100% line efficiency in real assembly lines?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Cycle Time (C)** | The maximum time allowed for a workstation to complete its assigned tasks — also the time interval between successive units coming off the line. |
| **Balance Delay (BD)** | The fraction of total available workstation time that is *wasted* as idle time: $\text{BD} = 1 - \eta$. |

100% efficiency is unattainable in practice because of:
1. **Precedence constraints** — tasks cannot be freely redistributed across stations even if idle time exists.
2. **Task indivisibility** — a task cannot be split across two workstations.
3. **Cycle time discreteness** — sum of task times is rarely an exact multiple of C.
4. **Workstation imbalance** — long bottleneck tasks force extra stations, inflating the denominator N×C.

*(3 marks: 1 each for definitions + 1 for reasons.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A).** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]
> An assembly line has **9 tasks** with the following data:

| Task | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time (s) | 3 | 4 | 2 | 5 | 1 | 3 | 4 | 2 | 6 |

**Precedence:**
- 1, 2 precede 3
- 2, 3 precede 4
- 1 precedes 5
- 4, 5 precede 6
- 6 precedes 7
- 5, 7 precede 8
- 8 precedes 9

**Cycle time C = 10 seconds.** Balance the line using the **LOT rule** and compute the **efficiency** and **balance delay**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks] — Compute LOT values and theoretical bounds.**

Immediate Successors:
- 1 → {3, 5}
- 2 → {3, 4}
- 3 → {4}
- 4 → {6}
- 5 → {6, 8}
- 6 → {7}
- 7 → {8}
- 8 → {9}
- 9 → {}

LOT Computation (bottom-up):
- LOT(9) = 1
- LOT(8) = 1 + LOT(9) = 2
- LOT(7) = 1 + LOT(8) = 3
- LOT(6) = 1 + LOT(7) = 4
- LOT(5) = 1 + LOT(6) + LOT(8) = 1 + 4 + 2 = 7
- LOT(4) = 1 + LOT(6) = 5
- LOT(3) = 1 + LOT(4) = 6
- LOT(2) = 1 + LOT(3) + LOT(4) = 1 + 6 + 5 = 12
- LOT(1) = 1 + LOT(3) + LOT(5) = 1 + 6 + 7 = 14

**LOT Ranking Table:**

| Task | tᵢ | LOT | Rank |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 3 | **14** | 1 |
| 2 | 4 | **12** | 2 |
| 3 | 2 | **6** | 3 |
| 4 | 5 | **5** | 4 |
| 5 | 1 | **7** | — (re-rank: 5 is rank 4) |

Total work: $T = 3+4+2+5+1+3+4+2+6 = 30$ s

$$N_{\min} = \lceil 30 / 10 \rceil = 3 \text{ stations (theoretical)}$$

**(b) [7 marks] — Station-by-Station Allocation and Performance Metrics.**

**Station 1 (C = 10 s):**
- Available: 1 (LOT=14), 2 (LOT=12). Pick 1 → time = 3 s. Available: 2, 3, 5. Pick 2 (LOT=12) → time = 7 s. Available: 3 (LOT=6), 5 (LOT=7). Pick 5 (higher LOT) → time = 8 s. Available: 3 (LOT=6). Try 3: 8 + 2 = 10 s ≤ 10 ✅. Assign 3 → time = 10 s.
- **Station 1 = {1, 2, 5, 3}, time = 10 s, idle = 0 s.**

**Station 2 (C = 10 s):**
- Available: 4 (LOT=5). Pick 4 → time = 5 s. Available: 6 (predecessors 4,5 done). Pick 6 → time = 8 s. Available: 7. Try 7: 8 + 3 = 11 s > 10 ❌.
- **Station 2 = {4, 6}, time = 8 s, idle = 2 s.**

**Station 3 (C = 10 s):**
- Available: 7. Pick 7 → time = 3 s. Available: 8. Pick 8 → time = 5 s. Available: 9. Try 9: 5 + 6 = 11 s > 10 ❌.
- **Station 3 = {7, 8}, time = 5 s, idle = 5 s.**

**Station 4 (C = 10 s):**
- Available: 9. Pick 9 → time = 6 s.
- **Station 4 = {9}, time = 6 s, idle = 4 s.**

**Performance Metrics:**

$$\eta = \frac{30}{4 \times 10} \times 100\% = \frac{30}{40} \times 100\% = 75\%$$

$$\text{BD} = 1 - 0.75 = 25\%$$

$$\text{Total Idle} = 0 + 2 + 5 + 4 = 11 \text{ seconds}$$

**Result:** N = 4, η = 75%, BD = 25%.

---

> **Q3 (B).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]
> A company wants to assemble a product with **7 tasks** having times: A=3, B=4, C=2, D=5, E=1, F=3, G=2 (seconds). Precedence: A, B precede C; B precedes D; C, D precede E; E, F precede G.
> **(a)** If the desired output is **1500 units per 8-hour day**, determine the **cycle time** and **minimum number of stations**.
> **(b)** Balance the line using the **LOT rule** and compute **efficiency**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks] — Cycle Time and Minimum Stations.**

Available production time per day = 8 × 60 × 60 = 28,800 s (assuming minutes: 480 min).
Using 480 minutes for simplicity:

$$C = \frac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Desired Output}} = \frac{480 \text{ min}}{1500} = 0.32 \text{ min/unit} = 19.2 \text{ s/unit}$$

Total work $T = 3+4+2+5+1+3+2 = 20$ s.

$$N_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{20}{19.2} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.04 \rceil = 2 \text{ stations}$$

**(b) [7 marks] — LOT Balancing.**

LOT Computation:
- LOT(G) = 1
- LOT(F) = 1 + LOT(G) = 2
- LOT(E) = 1 + LOT(G) = 2
- LOT(D) = 1 + LOT(E) = 3
- LOT(C) = 1 + LOT(E) = 3
- LOT(B) = 1 + LOT(C) + LOT(D) = 1 + 3 + 3 = 7
- LOT(A) = 1 + LOT(C) = 4

| Task | tᵢ | LOT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | 3 | 4 |
| B | 4 | 7 |
| C | 2 | 3 |
| D | 5 | 3 |
| E | 1 | 2 |
| F | 3 | 2 |
| G | 2 | 1 |

Cycle time C = 19.2 s.

**Station 1:** Available: A (4), B (7). Pick B → time = 4. Available: A, C, D. Pick A (LOT=4) → time = 7. Available: C (3), D (3). Tie — pick longest time: D → 7+5=12. Available: C → 12+2=14. Available: E (predecessor C, D done) → 14+1=15. Available: F (predecessor E done) → 15+3=18. Try G: 18+2=20 > 19.2 ❌.
- **Station 1 = {B, A, D, C, E, F}, time = 18 s, idle = 1.2 s.**

**Station 2:** Available: G. Pick G → time = 2 s, idle = 17.2 s.
- **Station 2 = {G}, time = 2 s, idle = 17.2 s.**

**Performance:**

$$\eta = \frac{20}{2 \times 19.2} \times 100\% = \frac{20}{38.4} \times 100\% \approx 52.08\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in LOT formula:** Students often compute LOT as just the *number of followers*, omitting the task itself. This is acceptable in some texts, but the KTU valuation key expects the recursive formula **LOT(i) = 1 + ΣLOT(j)**. Missing this costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Ignoring tie-breakers:** When two tasks share the same LOT, students arbitrarily pick one. Always state the tie-breaker explicitly: **longest task time** wins.
> 3. **Wrong cycle time formula:** Mixing up C = Available Time / Output (correct) with C = Output / Available Time (wrong). This is a **2-mark blunder** in 14-mark problems.
> 4. **Forgetting to close a station:** When a task does not fit, the station must be closed and a new one opened. Many students continue trying to fit the task, violating cycle time — **deduct 1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing seconds and minutes in efficiency calculation → **deduct 1 mark** if not uniform.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Assembly Line Balancing by LOT Rule**

- **ALB goal:** Minimize number of stations **N** subject to cycle time **C** and precedence constraints.
- **LOT = 1 + Σ(LOT of all immediate successors)** — recursive, bottom-up computation.
- **LOT priority is descending:** Highest LOT task assigned first at every station.
- **Tie-breaker hierarchy:** (1) Longest task time → (2) Most followers → (3) Alphabetical/earliest label.
- **Cycle time formula:** $C = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Desired Output Rate}}$.
- **Minimum stations:** $N_{\min} = \lceil T / C \rceil$ (theoretical lower bound).
- **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N \cdot C} \times 100\%$ — always multiply N (actual stations opened) by C, **not** $N_{\min}$.
- **Balance delay:** $\text{BD} = 1 - \eta$; always express as a fraction or percentage.
- **Total idle time** = $N \cdot C - \sum t_i$ = sum of idle time at all stations.
- **Heuristic nature:** LOT does *not* guarantee an optimal solution; it is a fast, near-optimal greedy approach.
- **LOT vs RPW distinction:** LOT counts *number* of tasks remaining; RPW (Ranked Positional Weight) sums *times* of successors + the task itself. Both are common in KTU Module 2 — do not confuse.
- **A "good" balance** typically has η ≥ 80% and idle time distributed as evenly as possible (no station with 100% load while another has 50% idle).
- **Practical extension:** In industry, LOT is often combined with COMSOAL (Computer Method of Sequencing Operations for Assembly Lines) for stochastic output lines.
- **Edge case:** If a task has LOT = 1, it is a *terminal* task (no successors) — always assigned last.
- **Always draw the precedence diagram first** — KTU examiners reward diagram-based clarity with **partial marks** even if the final allocation has minor errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Location decisions and models: facility location, objective, factors that influence location decision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Location decisions and models: facility location, objective, factors that influence location decision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Facility Location

**Facility Location** is a strategic, long-term capacity decision in Operations Management that determines the *geographic site* for establishing a manufacturing plant, warehouse, service center, retail outlet, or any other operational unit. It involves the systematic evaluation of multiple geographic alternatives to identify the optimal site that best aligns with the organization's strategic objectives, cost structure, and service requirements.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **location decision** is treated as a *strategic-level decision* because:
- It involves **substantial capital investment** (often irreversible or very costly to reverse).
- It commits the firm to a particular **labor market, supply network, and customer base** for a long horizon (typically **10–20 years**).
- It directly influences **operating costs, logistics costs, lead time, and competitive positioning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Memorize Verbatim for 3-mark Qs):**
> *"Facility location is a strategic decision in operations management that determines the geographic placement of a firm's physical facilities, with the objective of optimizing the trade-off between cost minimization, revenue maximization, and service quality, while balancing qualitative and quantitative factors."*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you are a student choosing **where to build your dream home**. You would not just pick a plot randomly. Instead, you would consider:
- **Distance to your college/workplace** (proximity to market/customer)
- **Quality of road and bus connectivity** (transportation infrastructure)
- **Nearby hospitals, schools, and shops** (community and supplier proximity)
- **Cost of land and construction** (cost factor)
- **Safety and neighborhood** (qualitative factors)
- **Future resale value** (strategic / long-term implication)

Once built, you **cannot easily move the house** to a new location. The same logic applies to a factory, warehouse, or retail store — a wrong location locks the company into high costs, poor service, and competitive disadvantage for decades.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic vs. Operational Location Decisions:**
> - **Strategic Location Decision (Global/Macro):** Country and region selection (e.g., India vs. Vietnam).
> - **Tactical Location Decision (Meso):** City and district selection.
> - **Operational Location Decision (Micro):** Specific plot/site selection within a city.
>
> The KTU module focuses on the **strategic and tactical levels** of this hierarchy.

---

## 1.3 Objectives of Facility Location

The core **objectives** that a facility location decision seeks to achieve are:

1. **Cost Minimization** — Minimize total landed cost (sum of fixed cost + variable transportation + labor + utility + tax costs).
2. **Revenue / Profit Maximization** — Maximize market coverage, customer accessibility, and service level.
3. **Strategic Fit** — Align with the firm's long-term competitive strategy (cost leadership vs. differentiation).
4. **Service Level Optimization** — Reduce lead time, improve responsiveness, and enhance customer satisfaction.
5. **Risk Diversification** — Avoid single-point failures, geopolitical risk, natural-disaster exposure.
6. **Community and Stakeholder Acceptance** — Ensure social license to operate, regulatory compliance, and CSR alignment.
7. **Scalability and Future Expansion** — Allow for future capacity expansion without relocation.
8. **Workforce Availability** — Secure a pool of skilled, semi-skilled, and unskilled labor at sustainable wage levels.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** A common KTU question asks — *"List the objectives of facility location."* Always present them as a **numbered list with brief justification** (1 line each). Do not merely state the heading.

---

## 1.4 Global vs. Domestic Location Decisions

| Dimension | Global Location | Domestic Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Cross-border, multi-country | Within a single country |
| **Key Drivers** | Tariffs, FDI rules, currency risk, labor arbitrage | State taxes, regional incentives, logistics cost |
| **Risk Level** | High (political, exchange-rate, legal) | Moderate |
| **Reversibility** | Very low (sunk cost) | Low to moderate |
| **Time Horizon** | 15–25 years | 10–20 years |
| **Example** | Apple shifting production to India | Flipkart opening a new warehouse in Kerala |

> [!NOTE]
> All numerical examples and KTU numerical questions in this module are typically framed at the **domestic level** (within a country), unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

## 1.5 Service Location vs. Manufacturing Location

| Feature | Manufacturing Plant | Service Facility |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Minimize production + logistics cost | Maximize customer accessibility |
| **Key Factor** | Proximity to raw material & labor | Proximity to market / population density |
| **Output** | Tangible goods | Intangible services |
| **Examples** | Steel plant, automobile assembly | Bank branch, hospital, restaurant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **service facilities**, the *revenue side* (demand catchment) outweighs the *cost side*. For **manufacturing facilities**, the *cost side* (raw material, labor, transport) usually dominates. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize this distinction in case-based questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Factors Influencing Location Decision

Location factors are broadly classified into **Primary (Quantitative)** and **Secondary (Qualitative)** factors. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know both categories with examples.

### A. Primary / Quantitative Factors (Cost & Revenue Drivers)

1. **Proximity to Markets**
   - Reduces outbound transportation cost and delivery lead time.
   - Critical for perishable, fragile, or high-value goods.
   - Metric: Distance to nearest major demand center, weighted by demand volume.

2. **Proximity to Suppliers / Raw Materials**
   - Reduces inbound transportation cost.
   - Critical for **weight-losing industries** (e.g., sugar refining, ore smelting) where raw material is bulkier/heavier than finished product.
   - Metric: Distance to primary supplier hubs, supplier reliability index.

3. **Labor Availability and Cost**
   - Includes wage rates, productivity, skill level, and unionization.
   - **Labor-intensive industries** (textiles, garments, BPO) are highly sensitive to labor cost.
   - Metric: Average wage per skill category, labor productivity index.

4. **Transportation and Logistics Infrastructure**
   - Access to highways, ports, airports, rail terminals.
   - Cost of freight (per ton-km) by available modes.
   - Reliability and frequency of transport services.

5. **Cost of Land and Construction**
   - Land acquisition cost, lease/rental cost, construction cost per sq. ft.
   - Higher in metropolitan areas; lower in Tier-2/3 cities.

6. **Utilities and Infrastructure**
   - Power availability and cost (industrial tariff vs. domestic tariff).
   - Water supply, effluent disposal, gas pipeline, broadband connectivity.

7. **Taxes and Government Incentives**
   - Corporate tax rate, GST implications.
   - Subsidies, tax holidays, capital grants (e.g., SEZ benefits, state industrial subsidies).

### B. Secondary / Qualitative Factors (Strategic & Environmental)

8. **Government Policies and Political Stability**
   - FDI caps, labor laws, environmental regulations, ease of doing business rank.

9. **Community Attitude and Social Acceptance**
   - Local population's perception of the industry (NIMBY — "Not In My Backyard" syndrome for polluting industries).
   - CSR, local employment generation.

10. **Environmental and Ecological Considerations**
    - Pollution control norms, carbon footprint, water-table impact.
    - Critical for chemical, petrochemical, and mining units.

11. **Competitor Location (Cluster Effect)**
    - Clustering near competitors (e.g., auto ancillaries near OEM plants) can yield agglomeration benefits.
    - Conversely, locating in a saturated market may intensify price competition.

12. **Quality of Life and Livability for Employees**
    - Housing, healthcare, education, climate.
    - Important for attracting and retaining skilled workforce.

13. **Future Expansion Possibility**
    - Availability of contiguous land for capacity expansion.

14. **Security and Risk Profile**
    - Crime rate, natural disaster exposure (floods, earthquakes, cyclones), political unrest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark-Worthy Insight:**
> *Industries that are **weight-losing** (lose weight during processing) should be located **near raw material source**. Industries that are **weight-gaining** (gain weight during processing) or are **perishable** should be located **near market**.*
> - **Weight-losing examples:** Sugar, cement, paper, ore smelting, refining.
> - **Weight-gaining examples:** Soft drink bottling, bakeries, paint mixing, vehicle assembly.
> - **Perishable / Fragile:** Bakery, dairy, fresh produce, glass items, pharmaceuticals.

---

## 2.2 The Location Decision Process (Step-by-Step)

A structured decision-making process is followed:

1. **Identify the need** for a new facility (capacity expansion, new product, market entry).
2. **Identify the relevant region/country** using macro-level factors.
3. **Identify the community/city** using labor, infrastructure, and tax filters.
4. **Identify specific sites** using detailed cost, layout, and qualitative factors.
5. **Evaluate alternatives** using quantitative models (Factor Rating, Break-Even, Center of Gravity, etc.).
6. **Select optimal location** based on composite score.
7. **Implement and monitor** — post-implementation review.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Model / Concept | Formula | Notation & Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Factor Rating Method (FRM)** | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | $S_i$ = score of site $i$; $w_j$ = weight of factor $j$ (sums to 1.0 or 100%); $r_{ij}$ = rating of site $i$ on factor $j$ (typically 1–10 scale) | Comparing 2+ sites across multiple qualitative + quantitative factors |
| 2 | **Total Cost Comparison** | $TC_i = FC_i + \sum_{k} v_{ik} \cdot Q_{ik}$ | $TC_i$ = total annual cost at site $i$; $FC_i$ = fixed cost; $v_{ik}$ = variable cost per unit of activity $k$; $Q_{ik}$ = quantity of activity $k$ at site $i$ | When costs are dominant and easily quantifiable |
| 3 | **Break-Even (Crossover) Analysis** | $Q^* = \dfrac{FC_A - FC_B}{v_B - v_A}$ | $Q^*$ = break-even volume; $FC$ = fixed cost; $v$ = variable cost per unit | Identifying the volume range where one site is cheaper than another |
| 4 | **Center of Gravity (CoG) — X coord.** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} x_i \cdot Q_i}{\sum_{i} Q_i}$ | $\bar{x}$ = optimal x-coordinate; $x_i$ = x-coord. of source $i$; $Q_i$ = load/demand at $i$ (tons, units) | Minimizing weighted distance for a single new facility serving known sources |
| 5 | **Center of Gravity — Y coord.** | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} y_i \cdot Q_i}{\sum_{i} Q_i}$ | $\bar{y}$ = optimal y-coordinate; $y_i$ = y-coord. of source $i$ | Same as above (must be used together with $\bar{x}$) |
| 6 | **Load-Distance Score** | $LD_i = \sum_{j} d_{ij} \cdot L_{ij}$ | $LD_i$ = load-distance for candidate site $i$; $d_{ij}$ = distance from $i$ to $j$; $L_{ij}$ = load moved | Ranking candidate sites by total weighted distance |
| 7 | **Transportation Cost** | $TC_{transport} = \sum_{i} \sum_{j} d_{ij} \cdot L_{ij} \cdot c_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}$ = cost per ton-km between $i$ and $j$ | Total logistics cost calculation |
| 8 | **Agglomeration Index (qualitative)** | $AI = \dfrac{\text{Benefits from cluster}}{\text{Disadvantages from competition}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Qualitative cluster decision support |

> [!WARNING]
> In the Factor Rating Method, ensure the **weights sum to 1.0 (or 100%)** and the **rating scale is consistent** (e.g., 1 = poor, 10 = excellent) across all factors. Inconsistency in the rating scale is the most common mistake.

---

## 2.4 Weight-Loss Rule — Worked Heuristic

The **Raw Material Oriented (RMO) vs. Market Oriented (MO)** decision is decided by the **Weight-Loss Index (WLI)**:

$$WLI = \dfrac{W_{RM} - W_{FP}}{W_{RM}} \times 100\%$$

Where $W_{RM}$ = weight of raw material input, $W_{FP}$ = weight of finished product.

- If $WLI > 0$ (weight loss) → **Locate near raw material source**.
- If $WLI < 0$ (weight gain) → **Locate near market**.
- If $WLI \approx 0$ → Other factors dominate (labor, infrastructure, etc.).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Industry Applications

- **Automobile OEMs (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai):** Located near Tier-2 cities (Gurgaon, Chennai) for skilled labor + port access for exports.
- **Cement Plants (e.g., UltraTech, ACC):** Raw-material-oriented because limestone (raw material) loses ~30% weight as dust during processing; located near limestone mines.
- **E-commerce Warehouses (Amazon, Flipkart):** Market-oriented, located near major consumption hubs (Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi-NCR).
- **Software Parks (Infosys, TCS):** Skilled-labor-oriented, located in cities with engineering college clusters.
- **BPO/KPO Units:** Low-cost-labor-oriented, located in Tier-2/3 cities with high English-speaking graduate population.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Factor Rating Method (Composite Score Model)

**Statement:** For $m$ candidate sites and $n$ decision factors, each with weight $w_j$ and rating $r_{ij}$ for site $i$ on factor $j$, the composite score of site $i$ is the **weighted sum of ratings**.

### Mathematical Derivation

Let:
- $S_i$ = composite score of site $i$, for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$
- $w_j$ = normalized weight of factor $j$, for $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$
- $r_{ij}$ = rating of site $i$ on factor $j$ (integer 1–10)
- Constraint: $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1.0$

By the **principle of additive weighted aggregation** (linear utility model), the total utility (score) of a site is the linear sum of the weighted utilities of each factor:

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

**Why linear and not multiplicative?**
- The linear model is preferred when factors are **mutually preference-independent** (the preference for one factor does not depend on the level of another). For most location decisions this assumption holds.
- A multiplicative model $\prod_j r_{ij}^{w_j}$ is rarely used because it is highly sensitive to any single factor being near zero.

The optimal site $i^*$ is:

$$
i^* = \arg\max_i \left\{ S_i \right\}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

A firm is choosing between **3 sites: A, B, C** for a new manufacturing plant. **4 factors** are considered. Weights sum to 1.0.

| Factor | Weight $w_j$ | Rating A | Rating B | Rating C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Labor cost | **0.30** | 7 | 8 | 6 |
| Proximity to market | **0.25** | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| Power availability | **0.20** | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Tax incentives | **0.25** | 9 | 6 | 8 |
| **Total Weight Check** | **1.00** | — | — | — |

**Step 1 — Compute composite score for Site A:**

$$
S_A = (0.30 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9)
$$

$$
S_A = 2.10 + 1.50 + 1.60 + 2.25 = 7.45
$$

**Step 2 — Compute composite score for Site B:**

$$
S_B = (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 6)
$$

$$
S_B = 2.40 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 1.50 = 7.55
$$

**Step 3 — Compute composite score for Site C:**

$$
S_C = (0.30 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_C = 1.80 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 2.00 = 7.35
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

Since $S_B = 7.55 > S_A = 7.45 > S_C = 7.35$, **Site B is selected**.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **Site A wins on Tax Incentives and Power**, but **Site B wins on Labor and Market** — the higher weights on labor + market (0.55 combined) tilt the decision toward B. This illustrates the **dominance of factor weights** in the FRM.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Center of Gravity (CoG) Method

**Statement:** Find the coordinates $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ of a new facility such that the **sum of weighted distances** to all existing sources/destinations is minimized.

### Mathematical Derivation

Let there be $n$ existing points (sources/destinations) with:
- Coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$
- Loads (demand or supply volume) $L_i$ at each point

The total load-distance is:

$$
LD(\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \cdot \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_i)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_i)^2}
$$

To minimize $LD(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$, take **partial derivatives** and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial LD}{\partial \bar{x}} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial LD}{\partial \bar{y}} = 0
$$

For point $i$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \bar{x}} \left[ L_i \sqrt{(\bar{x}-x_i)^2 + (\bar{y}-y_i)^2} \right] = L_i \cdot \frac{\bar{x} - x_i}{\sqrt{(\bar{x}-x_i)^2 + (\bar{y}-y_i)^2}} = 0
$$

Summing over all $i$ and solving:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot L_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i} \quad ; \quad \bar{y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \cdot L_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i}
$$

These are the **center of mass** formulas — analogous to physics, where loads $L_i$ play the role of masses.

> [!NOTE]
> The CoG method assumes **Euclidean (straight-line) distance**, **constant cost per unit distance per unit load**, and that the **new facility is a single point**. It gives a *starting point* for site search, not the final site.

### Worked Numerical Example

A company wants to locate a **central warehouse** serving **4 retail stores** with the following data:

| Store | X-coord $x_i$ (km) | Y-coord $y_i$ (km) | Monthly Demand $L_i$ (tons) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 20 | 50 |
| 2 | 30 | 15 | 80 |
| 3 | 25 | 40 | 60 |
| 4 | 40 | 30 | 110 |
| **Total** | — | — | **300** |

**Step 1 — Compute $\sum x_i \cdot L_i$:**

$$
10 \times 50 = 500
$$

$$
30 \times 80 = 2400
$$

$$
25 \times 60 = 1500
$$

$$
40 \times 110 = 4400
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} x_i \cdot L_i = 500 + 2400 + 1500 + 4400 = 8800
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sum y_i \cdot L_i$:**

$$
20 \times 50 = 1000
$$

$$
15 \times 80 = 1200
$$

$$
40 \times 60 = 2400
$$

$$
30 \times 110 = 3300
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} y_i \cdot L_i = 1000 + 1200 + 2400 + 3300 = 7900
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{8800}{300} = 29.33 \text{ km}
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{7900}{300} = 26.33 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The optimal warehouse location is approximately **(29.33, 26.33)**, which is heavily influenced by Store 4 (the largest demand of 110 tons).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Break-Even / Crossover Analysis for Two Sites

**Statement:** Find the production volume $Q^*$ at which the total cost of two alternative sites becomes equal.

### Mathematical Derivation

Total cost at site A: $TC_A(Q) = FC_A + v_A \cdot Q$

Total cost at site B: $TC_B(Q) = FC_B + v_B \cdot Q$

Setting $TC_A = TC_B$:

$$
FC_A + v_A \cdot Q^* = FC_B + v_B \cdot Q^*
$$

$$
FC_A - FC_B = (v_B - v_A) \cdot Q^*
$$

$$
Q^* = \frac{FC_A - FC_B}{v_B - v_A}
$$

**Decision rule:**
- If expected volume $Q < Q^*$ → choose the site with **lower variable cost** (assuming the other has lower fixed cost).
- If expected volume $Q > Q^*$ → choose the site with **higher fixed cost but lower variable cost**.

### Worked Numerical Example

| Site | Fixed Cost (₹/year) | Variable Cost (₹/unit) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 5,00,000 | 250 |
| B | 8,00,000 | 200 |

$$
Q^* = \frac{5,00,000 - 8,00,000}{200 - 250} = \frac{-3,00,000}{-50} = 6000 \text{ units/year}
$$

**Interpretation:** If expected annual production is **less than 6,000 units**, Site A is cheaper (lower fixed cost dominates). If production is **more than 6,000 units**, Site B is cheaper (lower variable cost dominates).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Quick Reference)

| Decision Scenario | Recommended Primary Factor | Recommended Model |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy weight-losing process (e.g., cement, sugar) | Raw material proximity | Total Cost / Weight-Loss Rule |
| Perishable / fragile product (e.g., dairy, bread) | Market proximity | Factor Rating (market weight > 0.4) |
| Labor-intensive (e.g., garments, BPO) | Labor cost & availability | Factor Rating (labor weight > 0.3) |
| Multiple geographic sources with weighted loads | Distance minimization | Center of Gravity |
| Cost data available for 2+ sites, volume uncertain | Crossover volume | Break-Even Analysis |
| Multiple qualitative + quantitative factors | Composite scoring | Factor Rating Method |
| Global entry into new country | Macro-political + labor cost | Country Portfolio Matrix (qualitative) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*"Define facility location. State any four objectives of facility location decision."*

**Model Answer (≈120 words):**

**Definition:** Facility location is a strategic decision in operations management that determines the *geographic placement* of a firm's manufacturing plant, warehouse, or service center, with the goal of optimizing the trade-off between cost, revenue, and service level.

**Four Objectives:**
1. **Minimize total cost** (land + labor + logistics + utilities + tax).
2. **Maximize market coverage and customer accessibility** for revenue growth.
3. **Ensure long-term strategic fit** with the firm's competitive strategy.
4. **Achieve high service level** through reduced lead time and reliable delivery.
5. (Optional) Risk diversification and community acceptance.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*"Distinguish between primary and secondary factors influencing facility location. Give two examples of each."*

**Model Answer (≈130 words):**

| Aspect | Primary (Quantitative) Factors | Secondary (Qualitative) Factors |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Measurable in monetary or numeric terms | Subjective, judgment-based |
| **Examples** | 1. Labor cost  2. Transportation cost | 1. Government policy stability  2. Community attitude |
| **Treatment in FRM** | Assigned weights and ratings | Assigned weights and ratings on a 1–10 scale |
| **Decision Impact** | Usually dominant in numerical scoring | Often used as a tie-breaker |

> Key contrast: *Primary factors are objective and financially quantifiable; secondary factors are strategic, environmental, or social in nature and require expert judgment.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Part B carries 14 marks, typically split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each: **(a)** descriptive/analytical, **(b)** numerical/case-based. Students answer **ONE full question** (either Choice A or Choice B).

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2, 2024 Scheme]*

**Q.A.** *(a)* Explain in detail the **various factors that influence facility location decisions**, classifying them as primary (quantitative) and secondary (qualitative). Highlight the **weight-loss vs. weight-gaining industry rule** with two examples each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm is evaluating **three sites: X, Y, Z** for a new plant. The decision factors, weights, and ratings (on a 1–10 scale) are given below. **Compute the composite score** using the Factor Rating Method and recommend the best site. **(7 marks)**

| Factor | Weight | Rating X | Rating Y | Rating Z |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Proximity to market | 0.30 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Labor cost & skill | 0.25 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Power & water supply | 0.20 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| Tax & incentives | 0.15 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| Land cost (lower is better) | 0.10 | 7 | 5 | 8 |
| **Total** | **1.00** | — | — | — |

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Theoretical Part (7 marks):**

The answer should cover the full taxonomy from **Section 2.1** of these notes:
- **Primary factors (with definitions):** Proximity to market, raw material, labor, transport, utilities, taxes.
- **Secondary factors:** Government policy, community attitude, environment, competition, quality of life, security.
- **Weight-Loss Rule** with formula:

$$
WLI = \frac{W_{RM} - W_{FP}}{W_{RM}} \times 100\%
$$

- **Weight-Losing Industries (RMO):** Cement, sugar, paper, ore smelting. (2 examples — 1 mark)
- **Weight-Gaining / Perishable Industries (MO):** Soft drink bottling, bakery, automobile assembly. (2 examples — 1 mark)
- Closing remark: For industries with $WLI \approx 0$, the **Factor Rating Method** is the most appropriate decision tool.

**(b) Numerical Part (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Verify weight sum:** $0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 2 — Compute composite score for Site X:**

$$
S_X = (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 7)
$$

$$
S_X = 2.40 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 0.90 + 0.70 = 7.55
$$

**Step 3 — Compute composite score for Site Y:**

$$
S_Y = (0.30 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 5)
$$

$$
S_Y = 2.10 + 2.25 + 1.60 + 1.20 + 0.50 = 7.65
$$

**Step 4 — Compute composite score for Site Z:**

$$
S_Z = (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_Z = 2.70 + 1.50 + 1.40 + 1.05 + 0.80 = 7.45
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$
S_Y = 7.65 > S_X = 7.55 > S_Z = 7.45
$$

✅ **Recommendation: Site Y is the optimal choice**, primarily driven by its strong rating on labor cost & skill (rating 9) and tax incentives (rating 8).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to verify that weights sum to 1.00 — a 1-mark deduction is applied if weights are not normalized. Always check **$\sum w_j = 1$** before computing the score.
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** A common arithmetic error is mixing up decimal weights (0.30) and percentage weights (30%). Confirm with the question stem. If weights are given as percentages, divide by 100 before multiplication.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 3, 2024 Scheme]*

**Q.B.** *(a)* Describe the **Center of Gravity (CoG) method** for facility location with its assumptions and limitations. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A company wants to establish a **central warehouse** to serve four distributors located at the following coordinates with the given monthly loads. Using the **Center of Gravity method**, determine the optimal location of the warehouse. **(7 marks)**

| Distributor | X (km) | Y (km) | Load (tons/month) |
|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | 5 | 10 | 200 |
| D2 | 20 | 5 | 300 |
| D3 | 15 | 25 | 400 |
| D4 | 30 | 20 | 500 |

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Theoretical Part (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Center of Gravity method is a quantitative location model that finds the optimal coordinates $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ for a single new facility such that the total weighted distance (load × distance) to all existing sources/destinations is minimized.

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i L_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i} \quad ; \quad \bar{y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i L_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i}
$$

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Distance is measured in **Euclidean (straight-line)** metric.
2. Cost per unit distance per unit load is **constant** across all pairs.
3. The new facility is a **single point** (not a region).
4. Loads are **stationary** (do not change with time).

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. Ignores **terrain and travel constraints** (e.g., rivers, mountains).
2. Does not consider **land cost, labor, or qualitative factors** at the recommended point.
3. Result is a **mathematical ideal point** that may not coincide with a buildable, zoned plot.
4. Assumes **continuous, unbounded plane** — but in reality, only discrete sites are available.

**(b) Numerical Part (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Tabulate products $x_i L_i$ and $y_i L_i$:**

| Distributor | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $L_i$ | $x_i L_i$ | $y_i L_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | 5 | 10 | 200 | 1000 | 2000 |
| D2 | 20 | 5 | 300 | 6000 | 1500 |
| D3 | 15 | 25 | 400 | 6000 | 10000 |
| D4 | 30 | 20 | 500 | 15000 | 10000 |
| **Total** | — | — | **1400** | **28000** | **23500** |

**Step 2 — Compute $\bar{x}$:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{28000}{1400} = 20.00 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\bar{y}$:**

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{23500}{1400} = 16.79 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

✅ **Optimal warehouse location is approximately (20.00, 16.79) km**, with the highest influence from **D4** (largest load of 500 tons) and **D3** (largest weighted Y-coordinate contribution).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** A common mistake is computing $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$ as **simple averages** (i.e., $(5+20+15+30)/4$) instead of **weighted averages** (load-weighted). The CoG method **must** use weighted coordinates — failing this loses 3 marks outright.
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #4:** Another frequent error is forgetting the units (km vs. m) and producing a final answer without units. Always state the units: *"(20.00 km, 16.79 km)"*.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warning (Consolidated)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Most Common Mark-Deduction Points in KTU Location-Decision Questions:**
> 1. **Failing to normalize weights** in the Factor Rating Method to sum = 1.0.
> 2. **Confusing weight-loss vs. weight-gaining industries** — a single wrong classification can cost 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Using simple average instead of weighted average** in the Center of Gravity method.
> 4. **Ignoring qualitative factors** entirely in numerical questions — examiners expect at least a 1–2 line discussion of qualitative factors.
> 5. **Not stating the decision** explicitly at the end of a numerical answer — always end with *"Hence, Site X is selected"* or *"Optimal location is (x̄, ȳ)."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Sheet — Location Decisions and Models**

### Core Concepts (Memorize)
- ☐ **Facility location** is a *strategic, long-term, irreversible* decision in OM.
- ☐ **Three decision levels:** Strategic (country) → Tactical (city) → Operational (site).
- ☐ **Objectives:** Cost minimization, revenue maximization, strategic fit, service level, risk diversification, scalability, community acceptance.
- ☐ **Two categories of factors:** Primary (quantitative) and Secondary (qualitative).

### Primary Factors (List with 1-line meaning)
- ☐ Proximity to **markets** (for perishable, weight-gaining, service units).
- ☐ Proximity to **raw material** (for weight-losing, bulk-reducing units).
- ☐ **Labor** cost, skill, productivity, and unionization.
- ☐ **Transportation** infrastructure and freight cost.
- ☐ **Land & utilities** (power, water, gas, broadband).
- ☐ **Taxes** and government incentives (SEZ, subsidies).

### Secondary Factors (List with 1-line meaning)
- ☐ **Government policy** and political stability.
- ☐ **Community attitude** and CSR.
- ☐ **Environmental** and ecological norms.
- ☐ **Competitor clustering** (agglomeration).
- ☐ **Quality of life** for employees.
- ☐ **Security and disaster risk**.

### Key Industry Rules
- ☐ **Weight-losing (WLI > 0)** → Locate near **raw material** (cement, sugar, smelting).
- ☐ **Weight-gaining (WLI < 0)** → Locate near **market** (bottling, bakery, FMCG).
- ☐ **Labor-intensive** → Locate near **skilled/cheap labor** (garments, BPO, software).
- ☐ **Perishable/fragile** → Locate near **market** (dairy, fresh produce, glass).

### Formula Sheet (Must remember for numerical Qs)
- ☐ **Factor Rating:** $S_i = \sum_j w_j r_{ij}$  (weights sum to 1.0).
- ☐ **Center of Gravity:** $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i L_i}{\sum L_i}$, $\bar{y} = \frac{\sum y_i L_i}{\sum L_i}$.
- ☐ **Break-Even:** $Q^* = \frac{FC_A - FC_B}{v_B - v_A}$.
- ☐ **Load-Distance:** $LD_i = \sum_j d_{ij} L_{ij}$.

### Model Selection Quick Guide
- ☐ Multiple factors, 2+ sites → **Factor Rating Method**.
- ☐ Weighted load minimization, 1 facility → **Center of Gravity**.
- ☐ Cost data + 2 sites, volume uncertainty → **Break-Even Analysis**.
- ☐ Cost data + multiple sites, deterministic volume → **Total Cost Comparison**.

### Final Exam Strategy
- ☐ In 14-mark answers, **always pair** a theoretical section (~5–6 marks) with a numerical section (~7–8 marks).
- ☐ **End every numerical answer with a one-line decision statement.**
- ☐ **Use 1–10 rating scale consistently** in FRM problems.
- ☐ **Draw a flowchart or table** wherever possible to score presentation marks.
- ☐ For 3-mark questions, **write 100–130 words** with crisp definitions and bullet points.

---

*End of Notes — Module 2, Topic: Location Decisions and Models*
