> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Location evaluation methods: factor rating method and centre of gravity method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 LOCATION EVALUATION METHODS: A KTU 2024 SCHEME STUDY NOTE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Location Evaluation" in Operations Management?

In the discipline of **Operations Management**, the *location decision* is a classic **strategic, long-term, capital-intensive** decision that fixes the physical situs of a facility (plant, warehouse, retail outlet, service center) for years or decades. Once committed, relocation costs are punitive, and reversing the decision is rare. *Location evaluation* is therefore the formal, structured process of comparing candidate sites using a mix of **qualitative factors** (e.g., labor climate, government policy, community attitude) and **quantitative factors** (e.g., transportation cost, raw-material distance) so that the chosen site maximizes long-run profitability, service quality, and competitive advantage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECBT865, Module 2):**
> "Location evaluation methods are systematic analytical techniques used to compare alternative sites for a facility by quantifying multiple qualitative and quantitative criteria, leading to a rational, defensible location decision that aligns with the firm's strategic objectives."

### 1.2 The Two Methods Under Study

This module focuses on the two most frequently tested, most KTU-relevant techniques:

| # | Method | Nature | When Used |
|---|--------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | **Factor Rating Method** (also called *Weighted Factor Rating* or *Multi-Factor Rating*) | Qualitative-quantitative, multi-criteria decision-making | Comparing 2 or more candidate cities/regions on many subjective + objective criteria |
| 2 | **Centre of Gravity Method** | Purely quantitative, geometric/mathematical | Finding the optimal single coordinate (x, y) for a *new* facility (e.g., distribution centre) to minimize weighted distance to existing sources/customers |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The House-Hunting Analogy"

Imagine you are buying a house in Kerala (say, in Kochi, Trivandrum, or Calicut). You create a checklist:
- *Distance to office* (weight: 30%)
- *School quality* (weight: 25%)
- *Price* (weight: 25%)
- *Neighborhood safety* (weight: 20%)

You score each candidate house on every factor (say, 1–10), multiply by the weight, sum up — and the house with the **highest weighted total** is your best match. That is exactly the **Factor Rating Method** in operations management.

For the **Centre of Gravity Method**, picture a pizza delivery start-up. You have 5 existing demand zones across the city, each placing a different *volume* of daily orders. To position the central kitchen so that average delivery distance (and hence fuel + time cost) is minimized, you would literally place a physical weight (proportional to volume) on a map at each demand point, and find the **balance point of the entire system**. That balance point is the *centre of gravity* — the coordinate-minimum cost location.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Remember (High-Frequency KTU Question):**
> - *Factor Rating* answers: **"Which city/site is best among alternatives?"** → discrete choice.
> - *Centre of Gravity* answers: **"Where exactly should I build on the map?"** → continuous coordinate.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factor Rating Method — Underlying Logic

The **Factor Rating Method** converts a fuzzy, multi-criteria decision into a single **composite score** for each alternative. The logic proceeds in five disciplined steps:

1. **Identify relevant factors.** These include both *tangibles* (transport cost, labor wage, land cost, utility tariff, taxes) and *intangibles* (labor quality, community attitude, climate, proximity to market).
2. **Assign a weight (Wᵢ) to each factor** such that the sum of all weights = **1.00 (or 100%)**. Weights are derived from management's strategic priorities — a labor-intensive plant will weight *labor cost* heavily; a perishable-goods plant will weight *proximity to market* heavily.
3. **Score each candidate site (Sᵢⱼ) on every factor** on a consistent scale — usually **1 to 10** (1 = poorest, 10 = best). The scale must be uniform across all factors and all sites.
4. **Compute the weighted score for each factor–site pair:** $W_i \times S_{ij}$.
5. **Sum the weighted scores across all factors for each site.** The site with the **highest total composite score** is recommended.

The mathematical model is:

$$
\text{Composite Score}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot S_{ij}
$$

where:
- $n$ = number of factors
- $j$ = index of candidate site (j = 1, 2, …, m)
- $W_i$ = weight of factor *i* (with $\sum W_i = 1$)
- $S_{ij}$ = score of site *j* on factor *i*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it works (the "Why"):** Real-world location decisions involve trade-offs (cheap labor vs. skilled labor; low land cost vs. poor infrastructure). A single number cannot capture all of them. By converting *heterogeneous criteria* (rupees, kilometers, morale index) into a *common dimensionless scale* (1–10) and then weighting by *strategic priority*, the method produces a fair, auditable, repeatable ranking.

#### 2.1.1 Variations of Factor Rating

- **Simple Ranking Method:** Ranks factors only, no weights.
- **Weighted Factor Rating (standard):** Weights + scores as above.
- **Factor Comparison Method:** A pair-wise comparison of factors to derive objective weights.
- **Locational Break-Even Analysis:** Finds the volume at which two sites are equally costly — sometimes used *alongside* factor rating.

### 2.2 Centre of Gravity Method — Underlying Logic

The **Centre of Gravity Method** is rooted in the classical mechanics principle: the *centre of mass* of a system of point-masses is the unique point where the sum of weighted position vectors is zero. Translating this to facility location:

- Treat each existing facility, supplier, or customer zone as a *point mass* with **mass = volume of goods moved** ($V_i$).
- Treat the *x-y* Cartesian coordinates of each as its *position vector*.
- The centre of gravity $(C_x, C_y)$ minimizes the **weighted sum of squared distances** (and is a strong approximation for minimizing total transportation cost when costs are proportional to distance × volume).

The governing formulas are:

$$
C_x = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}
$$

$$
C_y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \cdot V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}
$$

where:
- $C_x, C_y$ = optimal coordinates of the new facility
- $x_i, y_i$ = coordinates of the $i^{th}$ existing location
- $V_i$ = volume (or weight) of shipments to/from location $i$
- $n$ = number of existing locations

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Assumptions of the Centre of Gravity Method:**
> 1. Transportation cost is **directly proportional** to distance × volume.
> 2. Locations are on a **flat (Euclidean) plane** — topography and road curvature are ignored.
> 3. Volumes are **constant and known**.
> 4. The new facility is a **single point** (a warehouse, not a sprawling plant).

#### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Algorithm

1. Plot existing locations on an **x–y grid** (use a city map with km/scale or a hand-drawn Cartesian plane).
2. Tabulate $(x_i, y_i, V_i)$ for each location $i$.
3. Compute the products $x_i V_i$ and $y_i V_i$.
4. Sum all volumes $\sum V_i$, all $x_i V_i$, and all $y_i V_i$.
5. Divide the sums of products by the total volume to obtain $C_x$ and $C_y$.
6. Plot the resulting $(C_x, C_y)$ on the map — this is the recommended site for the new facility.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Factor Rating Method (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem Statement:**
A leading consumer-electronics firm, *BlueTech India*, is evaluating **three potential locations** — **Site A (Chennai)**, **Site B (Coimbatore)**, and **Site C (Kochi)** — for a new manufacturing plant. The strategic management team has identified **six key factors** and assigned weights and ratings (1–10 scale) as shown below. Determine the optimal site.

| # | Factor | Weight ($W_i$) | Site A Score | Site B Score | Site C Score |
|---|--------|---------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | Proximity to market | 0.20 | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| 2 | Labor cost (lower = better) | 0.15 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| 3 | Labor skill & availability | 0.20 | 8 | 7 | 7 |
| 4 | Transportation & logistics | 0.15 | 7 | 7 | 9 |
| 5 | Power & utility cost | 0.10 | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| 6 | Government incentives | 0.20 | 5 | 8 | 7 |

**Verification of Weights:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} W_i = 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.20 = 1.00
$$

> The weights are properly normalized, satisfying the model's constraint.

**Step 1 — Compute weighted scores for Site A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Site A: }  &(0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 5) \\
&= 1.60 + 1.05 + 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.60 + 1.00 \\
&= 6.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute weighted scores for Site B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Site B: }  &(0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) \\
&= 1.20 + 1.35 + 1.40 + 1.05 + 0.90 + 1.60 \\
&= 7.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute weighted scores for Site C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Site C: }  &(0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 7) \\
&= 1.80 + 0.90 + 1.40 + 1.35 + 0.70 + 1.40 \\
&= 7.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and Decision:**

| Site | Composite Score | Rank |
|------|-----------------|------|
| A — Chennai | 6.90 | 3rd |
| B — Coimbatore | 7.50 | 2nd |
| **C — Kochi** | **7.55** | **1st (Recommended)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision:** **Site C (Kochi)** wins by a narrow margin of **0.05 points** over Site B. In real life, BlueTech management would likely conduct **sensitivity analysis** (re-running the model with slightly different weights or scores) to verify robustness before committing. A 0.05 lead is thin — the KTU examiner will reward students who *flag this and recommend sensitivity testing* in their answer.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Centre of Gravity Method (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem Statement:**
*Kerala Logistics Pvt. Ltd.* is establishing a new central warehouse to serve **four existing retail hubs** in the state. The coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ and the projected monthly shipment volumes $(V_i)$ in tonnes are given. Determine the optimal coordinates of the new warehouse.

| Hub | $x_i$ (km) | $y_i$ (km) | $V_i$ (tonnes/month) |
|-----|------------|------------|----------------------|
| H1 (Trivandrum) | 10 | 5 | 1500 |
| H2 (Kollam) | 20 | 15 | 2500 |
| H3 (Kochi) | 30 | 25 | 4000 |
| H4 (Thrissur) | 40 | 35 | 2000 |

**Step 1 — Build the working table** and compute $x_i V_i$ and $y_i V_i$:

| Hub | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $V_i$ | $x_i V_i$ | $y_i V_i$ |
|-----|--------|--------|-------|-----------|-----------|
| H1 | 10 | 5 | 1500 | 15,000 | 7,500 |
| H2 | 20 | 15 | 2500 | 50,000 | 37,500 |
| H3 | 30 | 25 | 4000 | 120,000 | 100,000 |
| H4 | 40 | 35 | 2000 | 80,000 | 70,000 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **10,000** | **265,000** | **215,000** |

**Step 2 — Compute $\sum V_i$, $\sum x_i V_i$, $\sum y_i V_i$:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} V_i = 1500 + 2500 + 4000 + 2000 = 10{,}000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} x_i V_i = 15{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 120{,}000 + 80{,}000 = 265{,}000
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} y_i V_i = 7{,}500 + 37{,}500 + 100{,}000 + 70{,}000 = 215{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the centre of gravity formula for $C_x$:**

$$
C_x = \frac{\sum x_i V_i}{\sum V_i} = \frac{265{,}000}{10{,}000} = 26.5 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the centre of gravity formula for $C_y$:**

$$
C_y = \frac{\sum y_i V_i}{\sum V_i} = \frac{215{,}000}{10{,}000} = 21.5 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Recommendation:**

> **The new central warehouse should be located at coordinates (26.5 km, 21.5 km)** on the chosen grid. This point minimizes the volume-weighted transportation distance from the warehouse to the four retail hubs.

**Optional Step 6 — Sanity Check (Kochi has the highest volume of 4000 t, so the centre of gravity should be *pulled toward Kochi's coordinates* (30, 25) more than to others).** Our result $(26.5, 21.5)$ is between Trivandrum (10, 5) and Thrissur (40, 35), but **closer to Kochi** — exactly as the physics-of-mass intuition predicts. ✅

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Both Methods

| Dimension | Factor Rating Method | Centre of Gravity Method |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **Output** | Rank of discrete candidate sites | Continuous (x, y) coordinate of one new facility |
| **Data type** | Qualitative + quantitative | Purely quantitative |
| **Input data** | Weights + ratings (1–10) | Coordinates + volumes |
| **Assumptions** | Weights and ratings are honestly assigned | Cost ∝ distance × volume; flat plane |
| **Subjectivity** | High — depends on judgment of weights and scores | Low — purely arithmetic |
| **Strength** | Captures intangible factors (labor climate, policy) | Mathematically optimal for the given data |
| **Weakness** | Garbage-in-garbage-out; sensitive to weight choice | Ignores qualitative factors, terrain, road network |
| **KTU use case** | Choosing a city/region | Locating a warehouse within a chosen region |
| **Marks weight (typical KTU)** | 7 marks for sub-question | 7 marks for sub-question |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When a KTU question says *"compare and contrast the two methods"* — use the table above as your answer backbone; then add a 2-line "best practice" statement: *Use Factor Rating to **narrow down to a region/city**, then use Centre of Gravity **within that region** to pinpoint the exact location.*

Since **Kochi (H3) carries the largest volume (4000 t)**, the centre of gravity $(26.5, 21.5)$ is visibly pulled toward it — closer to $(30, 25)$ than to Trivandrum's $(10, 5)$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the "Factor Rating Method" of plant location. List any four factors that may be considered while evaluating a manufacturing plant site.*

**Model Answer:**
The Factor Rating Method is a multi-criteria decision-making technique in which key location factors are assigned weights based on their strategic importance, and each candidate site is rated on a uniform scale (commonly 1–10). The weighted scores are summed to produce a composite score; the site with the highest composite score is selected.

Four common factors:
1. **Proximity to market and customers** (reduces distribution cost and lead time).
2. **Availability and cost of labor** (skilled/unskilled, wage levels).
3. **Transportation and logistics infrastructure** (road, rail, port, airport access).
4. **Power, water, and other utility availability** (critical for continuous-process industries).

> *Valid additional factors: government incentives, taxes, land cost, community attitude, climate, raw-material proximity, environmental regulations.*

---

**A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the formula used in the Centre of Gravity Method for locating a new facility. What are the assumptions of this method?*

**Model Answer:**
The centre of gravity is computed using the weighted-average formulas:

$$
C_x = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}, \quad C_y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \cdot V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}
$$

where $(x_i, y_i)$ are the coordinates of the $i^{th}$ existing facility and $V_i$ is the volume of goods moved to/from it.

**Assumptions:**
1. Transportation cost is **directly proportional to distance × volume**.
2. The map is a **flat Euclidean plane** (curvature, hills, rivers ignored).
3. Volumes $V_i$ are **constant and known in advance**.
4. The new facility is a **single point** and serves all existing locations.
5. All existing locations are treated as **points on a Cartesian grid**.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **Instructions (as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** *Answer any one full question. Each full question carries 14 marks, split into Part (a) — 7 marks, and Part (b) — 7 marks.*

---

#### ✅ CHOICE 1: Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question aligned with Dec 2023 / May 2024 pattern]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Factor Rating Method** of plant location in detail. What are its merits and limitations?

**Model Answer:**

The **Factor Rating Method** (also called the *Weighted Factor Rating Method*) is a structured, semi-quantitative technique used in Operations Management to compare multiple alternative plant locations. The procedure is as follows:

1. **Identify key factors** that affect the location decision — these may be qualitative (labor climate, government attitude) or quantitative (transport cost, wage rate). Typically, 5–10 factors are chosen.
2. **Assign a weight $W_i$ to each factor** such that $\sum W_i = 1.00$. The weights reflect the strategic priority of each factor.
3. **Score each candidate site $j$ on each factor $i$** on a uniform scale, normally **1 to 10** (1 = worst, 10 = best).
4. **Multiply weight by score** to obtain the weighted score: $W_i \times S_{ij}$.
5. **Sum across all factors** for each site:

$$
\text{Composite Score}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot S_{ij}
$$

6. The site with the **highest composite score** is recommended.

**Merits:**
- Captures both **tangible and intangible** criteria in a single composite metric.
- **Easy to understand and apply** — does not require advanced mathematics.
- Encourages **structured discussion** among management on factor weights.
- **Flexible** — additional factors can be inserted or weights re-tuned to test sensitivity.

**Limitations:**
- **Subjective** — different managers may assign different weights and scores, leading to different decisions.
- **Garbage-in-garbage-out** — biased or inaccurate ratings can mislead the ranking.
- **Difficult to compare** sites with very different characteristics (e.g., a coastal SEZ vs. an inland industrial estate).
- Does **not yield an exact coordinate** — only a relative ranking.
- The 1–10 rating scale can suffer from **compression effects** (a "7" from one evaluator may be a "5" from another).

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** A pharma company is comparing **three locations** (X, Y, Z) for a new formulation plant. Weights and ratings are given. Determine the best site.

| # | Factor | Weight | X | Y | Z |
|---|--------|--------|---|---|---|
| 1 | Skilled labor | 0.25 | 8 | 7 | 6 |
| 2 | Power reliability | 0.20 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| 3 | Transport cost (low = good) | 0.15 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| 4 | Proximity to market | 0.20 | 9 | 6 | 7 |
| 5 | Government subsidy | 0.10 | 5 | 9 | 8 |
| 6 | Land cost (low = good) | 0.10 | 6 | 7 | 9 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify weights:**

$$
\sum W_i = 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Composite score for Site X:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_X &= (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 6) \\
&= 2.00 + 1.40 + 0.90 + 1.80 + 0.50 + 0.60 \\
&= 7.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Composite score for Site Y:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Y &= (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 1.75 + 1.60 + 1.20 + 1.20 + 0.90 + 0.70 \\
&= 7.35
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Composite score for Site Z:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Z &= (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 9) \\
&= 1.50 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 1.40 + 0.80 + 0.90 \\
&= 7.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rank and Conclude:**

| Site | Composite Score | Rank |
|------|-----------------|------|
| X | 7.20 | 3rd |
| Y | 7.35 | 2nd |
| **Z** | **7.45** | **1st (Recommended)** |

> ✅ **Recommended site: Z** with a composite score of **7.45**. The margin of 0.10 over Y is narrow, so the company is advised to perform sensitivity analysis before final commitment.

---

#### ✅ CHOICE 1: Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question aligned with KTU 2024 pattern]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Centre of Gravity Method** of plant location. State the formulae and the assumptions clearly. Mention its advantages and disadvantages.

**Model Answer:**

The **Centre of Gravity Method** is a quantitative technique used in Operations Management to determine the *optimal geographic location* of a new facility (typically a warehouse or distribution centre) such that the total weighted transportation cost to existing sources/customers is minimized.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
C_x = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}, \quad C_y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}
$$

where $C_x, C_y$ are the optimal coordinates of the new facility, $(x_i, y_i)$ are coordinates of the $i^{th}$ existing facility, and $V_i$ is the volume of goods shipped to/from that facility.

**Assumptions:**
1. Transportation cost is **directly proportional to distance × volume** moved.
2. The map is a **flat 2-D Cartesian plane** (Euclidean geometry).
3. The set of existing locations and their volumes are **known and constant**.
4. The new facility is a **single point**.
5. **No qualitative factors** (e.g., land availability, labor quality) are considered in the calculation.

**Advantages:**
- Provides an **objective, numerical solution** — no subjective scoring required.
- The arithmetic is **simple and transparent**.
- Useful for **distribution-centre and warehouse-siting** problems.
- Easily extendable to include **reverse logistics** (returns, recycling).

**Disadvantages:**
- The straight-line distance used **ignores real road networks, rivers, mountains, traffic**.
- Assumes **constant volumes** — cannot handle seasonal or rapidly growing demand.
- Yields a **coordinate that may not correspond to a buildable site** (e.g., centre of a lake, forest, or restricted military zone). Hence the computed point should be *validated* against land-use constraints.
- Ignores **qualitative factors** such as labor climate, government policy, and community acceptance.

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** A FMCG distributor has four existing depots with the following coordinates $(x, y)$ and monthly throughput volumes. Find the optimal location $(C_x, C_y)$ for the new central warehouse using the Centre of Gravity Method.

| Depot | $x$ (km) | $y$ (km) | Volume (tonnes) |
|-------|----------|----------|------------------|
| D1 | 5 | 10 | 800 |
| D2 | 15 | 5 | 1200 |
| D3 | 20 | 20 | 2000 |
| D4 | 10 | 25 | 1000 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Working table:**

| Depot | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $V_i$ | $x_i V_i$ | $y_i V_i$ |
|-------|--------|--------|-------|-----------|-----------|
| D1 | 5 | 10 | 800 | 4,000 | 8,000 |
| D2 | 15 | 5 | 1200 | 18,000 | 6,000 |
| D3 | 20 | 20 | 2000 | 40,000 | 40,000 |
| D4 | 10 | 25 | 1000 | 10,000 | 25,000 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **5000** | **72,000** | **79,000** |

**Step 2 — Compute sums:**

$$
\sum V_i = 800 + 1200 + 2000 + 1000 = 5{,}000
$$

$$
\sum x_i V_i = 4{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 72{,}000
$$

$$
\sum y_i V_i = 8{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 25{,}000 = 79{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_x$ and $C_y$:**

$$
C_x = \frac{72{,}000}{5{,}000} = 14.4 \text{ km}
$$

$$
C_y = \frac{79{,}000}{5{,}000} = 15.8 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

> ✅ **The new central warehouse should be located at (14.4 km, 15.8 km)** on the Cartesian grid. This point minimizes the volume-weighted transportation distance to the four existing depots.
>
> **Sanity check:** D3 has the largest volume (2000 t) and lies at $(20, 20)$. The result $(14.4, 15.8)$ is **closer to D3** than to D2 $(15, 5)$ or D1 $(5, 10)$ — consistent with the pull of the largest mass. ✅

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify that $\sum W_i = 1.00$** in the Factor Rating Method. Examiners immediately deduct 1 mark if weights don't sum to 1. Always show this verification step.
> 2. **Mixing up $x_i V_i$ with $V_i / x_i$** in the Centre of Gravity Method. The numerator is **product**, not a ratio. A common slip is writing $C_x = \frac{\sum x_i}{\sum V_i}$ — that is **wrong**.
> 3. **Not labelling the units** of $C_x, C_y$ (must be in km or miles to match the input coordinates).
> 4. **Skipping the "Sensitivity Analysis" remark** in Factor Rating — the KTU 2024 marking scheme allots 1 mark for the practical caveat that small score differences warrant re-evaluation.
> 5. **Recommending the centre of gravity without qualitative validation** — the KTU rubric awards a mark for stating *"this coordinate must be checked for land availability, road access, and zoning."*
> 6. **Confusing the two methods** — students sometimes apply weights *to the centre of gravity* formula or compute $(x, y)$ *in factor rating*. Read the question carefully!
> 7. **Not showing the working table** in the centre of gravity — at least 1 mark is reserved for a clean tabular presentation.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Factor Rating Method** is for **discrete choice among candidate sites** (city/region level).
- ⭐ Weights $W_i$ must be assigned such that **$\sum W_i = 1.00$** — always verify.
- ⭐ Ratings $S_{ij}$ are on a **uniform 1–10 scale** (or 0–100, but be consistent).
- ⭐ Composite score formula: $S_j = \sum W_i S_{ij}$ — **highest score wins**.
- ⭐ Always conduct **sensitivity analysis** to test the robustness of the result.
- ⭐ **Centre of Gravity Method** is for **continuous coordinate determination** (warehouse/distribution-centre level).
- ⭐ Formulae:
  - $C_x = \dfrac{\sum x_i V_i}{\sum V_i}$
  - $C_y = \dfrac{\sum y_i V_i}{\sum V_i}$
- ⭐ $V_i$ is the **volume (or weight, or trip count)** of goods moved to/from location $i$.
- ⭐ Assumes **cost ∝ distance × volume**, **flat plane**, **constant $V_i$**.
- ⭐ Computed $(C_x, C_y)$ is a **starting point** — must be **validated for land, road, zoning**.
- ⭐ When the **largest volume** location is far away, the centre of gravity is **pulled toward it**.
- ⭐ **Hybrid approach** is industry best practice: **Factor Rating** to pick the city, then **Centre of Gravity** to pick the exact site within that city.
- ⭐ **KTU 2024 weightage trend:** Expect 7 marks in Part B (either method as a full 7-mark sub-question) and possibly 3 marks in Part A (definition/formula).
- ⭐ **Common mistakes to avoid:** forgetting weight normalization, using distance ÷ volume instead of product, omitting units, and skipping the qualitative validation step.

> **One-Line Mantra for Exam Hall:**
> *"Factor Rating picks the CITY; Centre of Gravity picks the SPOT."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity planning: definition, measures of capacity (input and output), types of planning over time horizon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Capacity planning: definition, measures of capacity (input and output), types of planning over time horizon ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Capacity Planning** is the long-term strategic process of determining the production capacity an organization requires to meet the anticipated demand for its products or services, considering the resources (machines, labor, materials, and facilities) available to it. According to KTU 2024 Scheme Operations Management framework, capacity refers to the **maximum output rate** that a system, facility, or process can produce under **ideal conditions** over a specified period. Capacity planning ensures the right balance between internal capabilities and external market demand to avoid **bottlenecks** (under-capacity) or **idle resources** (over-capacity). > [!NOTE] > **Three pillars of Capacity Decisions:** > 1. **Acquiring** capacity (building/buying) > 2. **Shedding** capacity (divesting/downsizing) > 3. **Stretching** capacity (overtime, outsourcing) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are organizing a **birthday party at home**. You must decide: - **How many chairs** to arrange (long-term capacity - facility), - **How many cooks** to call and **how much food** to order 2 weeks before (intermediate - workforce/materials), - **What time to start cooking** and **serving sequence** on the party day (short-term - scheduling). If you have **too few chairs** → guests are uncomfortable. If you have **500 chairs** for 20 guests → waste of money. **Capacity planning** is this exact balancing act applied to manufacturing or service operations. ### 1.3 Physical/Standard Metrics Highlighted - **Design Capacity (DC):** The theoretical maximum output (e.g., **1000 units/day**). - **Effective Capacity (EC):** The realistic output considering allowances (e.g., **850 units/day**). - **Actual Output (AO):** The real production achieved (e.g., **720 units/day**). - **Utilization (U):** $\frac{AO}{DC} \times 100\%$ - **Efficiency (E):** $\frac{AO}{EC} \times 100\%$ ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Measures of Capacity (Input vs. Output) Capacity can be measured from **two perspectives**: #### A. Input Measures of Capacity Input measures quantify the **resources consumed** by a system. They answer: *"How much did we put in?"* | Type of Operation | Input Measure | |------------------|----------------| | Hospital | Beds available, Doctor hours | | Airline | Seats, Crew hours | | Factory | Machine hours, Labor hours, Material kg | | Call Center | Number of agents, Trunk lines | > [!NOTE] > Input measures are **easier to control** by managers and are used predominantly in **service operations** where output is variable (e.g., a restaurant cannot control exactly how many customers arrive). #### B. Output Measures of Capacity Output measures quantify the **result produced** by the system. They answer: *"How much came out?"* | Type of Operation | Output Measure | |------------------|----------------| | Automobile Plant | Cars per shift | | Steel Mill | Tonnes of steel per day | | Power Plant | Megawatts (MW) per hour | | School | Students graduated per year | > [!NOTE] > Output measures are **standardized** and easily comparable across time. Manufacturing firms prefer output measures because units are homogeneous. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Capacity Hierarchy 1. **Step 1 - Identify the bottleneck resource**: The slowest machine/process dictates system capacity. 2. **Step 2 - Compute Design Capacity (DC)**: Maximum theoretical output under ideal conditions. 3. **Step 3 - Subtract allowances** (breaks, maintenance, changeovers) → get **Effective Capacity (EC)**. 4. **Step 4 - Compare Actual Output (AO)** against both DC and EC. 5. **Step 5 - Compute Utilization and Efficiency** to find slack. > **Real-world engineering utility:** Used in **Bottleneck Analysis (Theory of Constraints)**, **Lean Manufacturing**, and **Six Sigma capacity certification** to optimize throughput. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Metric | Formula | Typical Range | Unit | |---|--------|---------|----------------|------| | 1 | Design Capacity | Maximum theoretical output (given) | $100\%$ reference | units/time | | 2 | Effective Capacity | $EC = DC - \text{Allowances}$ | $80\% - 90\%$ of DC | units/time | | 3 | Utilization | $U = \frac{AO}{DC} \times 100$ | $85\% - 100\%$ desired | $\%$ | | 4 | Efficiency | $E = \frac{AO}{EC} \times 100$ | Often $> 100\%$ possible | $\%$ | | 5 | Preferred Operating Level | Optimal % of DC | Varies by industry | $\%$ | | 6 | Capacity Cushion | $CC = 100\% - U$ | $5\% - 20\%$ safe range | $\%$ | | 7 | Break-Even Capacity | $Q = \frac{F}{P - V}$ | Decision threshold | units | > **Note on symbols:** The vertical bar symbol inside formulas above (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) is rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. > **Why these formulas matter:** A low Utilization means capital is wasted; a high Utilization (>95\%) means **no buffer for breakdowns**, leading to missed deliveries. Managers aim for an optimum **Capacity Cushion**. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Utilization-Efficiency Relationship We start from the two definitions and derive a key relationship. $$ U = \frac{AO}{DC} $$ $$ E = \frac{AO}{EC} $$ Dividing the first equation by the second: $$ \frac{U}{E} = \frac{AO/\,DC}{AO/\,EC} = \frac{EC}{DC} $$ Therefore, the **cross-relationship** becomes: $$ U = E \times \frac{EC}{DC} $$ $$ AO = U \times DC = E \times EC $$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem:** A textile mill has a **Design Capacity** of **5000 meters of cloth per day**. Due to scheduled maintenance, breaks, and changeovers, the **Effective Capacity** is only **4000 meters/day**. Last week, the actual production averaged **3600 meters/day**. Compute (a) Utilization, (b) Efficiency, and (c) Capacity Cushion. **Step 1: Identify given values.** $$ DC = 5000 \; \text{m/day}, \quad EC = 4000 \; \text{m/day}, \quad AO = 3600 \; \text{m/day} $$ **Step 2: Calculate Utilization.** $$ U = \frac{AO}{DC} \times 100 = \frac{3600}{5000} \times 100 $$ $$ U = 0.72 \times 100 = 72\% $$ **Step 3: Calculate Efficiency.** $$ E = \frac{AO}{EC} \times 100 = \frac{3600}{4000} \times 100 $$ $$ E = 0.90 \times 100 = 90\% $$ **Step 4: Calculate Capacity Cushion.** $$ CC = 100\% - U = 100\% - 72\% = 28\% $$ **Interpretation:** The mill uses only 72\% of its design capacity (a cushion of 28\%), but operates at 90\% efficiency relative to its realistic effective capacity. The high cushion indicates **excess installed capacity** or a **demand problem** — the firm may consider de-bottlenecking, selling the extra line, or stimulating demand via marketing. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When Utilization is High vs. Low | Condition | Implication | Managerial Action | |-----------|--------------|--------------------| | $U < 70\%$ | Severe underuse; capital tied up | Layoffs, divest, or boost demand | | $U \in [70\%, 85\%]$ | Healthy cushion | Maintain current operations | | $U \in [85\%, 95\%]$ | Tight, watch bottlenecks | Add shifts, preventive maintenance | | $U > 95\%$ | No slack; breakdown imminent | Expand capacity immediately | ### 3.4 Derivation of Break-Even Capacity (Quick) A firm must produce at least $Q_{BE}$ units to cover fixed costs $F$, where $P$ is price and $V$ is variable cost per unit. $$ \text{Total Cost} = \text{Total Revenue} $$ $$ F + V \cdot Q = P \cdot Q $$ $$ F = (P - V) \cdot Q $$ $$ Q_{BE} = \frac{F}{P - V} $$ > **Engineering utility:** Used in **"make or buy"** capacity decisions and in deciding whether to **build a new plant** vs. **outsource production**. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Time-Hierarchy Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Capacity Planning
Strategic Horizon] --> B[Long-Range Planning
1 to 5 Years] A --> C[Intermediate-Range Planning
3 to 18 Months] A --> D[Short-Range Planning
Less than 3 Months] B --> B1[Facilities Expansion] B --> B2[Major Equipment Purchase] B --> B3[Technology Selection] B --> B4[Plant Location] C --> C1[Workforce Hiring or Layoffs] C --> C2[Subcontracting Decisions] C --> C3[Inventory Build-up] C --> C4[Shift Adjustments] D --> D1[Daily Job Scheduling] D --> D2[Overtime Authorization] D --> D3[Machine Loading] D --> D4[Worker Allocation] style A fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style C fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000 style D fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Capacity Measures Relationship Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR DC[Design Capacity
100% Theoretical Max] -->|minus allowances| EC[Effective Capacity
80 to 90% of DC] EC -->|actual production| AO[Actual Output
Below or Equal to EC] DC -->|actual production| AO DC -.->|ratio| U[Utilization = AO / DC] EC -.->|ratio| E[Efficiency = AO / EC] style DC fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style EC fill:#1565c0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style AO fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style U fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Decision Flow: Capacity Planning Process ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Forecast Demand] --> S2[Step 2: Estimate Current Capacity] S2 --> S3{Demand vs Capacity Gap?} S3 -->|Shortage Exists| S4[Step 3a: Add Capacity
Build, Buy, Outsource] S3 -->|Surplus Exists| S5[Step 3b: Reduce Capacity
Sell, Divest, Layoff] S3 -->|Balanced| S6[Step 3c: Maintain Status Quo] S4 --> S7[Step 4: Implement and Monitor] S5 --> S7 S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8[Step 5: Review and Adjust] style S1 fill:#37474f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style S3 fill:#d84315,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style S7 fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style S8 fill:#4a148c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define capacity planning. List its three strategic decisions.** > **Model Answer:** > **Capacity planning** is the process of determining the production capacity required by an organization to meet anticipated demand. > The three strategic decisions are: > 1. **Acquiring** capacity (add resources) > 2. **Shedding** capacity (eliminate resources) > 3. **Stretching** capacity (maximize use of existing resources) **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between Utilization and Efficiency with formulas.** > **Model Answer:** > - **Utilization** = $\frac{Actual\ Output}{Design\ Capacity} \times 100$ > - **Efficiency** = $\frac{Actual\ Output}{Effective\ Capacity} \times 100$ > Utilization compares output against the **theoretical maximum**; Efficiency compares it against the **realistic achievable** level after allowances. --- ### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Design Capacity with Effective Capacity** and use the wrong denominator in Utilization/Efficiency formulas. This single error can cost **3 to 5 marks**. Always re-read the problem statement and label DC, EC, AO clearly before writing the formula. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Pattern]* **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different **types of capacity planning** based on the **time horizon**, with one industrial example for each. **Model Solution:** Capacity planning is classified into three time-horizon categories: 1. **Long-Range Capacity Planning (1 to 5 years)** - Concerns **strategic, capital-intensive** decisions. - Includes plant construction, new technology adoption, factory location. - **Example:** A car manufacturer deciding to build a new EV assembly plant in 3 years. 2. **Intermediate-Range Capacity Planning (3 to 18 months)** - Focuses on **tactical** changes like workforce levels, subcontracting, and inventory policies. - **Example:** A garment company hiring 200 extra tailors before the Diwali festive season. 3. **Short-Range Capacity Planning (< 3 months)** - Deals with **operational** scheduling: daily machine loading, overtime, job sequencing. - **Example:** A bakery deciding to run a double shift for one week to fulfill a wedding order. > **Key linkage:** The three horizons form a **cascade** — short-range decisions must align with intermediate plans, which must align with long-term strategy. **(b) [7 Marks]** A machine shop has a Design Capacity of **1200 units/day** and an Effective Capacity of **900 units/day**. The shop produced **750 units** last week. Compute (i) Utilization, (ii) Efficiency, (iii) Capacity Cushion, and (iv) Comment on the result. **Model Solution:** Given: $$ DC = 1200 \; \text{u/day}, \quad EC = 900 \; \text{u/day}, \quad AO = 750 \; \text{u/day} $$ **(i) Utilization:** $$ U = \frac{AO}{DC} \times 100 = \frac{750}{1200} \times 100 = 62.5\% $$ **(ii) Efficiency:** $$ E = \frac{AO}{EC} \times 100 = \frac{750}{900} \times 100 = 83.33\% $$ **(iii) Capacity Cushion:** $$ CC = 100\% - U = 100 - 62.5 = 37.5\% $$ **(iv) Comment:** The shop operates at only 62.5\% utilization, leaving a **37.5\% cushion** — a strong sign of **excess capacity**. Although the efficiency of 83.33\% is reasonable, the large cushion indicates that the firm is either facing weak demand or has over-invested in machinery. Recommended actions: **stimulate demand via promotions, subcontract work to third parties, or sell the surplus equipment**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Input and Output measures of capacity**. Give **two examples** of each from a manufacturing and a service context. **Model Solution:** | Aspect | Input Measures | Output Measures | |--------|----------------|------------------| | Definition | Quantity of resources consumed | Quantity of goods/services produced | | Managerial Control | Easier to manage | Harder if demand is variable | | Common In | Service operations (hospitals, banks) | Manufacturing operations | | Stability | Stable, predictable | Variable, demand-driven | | Examples (Manufacturing) | Machine hours, Labor hours, kWh of electricity | Units produced, Tonnes of steel, Cars per shift | | Examples (Service) | Beds, Doctor hours, Agent count | Patients treated, Calls resolved, Students graduated | > **Trade-off:** Manufacturing firms prefer **output** measures for benchmarking; service firms often rely on **input** measures because outputs (e.g., customer satisfaction) are intangible. **(b) [7 Marks]** A restaurant's design capacity is **200 covers/day**. Effective capacity is **170 covers/day**. On a particular day, it served **150 covers**. The restaurant owner wants to expand to serve **250 covers/day**. Suggest the **type of capacity decision** required and justify. **Model Solution:** Given: $$ DC = 200, \quad EC = 170, \quad AO = 150 $$ Current Utilization: $$ U = \frac{150}{200} \times 100 = 75\% $$ Current Efficiency: $$ E = \frac{150}{170} \times 100 = 88.24\% $$ **Capacity Cushion** = 25\%. The restaurant is currently within a safe range, but **demand of 250 covers** exceeds Design Capacity (200) — clearly a **capacity shortage**. **Required Decision Type: Acquiring (Expanding) Capacity – Long-Range Planning** Justification: - Since the desired output (250) **exceeds even the design capacity (200)**, the bottleneck is fundamental — more tables, kitchen equipment, and seating area are required. - This is a **long-term strategic** decision, typically spanning 1–3 years. - Alternative: a **short-term stretch** (overtime, extra shifts) could temporarily meet demand, but the persistent 25\% gap demands a **long-range capacity expansion** such as renting a larger premises or opening a second branch. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Capacity planning** = balancing demand with resource availability across three horizons. - **Design Capacity** is the theoretical ceiling; **Effective Capacity** is the realistic ceiling after allowances; **Actual Output** is what is achieved. - **Utilization** uses Design Capacity as the denominator; **Efficiency** uses Effective Capacity. - **Capacity Cushion** = $100\% - \text{Utilization}$. A small cushion = high risk; a large cushion = wasted capital. - The **three time horizons** are: * **Long-range (1–5 years):** Facilities, technology, location. * **Intermediate (3–18 months):** Workforce, inventory, subcontracting. * **Short-range (< 3 months):** Scheduling, overtime, loading. - **Input measures** suit service operations; **output measures** suit manufacturing. - **Preferred operating level** is the point where cost per unit is minimized. - **Break-even capacity** is the minimum output needed to recover fixed costs: $Q = \frac{F}{P - V}$. - Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ notation for absolute value in formulas to avoid markdown table breakage. - **Common valuation trap:** Confusing DC and EC in formulas — read the problem statement twice before solving.

topics.title: Decision trees analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decision trees analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Tree Analysis** is a quantitative, diagrammatic decision-making tool used under conditions of **risk and uncertainty**, in which the decision-maker maps out all feasible alternative courses of action, the possible **states of nature** (chance events) branching out of each alternative, the associated probabilities of those states, and the **payoffs** (monetary outcomes) — then evaluates each alternative by computing its **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** through the *rollback* (fold-back) method.

In KTU Operations Management (PECBT865, Module 2), decision trees are studied as a structured extension of the **Expected Value (EV) criterion** of decision theory, specifically designed to handle **sequential decisions** and **conditional outcomes** in operations contexts such as product launch, capacity expansion, equipment replacement, and make-or-buy decisions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Road Trip Decision:**
> Imagine you are driving from Kochi to Bengaluru, and your GPS offers you three routes. You don't know the traffic, but you roughly know that route A is *usually* fast, route B is *usually* slow, and route C might have a roadblock. You draw a small tree in your head: at the root is the *decision* (which route?), and from each route branch hang *chances* (traffic light, accident, roadblock) with rough probabilities. Each ending leaf has a *time cost*. You multiply and add, and pick the route with the smallest expected time. **That is exactly how a decision tree works — only the "leaves" carry money instead of minutes.**

The tree grows **left-to-right**: at each *square* you decide, at each *circle* fate decides, and at the *triangles* you collect the final payoffs. Working backwards from the leaves to the trunk, you fold up the chance branches and select the best decision branch.

## 1.3 Salient Symbols Used in Decision Trees (KTU Convention)

| Symbol | Meaning | Role in Tree |
|---|---|---|
| **Square (□)** | **Decision Node** | Point of control by the decision-maker |
| **Circle (○)** | **Chance Node** | State-of-nature outcome with probability attached |
| **Triangle (△)** | **End/Result Node** | Terminal payoff (profit / loss) |
| **Branch line** | Action or outcome | Connector between nodes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric Highlight:** The single most important numerical output of a decision tree is the **EMV (Expected Monetary Value)** — it is the **weighted-average payoff**, where each payoff is weighted by the probability of its associated chance branch. Decision rule: **choose the alternative branch with the highest EMV.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Building Blocks of a Decision Tree (Logic Steps)

A decision tree in Operations Management is constructed by sequentially applying the following **six engineering-grade logic steps**:

1. **Identify the decision point(s).** Locate where management must *choose* between alternatives (e.g., *launch / don't launch*, *expand / hold*).
2. **Enumerate the decision alternatives.** List every *feasible* course of action (typically 2–4 in KTU problems).
3. **Enumerate the states of nature.** For each alternative, list every possible *chance event* (favourable market, machine failure, raw-material shortage, etc.).
4. **Assign probabilities.** Estimate prior probabilities $P(S_i)$ from past data, expert judgement, or historical records. **All branch probabilities out of a chance node must sum to 1.**
5. **Attach payoffs.** For each (alternative × state) pair, attach the monetary outcome $V_{ij}$.
6. **Roll-back (fold-back) computation:**
   - At every **chance node**: compute EMV using $$\text{EMV} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(S_i) \cdot V_i$$
   - At every **decision node**: select the branch with the most favourable EMV.
   - Carry the chosen EMV upwards (this is the *rollback*).

## 2.2 Why and How — The Pedagogical Layer

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a tree and not a payoff table?**
> When the decision sequence has **multiple stages** (e.g., *first test, then decide to launch or abandon*), a payoff table explodes combinatorially. A decision tree **preserves chronology**, naturally embeds **conditional probabilities** via Bayes' theorem, and makes the *roll-back* transparent to the examiner.

> [!NOTE]
> **How does rollback work?**
> The tree is solved **right-to-left**: leaves first, then chance nodes (average them), then decision nodes (select max/min). This is mathematically identical to solving the decision problem by dynamic programming.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Verifiable, Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable and conform to KTU 2024 Operations Management notation.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected Monetary Value | $\text{EMV} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot V_i$ | Average payoff weighted by probabilities |
| 2 | Expected Profit (decision rule) | $E^* = \displaystyle\max_{j} \left\{\sum_i P_i V_{ij}\right\}$ | Pick the alternative with highest EMV |
| 3 | Bayes' Posterior | $P(S_i \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid S_i) P(S_i)}{\displaystyle\sum_{k} P(E \mid S_k) P(S_k)}$ | Updates probability after a sample/test signal |
| 4 | EVPI (Perfect Information) | $\text{EVPI} = \text{EMV}_{\text{perfect}} - \text{EMV}_{\text{best}}$ | Max. price to pay for perfect foresight |
| 5 | EVwPI (with perfect info) | $\text{EMV}_{\text{perfect}} = \displaystyle\sum_i P_i \cdot V^{*}_i$ | EMV if you *knew* the state in advance |
| 6 | EVSI (Sample Information) | $\text{EVSI} = \text{EMV}_{\text{with sample}} - \text{EMV}_{\text{without sample}}$ | Max. price to pay for *imperfect* sample info |
| 7 | Net Gain from Information | $\text{Net} = \text{EVSI} - C_{\text{info}}$ | If $\text{Net} > 0$, buy the information |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb examiners use:**
> $$\text{Acquire info} \iff \text{EVSI} \geq C_{\text{info}}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Operations

Decision tree analysis is the operational analyst's *stethoscope* in:

- **Capacity planning** — *expand / wait / contract* decisions under demand uncertainty.
- **R&D portfolio management** — *commercialise / prototype / abandon* drug or chip projects.
- **Equipment replacement** — *replace now / overhaul / keep* under stochastic failure rates.
- **Make-or-buy / sourcing** — supplier reliability under varying demand.
- **New product launch** — *launch directly / test-market / abandon*.
- **Inventory & supply-chain risk** — selecting between JIT, safety-stock, and dual-sourcing.

Every MBA/Operations textbook (Heizer/Render, Krajewski, Stevenson) lists decision trees under *quantitative decision aids* — exactly where KTU's Module 2 places them.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Master Problem):** A pharmaceutical company must decide whether to launch a new drug. Three strategies are on the table. The full decision tree, Bayes' revision, EMV computation, and EVPI are derived below.

## 3.1 Problem Statement

A pharmaceutical firm in Kerala is planning to launch a new drug. Three alternatives exist:

- **Strategy A — Launch directly** (Investment = **Rs. 5,00,000**)
- **Strategy B — Test-market first**, then decide (Test cost = **Rs. 50,000**; if launched, additional cost = **Rs. 4,50,000**)
- **Strategy C — Do not launch** (Profit = **Rs. 0**)

**Prior probabilities (without test):**
$$P(\text{Favourable Market}) = 0.60 \qquad P(\text{Unfavourable Market}) = 0.40$$

**Conditional probabilities of the test result:**
$$
P(+\mid \text{Fav}) = 0.80 \quad P(-\mid \text{Fav}) = 0.20
$$
$$
P(+\mid \text{Unfav}) = 0.10 \quad P(-\mid \text{Unfav}) = 0.90
$$

**Revenue if launched (gross):**
$$
R(\text{Fav}) = \text{Rs. }10{,}00{,}000 \qquad R(\text{Unfav}) = \text{Rs. }2{,}00{,}000
$$

**Required:** (a) Construct the decision tree, apply Bayes' theorem, compute the EMV of each strategy and select the best.
(b) Compute EVPI and EVSI. Should the company conduct the test?

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution — Part (a) — Bayes' Revision

### Step 1 — Compute joint probabilities

Using the multiplication rule $P(A \cap B) = P(B\mid A) P(A)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Fav} \cap +) &= 0.80 \times 0.60 = 0.480 \\
P(\text{Fav} \cap -) &= 0.20 \times 0.60 = 0.120 \\
P(\text{Unfav} \cap +) &= 0.10 \times 0.40 = 0.040 \\
P(\text{Unfav} \cap -) &= 0.90 \times 0.40 = 0.360
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Marginal probabilities of the test signal (Total Probability Theorem)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(+) &= P(\text{Fav}\cap+) + P(\text{Unfav}\cap+) = 0.480 + 0.040 = 0.520 \\
P(-) &= P(\text{Fav}\cap-) + P(\text{Unfav}\cap-) = 0.120 + 0.360 = 0.480
\end{aligned}
$$

*Check:* $P(+) + P(-) = 0.520 + 0.480 = 1.000$ ✓

### Step 3 — Posterior probabilities (Bayes' Theorem)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Fav}\mid +) &= \frac{P(+\mid \text{Fav})P(\text{Fav})}{P(+)} = \frac{0.480}{0.520} = 0.9231 \\
P(\text{Unfav}\mid +) &= \frac{0.040}{0.520} = 0.0769 \\
P(\text{Fav}\mid -) &= \frac{0.120}{0.480} = 0.2500 \\
P(\text{Unfav}\mid -) &= \frac{0.360}{0.480} = 0.7500
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Solution — Payoffs (Rs. in lakhs)

**Net payoff if launched** (Revenue − Total Cost):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct launch (Cost Rs. 5 L):} \\
V(\text{Fav}) &= +10 - 5 = +5 \text{ lakh} \\
V(\text{Unfav}) &= +2 - 5 = -3 \text{ lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Launch *after* test (Total Cost Rs. 5 L = 0.5 L test + 4.5 L launch):} \\
V(\text{Fav after } +) &= +10 - 5 = +5 \text{ lakh} \\
V(\text{Unfav after } +) &= +2 - 5 = -3 \text{ lakh} \\
V(\text{Don't launch after } -) &= -0.5 \text{ lakh (test cost only)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Roll-Back Computation of EMVs

### Strategy A — Direct Launch (chance node only)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(A) &= P(\text{Fav}) \cdot V(\text{Fav}) + P(\text{Unfav}) \cdot V(\text{Unfav}) \\
&= 0.60 \times (+5) + 0.40 \times (-3) \\
&= 3.000 - 1.200 \\
&= \boxed{+\text{Rs. }1{,}80{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Strategy C — Do Not Launch

$$\text{EMV}(C) = 0$$

### Strategy B — Test Market, Then Decide (roll-back from right to left)

**(i) Chance node after a POSITIVE test result:**

We must decide *Launch* vs *Don't Launch*. The launch-branch EMV is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(\text{Launch}\mid +) &= P(\text{Fav}\mid +) \cdot (+5) + P(\text{Unfav}\mid +) \cdot (-3) \\
&= 0.9231 \times 5 + 0.0769 \times (-3) \\
&= 4.6155 - 0.2307 \\
&= +\text{Rs. }4{,}38{,}480
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare with **Don't Launch** = −Rs. 50,000 (test cost only). Since $4{,}38{,}480 \gg -50{,}000$, **choose LAUNCH.** Carry up: **+Rs. 4,38,480**.

**(ii) Chance node after a NEGATIVE test result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(\text{Launch}\mid -) &= P(\text{Fav}\mid -) \cdot (+5) + P(\text{Unfav}\mid -) \cdot (-3) \\
&= 0.2500 \times 5 + 0.7500 \times (-3) \\
&= 1.2500 - 2.2500 \\
&= -\text{Rs. }1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare with **Don't Launch** = −Rs. 50,000. Since $-1{,}00{,}000 < -50{,}000$, **choose DON'T LAUNCH.** Carry up: **−Rs. 50,000**.

**(iii) Top-level chance node (test signal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(B) &= P(+) \cdot \text{EMV}(\text{Launch}\mid +) + P(-) \cdot \text{EMV}(\text{Don't Launch}\mid -) \\
&= 0.520 \times 4{,}38{,}480 + 0.480 \times (-50{,}000) \\
&= 2{,}28{,}010 - 24{,}000 \\
&= \boxed{+\text{Rs. }2{,}04{,}010}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Summary Table (Roll-Back Results)

| Strategy | EMV (Rs.) | Decision |
|---|---:|---|
| A — Direct launch | **1,80,000** | Sub-optimal |
| **B — Test market, then decide** | **2,04,010** | **OPTIMAL** ✓ |
| C — Do not launch | **0** | Sub-optimal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimal Decision:** The company should **conduct the test marketing**, launch if the test result is *positive*, and abandon if the test result is *negative*. Expected profit = **Rs. 2,04,010**.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Solution — Part (b) — EVPI and EVSI

### EVPI (Expected Value of Perfect Information)

With perfect information, the company launches *only* when the market is favourable:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}_{\text{perfect}} &= P(\text{Fav}) \cdot V(\text{Fav}) + P(\text{Unfav}) \cdot V(\text{Don't Launch}) \\
&= 0.60 \times (+5) + 0.40 \times (0) \\
&= +\text{Rs. }3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EVPI} &= \text{EMV}_{\text{perfect}} - \text{EMV}^*_{\text{no info}} \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}04{,}010 \\
&= \boxed{\text{Rs. }95{,}990}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The company should *never* pay more than **Rs. 95,990** for perfect market information.

### EVSI (Expected Value of Sample / Test Information)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EVSI} &= \text{EMV}(\text{with test, optimal}) - \text{EMV}(\text{without test, optimal}) \\
&= 2{,}04{,}010 - 1{,}80{,}000 \\
&= \boxed{\text{Rs. }24{,}010}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:**
$$
\text{EVSI} = \text{Rs. }24{,}010 \;\;<\;\; C_{\text{test}} = \text{Rs. }50{,}000
$$

Since the test cost **exceeds** the EVSI, **the test is *not* worth acquiring purely from an EMV standpoint.** However, in the decision tree itself, Strategy B still emerges as the *highest-EMV branch* in the rollback because the conditional decisions salvage much of the upside. The discrepancy arises because EVSI measures *only* the information value; the *full* strategy EMV also benefits from operational reversibility (the option to abandon).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Recommendation (Examiner's expected answer line):** *Test-market the drug. Launch only if the test is positive; abandon otherwise. Expected profit = Rs. 2,04,010. The test is justifiable on operational grounds (the abandonment option), although pure EVSI < test cost. Maximum payable for perfect information = Rs. 95,990.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Decision Tree (Master Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** `n1` and `n2` are decision nodes (squares in textbook notation). `n3`, `n4`, `n5` are chance nodes (circles). `n6`–`n13` are terminal result nodes (triangles). Payoffs are written in **Rs. (lakhs)** for compactness.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A([START]) --> n1{L1: Strategy?}
    n1 -->|"A: Launch Directly"| n2{C2: Market?}
    n1 -->|"B: Test Market First"| n3{C3: Test Signal?}
    n1 -->|"C: Don't Launch"| n6["Payoff = 0"]

    n2 -->|"Favourable, P=0.60"| n7["+5.00 L"]
    n2 -->|"Unfavourable, P=0.40"| n8["-3.00 L"]

    n3 -->|"Positive, P=0.520"| n4{C4: After +}
    n3 -->|"Negative, P=0.480"| n5{C5: After -}

    n4 -->|"Launch"| n9["+5.00 L  P=0.9231"]
    n4 -->|"Launch"| n10["-3.00 L  P=0.0769"]
    n5 -->|"Don't Launch"| n11["-0.50 L  P=1.00"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Decision Tree Analysis

This block diagram explains *how the analyst flows through the modules of decision-tree analysis*, mapping the engineering interaction rather than the tree itself.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Step 1: Frame Decision Problem"] --> B["Step 2: Enumerate Alternatives"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Identify States of Nature"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Estimate Prior Probabilities P(Si)"]
    D --> E{"Sample Information Available?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Step 5: Apply Bayes Theorem"]
    E -- "No" --> G["Step 6: Attach Payoffs Vij"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Step 7: Rollback Computation EMV"]
    H --> I["Step 8: Choose Branch Max EMV"]
    I --> J["Step 9: Sensitivity Analysis EVPI EVSI"]
    J --> K["Step 10: Final Recommendation"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Node vs Chance Node — Visual Contrast

```mermaid
graph TD
    sq["Square: DECISION NODE — Analyst chooses branch"] --> sq1["Action 1"]
    sq --> sq2["Action 2"]
    sq --> sq3["Action 3"]
    ci["Circle: CHANCE NODE — Nature chooses outcome"] --> ci1["Outcome A, P=0.6"]
    ci --> ci2["Outcome B, P=0.3"]
    ci --> ci3["Outcome C, P=0.1"]
```

## 4.4 Legend for KTU Board-Exam Style

| Symbol | Meaning | Examiner's Marking Cue |
|---|---|---|
| □ (Square) | Decision node — manager's choice | "Compute EMV at parent, choose max" |
| ○ (Circle) | Chance node — probabilistic event | "Apply Total Probability / Bayes" |
| △ (Triangle) | Terminal payoff | "Last column of the table" |
| → | Branch line | "Label with probability AND payoff" |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern: Part A (3 marks — short answer) and Part B (14 marks — internal choice with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks). Mark allocation strictly follows the revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Apply / Analyse / Evaluate).

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define a decision tree. List its main components.**
*(Cognitive Level: Remember / Understand — 3 marks)*

**Model Answer (Examiner-Key Style):**
A decision tree is a **graphical model** that represents a sequential decision problem under uncertainty, displaying alternative actions, chance events, probabilities, and payoffs in a tree-like structure branching from left to right.

**Main Components:**
1. **Decision nodes (□)** — points where the decision-maker chooses.
2. **Chance nodes (○)** — points where outcomes occur probabilistically.
3. **Branches** — alternative actions (from decision nodes) or outcomes (from chance nodes).
4. **Probabilities** — assigned to each chance branch.
5. **Payoffs** — monetary outcomes at terminal (leaf) nodes.
6. **EMV labels** — folded-back expected values.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Distinguish between EVPI and EVSI.**
*(Cognitive Level: Understand — 3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **EVPI** (Expected Value of Perfect Information) | **EVSI** (Expected Value of Sample Information) |
|---|---|---|
| Nature of info | Hypothetical, 100% accurate | Realistic, imperfect test result |
| Formula | $\text{EVPI} = \text{EMV}_{\text{perfect}} - \text{EMV}_{\text{best no-info}}$ | $\text{EVSI} = \text{EMV}_{\text{with sample}} - \text{EMV}_{\text{no sample}}$ |
| Practical Use | Upper bound on what to pay for any information | Tells whether the specific sample/test is worth its cost |
| Decision Rule | Acquire if $\text{EVPI} \geq C_{\text{info}}$ | Acquire if $\text{EVSI} \geq C_{\text{info}}$ |

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Pick Either A or B)

> Each sub-question carries **7 marks**. Solutions are written in the *board-exam step-format* examiners prefer.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

A startup plans to launch a new consumer-electronics product. Three options are considered:

- **A1:** Launch directly — Investment **Rs. 8,00,000**.
- **A2:** Test-market first (cost **Rs. 1,00,000**); if test favourable, launch with additional cost **Rs. 7,00,000**; if test unfavourable, abandon.
- **A3:** Don't launch.

**Prior data:**
$$
P(\text{Fav}) = 0.65,\; P(\text{Unfav}) = 0.35
$$

**Test accuracy:**
$$
P(+\mid \text{Fav}) = 0.75,\; P(+\mid \text{Unfav}) = 0.20
$$

**Revenue if launched:**
$$
R(\text{Fav}) = \text{Rs. }20{,}00{,}000, \quad R(\text{Unfav}) = \text{Rs. }5{,}00{,}000
$$

**(a)** Apply Bayes' theorem to revise probabilities, construct the decision tree, and compute the EMV of each strategy. Which strategy is optimal? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the EVPI. Also compute the EVSI and state whether the test marketing is economically justified. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Joint probabilities (in Rs. lakhs for clarity; 1 L = Rs. 1,00,000):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Fav} \cap +) &= 0.75 \times 0.65 = 0.4875 \\
P(\text{Fav} \cap -) &= 0.25 \times 0.65 = 0.1625 \\
P(\text{Unfav} \cap +) &= 0.20 \times 0.35 = 0.0700 \\
P(\text{Unfav} \cap -) &= 0.80 \times 0.35 = 0.2800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Marginal probabilities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(+) &= 0.4875 + 0.0700 = 0.5575 \\
P(-) &= 0.1625 + 0.2800 = 0.4425
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Posterior (Bayes') probabilities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Fav}\mid +) &= \frac{0.4875}{0.5575} = 0.8744 \\
P(\text{Unfav}\mid +) &= \frac{0.0700}{0.5575} = 0.1256 \\
P(\text{Fav}\mid -) &= \frac{0.1625}{0.4425} = 0.3672 \\
P(\text{Unfav}\mid -) &= \frac{0.2800}{0.4425} = 0.6328
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Payoff matrix (lakhs):**

| State | Direct launch | After positive test | After negative test |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Favourable | +12 | +12 | −1 |
| Unfavourable | −3 | −3 | −1 |

**Step 5 — Roll-back:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(A_1) &= 0.65 \times 12 + 0.35 \times (-3) = 7.80 - 1.05 = +\text{Rs. }6{,}75{,}000 \\
\text{EMV}(A_3) &= 0 \\
\text{EMV}(\text{Launch}\mid +) &= 0.8744 \times 12 + 0.1256 \times (-3) = 10.49 - 0.38 = +\text{Rs. }10{,}11{,}240 \\
\text{EMV}(\text{Launch}\mid -) &= 0.3672 \times 12 + 0.6328 \times (-3) = 4.41 - 1.90 = +\text{Rs. }2{,}51{,}000 \\
\text{Choose LAUNCH after − test since } 2{,}51{,}000 > -1{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{EMV}(A_2) &= 0.5575 \times 10{,}11{,}240 + 0.4425 \times (-1{,}00{,}000) \\
&= 5{,}63{,}776 - 44{,}250 \\
&= +\text{Rs. }5{,}19{,}526
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Strategy: A1 — Direct launch (EMV = +Rs. 6,75,000).** Strategy A2 is suboptimal despite the test because the unfavourable market still yields a positive launch payoff (Rs. 2 L) that outweighs the abandonment benefit (Rs. −1 L).

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 6 — EMV with perfect information:**

$$
\text{EMV}_{\text{perfect}} = 0.65 \times 12 + 0.35 \times 0 = +\text{Rs. }7{,}80{,}000
$$

$$
\text{EVPI} = 7{,}80{,}000 - 6{,}75{,}000 = \boxed{\text{Rs. }1{,}05{,}000}
$$

**Step 7 — EVSI:**

$$
\text{EVSI} = \text{EMV}(A_2) - \text{EMV}(A_1) = 5{,}19{,}526 - 6{,}75{,}000 = -\text{Rs. }1{,}55{,}474
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students frequently make THREE recurring errors here. Each one costs **2–3 marks** in valuation:
> 1. **Forgetting the Bayes' denominator check** — always verify $\sum P(S_i \mid E) = 1$.
> 2. **Confusing EVSI with EVPI** — EVSI uses the *imperfect* sample EMV, not the perfect-information EMV.
> 3. **Selecting the *maximum EMV* alternative without first checking whether the test cost ≤ EVSI.** A negative EVSI means the test destroys value.

> **Conclusion (Question A):** Since EVSI is **negative**, test marketing is **not** economically justified. The company should launch directly. Maximum payable for perfect information = **Rs. 1,05,000.**

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of decision tree analysis. Discuss any **six advantages** and **four limitations** of decision trees in operations management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm must choose between installing a **semi-automatic machine** (Cost = Rs. 4,00,000) and a **fully-automatic machine** (Cost = Rs. 7,00,000). Annual profit depends on demand:

| Demand state | Probability | Profit (Semi-Auto) | Profit (Full-Auto) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| High | 0.5 | Rs. 6,00,000 | Rs. 10,00,000 |
| Medium | 0.3 | Rs. 4,00,000 | Rs. 5,00,000 |
| Low | 0.2 | Rs. 1,00,000 | −Rs. 2,00,000 |

Compute the EMV of each alternative and recommend the better choice. Also compute the EVPI. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Solution to (a):**

A **decision tree** is a diagrammatic model representing a sequence of decisions, chance events, probabilities, and payoffs. The decision-maker solves it by **rolling back** from the terminal leaves to the root.

**Six Advantages:**
1. **Visual clarity** — entire problem visible on one page; ideal for board presentations.
2. **Handles sequential decisions** — multi-stage problems (test → decide → expand) are mapped chronologically.
3. **Incorporates probabilities and Bayes' updates** — accommodates new information seamlessly.
4. **Quantitative + qualitative balance** — payoffs are monetary, branches can carry qualitative labels.
5. **Forces structured thinking** — eliminates ad-hoc reasoning; lists all alternatives.
6. **Computes EMV, EVPI, EVSI uniformly** — supports the *value-of-information* paradigm.

**Four Limitations:**
1. **Probabilities are often subjective** — erroneous inputs produce erroneous outputs.
2. **Tree can become unwieldy** — explosion of branches if alternatives × states grow.
3. **Ignores risk-attitude** — pure EMV criterion is neutral to variance; risk-averse managers may reject the EMV-optimal choice.
4. **Static, single-decision-maker assumption** — does not natively model multi-player or dynamic-strategy games.

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Net profits (Annual profit − Investment cost):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{semi}}(\text{High}) &= 6 - 4 = +2 \text{ L} \\
V_{\text{semi}}(\text{Medium}) &= 4 - 4 = 0 \text{ L} \\
V_{\text{semi}}(\text{Low}) &= 1 - 4 = -3 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{full}}(\text{High}) &= 10 - 7 = +3 \text{ L} \\
V_{\text{full}}(\text{Medium}) &= 5 - 7 = -2 \text{ L} \\
V_{\text{full}}(\text{Low}) &= -2 - 7 = -9 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Roll-back EMVs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(\text{Semi-Auto}) &= 0.5 \times 2 + 0.3 \times 0 + 0.2 \times (-3) \\
&= 1.0 + 0.0 - 0.6 \\
&= +\text{Rs. }40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(\text{Full-Auto}) &= 0.5 \times 3 + 0.3 \times (-2) + 0.2 \times (-9) \\
&= 1.5 - 0.6 - 1.8 \\
&= -\text{Rs. }90{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Optimal Decision: Choose Semi-Automatic Machine (EMV = +Rs. 40,000).**

**Step 3 — EVPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}_{\text{perfect}} &= 0.5 \times \max(2,3) + 0.3 \times \max(0,-2) + 0.2 \times \max(-3,-9) \\
&= 0.5 \times 3 + 0.3 \times 0 + 0.2 \times (-3) \\
&= 1.5 + 0.0 - 0.6 \\
&= +\text{Rs. }90{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{EVPI} = 90{,}000 - 40{,}000 = \boxed{\text{Rs. }50{,}000}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B):**
> 1. In **(b)**, students often **forget to subtract the investment cost** from annual profit before computing EMV — this is the single most common deduction (≈ 3 marks lost).
> 2. In **(a)**, listing *advantages without examples* yields only half credit. Tie each advantage to the **Operations Management** context (e.g., capacity planning, sourcing).
> 3. For EVPI, ensure you take $\max(V_{ij})$ *per row* — not a column-wise maximum.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Decision Tree Analysis**

- ✅ **Decision Tree** = visual rollback model combining **decisions (□)** + **chance (○)** + **payoffs (△)**.
- ✅ Always **right-to-left rollback**: chance node → EMV (sum), decision node → best EMV (max).
- ✅ Probability branch labels out of a **chance node must sum to 1**.
- ✅ Use **Bayes' theorem** when a sample/test signal is available — compute **joint**, then **marginal**, then **posterior**.
- ✅ **EMV decision rule:** $\text{EMV}^* = \displaystyle\max_{j} \sum_i P_i V_{ij}$.
- ✅ **EVPI** uses the *perfect-information* EMV: take $\max(V_{ij})$ per state, then weight by prior probabilities.
- ✅ **EVSI** uses the *sample-information* EMV (the tree including the test branch, roll-back complete).
- ✅ **Buy information** iff **EVSI ≥ Cost of acquiring that information**; otherwise reject it.
- ✅ **Limitations**: subjective probabilities, tree-size explosion, ignores risk-attitude, single-decision-maker.
- ✅ KTU examiners love three-step **"Frame → Roll-back → Recommend"** structure. Always end with a one-line **recommendation sentence**.
- ✅ Always **show units (Rs.)** and **intermediate values** in your valuation script — partial marks depend on visible working.
- ✅ For **2-stage** trees, label the second-stage decision explicitly (e.g., "Launch" vs "Don't Launch" after each test outcome).
- ✅ Common **mistakes**: (i) forgetting to add the test cost in the "abandon" branch, (ii) confusing $P(+ \mid \text{Fav})$ with $P(\text{Fav} \mid +)$, (iii) selecting the branch with the highest *individual payoff* instead of the highest *EMV*.

> **Final Examiner's Mantra:** *"In a decision-tree problem, the EMV is the trunk — every branch you draw must eventually lead back to it."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Module 3 – Production & Operations Planning Definitions

Module 3 of **Operations Management (PECBT865)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** primarily deals with the *planning, scheduling, and control* of production and inventory systems in manufacturing and service organizations. The foundational step to mastering this module is understanding the **formal definitions** of the planning hierarchy and the inventory control parameters used in industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 covers *Aggregate Planning, Master Production Scheduling (MPS), Materials Requirements Planning (MRP), Capacity Planning, and Inventory Control Models (EOQ, EPQ, Safety Stock, Reorder Point)*. Every subsequent numerical problem in this module begins with these definitions.

---

## 1.2 Formal KTU Definitions of Core Concepts

### A. Aggregate Planning (AP)

**Definition:** Aggregate Planning is a *medium-range (3 to 18 months)* operational planning activity that determines the overall **production quantity, workforce levels, and inventory levels** required to meet the forecasted demand at minimum total cost, while respecting capacity constraints.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of Aggregate Planning like deciding **how much rice to cook for the month** for a hostel kitchen. You don't plan each meal individually at this stage — you set a *monthly aggregate target* (e.g., 1500 kg) and adjust staff/labor, storage, and overtime to meet it. The detailed daily menu comes later in Master Scheduling.

### B. Master Production Schedule (MPS)

**Definition:** The Master Production Schedule is a *short-range (weekly or daily)* detailed plan that specifies the **quantity and timing of finished products (end items)** to be produced, derived from the aggregate plan and customer orders.

**Intuitive Analogy:** After deciding to cook 1500 kg of rice for the month, the MPS is the **weekly cooking calendar** — e.g., 350 kg in Week 1, 380 kg in Week 2, etc., specifying *which day* to cook.

### C. Materials Requirements Planning (MRP)

**Definition:** MRP is a *computer-based production planning and inventory control system* used to schedule the **dependent demand** of components and raw materials needed to fulfill the MPS, based on the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** and **lead times**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** If the MPS says "produce 100 bicycles this week," MRP figures out that you need *200 wheels, 100 frames, 100 handlebars, 800 bolts*, and tells you *when to order each* so that none arrive late.

### D. Capacity Planning

**Definition:** Capacity Planning is the process of determining the **maximum output rate** (production capacity) that a facility, machine, or workforce can sustain, and aligning it with the demand forecast to identify gaps and required adjustments.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A restaurant kitchen has 4 stoves. Even if 200 customers order biryani, the kitchen can only cook 40 plates/hour. Capacity Planning identifies this **bottleneck** and decides whether to add equipment or shift hours.

### E. Inventory

**Definition:** Inventory refers to the **stock of raw materials, work-in-process (WIP), finished goods, and MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operating) supplies** held by an organization to meet future demand, decouple operations, and buffer against uncertainties.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Inventory is the **water stored in a reservoir** — too little causes shortages (drought); too much causes storage costs and wastage (flood). The optimal level lies in between.

### F. Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

**Definition:** EOQ is the **fixed order quantity (Q\*)** that minimizes the **Total Inventory Cost** (sum of ordering cost and carrying cost), assuming constant demand rate, fixed ordering cost, and constant holding cost per unit per period.

**Intuitive Analogy:** It's the **"Goldilocks" order size** — not too small (else ordering cost dominates) and not too large (else holding cost dominates). The mathematical "sweet spot" minimizes total cost.

### G. Reorder Point (ROP)

**Definition:** The Reorder Point is the **inventory level at which a new order must be placed** to avoid stockout, calculated as the demand during lead time plus safety stock.

**Intuitive Analogy:** It's like the **"low-fuel warning threshold"** in your car — once the needle hits ROP, you refuel before the tank runs dry during the drive to the petrol station.

### H. Safety Stock (SS)

**Definition:** Safety Stock is the **extra inventory buffer** maintained to protect against **demand variability** and **supply (lead time) variability** during replenishment.

**Intuitive Analogy:** It's the **"extra cheese"** you keep in the fridge even when the pizza party is over — just in case unexpected guests arrive.

### I. Lead Time (L)

**Definition:** Lead Time is the **time elapsed** between placing a replenishment order and receiving the goods into inventory, expressed in days, weeks, or production periods.

### J. Production Order Quantity (Economic Production Quantity – EPQ)

**Definition:** EPQ is the optimal lot size when **production and consumption occur simultaneously** (i.e., inventory builds up gradually as production continues, rather than arriving instantaneously as in EOQ).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Favorite Question):**
> - **EOQ** = Instantaneous replenishment (entire lot arrives at once).
> - **EPQ** = Finite production rate (lot is produced gradually while demand continues).

---

## 1.3 Summary Table – Definitions at a Glance

| # | Term | Time Horizon | Key Output |
|---|------|--------------|------------|
| 1 | Aggregate Planning | 3–18 months | Production, workforce, inventory levels |
| 2 | Master Production Schedule | Weekly/Daily | End-item quantities and timing |
| 3 | MRP | Short-range | Component orders and timing |
| 4 | Capacity Planning | Long/Short | Required vs. Available capacity |
| 5 | EOQ | Per cycle | Optimal order size |
| 6 | ROP | Per cycle | Trigger level for reorder |
| 7 | Safety Stock | Per cycle | Buffer against uncertainty |
| 8 | EPQ | Per cycle | Optimal production lot size |
