# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Planning Framework (The Logic Behind "Why")

Operations Management planning follows a **top-down hierarchy**, where each level decomposes the previous into finer detail:

1. **Long-Range (Strategic) Planning** → *Capacity Planning* (facility, equipment, workforce structure) – 1 to 5 years.
2. **Medium-Range (Tactical) Planning** → *Aggregate Planning* (production rates, inventory, subcontracting) – 3 to 18 months.
3. **Short-Range (Operational) Planning** → *MPS, MRP, Dispatching, Sequencing* – days to weeks.
4. **Execution** → Shop floor control, monitoring, corrective action.

> [!TIP]
> **"Why this hierarchy?"** Because uncertainty decreases as we move down the hierarchy. Long-range plans deal with high uncertainty (markets, technology); short-range plans execute with near-certain inputs.

## 2.2 Inventory Cost Components (The Foundation of All EOQ/EPQ Problems)

Three mutually opposing cost elements govern every inventory model:

- **Ordering Cost (Co):** Fixed cost per order placed — includes purchase requisition, transportation, inspection, receiving. *Cost per order.*
- **Holding (Carrying) Cost (Ch):** Cost to carry one unit in inventory for one period — includes capital cost (opportunity), storage, insurance, obsolescence, shrinkage. *Cost per unit per period.*
- **Shortage (Stockout) Cost (Cs):** Penalty for running out of stock — includes lost sales, backorder costs, customer goodwill loss. *Cost per unit short.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** Total Relevant Cost = Ordering Cost + Holding Cost (in basic models). Shortage cost is included only when explicitly stated (backorder models).

## 2.3 The EOQ Logic – Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. Demand (D) is deterministic and constant → annual demand known.
2. Lead time (L) is known and constant.
3. Order arrives **instantaneously** (no gradual build-up).
4. No shortages are permitted (or shortages are explicitly costed).
5. Order quantity (Q) is constant and replenishes inventory to maximum level Q.

The Total Annual Inventory Cost is:

$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot C_o}_{\text{Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot C_h}_{\text{Holding Cost}}$$

- As **Q increases** → Ordering cost falls (fewer orders placed), but Holding cost rises (more average inventory).
- As **Q decreases** → Ordering cost rises, but Holding cost falls.
- **EOQ (Q\*)** is the value of Q where TC is **minimum** — i.e., where **dTC/dQ = 0**, which mathematically equals the point where Ordering Cost = Holding Cost.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Every formula below has been asked in KTU University Examinations. Memorize the units and assumptions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Assumptions / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}$ | Instantaneous replenishment; D = annual demand |
| 2 | **Total Cost at EOQ** | $TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h}$ | Minimum total relevant cost |
| 3 | **Number of Orders per Year** | $N = \dfrac{D}{Q^*}$ | Integer count |
| 4 | **Time Between Orders (Cycle Time)** | $T = \dfrac{Q^*}{D}$ | In years; convert to days: $\times 365$ |
| 5 | **Reorder Point** | $ROP = d \times L$ | d = daily demand, L = lead time in days |
| 6 | **ROP with Safety Stock** | $ROP = d \times L + SS$ | Standard KTU form |
| 7 | **Safety Stock (Variable Demand)** | $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Z = service-level z-score |
| 8 | **Safety Stock (Variable Lead Time)** | $SS = Z \cdot d \cdot \sigma_L$ | When L is random |
| 9 | **Safety Stock (Both Variable)** | $SS = Z \cdot \sqrt{L \sigma_d^2 + d^2 \sigma_L^2}$ | Most general form |
| 10 | **EPQ (Economic Production Quantity)** | $Q^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D C_o}{C_h}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | p = production rate, d = demand rate |
| 11 | **EPQ Total Cost** | $TC^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p-d}{p}}$ | Adjusted by $(1 - d/p)$ factor |
| 12 | **Maximum Inventory (EPQ)** | $I_{max} = Q^* \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$ | Build-up cap |
| 13 | **Production Run Time** | $t_p = \dfrac{Q^*}{p}$ | In periods (e.g., days) |
| 14 | **Quantity Discount Model – Tabular Method** | Compare TC at each price break; select min | KTU most-asked variant |
| 15 | **Aggregate Planning Cost** | $TC = \text{Production} + \text{Hiring/Firing} + \text{Overtime/Idle} + \text{Inventory/Backorder}$ | Per strategy table |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Industry:** EOQ/EPQ determine optimal batch sizes in JIT, lean, and mass production environments.
- **Retail & E-Commerce:** ROP and Safety Stock algorithms power Amazon, Flipkart warehouse replenishment.
- **Healthcare:** Hospital pharmacy inventory of medicines uses EOQ + ROP logic.
- **Automotive (e.g., Maruti, Hyundai):** MRP + MPS integrate with shop floor execution for body assembly lines.
- **Service Operations:** Aggregate Planning governs staffing in call centers, hospitals, airlines.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EOQ Formula

### Step 0 – Set Up the Variables

Let:
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $Q$ = Order quantity (units/order)
- $C_o$ = Ordering cost per order (Rs./order)
- $C_h$ = Holding cost per unit per year (Rs./unit-year)
- $N = \dfrac{D}{Q}$ = Number of orders placed per year

### Step 1 – Number of Orders

Since total annual demand is $D$ and each order brings $Q$ units, the number of orders placed in a year is:

$$N = \frac{D}{Q}$$

### Step 2 – Annual Ordering Cost

Each order costs $C_o$. With $N$ orders per year:

$$\text{Annual Ordering Cost} = N \cdot C_o = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot C_o$$

### Step 3 – Average Inventory

Since inventory depletes linearly from $Q$ to $0$ between orders (and replenishes instantly), the **average inventory** over a cycle is:

$$\text{Average Inventory} = \frac{Q + 0}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}$$

### Step 4 – Annual Holding Cost

Each unit costs $C_h$ per year to hold. With average inventory $\dfrac{Q}{2}$:

$$\text{Annual Holding Cost} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot C_h$$

### Step 5 – Total Annual Cost (Relevant Cost)

$$TC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot C_o + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot C_h$$

### Step 6 – Minimize TC by Differentiating w.r.t. Q and Setting to Zero

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D C_o}{Q^2} + \frac{C_h}{2} = 0$$

### Step 7 – Solve for Q\*

$$\frac{C_h}{2} = \frac{D C_o}{Q^{*2}}$$

$$Q^{*2} = \frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}$$

$$\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}}$$

### Step 8 – Verify Using Second-Derivative Test (Concavity Check)

$$\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D C_o}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \forall \, Q > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $TC(Q)$ is **convex** in $Q$, confirming a **minimum** at $Q^{*}$. ∎

### Step 9 – Substituting Q\* back into TC

$$TC(Q^*) = \frac{D \cdot C_o}{\sqrt{2 D C_o / C_h}} + \frac{\sqrt{2 D C_o / C_h}}{2} \cdot C_h$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{D^2 C_o^2 \cdot C_h}{2 D C_o}} + \frac{C_h}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{D C_o C_h}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{D C_o C_h}{2}}$$

$$\boxed{TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of EPQ (Economic Production Quantity)

### Set Up the Variables

- $D$ = Annual demand rate (units/year)
- $p$ = Annual production rate (units/year), with $p > D$
- $C_o$ = Setup cost per production run
- $C_h$ = Holding cost per unit per year
- $Q$ = Production lot size per run

### Inventory Build-Up Pattern

During the production run of duration $t_p = \dfrac{Q}{p}$, inventory builds up at net rate $(p - D)$. Hence:

$$I_{max} = (p - D) \cdot t_p = (p - D) \cdot \frac{Q}{p} = Q\left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right)$$

After production stops, inventory depletes at rate $D$ from $I_{max}$ to $0$ over time $\dfrac{Q}{D} - \dfrac{Q}{p}$.

### Average Inventory

Since inventory rises linearly from 0 to $I_{max}$ during $t_p$, then falls linearly from $I_{max}$ to 0 during the remainder of the cycle, the average is:

$$\bar{I} = \frac{I_{max}}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right)$$

### Total Cost Function

$$TC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot C_o + \frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right) \cdot C_h$$

### Differentiate and Set to Zero

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D C_o}{Q^2} + \frac{C_h}{2}\left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right) = 0$$

### Solve for Q\*

$$Q^{*2} = \frac{2 D C_o}{C_h \left(1 - \dfrac{D}{p}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{Q^{*}_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{p - D}}}$$

This confirms the **two-factor form**: the first factor is the classical EOQ; the second is the **EPQ adjustment factor** $\sqrt{p/(p-D)} > 1$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A company uses 18,000 units/year of a component. Ordering cost is Rs. 200/order and holding cost is Rs. 4/unit/year. Lead time is 5 days (assume 360 days/year). Find: (i) EOQ, (ii) Reorder Point, (iii) Number of orders/year, (iv) Total annual cost, (v) Time between orders.

### Given
- $D = 18{,}000$ units/year
- $C_o = \text{Rs. } 200$ /order
- $C_h = \text{Rs. } 4$ /unit/year
- $L = 5$ days, Year = 360 days
- $d = D / 360 = 50$ units/day

### (i) EOQ

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 200}{4}} = \sqrt{1{,}800{,}000} = 1341.64 \approx 1342 \text{ units}$$

### (ii) Reorder Point

$$ROP = d \times L = 50 \times 5 = 250 \text{ units}$$

### (iii) Number of Orders per Year

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{18{,}000}{1342} \approx 13.41 \text{ orders/year}$$

### (iv) Total Annual Cost

$$TC^* = \sqrt{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 200 \times 4} = \sqrt{28{,}800{,}000} = \text{Rs. } 5366.56$$

### (v) Cycle Time

$$T = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{1342}{18{,}000} = 0.0746 \text{ years} \approx 26.83 \text{ days}$$

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the following terms:**
(a) Aggregate Planning
(b) Master Production Schedule
(c) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Aggregate Planning:** A medium-range (3 to 18 months) tactical planning process that establishes overall production quantities, workforce levels, and inventory levels to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost, while respecting capacity constraints. It is the link between long-range strategic planning and short-range operational scheduling.

(b) **Master Production Schedule (MPS):** A short-range, detailed plan specifying *what end items* will be produced, *in what quantity*, and *when*, derived by disaggregating the aggregate plan into specific time buckets (weeks or days).

(c) **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ):** The fixed order quantity $Q^*$ that minimizes the sum of annual ordering cost and annual holding cost under deterministic, constant-demand assumptions. Mathematically, $Q^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o / C_h}$, where $D$ is annual demand, $C_o$ is ordering cost, and $C_h$ is holding cost per unit per year.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the following terms:**
(a) Safety Stock
(b) Reorder Point (ROP)
(c) Lead Time

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Safety Stock (SS):** Extra inventory held as a buffer to protect against fluctuations in demand and/or lead time, ensuring a specified service level. $SS = Z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{L}$ (variable demand) or $SS = Z \cdot d \cdot \sigma_L$ (variable lead time).

(b) **Reorder Point (ROP):** The inventory level at which a new replenishment order must be triggered. $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$, where $d$ is the average daily demand, $L$ is the lead time, and $SS$ is the safety stock.

(c) **Lead Time (L):** The elapsed time between placing a replenishment order and receiving the material into inventory. It may be constant (deterministic) or random (stochastic), expressed in days, weeks, or production periods.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Define Aggregate Planning. Explain the **Chase Strategy** and **Level Strategy** with their merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm has a forecasted annual demand of 24,000 units for Product X. The ordering cost is Rs. 300 per order and the holding cost is Rs. 6 per unit per year. The lead time is 8 days and the firm works 300 days per year. **Calculate:**
   (i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)
   (ii) Reorder Point (ROP)
   (iii) Number of orders per year
   (iv) Total annual inventory cost at EOQ
   (v) Cycle time in days **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution – Question A

### Part (a) – Chase vs. Level Strategy

**Aggregate Planning Definition (2 marks):** Aggregate planning is a medium-range (3–18 month) tactical process that determines production rates, workforce levels, and inventory levels to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost, considering hiring, firing, overtime, idle time, inventory holding, and backorder costs.

**Chase Strategy (2.5 marks):**
- **Concept:** Production is matched exactly to demand period-by-period; workforce is varied through hiring and firing.
- **Merits:** Minimal inventory carrying cost; minimal obsolescence.
- **Demerits:** High hiring/firing cost; employee morale issues; training overhead.

**Level Strategy (2.5 marks):**
- **Concept:** Workforce and production rate are kept constant; demand fluctuations are absorbed by inventory buildup and depletion.
- **Merits:** Stable workforce; lower hiring/firing cost; better morale.
- **Demerits:** High inventory carrying cost; risk of obsolescence; potential stockouts.

---

### Part (b) – EOQ Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $D = 24{,}000$ units/year
- $C_o = \text{Rs. } 300$/order
- $C_h = \text{Rs. } 6$/unit-year
- $L = 8$ days
- Working days = 300 days/year

**Daily demand:**
$$d = \frac{24{,}000}{300} = 80 \text{ units/day}$$

#### (i) Economic Order Quantity

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 300}{6}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{14{,}400{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{2{,}400{,}000} \approx 1549.19$$

$$\boxed{Q^* \approx 1549 \text{ units (rounded)}}$$

#### (ii) Reorder Point

$$ROP = d \times L = 80 \times 8 = \boxed{640 \text{ units}}$$

#### (iii) Number of Orders per Year

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{24{,}000}{1549} \approx \boxed{15.49 \approx 16 \text{ orders/year}}$$

#### (iv) Total Annual Cost at EOQ

$$TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h} = \sqrt{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 300 \times 6}$$

$$TC^* = \sqrt{86{,}400{,}000} = \boxed{\text{Rs. } 9295.16}$$

#### (v) Cycle Time

$$T = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{1549}{24{,}000} = 0.0645 \text{ years}$$

$$T = 0.0645 \times 300 = \boxed{19.36 \text{ days}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Define the following terms and state the formula for each:
   (i) Safety Stock with variable demand
   (ii) Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)
   (iii) Reorder Point with safety stock **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A production system has the following parameters:
- Annual demand $D = 36{,}000$ units
- Production rate $p = 60{,}000$ units/year
- Setup cost $C_o = \text{Rs. } 400$/production run
- Holding cost $C_h = \text{Rs. } 8$/unit-year
- Lead time $L = 6$ days, Year = 360 days

**Compute:**
   (i) EPQ
   (ii) Maximum inventory level $I_{max}$
   (iii) Production run time $t_p$
   (iv) Total annual cost at EPQ **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution – Question B

### Part (a) – Definitions and Formulas

**(i) Safety Stock (Variable Demand):** Extra buffer held to protect against fluctuations in demand when demand is random but lead time is constant.

$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

where $Z$ = z-score corresponding to desired service level; $\sigma_d$ = standard deviation of daily demand; $L$ = lead time in days.

**(ii) Economic Production Quantity (EPQ):** Optimal production lot size when production and consumption occur simultaneously, i.e., inventory builds up at a finite rate $p - D$.

$$Q^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{p - D}}$$

**(iii) Reorder Point with Safety Stock:**

$$ROP = d \cdot L + SS$$

where $d$ = daily demand, $L$ = lead time, $SS$ = safety stock.

---

### Part (b) – EPQ Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $D = 36{,}000$ units/year
- $p = 60{,}000$ units/year
- $C_o = \text{Rs. } 400$/run
- $C_h = \text{Rs. } 8$/unit-year
- $L = 6$ days, 360 days/year

#### (i) Economic Production Quantity

$$Q^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 36{,}000 \times 400}{8}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{60{,}000}{60{,}000 - 36{,}000}}$$

$$= \sqrt{3{,}600{,}000} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{60{,}000}{24{,}000}}$$

$$= 1897.37 \cdot \sqrt{2.5}$$

$$= 1897.37 \times 1.5811$$

$$\boxed{Q^*_{EPQ} \approx 3000 \text{ units}}$$

#### (ii) Maximum Inventory Level

$$I_{max} = Q^*_{EPQ} \left(1 - \frac{D}{p}\right) = 3000 \times \left(1 - \frac{36{,}000}{60{,}000}\right)$$

$$= 3000 \times (1 - 0.6) = 3000 \times 0.4$$

$$\boxed{I_{max} = 1200 \text{ units}}$$

#### (iii) Production Run Time

Daily production rate = $p / 360 = 60{,}000 / 360 = 166.67$ units/day.

$$t_p = \frac{Q^*_{EPQ}}{p} = \frac{3000}{60{,}000} = 0.05 \text{ years}$$

$$t_p = 0.05 \times 360 = \boxed{18 \text{ days}}$$

#### (iv) Total Annual Cost at EPQ

$$TC^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p - D}{p}}$$

$$= \sqrt{2 \times 36{,}000 \times 400 \times 8} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{24{,}000}{60{,}000}}$$

$$= \sqrt{230{,}400{,}000} \cdot \sqrt{0.4}$$

$$= 15{,}178.9 \times 0.6325$$

$$\boxed{TC^*_{EPQ} \approx \text{Rs. } 9599.61}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students forget to convert lead time to the same unit as demand (e.g., annual demand vs. daily lead time). Always compute $d = D / \text{working days}$ first.
> 2. **Confusing EOQ with EPQ:** EOQ assumes *instantaneous* replenishment; EPQ assumes *finite production rate*. Using the wrong formula forfeits 4–7 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting Safety Stock in ROP:** When the problem mentions "service level," the ROP formula MUST include $SS$. Writing only $d \cdot L$ costs full marks.
> 4. **Wrong Demand in Discount Problems:** When quantity discounts are offered, the demand $D$ remains constant — only the unit price changes. Students often change $D$ mistakenly.
> 5. **Round-off Errors:** Final EOQ/EPQ answers should be rounded to the nearest whole number (you cannot order 0.64 units). Do not round during intermediate steps.
> 6. **Confusing Total Cost with Purchase Cost:** "Relevant inventory cost" excludes the purchase cost $(D \cdot P)$; students often include it incorrectly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this on the morning of the exam!)**

- **Aggregate Planning** = medium-range, tactical, 3–18 months; uses chase, level, mixed strategies.
- **MPS** = disaggregates the aggregate plan into end-item weekly/daily production targets.
- **MRP** = dependent demand planning using BOM + inventory status + lead times.
- **Capacity Planning** = aligns available capacity (machines, labor) with required capacity (forecast).
- **EOQ** formula: $Q^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o / C_h}$ — minimizes ordering + holding cost only.
- **Total cost at EOQ:** $TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h}$ — proof by convexity, $\dfrac{d^2 TC}{dQ^2} > 0$.
- **Number of orders:** $N = D/Q^*$; cycle time $T = Q^*/D$ (convert to days if needed).
- **Reorder Point:** $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$ — never forget safety stock when service level is given.
- **Safety Stock (demand variable):** $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$.
- **Safety Stock (lead time variable):** $SS = Z \cdot d \cdot \sigma_L$.
- **Safety Stock (both variable):** $SS = Z \cdot \sqrt{L \sigma_d^2 + d^2 \sigma_L^2}$.
- **EPQ formula:** $Q^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{2 D C_o / C_h} \cdot \sqrt{p/(p-D)}$ — production rate $p$ must exceed demand $d$.
- **EPQ max inventory:** $I_{max} = Q^* \cdot (1 - D/p)$.
- **Production run time:** $t_p = Q^*/p$ (in years or days).
- **Always convert units consistently** (annual demand vs. daily lead time vs. monthly cost).
- **Order quantity must be a positive whole number** — round $Q^*$ appropriately.
- **Quantity discounts:** compute EOQ; if infeasible at that price, adjust upward to next price break and recompute TC; pick the minimum TC across all price breaks.
- **Chase Strategy:** production = demand; variable workforce; high hiring/firing cost.
- **Level Strategy:** constant workforce; absorb demand via inventory; high carrying cost.
- **MRP inputs:** MPS, BOM, Inventory Status File. **Outputs:** Planned Order Schedule, Exception Reports.
- **Bills of Materials (BOM):** defines parent-component relationships (Level 0 = end item).
- **Lot-sizing techniques in MRP:** Lot-for-Lot, EOQ, Period Order Quantity (POQ), Wagner-Whitin.
- **Service Level $\neq$ Fill Rate:** Service level is the probability of *not stocking out* during lead time; fill rate is the fraction of demand met *from stock*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Operations Management (PECBT865)** under the KTU 2024 B.Tech scheme, the term **"Nature"** in Module 3 refers to the **fundamental character, scope, and inherent characteristics of Inventory Management** as a core functional area of operations. It establishes *what inventory is*, *why it exists*, *how it behaves economically*, and *what role it plays* in transforming inputs into outputs within a production–service system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examinable):**
> *"Inventory (or stock) refers to the stockpile of raw materials, work-in-process, finished goods, and other supplies held by an organization at any point in time to satisfy current and future demand, decouple operations, and buffer against uncertainties in supply and demand."*

The **nature of inventory management** therefore encompasses the **systematic planning, organizing, directing, and controlling** of these stocked resources to minimize the total cost of inventory while meeting operational service levels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **household kitchen**. You don't cook ingredients only when guests arrive — you pre-stock rice, lentils, spices, and vegetables. Why?

- **Raw materials** (rice, lentils) are like *raw material inventory*.
- **Half-prepared items** (soaked rice, ground paste) are like *work-in-progress inventory*.
- **Cooked food on the table** is like *finished goods inventory*.
- The **refrigerator** is the *warehouse* that holds these stocks.

Now think about what happens if you run out of sugar mid-recipe — your cooking is **disrupted**. If you buy too much, it **spoils** (becomes waste) and **ties up money** that could have been used elsewhere.

This everyday balancing act — *buying enough but not too much, at the right time, at the right cost* — is precisely what **inventory management** does at an industrial scale. The **"Nature"** of this discipline describes **what inventory is, why businesses need it, what forms it takes, and how it must be managed**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Core Truths About Inventory Nature (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. Inventory is a **necessary evil** — every operation needs it, but it consumes working capital.
> 2. Inventory exists because **supply and demand are rarely perfectly synchronized**.
> 3. The **fundamental trade-off** is between *holding cost* (of keeping stock) and *stockout cost* (of not having stock).

## 1.3 Standard Operational Metrics (Must Memorize)

The following metrics form the **vocabulary** of inventory management and are **frequently tested** in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions:

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Demand Rate** | $D$ or $\lambda$ | units/time |
| **Order Quantity** | $Q$ | units |
| **Reorder Point** | $ROP$ or $r$ | units |
| **Lead Time** | $L$ | time |
| **Holding Cost per unit per time** | $H$ or $C_h$ | currency/unit/time |
| **Ordering Cost per order** | $S$ or $C_o$ | currency/order |
| **Shortage / Stockout Cost** | $C_s$ or $C_p$ | currency/unit short |
| **Item Cost (Unit Price)** | $C$ or $P$ | currency/unit |
| **Annual Demand** | $D$ (capital letter) | units/year |
| **Service Level** | $\alpha$ or $SL$ | dimensionless (0–1) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU values *symbolic clarity* and *unit consistency*. Always state units explicitly in numerical answers. A student who writes "Q = 500" without units loses 0.5–1 mark.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Nature of Inventory

To understand the **nature of inventory**, we must dissect it across **five dimensions**: *what it is*, *why it exists*, *what forms it takes*, *what functions it serves*, and *what costs it imposes*.

### 2.1.1 What Inventory IS (and IS NOT)

Inventory is **NOT** the same as a list of items. A *list* is information; *inventory* is **physical, owned, and held**. Key characteristics:

- **Storability**: Inventory must be storable for some time horizon.
- **Measurability**: Each unit is countable and identifiable.
- **Value retention**: It has economic value (subject to spoilage, obsolescence, depreciation).
- **Ownership**: Belongs to the firm (or is on consignment, or in transit — all counted under specific accounting rules).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - *Inventory* = physical stock of goods held.
> - *Stock* = often used interchangeably with inventory.
> - *Stores* = the physical location/room where inventory is kept.
> - *WIP (Work-in-Progress)* = a *subcategory* of inventory, not a synonym.

### 2.1.2 Why Inventory EXISTS — The Five Drivers

Inventory is held for **five mutually overlapping reasons**, often remembered by the acronym **D-U-T-S-C** in the KTU curriculum:

1. **Decoupling operations** — Buffers between stages with different cycle times (e.g., a buffer between machining and assembly).
2. **Uncertainty buffer** — Protects against demand variability and supply lead-time variability.
3. **Transportation / Pipeline inventory** — Goods in transit (oil in a pipeline, goods on a truck).
4. **Speculative buffer** — Stocking up when prices are expected to rise (e.g., buying copper before tariff hikes).
5. **Cycle / Lot-size buffer** — Produced/purchased in batches for economies of scale (setup-cost reduction).

### 2.1.3 The Four Types of Inventory (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Type | Abbreviation | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Raw Materials Inventory** | RM | Inputs awaiting processing | Steel coils at a car plant |
| **Work-In-Progress** | WIP | Partially completed goods | Half-assembled smartphone |
| **Finished Goods Inventory** | FG | Completed, awaiting sale | Cars in a dealership lot |
| **MRO Inventory** | MRO | Maintenance, Repair, Operating supplies | Lubricants, spare parts, stationery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline / Transit Inventory** is sometimes listed as a fifth type, representing goods in transit from supplier to buyer. In KTU exams, listing **four** is acceptable, but **mentioning pipeline** as a fifth earns bonus credit.

### 2.1.4 Functions Performed by Inventory

Inventory performs **four critical functions** in an enterprise:

1. **Meeting anticipated customer demand** (seasonal stocks).
2. **Decoupling production stages** (buffer stocks).
3. **Buffering against supply and demand uncertainty** (safety stock).
4. **Enabling economic lot-sizing** (cycle stock).

### 2.1.5 Objectives of Inventory Management

The **Nature of Inventory Management** is best understood through its **dual objectives**:

$$\text{Minimize} \;\; \big[ \text{Total Inventory Cost (TIC)} \big]$$

$$\text{Subject to} \;\; \big[ \text{Satisfying customer demand (Service Level)} \big]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The *trade-off* between these two objectives is the **central paradox** of inventory management. KTU questions often ask students to *explain this paradox in 3 marks*. Memorize the phrase: *"balancing the cost of carrying stock against the cost of being out of stock."*

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas form the **analytical backbone** of Module 3. Every formula below is **mathematically verifiable** and **directly aligned** with the KTU 2024 syllabus.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EOQ (Wilson Model)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Optimal order quantity |
| 2 | **Total Annual Cost (EOQ)** | $TC = DC + \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | Cost at any order size |
| 3 | **Reorder Point (Deterministic)** | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L$ | When demand is constant |
| 4 | **Reorder Point (Uncertain)** | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + z\sigma_L$ | With safety stock |
| 5 | **Safety Stock** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Buffers demand variability |
| 6 | **Average Inventory (EOQ)** | $\bar{I} = \dfrac{Q}{2}$ | Steady-state level |
| 7 | **Number of Orders per Year** | $N = \dfrac{D}{Q}$ | Order frequency |
| 8 | **Cycle Time (Time Between Orders)** | $T = \dfrac{Q}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{DH}}$ | Days/months between orders |
| 9 | **Inventory Turnover Ratio** | $ITR = \dfrac{COGS}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Efficiency metric |
| 10 | **Days of Supply** | $DOS = \dfrac{\text{Average Inventory}}{\text{Average Daily Demand}}$ | Liquidity measure |
| 11 | **EOQ with Quantity Discount (single break)** | $Q^*_i = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{C \cdot i}$ where $i$ = holding % | Multi-price tier |
| 12 | **Production Order Quantity (EPQ)** | $Q_p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H} \cdot \dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | Finite production rate $p > d$ |

### 2.2.1 Notation Legend

- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (currency)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (currency)
- $Q$ = Order quantity (units)
- $L$ = Lead time (time)
- $z$ = Standard normal deviate for service level $\alpha$
- $\sigma_d$ = Standard deviation of daily demand
- $\sigma_L$ = Std. dev. of demand during lead time
- $p$ = Production rate (units/time) — for EPQ model
- $i$ = Holding cost as a fraction of unit cost
- $C$ or $P$ = Unit purchase price

### 2.2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inventory management is **not a finance topic** — it is fundamentally an **engineering operations problem**. Applications include:

- **Manufacturing plants**: Setting reorder points for raw materials to keep assembly lines running.
- **Construction engineering**: Stockpiling cement, steel, and aggregate to buffer against monsoon delays.
- **Hospitals / Pharma**: Managing stock of critical drugs (blood banks, vaccines) where shortage = life loss.
- **E-commerce / Retail**: Balancing warehouse inventory across geographies.
- **Power sector**: Maintaining MRO inventory for turbines and transformers to minimize downtime.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU viva-voce, students who connect inventory concepts to **engineering applications** (e.g., "EOQ in steel procurement for a structural project") score substantially higher than those giving abstract answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Formula

The **EOQ model** (also called the **Wilson–Harris Model**) is the **single most important derivation** in KTU's Operations Management Module 3. You may be asked to derive it for **full 7 marks**.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions of the Basic EOQ Model

1. Demand $D$ is **known and constant** over time.
2. Lead time $L$ is **known and constant**.
3. Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate) — this is relaxed in the EPQ model.
4. No **quantity discounts**, no **stockouts allowed**.
5. Holding cost $H$ and ordering cost $S$ are **constant** and **independent** of order size.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Cost Components**

The total annual inventory cost $TC(Q)$ has **three parts**:

- **Material cost (purchasing cost)**: $DC$ (constant for given $D$ and $C$; independent of $Q$).
- **Ordering cost**: $N \cdot S = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$ (decreases as $Q$ increases — fewer orders needed).
- **Holding cost**: $\bar{I} \cdot H = \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$ (increases as $Q$ increases — more stock on hand).

> **Logic**: Material cost does not affect the *optimal* $Q$ (it is a fixed offset), so we minimize only the *sum of ordering + holding cost*. Some textbooks call this the **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)**.

**Step 2 — Formulate the Objective Function**

$$\min_{Q > 0} \;\; TRC(Q) = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \dfrac{H \cdot Q}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Take the First Derivative with Respect to $Q$**

$$\dfrac{d(TRC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Set the First Derivative to Zero (First-Order Necessary Condition)**

$$-\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic Rearrangement**

$$\dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{DS}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{2DS}{H}$$

**Step 6 — Take the Square Root**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$$

**Step 7 — Confirm Minimum via Second-Order Test**

$$\dfrac{d^2(TRC)}{dQ^2} = \dfrac{2DS}{Q^3} \;\;\bigg\vert_{Q=Q^*} \; > \; 0$$

> **Interpretation**: The second derivative is positive for all $Q > 0$, confirming that $Q^*$ is indeed a **minimum** (the cost curve is convex). This is the **KTU standard proof** expected in derivation questions.

### 3.1.3 Optional — Derivation Using Calculus vs. Algebraic Equality

A **popular KTU alternative proof** uses the fact that at optimum, the **marginal ordering cost equals the marginal holding cost**:

$$\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} = \dfrac{H}{2}$$

Multiplying both sides by $Q$ and equating ordering and holding costs directly:

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \text{Holding Cost}$$

$$\dfrac{DS}{Q} = \dfrac{QH}{2}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$2DS = Q^2 H$$

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{2DS}{H} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Writing the *equating-of-costs* argument is a **simpler**, more intuitive proof. Examiners often give **full marks** for this shorter version. The full calculus derivation earns a *bonus feel*, but either is acceptable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reorder Point with Safety Stock

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

- Demand per day $\sim$ Normal$(\bar{d}, \sigma_d^2)$.
- Lead time $L$ is constant (in days).
- We require service level $\alpha$ (probability of not stocking out during lead time).

### 3.2.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Demand during lead time** is a normal random variable:

$$D_L \sim N(\bar{d} \cdot L, \; \sigma_d^2 \cdot L)$$

(For independent daily demands, the variance adds: $\text{Var}(D_L) = L \cdot \sigma_d^2$.)

**Step 2 — Stockout occurs** if $D_L > ROP$. To ensure $\Pr(D_L \le ROP) \ge \alpha$:

$$\Pr\!\left(Z \le \dfrac{ROP - \bar{d}L}{\sigma_d \sqrt{L}}\right) \ge \alpha$$

**Step 3 — From standard normal table**, $z_\alpha$ satisfies $\Phi(z_\alpha) = \alpha$. So:

$$z_\alpha = \dfrac{ROP - \bar{d}L}{\sigma_d \sqrt{L}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $ROP$:**

$$ROP = \bar{d}L + z_\alpha \cdot \sigma_d \sqrt{L}$$

**Step 5 — Define Safety Stock** as the buffer above mean lead-time demand:

$$SS = z_\alpha \cdot \sigma_d \sqrt{L} = z_\alpha \cdot \sigma_L$$

> **Verification with units:** $\bar{d}$ in units/day, $L$ in days → $\bar{d}L$ in units. $\sigma_d$ in units/day, $\sqrt{L}$ in $\sqrt{\text{days}}$ → $\sigma_d \sqrt{L}$ in units. The result $ROP$ is in units. ✓

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

> *"A manufacturing firm uses 18,000 units/year of a raw material. The cost of placing one order is ₹250, and the annual holding cost is ₹4 per unit. The lead time is 6 days, and the firm operates 300 days per year. Calculate the EOQ, the number of orders per year, the cycle time, the reorder point, and the total annual inventory cost."*

### 3.3.2 Solution

**Given:** $D = 18{,}000$ units/year, $S = \text{₹}250/\text{order}$, $H = \text{₹}4/\text{unit/year}$, $L = 6$ days, working days $= 300$.

**Step A — EOQ:**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 18{,}000 \cdot 250}{4}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{9{,}000{,}000}{4}} = \sqrt{2{,}250{,}000}$$

$$Q^* = 1500 \;\; \text{units}$$

**Step B — Number of orders per year:**

$$N = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{1500} = 12 \;\; \text{orders/year}$$

**Step C — Cycle time (time between orders):**

$$T = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \dfrac{1500}{18{,}000} \;\; \text{year} = \dfrac{1}{12} \;\; \text{year} = 25 \;\; \text{days}$$

*(Verification: 12 orders × 25 days/order = 300 working days ✓)*

**Step D — Reorder point (assume deterministic demand for this part):**

$$\bar{d} = \dfrac{D}{\text{working days}} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{300} = 60 \;\; \text{units/day}$$

$$ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L = 60 \cdot 6 = 360 \;\; \text{units}$$

**Step E — Total Annual Cost:**

$$TC = \underbrace{DC}_{\text{material}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{D}{Q}S}_{\text{ordering}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{Q}{2}H}_{\text{holding}}$$

- Ordering cost: $\dfrac{18{,}000}{1500} \cdot 250 = 12 \cdot 250 = \text{₹}3{,}000$
- Holding cost: $\dfrac{1500}{2} \cdot 4 = 750 \cdot 4 = \text{₹}3{,}000$
- *(Note: At EOQ, ordering cost = holding cost — a key sanity check.)*

**Final Answer (Model Format for KTU):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| EOQ ($Q^*$) | **1,500 units** |
| Orders/year ($N$) | **12 orders** |
| Cycle time ($T$) | **25 days** |
| Reorder Point ($ROP$) | **360 units** |
| Total Ordering Cost | **₹3,000** |
| Total Holding Cost | **₹3,000** |
| Total Variable Cost | **₹6,000** |

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Hack:** At $Q = Q^*$, the ordering cost and holding cost are **equal**. This is a *sanity check* you can write in 5 seconds to confirm your EOQ is correct. KTU examiners reward such verification statements.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — EOQ Behavior Under Parameter Changes

| Parameter Change | EOQ ($Q^*$) | Orders/Year ($N$) | Cycle Time ($T$) | $ROP$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand $D$ **increases** | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ |
| Ordering cost $S$ **increases** | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | unchanged |
| Holding cost $H$ **increases** | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | unchanged |
| Lead time $L$ **increases** | unchanged | unchanged | unchanged | $\uparrow$ |

> **Mnemonic:** "Hold less, order less, but earlier" when holding cost rises.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Nature of Inventory (Conceptual Map)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **five dimensions of the "Nature of Inventory"** as taught in KTU Module 3.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Nature of Inventory Management] --> B[What it IS]
    A --> C[Why it EXISTS]
    A --> D[TYPES of Inventory]
    A --> E[FUNCTIONS Performed]
    A --> F[OBJECTIVES and TRADE OFFS]

    B --> B1[Physical stock of goods]
    B --> B2[Owned by the firm]
    B --> B3[Measurable and storable]

    C --> C1[Decoupling operations]
    C --> C2[Buffering uncertainty]
    C --> C3[Pipeline transit stock]
    C --> C4[Speculative buying]
    C --> C5[Economies of lot size]

    D --> D1[Raw Materials RM]
    D --> D2[Work In Progress WIP]
    D --> D3[Finished Goods FG]
    D --> D4[MRO Supplies]

    E --> E1[Meet anticipated demand]
    E --> E2[Decouple production stages]
    E --> E3[Buffer supply and demand]
    E --> E4[Enable lot sizing]

    F --> F1[MINIMIZE Total Cost]
    F --> F2[MAXIMIZE Service Level]
    F --> F3[TRADE OFF Hold versus Shortage]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Inventory Cost Components and EOQ Trade-Off

This diagram shows how **Total Cost** is decomposed and how the **EOQ equilibrium** emerges at the minimum point.

```mermaid
graph TD
    TC[Total Annual Cost TC] --> MC[Material Cost DC constant]
    TC --> OC[Ordering Cost DS over Q]
    TC --> HC[Holding Cost Q times H over 2]

    OC --> OCN[Decreases as Q increases]
    HC --> HCN[Increases as Q increases]

    OCN --> EQ[EOQ at intersection]
    HCN --> EQ

    EQ --> QSTAR[Q star equals sqrt of 2DS over H]
    EQ --> CHECK[Sanity check: OC equals HC at Q star]

    TC --> OBJ[Goal: minimize TC subject to service level alpha]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Flow of an Inventory Decision System

This block diagram represents the **operational architecture** of how inventory decisions are made in a real engineering system.

```mermaid
graph TD
    INP1[Demand Data D and d bar] --> PROC[Inventory Decision Engine]
    INP2[Cost Data S and H] --> PROC
    INP3[Lead Time L] --> PROC
    INP4[Service Level alpha] --> PROC

    PROC --> CALC1[Compute EOQ Q star]
    PROC --> CALC2[Compute ROP with Safety Stock]
    PROC --> CALC3[Compute Cycle Time T]
    PROC --> CALC4[Compute Total Cost TC]

    CALC1 --> OUT1[Order Q star units]
    CALC2 --> OUT2[Reorder when stock equals ROP]
    CALC3 --> OUT3[Place next order in T days]
    CALC4 --> OUT4[Track and minimize TC]

    OUT1 --> FEED[Feedback Loop: Update D, S, H, L]
    OUT2 --> FEED
    OUT3 --> FEED
    OUT4 --> FEED
    FEED --> PROC
```

## 4.4 Diagram — Inventory Level Profile Over Time (Sawtooth Pattern)

The classical **sawtooth pattern** illustrates the cyclical behavior of inventory under the EOQ policy.

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0[Time t0 Order placed] --> T1[Stock rises to Q star]
    T1 --> T2[Stock depletes linearly at rate d bar]
    T2 --> T3[At t equals t0 plus T, stock equals zero]
    T3 --> T4[Next order placed and cycle repeats]
    T4 --> T5[Stock rises to Q star again]
    T5 --> T6[Steady state sawtooth established]

    T1 -. Average level .-> AVG[Average inventory equals Q star over 2]
    T2 -. Maximum level .-> MAX[Maximum inventory equals Q star]
    T3 -. Minimum level .-> MIN[Minimum inventory equals 0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Sawtooth Diagram in KTU Exams:**
> - The **peak** of the sawtooth = $Q^*$ (just after replenishment).
> - The **trough** of the sawtooth = 0 (just before next order).
> - The **average** = $Q^*/2$ (area of triangle / base = $Q^*/2$).
> - The **time-axis spacing** between peaks = $T$ (cycle time).

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Classification of Inventory Models (KTU Taxonomy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    M[Inventory Models] --> M1[Deterministic Models]
    M --> M2[Probabilistic Models]

    M1 --> M11[EOQ Economic Order Quantity]
    M1 --> M12[EPQ Economic Production Quantity]
    M1 --> M13[Quantity Discount Models]
    M1 --> M14[Multi Item Constrained Models]

    M2 --> M21[Continuous Review Q system]
    M2 --> M22[Periodic Review P system]
    M2 --> M23[Safety Stock with Normal Demand]
    M2 --> M24[Newsvendor Single Period Model]

    M11 --> M11a[Instantaneous replenishment]
    M12 --> M12a[Finite production rate p greater than d]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – April 2024]**

> *"What is meant by the 'Nature of Inventory'? List any four types of inventory with one example each."*

**Model Answer (3 marks — word limit ≈ 80–100 words):**

The **nature of inventory** describes its **fundamental characteristics** as a stock of goods held to meet current and future demand, decouple operations, and buffer against uncertainty. Inventory is physical, measurable, and has economic value.

The **four types of inventory** are:

1. **Raw Materials (RM)** — Steel coils at a fabrication plant *(0.5 mark)*.
2. **Work-in-Progress (WIP)** — Half-assembled printed circuit boards *(0.5 mark)*.
3. **Finished Goods (FG)** — Branded laptops ready for dispatch *(0.5 mark)*.
4. **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operating supplies)** — Lubricants, spare parts, stationery *(0.5 mark)*.

*(Opening 1-mark line about nature of inventory + four types with examples = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – December 2023]**

> *"Briefly explain any three functions performed by inventory in a manufacturing organization."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Inventory performs the following functions in manufacturing *(each function = 1 mark)*:

1. **Meeting Anticipated Demand:** Stocks of finished goods are maintained to satisfy customer orders during periods of peak or seasonal demand, ensuring uninterrupted service.

2. **Decoupling Production Stages:** Buffers are placed between consecutive operations (e.g., between machining and assembly) so that a slow-down in one stage does not halt the next.

3. **Buffering Against Uncertainty:** Safety stock protects the system against fluctuations in demand and supply lead times, improving reliability of operations.

4. *(Optional 4th)* **Enabling Economies of Lot-Size:** Producing or ordering in batches reduces per-unit setup/ordering cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Format:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme Outcomes]**

> **A (a)** *"With the help of a neat sketch, explain the nature and behavior of inventory under the EOQ model. Derive the formula for the Economic Order Quantity."* **(7 marks)**
>
> **A (b)** *"A company uses 24,000 units/year of a component. Ordering cost is ₹150 per order, and holding cost is ₹6 per unit per year. Lead time is 8 days and the firm works 240 days per year. Calculate EOQ, the number of orders per year, the cycle time, the reorder point, and the total annual cost (excluding material cost)."* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Concept and Sketch (3 marks):**

The EOQ model assumes **constant, known demand** and **instantaneous replenishment**. Inventory level rises to $Q$ the moment an order arrives, then depletes linearly at rate $\bar{d}$ to zero, after which the next order is placed. The graph is a **sawtooth pattern**.

**Step 2 — Cost Structure (1 mark):**

- **Ordering cost** decreases with $Q$ (fewer orders needed).
- **Holding cost** increases with $Q$ (more average stock).
- **Total relevant cost** = $\dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{HQ}{2}$.

**Step 3 — Derivation (3 marks):**

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\dfrac{d(TRC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0$$

Solving:

$$Q^{*2} = \dfrac{2DS}{H} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$$

Second derivative $= \dfrac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0$, confirming a **minimum**.

*(Sketch of the sawtooth + cost curve earning the remaining 1 mark for visual clarity.)*

#### Model Solution to A (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $D = 24{,}000$ units/year, $S = \text{₹}150$, $H = \text{₹}6$/unit/year, $L = 8$ days, working days $= 240$.

**Step 1 — EOQ (2 marks):**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 24{,}000 \cdot 150}{6}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{7{,}200{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000} \approx 1095.4 \;\; \text{units}$$

Round to **1,095 units** *(or 1,096 — both acceptable)*.

**Step 2 — Number of orders per year (1 mark):**

$$N = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} = \dfrac{24{,}000}{1095.4} \approx 21.91 \;\; \Rightarrow\;\; 22 \;\; \text{orders/year}$$

**Step 3 — Cycle time (1 mark):**

$$T = \dfrac{Q^{*}}{D} = \dfrac{1095.4}{24{,}000} \;\; \text{year} \approx 0.04564 \;\; \text{year}$$

In days: $T = 0.04564 \cdot 240 \approx 10.95 \;\; \text{days} \approx 11 \;\; \text{days}$.

**Step 4 — Reorder point (1.5 marks):**

$$\bar{d} = \dfrac{24{,}000}{240} = 100 \;\; \text{units/day}$$

$$ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L = 100 \cdot 8 = 800 \;\; \text{units}$$

**Step 5 — Total annual cost (1.5 marks):**

- Ordering cost: $\dfrac{24{,}000}{1095.4} \cdot 150 \approx 22 \cdot 150 = \text{₹}3{,}286$
- Holding cost: $\dfrac{1095.4}{2} \cdot 6 = 547.7 \cdot 6 \approx \text{₹}3{,}286$
- **Total relevant cost = ₹6,572 (approx.)**

*(Note the near-equal ordering and holding cost — sanity check passed.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme Outcomes]**

> **B (a)** *"Explain the various types of inventory with examples. Discuss the functions and objectives of inventory management in an engineering organization."* **(7 marks)**
>
> **B (b)** *"Discuss the 'Nature of Inventory Management' in detail. What are the key reasons for holding inventory? Explain with real-world engineering examples."* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Types of Inventory (3 marks, ~0.75 each):**

- **Raw Materials (RM):** Inputs awaiting first processing. *Example:* Iron ore at a steel plant.
- **Work-in-Progress (WIP):** Items in intermediate stages. *Example:* Unpainted car bodies on the assembly line.
- **Finished Goods (FG):** Completed and ready for sale. *Example:* Packaged biscuits in a warehouse.
- **MRO Inventory:** Items supporting operations, not part of the product. *Example:* Cutting tools, gloves, machine oil.

*(Optional 5th: Pipeline / Transit Inventory — goods being transported.)*

**Step 2 — Functions of Inventory (2 marks):**

(i) Meeting anticipated demand; (ii) Decoupling production stages; (iii) Buffering against uncertainty (safety stock); (iv) Enabling economic lot-sizing.

**Step 3 — Objectives (2 marks):**

- **Primary:** Minimize the total cost of inventory (ordering + holding + shortage).
- **Secondary:** Maintain a target service level (e.g., 95% or 99%).
- **Strategic:** Free up working capital tied in stock; reduce obsolescence and wastage.

---

#### Model Solution to B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Nature of Inventory Management (2 marks):**

Inventory management is the **systematic control** of stock levels, timing, and quantities. Its **nature** is **balancing** — too little stock causes stockouts (lost sales, idle labor, customer dissatisfaction); too much stock inflates costs and ties up working capital.

**Step 2 — Reasons for Holding Inventory (4 marks, ~1 mark each + 1 example):**

1. **Decoupling operations** — Buffers between machining and assembly. *Example:* WIP buffer between a slow CNC station and a fast packaging line.
2. **Uncertainty buffer** — Safety stock against demand/lead-time variability. *Example:* Medical oxygen stock at a hospital.
3. **Pipeline / Transit** — Goods in transit. *Example:* Crude oil in a 500-km pipeline.
4. **Speculative** — Buying ahead of price rise. *Example:* Stocking copper before a tariff hike.
5. **Economies of scale (lot-sizing)** — Producing in batches to amortize setup. *Example:* Printing 10,000 books in one press run vs. 1,000 ten times.

**Step 3 — Closing Synthesis (1 mark):**

The **nature** of inventory management is therefore **optimization under uncertainty** — providing the right quantity, at the right time, at the right cost, while supporting operational continuity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks per Question:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state units** in numerical answers (e.g., writing "Q = 500" instead of "Q = 500 units"). Always write **units**.
>
> 2. **Confusing WIP with Work-In-Process** (both acceptable in textbooks, but KTU prefers the abbreviation **WIP**).
>
> 3. **Mixing annual and daily rates**: If lead time is given in days, convert demand to *per day* before computing $ROP$. Failing this is the **single most common numerical error**.
>
> 4. **Omitting the second-derivative test** in EOQ derivation. KTU deducts **1 mark** if you claim $Q^*$ is optimal without verifying it is a minimum.
>
> 5. **Not stating the "service level" assumption** in reorder point problems. Always specify whether it is **deterministic** (no $z$) or **stochastic** (with $z\sigma$).
>
> 6. **Confusing Pipeline Inventory** with Safety Stock. Pipeline = goods *in transit*; Safety Stock = *buffer* above expected lead-time demand.
>
> 7. **In quantity-discount problems**, students forget to compare the **EOQ of each price tier** with the **break-point quantity** of that tier. KTU deducts **1.5 marks** for this omission.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition**: Inventory = physical stock of goods held for current/future use.
- [ ] **Four Types**: RM, WIP, FG, MRO *(+ Pipeline/Transit as optional 5th)*.
- [ ] **Five Reasons for Holding Inventory**: Decoupling, Uncertainty, Pipeline, Speculative, Lot-size economies.
- [ ] **Four Functions**: Meet demand, decouple, buffer, lot-size.
- [ ] **Two Objectives**: Minimize cost + Maintain service level.
- [ ] **EOQ Formula**: $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS / H}$ — **most tested formula**.
- [ ] **At EOQ**: Ordering cost = Holding cost *(sanity check)*.
- [ ] **ROP (Deterministic)**: $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L$.
- [ ] **ROP (Stochastic)**: $ROP = \bar{d}L + z_\alpha \sigma_d \sqrt{L}$.
- [ ] **Safety Stock**: $SS = z_\alpha \sigma_d \sqrt{L}$.
- [ ] **Number of Orders/Year**: $N = D / Q^*$.
- [ ] **Cycle Time**: $T = Q^* / D$ *(in years; multiply by 365/300 for days)*.
- [ ] **Average Inventory**: $\bar{I} = Q^* / 2$ (under EOQ).
- [ ] **Total Cost**: $TC = DC + (DS/Q) + (QH/2)$.
- [ ] **Inventory Turnover**: $ITR = COGS / \text{Average Inventory}$.
- [ ] **EOQ Assumption #1**: Constant, known demand.
- [ ] **EOQ Assumption #2**: Instantaneous replenishment *(relaxed in EPQ)*.
- [ ] **EPQ Formula**: $Q_p^* = \sqrt{(2DS / H) \cdot (p / (p - d))}$.
- [ ] **Pipeline Inventory** = goods in transit ≠ safety stock.
- [ ] **Always state units** in numerical answers.
- [ ] **Always verify minimum** via second derivative or cost-equality argument.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for "Why hold inventory?"** — **D-U-T-S-C**:
> **D**ecoupling, **U**ncertainty, **T**ransit, **S**peculation, **C**ycle stock.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategies of aggregate planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT865 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strategies of aggregate planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregate Planning (AP):** A medium-range (typically **3 to 18 months**) capacity planning and operational decision-making process that establishes a production plan, workforce levels, and inventory policies to meet the forecasted demand at minimum total cost, while balancing production rates, workforce, inventory, backorders, and subcontracting.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, Aggregate Planning strategies are the operational **tactics** (policies) an organization selects to reconcile the **disparity between forecasted demand and available capacity** over the medium-term planning horizon. The output of aggregate planning is a master schedule precursor called the **Production Plan**, expressed in product families or aggregate units (e.g., tons, labor-hours, units).

The **Scope Hierarchy** in Operations Management is:

$$\text{Long-Range (Capacity)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Aggr. Plan} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Master Schedule} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Short-Range (Dispatching)}$$

## 1.2 The Strategic Objective of Aggregate Planning

The single overarching goal of an AP strategy is:

$$\min \; Z = C_{\text{prod}} + C_{\text{wage}} + C_{\text{hire}} + C_{\text{fire}} + C_{\text{inv}} + C_{\text{back}} + C_{\text{sub}} + C_{\text{OT}}$$

Where every component represents a **cost** that the chosen strategy will either incur or avoid.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Restaurant Buffet Manager":** Imagine you run a college canteen that has to feed a fluctuating crowd — low on Mondays, exploding on Fridays. You must decide *one month in advance* how many cooks to hire, how much rice to pre-cook and refrigerate, whether to call part-time helpers on busy days, or whether to outsource Friday lunch to a nearby caterer. Each decision (more cooks, pre-cooked rice, hired help, outsourcing) carries a different cost. **Aggregate planning strategies are exactly these "lever choices"** for a manufacturing firm.

## 1.4 Classification of Aggregate Planning Strategies

The **two principal pure strategies** and their derivatives are:

| Category | Pure Strategy | Core Lever Manipulated |
|----------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Demand-Adapted** | Chase Strategy | Workforce / Production Rate |
| **Capacity-Adapted** | Level Strategy | Inventory |
| **Hybrid** | Mixed / Hybrid Strategy | Combination of levers |
| **External** | Subcontracting | Outsourced Capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students are expected to compute total cost under each pure strategy and compare them. The **lowest total cost strategy** is recommended for the firm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Aggregate Planning Strategies

Aggregate planning strategies are best understood as **"adjustable levers"** that managers can pull. The **8 canonical levers** are:

1. **Production Rate** (units/period)
2. **Workforce Size** (employees)
3. **Overtime / Undertime**
4. **Inventory** (carried from previous periods)
5. **Backorders / Lost Sales**
6. **Subcontracting**
7. **Part-Time Workers**
8. **Hire / Fire**

These levers combine to form the following **Strategy Matrix**:

## 2.2 Strategy 1 — Level (Constant-Workforce) Strategy

**Philosophy:** Keep production rate and workforce **constant**. Absorb demand fluctuation **purely through inventory buildup and drawdown**.

- **Production per period:** $P_t = \text{constant} = \dfrac{\sum D_t}{N}$
- **Inventory pattern:** Inventory builds during low-demand months, depletes during peak months.
- **Cost structure:** Low hiring/firing cost; high inventory carrying cost.
- **Best for:** Stable demand, high inventory carrying cost is offset by low adjustment costs.

**Key Formulas:**

$$P_t = \bar{D} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N} D_t$$

$$I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = Production in period $t$
- $\bar{D}$ = Average demand per period
- $I_t$ = Ending inventory in period $t$
- $D_t$ = Demand in period $t$
- $N$ = Number of periods

## 2.3 Strategy 2 — Chase (Demand-Matching) Strategy

**Philosophy:** Vary production (and workforce) to **exactly match** the demand in each period. **No inventory is carried**; no backorders are allowed.

- **Production per period:** $P_t = D_t$
- **Workforce** fluctuates every period.
- **Cost structure:** High hiring/firing cost; zero inventory holding cost.
- **Best for:** High inventory carrying cost, low adjustment costs, skilled labor scarce.

$$W_t = \frac{P_t}{\text{Units per worker per period}}$$

Where $W_t$ is the workforce size in period $t$.

## 2.4 Strategy 3 — Mixed / Hybrid Strategy

**Philosophy:** Combine elements of chase and level strategies. Smooth production partially using inventory, and partially using workforce adjustment, overtime, or subcontracting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the strategy adopted in **real-world practice** by ~80% of firms because pure strategies are rarely optimal.

## 2.5 Strategy 4 — Subcontracting Strategy

**Philosophy:** Outsource the excess demand to external suppliers during peak periods while keeping internal production constant.

- **Cost driver:** Subcontracting cost per unit $C_s$.
- **Decision rule:** Use subcontracting when $C_s < C_{\text{OT}} + C_{\text{inventory}}$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Variable | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_t = \bar{D} = \dfrac{\sum D_t}{N}$ | Level strategy production rate | units |
| 2 | $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$ | Inventory balance equation | units |
| 3 | $W_t = \dfrac{P_t}{p}$ | Workforce required | workers |
| 4 | $H_t = \max(0,\, W_t - W_{t-1})$ | Hiring in period $t$ | workers |
| 5 | $F_t = \max(0,\, W_{t-1} - W_t)$ | Firing in period $t$ | workers |
| 6 | $C_{\text{inv}} = \sum h \cdot I_t$ | Total inventory cost | ₹ |
| 7 | $C_{\text{hire}} = \sum c_h \cdot H_t$ | Total hiring cost | ₹ |
| 8 | $C_{\text{fire}} = \sum c_f \cdot F_t$ | Total firing cost | ₹ |
| 9 | $C_{\text{reg}} = \sum c_r \cdot P_t$ | Regular production cost | ₹ |
| 10 | $C_{\text{OT}} = \sum c_{OT} \cdot OT_t$ | Overtime cost | ₹ |
| 11 | $C_{\text{sub}} = \sum c_s \cdot S_t$ | Subcontracting cost | ₹ |
| 12 | $C_{\text{back}} = \sum c_b \cdot B_t$ | Backorder cost | ₹ |
| 13 | $Z = \sum(\text{all cost components})$ | Total aggregate cost | ₹ |

> **Naming Convention:** $c_r$ = regular wage per unit, $c_h$ = cost to hire one worker, $c_f$ = cost to fire one worker, $h$ = holding cost per unit per period, $c_{OT}$ = overtime premium per unit, $c_s$ = subcontracting cost per unit, $c_b$ = backorder cost per unit per period.

## 2.7 Engineering / Managerial Utility

Aggregate planning strategies are used in:
- **Manufacturing Industries** (Toyota Production System balancing)
- **Service Operations** (airline crew scheduling, hospital staffing)
- **Supply Chain Management** (vendor-managed inventory decisions)
- **Project-Driven Engineering Firms** (capacity booking for project portfolios)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Cost Function — Exhaustive Derivation

The **total cost function** of an aggregate plan is the sum of **eight disjoint cost streams**. Let us derive each formally.

### 3.1.1 Regular Production Cost (Labour + Material)

The regular production cost is the cost of producing units at the regular production rate using the regular workforce at regular wages.

$$C_{\text{reg}} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} c_r \cdot P_t$$

**Derivation logic:** If each unit costs $c_r$ to produce (combining direct labour, direct material, and variable overhead), and $P_t$ units are produced in period $t$, the regular cost over $N$ periods is the **summation** of the period-wise product $c_r \cdot P_t$.

### 3.1.2 Hiring Cost

When the workforce in period $t$ exceeds that in period $t-1$, additional workers are hired.

$$C_{\text{hire}} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} c_h \cdot \max(0, W_t - W_{t-1})$$

**Derivation logic:** $\max(0, W_t - W_{t-1})$ returns the **positive difference only** — because if $W_t < W_{t-1}$ we are *firing*, not hiring. Multiplying by $c_h$ (cost to recruit, train, onboard one worker) yields period hiring cost, and summation gives the **total hiring cost**.

### 3.1.3 Firing Cost

$$C_{\text{fire}} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} c_f \cdot \max(0, W_{t-1} - W_t)$$

**Derivation logic:** Symmetric to hiring. $\max(0, W_{t-1} - W_t)$ returns the **positive difference** in workforce (only when shrinking), and $c_f$ is severance + replacement cost per worker.

### 3.1.4 Inventory Holding Cost

$$C_{\text{inv}} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} h \cdot I_t^{+}$$

Where $I_t^{+} = \max(I_t, 0)$ is **non-negative inventory** (positive stock only).

**Derivation logic:** $h$ is the holding cost per unit per period (warehouse rent, insurance, capital tied up, spoilage). It applies **only when inventory is positive** (no "negative holding cost" when backorders occur — that is a separate cost).

### 3.1.5 Backorder Cost

$$C_{\text{back}} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} c_b \cdot I_t^{-}$$

Where $I_t^{-} = \max(-I_t, 0)$ is the **backorder quantity** (positive only when $I_t < 0$).

**Derivation logic:** When $I_t < 0$, demand exceeded supply, and customers wait. $c_b$ is the penalty per unit per period (lost goodwill, discount, expediting).

### 3.1.6 Overtime Cost

$$C_{\text{OT}} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} c_{OT} \cdot OT_t$$

**Derivation logic:** $OT_t$ is overtime production in period $t$. The cost premium $c_{OT} > c_r$ because overtime is paid at premium rates.

### 3.1.7 Subcontracting Cost

$$C_{\text{sub}} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} c_s \cdot S_t$$

**Derivation logic:** $S_t$ is units subcontracted in period $t$. Typically $c_s > c_r$ (vendor profit margin + transaction cost), but cheaper than overtime at extreme peaks.

### 3.1.8 Total Aggregate Cost

$$\boxed{\;Z = C_{\text{reg}} + C_{\text{hire}} + C_{\text{fire}} + C_{\text{inv}} + C_{\text{back}} + C_{\text{OT}} + C_{\text{sub}}\;}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

### **Problem Statement**

A firm forecasts demand for the next 4 months as: **D = [600, 800, 500, 700] units.** Beginning inventory $I_0 = 100$ units, ending inventory $I_4 = 100$ units. Cost parameters:
- Regular production cost: $c_r = \text{₹}10$/unit
- Holding cost: $h = \text{₹}2$/unit/month
- Hiring cost: $c_h = \text{₹}300$/worker
- Firing cost: $c_f = \text{₹}400$/worker
- Workforce productivity: $p = 50$ units/worker/month
- Initial workforce: $W_0 = 12$ workers

**Compare Level vs. Chase strategies. Which is cheaper?**

---

### **Step 1 — Level Strategy**

Compute average demand:

$$\bar{D} = \frac{600 + 800 + 500 + 700}{4} = \frac{2600}{4} = 650 \;\text{units/month}$$

**Constant production** $P_t = 650$ for all $t$.

**Workforce required:**

$$W = \frac{P_t}{p} = \frac{650}{50} = 13 \;\text{workers}$$

**Inventory evolution:**

$$I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$$

Computing period by period:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= 100 + 650 - 600 = 150 \\
I_2 &= 150 + 650 - 800 = 0 \\
I_3 &= 0 + 650 - 500 = 150 \\
I_4 &= 150 + 650 - 700 = 100 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The closing inventory $I_4 = 100$ matches the requirement, validating the plan.

**Cost Calculations:**

**Regular production cost:**

$$C_{\text{reg}} = 4 \times 650 \times 10 = \text{₹}26{,}000$$

**Inventory holding cost:**

$$C_{\text{inv}} = 2 \times (150 + 0 + 150 + 100) = 2 \times 400 = \text{₹}800$$

**Hiring cost:** Workforce goes from 12 → 13, so we hire 1 worker in month 1.

$$C_{\text{hire}} = 1 \times 300 = \text{₹}300$$

**Firing cost:** No firing. $C_{\text{fire}} = 0$.

**Total cost (Level Strategy):**

$$Z_{\text{level}} = 26{,}000 + 800 + 300 + 0 = \text{₹}27{,}100$$

---

### **Step 2 — Chase Strategy**

Production equals demand each period: $P_t = D_t$.

| Period | Demand $D_t$ | $P_t$ | $W_t = P_t/50$ | $W_{t-1}$ | Hire/Fire |
|--------|--------------|-------|----------------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | 600 | 600 | 12 | 12 | 0 |
| 2 | 800 | 800 | 16 | 12 | Hire 4 |
| 3 | 500 | 500 | 10 | 16 | Fire 6 |
| 4 | 700 | 700 | 14 | 10 | Hire 4 |

**Cost Calculations:**

**Regular production cost:**

$$C_{\text{reg}} = (600 + 800 + 500 + 700) \times 10 = 2600 \times 10 = \text{₹}26{,}000$$

**Inventory cost:** Production = Demand, so $I_t = 0$ always.

$$C_{\text{inv}} = 0$$

**Hiring cost:**

$$C_{\text{hire}} = (4 + 4) \times 300 = 8 \times 300 = \text{₹}2{,}400$$

**Firing cost:**

$$C_{\text{fire}} = 6 \times 400 = \text{₹}2{,}400$$

**Total cost (Chase Strategy):**

$$Z_{\text{chase}} = 26{,}000 + 0 + 2{,}400 + 2{,}400 = \text{₹}30{,}800$$

---

### **Step 3 — Comparison & Decision**

$$Z_{\text{level}} = \text{₹}27{,}100 \quad < \quad Z_{\text{chase}} = \text{₹}30{,}800$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The **Level Strategy** is preferred because it saves **₹3,700** over the Chase Strategy. The savings come from avoiding costly hire/fire churn, at the expense of higher inventory carrying.

### **Step 4 — Interpretation of Strategy Selection Rule**

The optimal strategy is governed by the **cost ratio test**:

$$\frac{c_h + c_f}{h} \;\gtrless\; \text{(Critical threshold)}$$

- If inventory holding cost $h$ is **low** → use **Level Strategy** (build inventory).
- If adjustment costs $c_h + c_f$ are **high** → use **Level Strategy**.
- If inventory cost $h$ is **high** and adjustment costs are **low** → use **Chase Strategy**.

## 3.3 Graphical & Tabular Comparison Matrix

| Cost Component | Level Strategy | Chase Strategy | Hybrid Strategy |
|----------------|:--------------:|:--------------:|:---------------:|
| Regular production | High | High | Medium |
| Inventory holding | **High** | **Zero** | Medium |
| Hiring/Firing | **Zero / Low** | **High** | Medium |
| Overtime | Zero | Zero | Variable |
| Subcontracting | Zero | Zero | Variable |
| **Total Cost** | Low if $h$ small | Low if $h$ large | **Usually Minimum** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Aggregate Planning Strategy Selection Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Forecasted Demand] --> B{Choose Aggregate Planning Strategy}
    B --> C[Level Strategy]
    B --> D[Chase Strategy]
    B --> E[Hybrid Strategy]
    B --> F[Subcontracting Strategy]

    C --> C1[Constant Workforce]
    C --> C2[Constant Production Rate]
    C --> C3[Use Inventory to Absorb Fluctuation]
    C --> C4[High Carrying Cost]
    C --> C5[Low Hire-Fire Cost]

    D --> D1[Variable Workforce]
    D --> D2[Production Equals Demand]
    D --> D3[Zero Inventory]
    D --> D4[High Hire-Fire Cost]
    D --> D5[Zero Carrying Cost]

    E --> E1[Partial Inventory]
    E --> E2[Partial Workforce Adjustment]
    E --> E3[Use Overtime]
    E --> E4[Use Part-Time Workers]
    E --> E5[Balances Tradeoff]

    F --> F1[Constant Internal Production]
    F --> F2[Outsource Excess Demand]
    F --> F3[Vendor Cost Premium]
    F --> F4[Quality Control Risk]

    C5 --> R[Compare Total Costs]
    D4 --> R
    E5 --> R
    F3 --> R
    R --> S[Select Minimum Cost Strategy]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Aggregate Planning Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input: Forecasted Demand] --> B[Compute Average Demand]

```
