## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the SPT Optimality Theorem (Mean Flow Time Minimization)

**Theorem**: For $n$ jobs on a **single machine**, the schedule that orders jobs in **ascending order of processing time** ($p_1 \le p_2 \le \dots \le p_n$) **minimizes the mean flow time** $\bar{F}$.

**Proof (Pairwise Interchange Argument)**:

Consider two adjacent jobs $A$ and $B$ in some sequence, with $p_A > p_B$.

Their contribution to total flow time in this order is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{AB} &= p_A + (p_A + p_B) = 2p_A + p_B
\end{aligned}
$$

If we swap to $BA$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{BA} &= p_B + (p_B + p_A) = 2p_B + p_A
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the difference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{AB} - F_{BA} &= (2p_A + p_B) - (2p_B + p_A) \\
&= p_A - p_B \\
&> 0 \quad \text{(since } p_A > p_B\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the swapped order **reduces** total flow time. Repeating this pairwise swap on every inversion eventually yields the SPT order, which must therefore be **optimal** for minimizing $\bar{F}$.

$\blacksquare$

> **Engineering Takeaway**: SPT is provably **optimal** for $\bar{F}$ on a single machine — a high-yield 7–14 mark KTU question stems from this proof.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Little's Law ($L = \lambda W$)

**Step 1 — Definitions**:
- $\lambda$ = long-run average arrival rate (jobs/time).
- $W$ = long-run average time a job spends in the system.
- $L$ = long-run average number of jobs in the system.

**Step 2 — Intuitive Proof** (counting argument over a horizon $T$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of arrivals in } T &= \lambda T \\
\text{Each arrival eventually leaves after time } W &\Rightarrow \text{total job-time accumulated} = W \cdot (\lambda T) \\
\text{But total job-time} &= (\text{avg system occupancy}) \times T = L \cdot T \\
\therefore \; L \cdot T &= \lambda T \cdot W \\
L &= \lambda W
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering consequence**: To **reduce WIP** ($L$) by half while keeping $\lambda$ fixed, you **must** halve $W$ (flow time). This is why **scheduling goals are inter-locked** — you cannot minimize WIP in isolation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Goals vs Common Scheduling Rules

| Scheduling Rule | Minimize $C_{max}$ | Minimize $\bar{F}$ | Minimize Tardiness | Maximize Utilization | Notes |
|-----------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| **FCFS** (First Come First Served) | Neutral | Neutral | Neutral | Neutral | Fair, no optimization |
| **SPT** (Shortest Processing Time) | Poor | **Optimal** ✓ | Good | Low (small jobs starve big) | Best for $\bar{F}$ |
| **EDD** (Earliest Due Date) | Neutral | Neutral | Good | Neutral | Simple, due-date aware |
| **CR** (Critical Ratio) | Neutral | Good | **Best** ✓ | Good | Dynamic priority |
| **Johnson's Rule** | **Optimal** ✓ (2-machine) | Good | Good | Good | Flow shop only |
| **CDS Heuristic** | Good ($m$-machines) | Good | Moderate | Good | $k = m(m-1)/2$ passes |
| **Palmer's Heuristic** | Good (slope index) | Moderate | Moderate | Good | Flow shop slope method |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No single rule is "best" for all goals** — the choice of rule is itself a **scheduling decision** based on which goal the firm prioritizes.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem**: 3 jobs arrive at time 0 on a single machine.

| Job | Processing Time $p_i$ (min) | Due Date $d_i$ (min) |
|-----|---:|---:|
| A   | 8  | 10 |
| B   | 4  | 12 |
| C   | 6  | 15 |

**Schedule S1: FCFS (A, B, C)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A &= 8, \quad T_A = \max(0, 8-10) = 0 \\
C_B &= 8+4 = 12, \quad T_B = \max(0, 12-12) = 0 \\
C_C &= 12+6 = 18, \quad T_C = \max(0, 18-15) = 3 \\
C_{max} &= 18,\quad \bar{F} = \frac{8+12+18}{3} = 12.67,\quad \sum T = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Schedule S2: SPT (B, C, A)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= 4, \quad T_B = 0 \\
C_C &= 4+6 = 10, \quad T_C = 0 \\
C_A &= 10+8 = 18, \quad T_A = 8 \\
C_{max} &= 18,\quad \bar{F} = \frac{4+10+18}{3} = 10.67,\quad \sum T = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Schedule S3: EDD (A, B, C) — same as FCFS in this case.**

**Schedule S4: CR (computed at time 0)**
- $CR_A = (10-0)/8 = 1.25$
- $CR_B = (12-0)/4 = 3.00$
- $CR_C = (15-0)/6 = 2.50$

Sequence: B → C → A (same as SPT here).

**Comparison Table**:

| Rule | $C_{max}$ | $\bar{F}$ | $\sum T$ |
|------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| FCFS | 18 | 12.67 | 3 |
| SPT  | 18 | **10.67** ✓ | 8 |
| EDD  | 18 | 12.67 | 3 |
| CR   | 18 | 10.67 | 8 |

> **Insight**: SPT wins on $\bar{F}$ (Goal 4) but loses on $\sum T$ (Goal: minimize tardiness). The **trade-off** is now numerically visible — this is the heart of KTU Module 3.

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any **four** goals of short-term scheduling in operations management and state whether each should be maximized or minimized.*

**Model Answer (4 marks equivalent — 3 marks for content)**:

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Minimize completion time / makespan ($C_{max}$)** – minimize.
> 2. **Maximize machine / labor utilization ($U$)** – maximize.
> 3. **Minimize work-in-process (WIP) inventory** – minimize.
> 4. **Minimize customer waiting time / mean flow time ($\bar{F}$)** – minimize.
> *(Acceptable alternatives: minimize idle time, minimize tardiness, maximize throughput.)*

(1 mark for direction; 2 marks for correctly identifying four distinct goals.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State **Little's Law** and explain how it connects two short-term scheduling goals.*

**Model Answer**:

> **Little's Law**: $L = \lambda W$, where $L$ = average number of jobs in the system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = average time in system.
>
> It links **Minimize WIP ($L$)** and **Minimize Flow Time ($W$)** at a given throughput ($\lambda$). To halve WIP, flow time must also halve; they cannot be optimized independently.

(1 mark statement + 1 mark variables + 1 mark application.)

### 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *Trade-off Analysis & Numerical Application*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

> **(a)** *Explain the **goals of short-term scheduling** in detail. Discuss the **trade-off** between **maximizing machine utilization** and **minimizing WIP inventory** with the help of **Kingman's Formula** and **Little's Law**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *Five jobs are to be processed on a single machine. Determine the schedule that **minimizes mean flow time** and compute the resulting **mean tardiness** and **makespan**.* **(7 marks)**

**Job Data**:

| Job | Processing Time $p_i$ (days) | Due Date $d_i$ (days from now) |
|-----|---:|---:|
| J1  | 3 | 7  |
| J2  | 7 | 10 |
| J3  | 5 | 12 |
| J4  | 2 | 4  |
| J5  | 6 | 15 |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Enumerate Goals (2 marks)**:

The six canonical goals of short-term scheduling are:
1. Minimize makespan ($C_{max}$)
2. Minimize mean flow time ($\bar{F}$)
3. Minimize WIP inventory
4. Minimize tardiness ($\sum T_i$)
5. Minimize idle time
6. Maximize utilization; Maximize throughput

**Step 2 — Trade-off (3 marks)**:

Pushing utilization close to 100% forces jobs to **queue** because the system has **no slack** to absorb variability. Queued jobs = **WIP**. Hence:
- **High Utilization** → **High WIP** → **High Flow Time**
- **Low WIP** → **Slack** → **Lower Utilization**

**Step 3 — Kingman's Formula (1 mark)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_q &\approx \left(\frac{c_a^2 + c_e^2}{2}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{1-\rho}\right) \cdot t_e
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\rho$ = utilization, $t_e$ = expected processing time, $c_a$ = coefficient of variation of arrivals, $c_e$ = coefficient of variation of service. As $\rho \to 1$, $W_q \to \infty$.

**Step 4 — Little's Law (1 mark)**: $L = \lambda W$. Higher $W$ (Kingman) at fixed $\lambda$ → higher WIP $L$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Apply SPT Rule** — sort jobs in ascending order of $p_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SPT Order: } J4(2) \rightarrow J1(3) \rightarrow J3(5) \rightarrow J5(6) \rightarrow J2(7)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute completion times**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{J4} &= 2 \\
C_{J1} &= 2 + 3 = 5 \\
C_{J3} &= 5 + 5 = 10 \\
C_{J5} &= 10 + 6 = 16 \\
C_{J2} &= 16 + 7 = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute Tardiness** $T_i = \max(0, C_i - d_i)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{J4} &= \max(0, 2-4) = 0 \\
T_{J1} &= \max(0, 5-7) = 0 \\
T_{J3} &= \max(0, 10-12) = 0 \\
T_{J5} &= \max(0, 16-15) = 1 \\
T_{J2} &= \max(0, 23-10) = 13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute KPIs**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= 23 \text{ days} \\
\bar{F} &= \frac{2+5+10+16+23}{5} = \frac{56}{5} = 11.2 \text{ days} \\
\sum T &= 0+0+0+1+13 = 14 \text{ days}, \quad \bar{T} = 2.8 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict**: SPT achieves the **optimal mean flow time** of 11.2 days for a single machine (provable by interchange argument). However, it allows heavy tardiness (J2 by 13 days) — illustrating the **trade-off** discussed in part (a).

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice on Goal-Based Rule Selection*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

> **(a)** *Differentiate between **strategic planning, aggregate planning, master scheduling, and short-term scheduling**. State where the **goals of short-term scheduling** fit in this hierarchy. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *Four jobs with the following data must be scheduled. **Compute the sequence using (i) SPT, (ii) EDD, and (iii) Critical Ratio**, and identify which rule best satisfies the goal of **minimizing tardiness**. **(7 marks)*

**Job Data**:

| Job | $p_i$ (hrs) | $d_i$ (hrs) |
|-----|---:|---:|
| P   | 6 | 15 |
| Q   | 4 | 9  |
| R   | 8 | 20 |
| S   | 3 | 6  |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Hierarchy Table (4 marks)**:

| Level | Horizon | Decision | Example Tool |
|-------|---------|----------|--------------|
| Strategic | 1–5 yr | Capacity, facility, technology | Long-range forecast |
| Aggregate | 6–18 mo | Output rate, inventory, workforce | Linear programming |
| Master Production Schedule | weeks | What & how much, by week | MPS sheet |
| **Short-Term Scheduling** | **hours–days** | **Job order, machine, time** | **SPT, EDD, CR** |

**Role of short-term scheduling goals (3 marks)**:
- Translates the **MPS into shop-floor actions**.
- The goals (min $C_{max}$, min $\bar{F}$, min WIP, etc.) become the **criteria** by which alternative job sequences are evaluated and selected.
- Feeds **feedback** to aggregate and master plans via actual KPI performance.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) SPT order**: Sort by $p_i$ ascending → **S(3), Q(4), P(6), R(8)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_S &= 3,\; C_Q = 7,\; C_P = 13,\; C_R = 21 \\
T_S &= 0,\; T_Q = 0,\; T_P = 0,\; T_R = 1 \\
\sum T_{SPT} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) EDD order**: Sort by $d_i$ ascending → **S(6), Q(9), P(15), R(20)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_S &= 3,\; C_Q = 7,\; C_P = 13,\; C_R = 21 \\
T_S &= 0,\; T_Q = 0,\; T_P = 0,\; T_R = 1 \\
\sum T_{EDD} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Critical Ratio (computed at $t=0$)**:
- $CR_P = (15-0)/6 = 2.50$
- $CR_Q = (9-0)/4 = 2.25$
- $CR_R = (20-0)/8 = 2.50$
- $CR_S = (6-0)/3 = 2.00$ ← smallest, so S first

When **recalculated dynamically** after each completion, CR can produce an **adaptive sequence**. In this dataset, initial CR order = S → Q → {P, R tied}.

**Best rule for minimizing tardiness**: **EDD or SPT** (both give $\sum T = 1$ in this case). In general, **EDD** is the canonical answer for the *minimize tardiness* goal when due dates dominate; **CR** adapts dynamically as the schedule unfolds.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks in Module 3 questions on Goals of Short-Term Scheduling:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the direction** of the goal (writing "maximize makespan" — a fatal sign of not reading the syllabus carefully). 1 mark lost per wrong direction.
> 2. **Confusing Makespan $C_{max}$ with Flow Time $F_i$** — they are **different**. $C_{max}$ is the maximum completion; $F_i$ is per-job. Mixing them loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the trade-off discussion** in 7-mark "goals" questions. KTU examiners explicitly award 2–3 marks for recognizing that **goals conflict**.
> 4. **Skipping the SPT interchange proof** when asked to justify SPT. The proof is worth at least 3 marks; stating "SPT is best" with no proof is treated as incomplete.
> 5. **Not naming Little's Law** when discussing WIP vs Flow Time. Always cite it as $L = \lambda W$.
> 6. **Wrong CR calculation** — forgetting that CR must be **recomputed** as time advances; the static CR at $t=0$ alone is insufficient for full marks.
> 7. **In numerical problems, forgetting to compute both $C_{max}$ AND $\bar{F}$** — both are usually required by the rubric.
