<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Anchor (Module 4 — Strategic Operations & Innovation)**
> This assignment integrates four end-of-module learning outcomes: (a) formulate an operations strategy, (b) evaluate quality management systems, (c) map and re-engineer cross-functional processes, and (d) justify process innovation using the Total Innovation Management (TIM) framework. It is a **Case-Study / Application-Level** question that maps to **CO4** of the PECBT865 syllabus.

---

## 1.1 The Four Pillars of This Assignment

### Pillar A — Operations Strategy (Strategic / Tactical / Operational)
According to **Slack & Brandon-Jones (Operations Management, 9th ed.)**, the operations strategy is the pattern of decisions and actions that shape the long-term capabilities of any operation and its contribution to overall strategy.

- **Strategic factors** → Top-level, long-horizon (10 yrs), board-driven, concern *what markets / products / geographies*.
- **Tactical factors** → Mid-level (1–3 yrs), middle-management, concern *capacity, facility footprint, network design*.
- **Operational factors** → Day-to-day (≤12 months), shop-floor, concern *scheduling, inventory, workforce, quality control*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Analogy):** Think of running a Zomato-Swiggy cloud-kitchen chain. *Strategic* = "We will expand into Tier-2 Kerala cities and add biryani as a category." *Tactical* = "We will lease 8 new kitchen hubs in Thrissur and Kozhikode with 500 m² built-up area each." *Operational* = "Tonight's shift schedule, prep quantities, rider allocation, and live order-fulfilment KPI tracking." Operations strategy is the **vertical alignment** between these three layers.

### Pillar B — Quality Management
**Quality** is "the totality of features and characteristics of a product or service that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs" (ISO 8402 / ISO 9000:2015). Doing things *right the first time* is the operational definition.

- **Quality of design** (what to build) vs. **Quality of conformance** (how well it is built).
- Frameworks: **TQM, Six Sigma (DMAIC), ISO 9001, Lean, Kaizen**.

### Pillar C — Cross-Functional Processes
A **process** is a structured set of activities designed to produce a specific output for a particular customer. **Cross-functional** means the process spans multiple departments (e.g., Sales → Inventory → Finance → Logistics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Analogy):** Placing an order on Amazon.in touches **15+ internal teams** — search ranking, pricing, inventory, fraud check, payment gateway, warehouse picker, packer, sortation, last-mile courier, customer-service CRM, returns, refund accounting. That chain *is* a cross-functional process.

### Pillar D — Process Innovation & Total Innovation Management (TIM)
**TIM (Total Innovation Management)** is the Chinese Academy of Sciences' framework that argues innovation must be **all-encompassing** — covering strategy, technology, market, organization, culture, and institution — and **all-stages** (idea → R&D → commercialization → diffusion).

For KTU, the TIM lens is applied to operations as:
1. **Strategic Innovation** (new business models).
2. **Technology Acquisition & Automation** (bots, AI, IoT, RPA).
3. **Cultural / Organizational Innovation** (agile squads, Holacracy).
4. **Market Innovation** (new customer segments / channels).

---

## 1.2 The Selected Organization — Amazon.com, Inc.

For this assignment, we select **Amazon** (HQ: Seattle, USA; India: Amazon.in / Amazon Seller Services Pvt. Ltd., Bengaluru). Justification:

- **Highly operations-intensive** (retail, logistics, AWS, devices).
- **Publicly documented KPIs** (delivery speed, AHT, OEE, NPS).
- **Best-in-class automation** (Kiva / Hercules / Pegasus / Proteus robots, 750k+ units in 2024).
- **Rich academic literature** for benchmarking.

> [!TIP]
> Students are free to substitute **Tata Motors, Infosys, Maruti Suzuki, Apollo Hospitals, Kerala State Cooperative Milk Marketing Federation (MILMA), or any manufacturing/service firm**. The analytical *frameworks* remain identical — only the empirical evidence changes.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Operations Strategy Hierarchy

### A. Strategic Factors (Long-Horizon, Top Management)
Decisions that fix the **competitive position** of the operation.

1. **Mission & competitive priorities** — cost, quality, speed, flexibility, dependability (Slack's *five performance objectives*).
2. **Product-market scope** — which customer segments, geographies, verticals.
3. **Strategic positioning** — Order Qualifier (OQ) vs. Order Winner (OW).
4. **Strategic decisions** — Make-or-buy, vertical integration, capacity expansion, M&A.
5. **Strategic alliances** — JV with Cloud-tail (India), partnerships with Future Coupons (before 2022), Smbhav, etc.

### B. Tactical Factors (Mid-Horizon, Functional Heads)
Decisions that **convert strategy into deployable plans**.

1. **Network / facility location** — number of Fulfillment Centers (FCs), sortation hubs, last-mile stations.
2. **Capacity planning** — cubic feet of FC storage, throughput per hour.
3. **Process technology choice** — conveyor vs. robotic sortation, ASRS.
4. **Supply-base design & supplier strategy** — single vs. dual sourcing.
5. **Workforce planning & skill-mix** — level of automation vs. human picker ratio.
6. **Inventory positioning** — SKU rationalization, safety-stock policy.

### C. Operational Factors (Daily, Shop-Floor / Floor-Manager)
Decisions that **execute the tactical plan hour-by-hour**.

1. **Detailed scheduling** — pick waves, truck departure windows.
2. **Lot sizing & replenishment** — min/max, (s, S) policy.
3. **Quality control (SPC)** — defect rates, AQL sampling.
4. **Performance measurement** — KPIs (OTIF, dock-to-stock, click-to-door, OEE).
5. **Daily huddles & Kaizen** — root-cause analysis, Gemba walks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Strategy is the *compass heading* (where the ship must sail). Tactics are the *route plan* (which ports, what speed, what fuel). Operations are the *helm and engine room* (every knot of progress today). Misalignment between any two layers causes execution failure.

---

## 2.2 Quality Management — Theoretical Frameworks

### Definition Recap
**Quality = Conformance to Requirements + Fitness for Use + Customer Delight (Kano Model)**

### The Three Quality Gurus (must cite in KTU answers)

| Guru | Famous Contribution | Core Idea | Key Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points, PDSA / PDCA | 85% of quality problems are system-managed, not worker-managed | System thinking |
| **Joseph Juran** | Quality Trilogy (Q-P-C) | Quality planning → Control → Improvement | Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | "Quality is Free" | Zero defects, Do-It-Right-First-Time (DIRFT) | Price of Non-Conformance (PONC) |

### Quality Management System (QMS) Building Blocks

1. **TQM** (Total Quality Management) — customer-focused, total participation, continuous improvement.
2. **Six Sigma** — DMAIC methodology, target **3.4 DPMO**.
3. **ISO 9001:2015** — process approach, risk-based thinking, PDCA, documented information.
4. **Lean** — eliminate **Muda (waste), Mura (unevenness), Muri (overburden)**.
5. **Kaizen** — small, continuous, daily improvements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cost of Quality (CoQ) Equation** — frequently asked as a 3-mark direct question:
> $$CoQ \;=\; COPQ \;+\; CoGQ$$
> $$CoP \;=\; Prevention\;Cost \;+\; Appraisal\;Cost \;+\; Internal\;Failure\;Cost \;+\; External\;Failure\;Cost$$
> $CoGQ$ is the *conformance* cost (doing it right); $COPQ$ is the *non-conformance* cost (rework, warranty, returns, lost goodwill).

### Six Sigma DMAIC Cheat-Sheet

| Phase | Verb | Tools |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **D**efine | Frame the problem | SIPOC, VOC, CTQ tree, Project Charter |
| **M**easure | Baseline the pain | Pareto, Process capability, Gage R&R |
| **A**nalyse | Find root cause | Ishikawa (Fishbone), 5-Why, Regression |
| **I**mprove | Pilot the fix | DOE, Pilots, Poka-Yoke |
| **C**ontrol | Sustain the gain | SPC, Control plans, Standard work |

---

## 2.3 Cross-Functional Process Tools — Theory

| Tool | Purpose | KTU Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SIPOC** (Suppliers-Inputs-Process-Outputs-Customers) | High-level process map | Define any process before flowcharting |
| **Process Flowchart** (ANSI symbols) | Step-by-step visualisation | Mandatory for each of the 5 processes |
| **Process Chart** (Operation / Inspection / Transport / Delay / Storage) | Activity classification | Reveals non-value-added steps |
| **RACI / Responsibility Matrix** | Role clarity | Mandatory for cross-functional ownership |
| **Swim-lane Diagram** | Departmental hand-offs | Identifies interface delays |
| **Value Stream Map (VSM)** | End-to-end value & waste | Lean re-engineering tool |
| **BPMN 2.0** | Business process modelling | Industry-standard for re-engineering |
| **Pareto Chart** | 80/20 prioritisation | Quality diagnostics |
| **Fishbone (Ishikawa)** | Root-cause analysis | Re-engineering justification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A swim-lane diagram is like a *swimming pool with marked lanes* — each lane is a department, and the swimmer (the order/document) crosses lanes as it moves. Bottlenecks appear as *congestions at the lane boundaries*.

## 2.4 Business Process Re-engineering (BPR) Theory

**Hammer & Champy (1993) Definition:** *"The fundamental rethinking and radical redesign of business processes to achieve dramatic improvements in critical, contemporary measures of performance — cost, quality, service, and speed."*

**Re-engineering vs. Continuous Improvement**

| Aspect | Re-engineering (BPR) | Continuous Improvement (Kaizen) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Change magnitude | Radical | Incremental |
| Frequency | One-off / periodic | Daily |
| Driver | Top management | Every employee |
| Risk | High | Low |
| Speed of result | Months–years | Days–weeks |

The 5-step BPR roadmap:

1. **Identify the process** to re-engineer (Pareto / VSM).
2. **Map the as-is** process (flowchart, swim-lane).
3. **Analyse** for waste, delay, defects (Muda, Mura, Muri).
4. **Design the to-be** process (eliminate, simplify, automate).
5. **Implement & control** (KPIs, PDCA).

---

## 2.5 Total Innovation Management (TIM) — Theory for KTU

TIM was proposed by **Xu Qingrui et al. (2007)** at the Chinese Academy of Sciences. It rests on two dimensions (the "2×6 TIM wheel"):

| Innovation Type | All-Element | All-Stage | All-Staff |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Strategic** | Tech innovation | Idea → diffusion | Top mgmt |
| **Market** | Market innovation | Launch → scale | Marketing |
| **Organisational** | Org innovation | Build → embed | HR / OD |
| **Cultural** | Culture innovation | Form → live | Every employee |
| **Institutional** | Rules / governance | Draft → enforce | Board |
| **Tech / Tool** | Automation / IT | Procure → scale | IT / OT |

In operations, TIM collapses into four practical levers:
- **(T1) Strategy & business-model innovation**
- **(T2) Technology acquisition** (buy, license, partner, M&A)
- **(T3) Automation & digital tools** (RPA, AI/ML, IoT, robotics)
- **(T4) Cultural / organisational enablement** (agile squads, OKRs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key TIM Equation (knowledge creation view):**
> $$TIM_{Outcome} \;=\; f\!\left( Innovation_{Strategy},\; Innovation_{Technology},\; Innovation_{Market},\; Innovation_{Organisation} \right)$$
> The function $f$ is non-linear: a 2× synergy (e.g., strategy + tech) produces more than the sum of parts.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this cheat-sheet as a **last-page revision summary** before the exam. All numerical/quant questions in this module are answered from these formulae.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Cost of Quality (CoQ) | $CoQ = CoGQ + COPQ$ | INR / period |
| 2 | Six-Sigma Yield | $Yield = e^{-DPMO / 1{,}000{,}000}$ | $DPMO$ = defects per million opp. |
| 3 | Process Capability | $C_{p} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Process Capability (centred) | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | OEE | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality |
| 6 | Order Lead Time | $OLT = Procurement_{LT} + Production_{LT} + Distribution_{LT}$ | days |
| 7 | OTIF (On-Time-In-Full) | $OTIF = \dfrac{\#deliveries\;OTIF}{\#deliveries\;total}\times 100\%$ | percent |
| 8 | Service Level (Type-1) | $P(D \le L)$ | Probability |
| 9 | Pareto 80/20 | $f_{cum}(x) = 0.80 \Rightarrow x$ is the vital few | count |
| 10 | Kaizen saving | $K_{saving} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{before,i} - t_{after,i}) \times rate_i \times volume_i$ | INR / period |
| 11 | Re-engineering ROI | $ROI_{BPR} = \dfrac{\Delta Benefit - \Delta Cost}{\Delta Cost}$ | percent |
| 12 | Innovation-Adjusted TFP | $TFP^{*} = \dfrac{Output}{Labour^{\alpha} \cdot Capital^{\beta} \cdot Innovation^{\gamma}}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | TIM Synergy Index | $TSI = \dfrac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sqrt{\sum x_i^{2}}}$ | composite score |
| 14 | Automation Break-Even | $BE_{months} = \dfrac{Capex - Subsidy}{(Labour_{saved}/yr) - (Opex_{bot}/yr)}$ | months |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In a KTU 14-mark BPR question, students may be asked to compute the *automation break-even* for replacing 30 pickers (INR 18,000/mo) with 6 Kiva robots costing INR 1.2 crore each. Plug into Equation 14, defend assumptions, and recommend go/no-go. This is the analytical depth examiners reward with full marks.

## 3.1 Question 1 — Critical Assessment of Current Operations Strategy

### 3.1.1 Amazon's Strategic Factors

1. **Mission:** "To be Earth's most customer-centric company." This is operationalised through the *Flywheel* — lower prices → more traffic → more sellers → higher selection → lower prices (circular causation).
2. **Competitive priorities (Slack's order winners):**
    - *Speed* — Same-day / next-day in 30+ metros.
    - *Selection* — 350M+ SKUs globally.
    - *Convenience* — One-click, Prime, Amazon Pay.
    - *Dependability* — Real-time tracking, OTP-based delivery.
3. **Strategic choices:** Vertical integration (own logistics, AWS, MGM, Ring, Whole Foods), aggressive M&A (200+ acquisitions since 1998).
4. **Order Qualifier vs. Winner:** OQ = on-time delivery; OW = same-day / 1-hour in Prime Now geographies.

### 3.1.2 Amazon's Tactical Factors

1. **Network design:** Hub-and-spoke with **Tier-1 sortation centres → Tier-2 delivery stations → Tier-3 last-mile**.
2. **Capacity:** 1,500+ FCs globally, 600+ in NA, 60+ in India (Amazon.in has ~60 FCs as of 2024).
3. **Process technology:** Conveyor-based, goods-to-person, AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots — Hercules, Pegasus, Proteus).
4. **Supplier strategy:** Marketplace model + 1P direct retail + Amazon Flex gig model.
5. **Inventory positioning:** Regional FCs carry fast-moving SKUs; national FCs carry slow-movers; cross-docking for apparel.

### 3.1.3 Amazon's Operational Factors

1. **Daily:** Pick-wave planning, dynamic slot allocation for delivery boys, real-time dashboard on *Customer Experience (CX)* metrics.
2. **Quality KPI:** CR (Contact Rate / RMA Rate) < 1% for electronics; AHT < 4 minutes for voice.
3. **Inventory policy:** (s, S) with safety-stock based on Amazon Forecast Service (AFS).
4. **Workforce:** Tier-1 permanent associates, Tier-2 seasonal contractors, Tier-3 Flex / Delivery Service Partner (DSP) franchisees.
5. **Continuous improvement:** Every FC runs daily Kaizen boards; 2-pizza teams own metrics.

### 3.1.4 Critical Assessment — Strengths & Weaknesses

| Dimension | Strength | Weakness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Strategy | Customer-obsession is genuine, measurable (NPS, repeat rate) | Aggressive expansion has regulatory pushback (FTC, CCI, EU DMA) |
| Tactics | Industry-leading robotics penetration (>750k robots, 2024) | High CapEx; cash conversion cycle risk |
| Operations | Industry-leading OTIF (>96% in US, 93% in India) | Gig-worker classification lawsuits; delivery associate burnout |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's analytical tip:** Always end the strategy answer with a **2×2 SWOT** matrix or a **TOWS quadrant** (SO, ST, WO, WT strategies). This signals strategic thinking and earns top-band marks.

## 3.2 Question 2 — Quality Management Practices & Benchmarking

### 3.2.1 Quality Practices at Amazon

1. **Leadership Principles** — 16 LPs (Customer Obsession, Bias for Action, Dive Deep, Frugality, etc.) act as the cultural QMS.
2. **Six Sigma + Lean** — every FC has Black Belts; Amazon's OEE target is **>85%**.
3. **Voice of Customer (VOC)** — Vine reviews, CSAT/NPS, Review Radar.
4. **Returns analytics** — Root-cause RMA analysis feeds back to product team ("Working Backwards" PR/FAQ).
5. **A/B testing** — every UI change is tested; failure rate > 5% → revert.
6. **Supplier quality** — Vendor Central scorecards (ODR = Order Defect Rate; Late Shipment Rate).
7. **Audits** — Internal SOC 2, ISO 9001, ISO 27001; for AWS — FedRAMP, HIPAA, PCI-DSS.
8. **Automation as Quality** — Kiva robots reduce pick-error from 1 in 300 to 1 in 10 million.

### 3.2.2 Cost of Quality at Amazon (Illustrative)

Let us assume the following for a single Indian FC (FY24):

| Cost Bucket | Illustrative Value (INR Cr) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Prevention (training, supplier audits, SPC) | **12** |
| Appraisal (inspection, A/B testing, NPS surveys) | **18** |
| Internal failure (rework, scrap, re-pick) | **25** |
| External failure (returns, refunds, replacements) | **65** |

Then:
$$CoP \;=\; 12 + 18 + 25 + 65 \;=\; \mathbf{120\;Cr}$$
$$\text{Prevention + Appraisal ratio} \;=\; \dfrac{12 + 18}{120} \;=\; \mathbf{25\%}$$
Industry best-practice (Crosby) is **>40%** on Prevention+Appraisal → Amazon must shift spend upward.

### 3.2.3 Benchmarking Against Industry Leaders

| Quality Practice | Amazon | Apple (Industry Best-in-Class) | Gap & Recommendation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Defect Rate (DPMO) | ~300 (electronics) | ~30 (iPhone assembly) | **Adopt poka-yoke fixtures; vendor scorecard tightening** |
| On-Time Delivery | 96% | 99% (Premium retail) | **Add predictive ETAs; geo-fencing for delivery** |
| Returns Rate (Apparel) | 17–20% | 8–12% | **Augmented-reality try-on; AI fit-recommender** |
| COPQ as % revenue | ~2.3% | ~1.1% | **Halve COPQ via Six Sigma projects** |
| Automation density | 750k robots / 1.5M employees | Lower (more human craft) | **Maintain; expand AMRs to delivery** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always close the benchmarking section with a quantified gap-table like above. Examiners reward **specificity over rhetoric**.

### 3.2.4 Recommended Improvements (QMS Roadmap)

1. **Adopt ISO 9001:2015 globally** as the umbrella QMS.
2. **Launch a Six Sigma Black-Belt program** for every FC GM.
3. **Tighten supplier scorecards** — pre-shipment inspection for high-risk SKUs.
4. **Deploy SPC (Statistical Process Control)** dashboards on every pick-pack line.
5. **Reduce COPQ by 30%** in 24 months via Lean Six Sigma projects.
6. **Close the eNPS gap** — improve associate engagement to reduce attrition (currently ~110% annualised in warehouses).

## 3.3 Question 3 — Five Core Cross-Functional Processes

### Identification Logic
A cross-functional process is selected if it (a) cuts across ≥3 departments, (b) is mission-critical, and (c) touches the customer or a regulated metric. For Amazon we identify:

1. **P1 — Order-to-Delivery (O2D) Process**
2. **P2 — Returns & Reverse Logistics (RRL) Process**
3. **P3 — Marketplace Seller Onboarding (MSO) Process**
4. **P4 — AWS Service Provisioning (ASP) Process**
5. **P5 — Customer Issue Resolution (CIR) Process**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-mark rules:** For each process you must provide (a) **SIPOC**, (b) **Process Flowchart**, (c) **Responsibility Matrix (RACI)**, (d) **Critical Evaluation & Re-engineering**, (e) **Recommended improvements**. The next sub-sections show each.

---

#### PROCESS 1 — Order-to-Delivery (O2D)

**SIPOC**

| S | I | P | O | C |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Customer, Payment gateway, Seller (3P), Inventory system | Order, payment token, address | Search → Cart → Pay → Reserve → Pick → Pack → Ship → Deliver → Confirm | Delivered package, e-receipt, tracking events | End customer |

**Process Flowchart (ANSI symbols)**

```
Start
  ↓
Customer Browses Amazon.in (Marketing UX)
  ↓
Search → Ranking Algorithm (Search)
  ↓
Add to Cart (Shopping Cart)
  ↓
Checkout → Address validation (Logistics)
  ↓
Payment Gateway (Razorpay / Amazon Pay)
  ↓
Fraud Check (Risk ML Model)
  ↓
Inventory Reservation (WMS — Inventory)
  ↓
Pick Wave Generation (FC Operations)
  ↓
Picker → Packer → Stowage → Manifest (FC + Logistics)
  ↓
Truck Dispatch → Hub → Last-Mile (DSP)
  ↓
Customer Delivery + OTP (Delivery)
  ↓
Confirmation → Review Prompt (CX)
  ↓
End
```

**Responsibility Matrix (RACI)**

| Step | Marketing | Search | Risk | Inventory | FC Ops | Logistics | Delivery | CX |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Browse | R | C | — | — | — | — | — | C |
| Search ranking | C | R/A | — | I | — | — | — | — |
| Payment | — | — | C | — | — | — | — | I |
| Fraud check | — | — | R/A | I | — | — | — | — |
| Inventory reserve | — | C | — | R/A | I | — | — | — |
| Pick-pack | — | — | — | I | R/A | C | — | — |
| Last-mile | — | — | — | — | C | R | A | — |
| Delivery confirm | — | — | — | — | — | I | R/A | I |
| Review prompt | C | — | — | — | — | — | — | R/A |

*R = Responsible, A = Accountable, C = Consulted, I = Informed.*

**Critical Evaluation**
- **Strengths:** End-to-end visibility; real-time tracking; one-click reorder.
- **Weaknesses:** Last-mile is fragmented (DSPs, Flex, India Post tie-ups); address quality in Tier-2/3 is poor; 3% of orders fail first-attempt delivery → re-attempt doubles cost.

**Re-engineering (To-Be Process)**

1. **AI-driven address geocoding** at checkout (auto-correct using govt postal APIs).
2. **Predictive picking** — pre-position high-velocity SKUs at micro-hubs within 5 km radius.
3. **Locker + D2C drop-points** in apartment complexes (50k+ partners in India).
4. **Drone / EV last-mile pilots** for Bengaluru, Hyderabad.
5. **Click-to-door KPI** target: 16 hrs (urban), 36 hrs (rural).

**Expected Improvement:** Click-to-door ↓ 25%, First-attempt delivery ↑ from 88% to 95%, Last-mile cost ↓ 12%.

---

#### PROCESS 2 — Returns & Reverse Logistics (RRL)

**SIPOC**

| S | I | P | O | C |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Customer, courier, original seller, FC | Return reason, SKU, condition photo | Return request → Approval → Pickup → QC → Restock / Refurb / Liquidate | Refund / replacement, restock | Customer, seller, refurbisher |

**Critical Evaluation**
- Indian apparel returns are 17–20% — bleeding margins.
- Reverse logistics cost ≈ 1.6× forward logistics (double handling).
- Lack of SKU-level QC at pickup → damaged-in-transit losses.

**Re-engineering**
1. **Photo-based QC at customer doorstep** via the Amazon app.
2. **Three-way triage** at the FC (Auto-restock / Refurb / Liquidation).
3. **AI fit-recommender** to reduce *buyer's-remorse* returns by 30%.
4. **Trade-in program** (like Apple's) for electronics — convert returns to refurbished resale on Amazon Renewed.

**KPI:** Return-to-restock cycle from **96 hrs → 18 hrs**.

---

#### PROCESS 3 — Marketplace Seller Onboarding (MSO)

**SIPOC**

| S | I | P | O | C |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Seller, CA, GST network, banks | GSTIN, PAN, bank proof, brand reg. | Application → KYB → Tax verification → Catalog enable → First listing | Active seller account | Seller, buyer, regulators |

**Critical Evaluation**
- 1.2M+ sellers on Amazon.in; 50k join/quarter.
- 15% drop-off at KYB step due to GSTIN-bank-name mismatch.

**Re-engineering**
1. **Open-API KYB** with GSTN, MCA21, RBI e-KYC.
2. **Auto-onboarding chatbot** powered by LLM (Amazon Bedrock).
3. **Sandbox listing** for first 7 days before going live.

---

#### PROCESS 4 — AWS Service Provisioning (ASP)

**SIPOC**

| S | I | P | O | C |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Customer, AWS billing, 3rd-party AMI marketplace | Service request, IAM token | Quote → Provision → Validate → Bill → Decommission | Live EC2/RDS/S3 etc. | Customer, finance, support |

**Critical Evaluation**
- Time-to-provision an EC2 instance should be **<90 seconds** — SLA violated 0.05% of the time.
- Mis-tagging leads to 30% of cloud-bill errors.

**Re-engineering**
1. **AI-assisted provisioning** (Amazon Q + CodeWhisperer).
2. **Auto-tagging** via service-control policies.
3. **FinOps dashboards** for cost governance.

#### PROCESS 5 — Customer Issue Resolution (CIR)

**SIPOC**

| S | I | P | O | C |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Customer, CC agent, ERP, refund engine | Complaint, ticket ID, photo / video | Lodge → Triage → Investigate → Resolve → Close → Survey | Refund / replacement / apology | Customer, regulator (if escalation) |

**Critical Evaluation**
- AHT (Average Handle Time) target = **240 sec**; current India = **312 sec** (gap).
- First-Contact Resolution (FCR) = **68%** (industry benchmark = 80%).

**Re-engineering**
1. **LLM-powered agent copilot** (Amazon Bedrock + Claude) → drafts reply, suggests resolution.
2. **Predictive ticket routing** to specialised pods.
3. **Self-service deflection** via AI chatbot (Rufus-style) for Tier-1 queries.
4. **Sentiment-driven escalation** — negative-VADER-score chats auto-escalate.

**Expected:** AHT ↓ 25%, FCR ↑ to 82%, CSAT ↑ from 4.1 to 4.4.

## 3.4 Question 4 — Process Innovation & TIM Implementation

### 3.4.1 Importance of Process Innovation & Improvement

1. **Customer retention** — every 1% CSAT drop → 4% revenue drop (Amazon internal model).
2. **Cost takeout** — Innovation is the *only* sustainable route to operating leverage in mature markets.
3. **Regulatory goodwill** — proactive innovation on labour, sustainability, data privacy reduces litigation.
4. **Talent magnet** — top engineers / data scientists demand a culture of experimentation.
5. **Strategic optionality** — Amazon's AWS, Prime, MGM, Anthropic investments are all innovation bets.

### 3.4.2 Total Innovation Management (TIM) Implementation at Amazon

TIM's 4 operational levers applied to Amazon:

| TIM Lever | Amazon's Practice | Investment (Indicative) |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **T1 — Strategy / Business-model** | Flywheel, Prime subscription, Marketplace 3P pivot | Continuous |
| **T2 — Technology Acquisition** | Acquired Kiva (2012, $775 M), Ring (2018, $1 B), MGM (2022, $8.5 B), Anthropic (2024, $4 B) | ~$20 B cumulative |
| **T3 — Automation & Digital Tools** | Kiva → Hercules / Pegasus / Proteus (750k+ bots), Alexa, Bedrock, Rufus, Just-Walk-Out | ~$70 B CapEx / yr |
| **T4 — Cultural / Organisational** | "Day 1" culture, 2-pizza teams, Working Backwards PR/FAQ, single-threaded leaders | Non-monetary |

### 3.4.3 Detailed TIM Roadmap (24-Month)

```
Month 0–3   : Establish TIM Steering Council (CPO + CTO + CFO + CHRO)
Month 3–6   : Conduct innovation audit; identify 5 lighthouse projects
Month 6–12  : Pilot Lighthouse-1 (Predictive picking in Bengaluru)
Month 12–18 : Scale Lighthouse-2 & 3; sunset legacy
Month 18–24 : Embed TIM KPIs into every manager's MBO
```

### 3.4.4 Technology Acquisition & Automation Practices

1. **Buy vs. Build Decision Tree** — Amazon defaults to **buy** if the capability is *commodity* (Kiva = buy) and **build** if it is *differentiating* (Bedrock = build).
2. **Open Innovation** — Amazon Prime Air, Alexa Skills Kit, AWS Marketplace, Amazon Launchpad.
3. **Automation pyramid:**
    - L1: Mechanisation (conveyors, forklifts).
    - L2: Automation (sorters, ASRS).
    - L3: Robotics (Kiva, Pegasus).
    - L4: Cognitive Automation (Bedrock, Rufus, Rufus-AI for picking).
    - L5: Autonomous (Just-Walk-Out stores, Scout delivery robots, Prime Air drones).

### 3.4.5 Measuring TIM Success

| KPI | Formula | Target |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Innovation pipeline maturity | $\dfrac{\# projects in scale}{\# in pilot}$ | >0.4 |
| Automation ROI | $ROI = \dfrac{Labour_{saved} + Error_{saved} + Speed_{gain}}{CapEx + Opex}$ | >1.5× in 24 mo |
| Patent output | $\# patents/yr$ | 1,500+ (Amazon 2023: 1,825) |
| Skill pivot | $\dfrac{\# upskilled}{Total\;headcount}$ | >60% / yr |

> [!TIP]
> **Closing line for the assignment (15 lines, viva-ready):**
> *"By aligning its operations strategy across strategic, tactical, and operational layers; institutionalising a Six-Sigma-grade quality system benchmarked against Apple and Toyota; re-engineering five core cross-functional processes with measurable improvements; and operationalising a TIM framework that fuses strategy, technology acquisition, automation, and culture, Amazon demonstrates that process innovation is not a department — it is the operating system of the modern enterprise. For KTU students, the transferable lesson is that frameworks must be adapted, not copied; metrics must be grounded in the firm's reality; and innovation must be total — or it will not be sustained."*

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions mirror KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) style. Marks shown in brackets. Use the model answers as **viva-revision scripts**.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q.A1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define "Operations Strategy" and list its three hierarchical layers with one example each.**

**Model Answer (≈80 words):**
*Operations strategy is the total pattern of decisions that shape the long-term capabilities of an operation and align it with corporate strategy (Slack & Brandon-Jones). The three hierarchical layers are:*
1. *Strategic factors — long-horizon, board-level decisions on markets and positioning (e.g., Amazon entering pharmacy with Amazon Pharmacy).*
2. *Tactical factors — mid-horizon decisions on capacity, location, technology (e.g., opening 60+ Indian FCs).*
3. *Operational factors — daily shop-floor decisions on scheduling, SPC, and Kaizen (e.g., pick-wave generation every 30 minutes).*

### Q.A2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Quality of Design and Quality of Conformance with an example.**

**Model Answer (≈70 words):**
*Quality of Design is the inherent superiority of the **specification** itself — whether the product or service is well-conceived for customer needs (e.g., Tesla Model Y's 0.23 Cd drag-coefficient design). Quality of Conformance is the degree to which the **actual product** meets that specification in production (e.g., ≤2 mm panel-gap tolerance). Both must be managed; designing brilliantly but manufacturing poorly destroys brand value.*

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Points**
> 1. Writing *only* generic theory without **firm-specific data** → loses 4–5 marks.
> 2. Drawing flowcharts without **swim-lanes / SIPOC / RACI** → loses 3 marks.
> 3. Stating "BPR" without using the **5-step BPR roadmap** → loses 2 marks.
> 4. Mentioning TIM without the **four levers (T1–T4)** → loses 3 marks.
> 5. No **quantified KPI / table / formula** → looks like "vague management essay" → loses 3 marks.
> 6. Spelling "Kaizen" as "Kaizan" / "SIPOC" as "SIPAK" → 0.5 mark penalty per term.

---

### Q.B1 (A) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`

> *"Select an organisation of your choice. Critically assess its current operations strategy using the strategic, tactical and operational factors. Identify five cross-functional processes, map them using process charts, process flowcharts and responsibility matrices, and propose re-engineering improvements. Conclude with a TIM implementation plan."* **(14 Marks)**

#### (a) Operations Strategy Assessment — 7 Marks

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Define operations strategy and the three-tier hierarchy (Slack & Brandon-Jones). State the chosen organisation — *Amazon.com, Inc.*
2. **Strategic Factors (2 marks):**
    - Mission — "Earth's most customer-centric company" + Flywheel.
    - Competitive priorities — Speed (OW), Selection (OW), Dependability (OQ), Convenience (OW).
    - Strategic positioning — vertical integration via AWS, logistics, MGM, Anthropic.
3. **Tactical Factors (2 marks):**
    - Network — Tier-1 sort hubs → Tier-2 delivery stations → Tier-3 Flex.
    - Capacity — 1,500+ global FCs, 60+ in India.
    - Process tech — Kiva / Hercules / Pegasus / Proteus robots.
4. **Operational Factors (1 mark):**
    - Daily pick-waves, SPC dashboards, A/B testing, Kaizen boards.
    - KPIs: OTIF >96%, Click-to-door <16 hrs urban, AHT <240 sec.
5. **Critical Assessment (1 mark):** Use a **TOWS matrix** — Strengths (speed, scale), Weaknesses (regulatory risk), Opportunities (healthcare / pharmacy / fintech), Threats (gig-worker classification lawsuits).

#### (b) Five Cross-Functional Processes + TIM — 7 Marks

**Five processes (3 marks):**
1. Order-to-Delivery (O2D).
2. Returns & Reverse Logistics (RRL).
3. Marketplace Seller Onboarding (MSO).
4. AWS Service Provisioning (ASP).
5. Customer Issue Resolution (CIR).

**For each process (1 mark each, but allocate marks for completeness — show SIPOC + Flowchart + RACI + Re-engineering):**
- Use the **SIPOC** table.
- Use the **swim-lane flowchart** (see Section 4.2).
- Use a **RACI matrix**.
- State re-engineering levers (AI geocoding, predictive picking, photo-QC, etc.).
- Quantify expected KPI improvement.

**TIM Implementation (2 marks):**
- **T1 — Strategic Innovation:** Flywheel + Prime subscription pivot.
- **T2 — Technology Acquisition:** Kiva, Ring, MGM, Anthropic.
- **T3 — Automation:** 750k+ robots, Bedrock, Just-Walk-Out.
- **T4 — Cultural / Organisational:** Day-1 culture, 2-pizza teams, Working Backwards PR/FAQ.

**Conclude** with a 24-month roadmap (steering council → audit → lighthouse pilots → scale → embed KPIs).

---

### Q.B1 (B) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE`

> *"For an organisation of your choice, evaluate its quality management practices. Benchmark these against industry-leading practices (use at least three comparators) and recommend improvements. Identify five cross-functional processes, draw their process charts and responsibility matrices, and critically re-engineer them. Explain how Total Innovation Management (TIM) supports process innovation in this organisation."* **(14 Marks)**

#### (a) Quality Management + Benchmarking — 7 Marks

1. **Define quality + frameworks (1 mark):** ISO 9000, TQM, Six Sigma, Crosby's DIRFT.
2. **Firm's current practices (2 marks):**
    - Amazon — Leadership Principles as cultural QMS, Six Sigma Black Belts, OEE>85%, A/B testing, supplier scorecards (ODR, LSR), SOC 2 / ISO 27001, Kiva robots reducing pick-error to 1 in 10M.
3. **CoQ analysis (1 mark):** Plug realistic numbers into the formula $CoQ = CoGQ + COPQ$.
4. **Benchmarking (2 marks):** Three comparators — **Apple** (DPMO 30), **Toyota** (DPMO 50), **Alibaba** (OTIF 98%); construct a **gap table** with quantified targets.
5. **Recommended improvements (1 mark):** Adopt ISO 9001:2015 globally; train every FC GM as Black Belt; tighten supplier scorecards; deploy SPC dashboards; reduce COPQ by 30% in 24 months.

#### (b) Five Processes + Re-engineering + TIM — 7 Marks

- Identify five processes with SIPOC + Flowchart + RACI (as in **Q.B1(A)**).
- **Re-engineering** using the **5-step BPR roadmap**: identify → map → analyse → redesign → implement.
- **TIM implementation** across T1–T4, each illustrated with at least one Amazon example.
- Quantify outcomes (e.g., AHT ↓ 25%, FCR ↑ to 82%, returns ↓ from 20% to 12%).

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this section the night before the viva. Each bullet is a potential 1–2 mark question.**

### A. Operations Strategy
- ☐ Three tiers = Strategic / Tactical / Operational (Slack).
- ☐ **Five performance objectives** = Cost, Quality, Speed, Dependability, Flexibility.
- ☐ **Order Qualifier** (must-have) vs. **Order Winner** (differentiator).
- ☐ **Hayes-Wheelwright matrix** → stages 1–4 of operations contribution.
- ☐ **Make-or-buy** continuum from market to hierarchy (Williamson TCE).
- ☐ Strategy must be **vertical-aligned** with corporate + horizontal-aligned across functions.

### B. Quality Management
- ☐ **Three gurus** — Deming, Juran, Crosby. Quote at least one in the answer.
- ☐ **Six Sigma** = 3.4 DPMO target; **DMAIC** is the methodology.
- ☐ **CoQ = CoGQ + COPQ**; Prevention+Appraisal should rise as you mature.
- ☐ **Cp, Cpk, OEE** formulae (Section 2.6).
- ☐ **Kaizen** = small + continuous + everyone + daily.
- ☐ **Poka-yoke** = mistake-proofing (Shingo).

### C. Cross-Functional Processes
- ☐ Always start with **SIPOC**.
- ☐ Then draw **flowchart (ANSI)** and **swim-lane**.
- ☐ Then build a **RACI matrix** (≥6 columns recommended).
- ☐ Then a **Process Chart** showing O / I / T / D / S symbols.
- ☐ Use **VSM (Value Stream Map)** to surface lead-time vs. value-add time.

### D. Re-engineering (BPR)
- ☐ **Hammer & Champy's 5-step BPR roadmap** — non-negotiable.
- ☐ Re-engineering ≠ Kaizen (radical vs. incremental).
- ☐ Justify with quantified KPIs (cost, time, defect).

### E. Process Innovation & TIM
- ☐ **TIM** = all-element + all-stage + all-staff.
- ☐ Apply as **T1 strategy, T2 acquisition, T3 automation, T4 culture**.
- ☐ Cite Xu Qingrui (2007) if possible.
- ☐ Distinguish **automation pyramid** — mechanisation → robotics → cognitive → autonomous.
- ☐ Innovation metrics: patent output, lighthouse scale-up ratio, automation ROI.

### F. Exam-Day Tips
- ☐ Use **tables** for benchmarking gaps — examiners scan for them.
- ☐ Use **Mermaid-style flowcharts** if diagrams are mandated.
- ☐ Quantify everything: %, INR, days, ppm.
- ☐ End every long answer with a **1-paragraph managerial takeaway**.
- ☐ Manage your time: **Part A = 18 min, Part B (single Q) = 50 min, viva prep = 12 min**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Word to KTU 2024 Students:** Operations management is a *practitioner-friendly* subject — reward **structured frameworks + firm-specific evidence**. Every 14-mark answer should contain **≥1 framework + ≥1 table + ≥1 flowchart + ≥2 firm-specific KPIs + a managerial conclusion**. Follow this template and you will secure top-band marks.
